topics.title: Investment Theory and Structure of Interest Rates: Time Value of Money - Future Value
Present Value
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Present Value

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Present Value (PV)**, also referred to as the *discounted value* or *present discounted value (PDV)*, is the current worth of a future sum of money or a stream of future cash flows, given a specified rate of return (discount rate). Mathematically, it is the inverse operation of future value compounding and represents the foundational principle of the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** in financial economics.

$$PV = \frac{FV}{(1+r)^n}$$

Where:
- $PV$ = Present Value
- $FV$ = Future Value (the nominal cash flow at time $n$)
- $r$ = Discount rate (per period, expressed as a decimal)
- $n$ = Number of periods (typically years)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Present Value is the cornerstone concept of Module 1 in *Introduction to Financial Economics*. Every subsequent topic — NPV, IRR, bond pricing, capital budgeting — depends on a clear understanding of discounting future cash flows back to their present worth.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine your grandfather promises to give you **₹1,00,000** when you turn **25 years old**. You are currently **15 years old**.

Would you value that promise at exactly ₹1,00,000 today? No — because:
1. You cannot spend it today.
2. Inflation will erode its purchasing power.
3. You could invest a smaller amount today and *grow* it to ₹1,00,000 in 10 years.

So if a bank offers **8% per annum** interest, you would ask: *"What amount today, if deposited, will become ₹1,00,000 in 10 years?"* The answer is approximately **₹46,319**. That ₹46,319 is the **Present Value** of ₹1,00,000 receivable in 10 years at 8% discount rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Intuition:** A rupee *today* is worth **more** than a rupee *tomorrow*, because today's rupee can be invested to earn returns. Present Value quantifies exactly *how much more*.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions Used in KTU Valuation Problems

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $r$ | Discount rate / Cost of capital | % per annum |
| $i$ | Effective annual rate | % per annum |
| $n$ | Number of periods | Years / Months |
| $PV$ | Present Value | Currency units (₹, $, etc.) |
| $FV$ | Future Value | Currency units |
| $PMT$ | Annuity payment | Currency units per period |
| $C_0, C_1, \ldots, C_n$ | Cash flow at time $t$ | Currency units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU examination scripts, always **state the units** explicitly (e.g., "₹ in lakhs", "rate per annum") to score full marks on definition-style questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic of Discounting — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The Present Value concept rests on **three pillars** of financial logic:

1. **Productivity of Capital:** Money invested today generates returns (interest, dividends, capital gains).
2. **Inflation Erosion:** The general price level rises over time, reducing purchasing power of nominal cash flows.
3. **Risk Premium:** Future cash flows carry uncertainty; rational investors demand compensation for bearing this risk.

**The Discounting Equation** therefore must embed the discount rate $r$, which conceptually bundles these three factors into a single **opportunity cost of capital**.

### 2.2 The Master Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is your **revision bible** for every PV-related KTU question. Memorize the structure, not just the formula.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | When to Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Single Cash Flow PV | $PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+r)^n}$ | $FV$ = future amount, $r$ = rate/period, $n$ = periods | One lump sum at a future date |
| 2 | Single Cash Flow FV | $FV = PV \cdot (1+r)^n$ | $PV$ = present amount | Compounding forward |
| 3 | PV of Ordinary Annuity | $PV = PMT \cdot \dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | $PMT$ = equal periodic payment | Equal payments at end of each period |
| 4 | PV of Annuity Due | $PV_{due} = PV_{ord} \cdot (1+r)$ | Same as above | Payments at *beginning* of each period |
| 5 | PV of Perpetuity | $PV = \dfrac{PMT}{r}$ | $PMT$ = constant payment | Infinite stream of equal payments |
| 6 | Growing Perpetuity | $PV = \dfrac{PMT}{r-g}$ | $g$ = constant growth rate | Infinite stream with constant growth |
| 7 | Growing Annuity | $PV = \dfrac{PMT}{r-g} \cdot \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{1+g}{1+r}\right)^n\right]$ | $g \neq r$ | Finite stream with constant growth |
| 8 | Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $C_t$ = net cash flow at $t$ | Capital budgeting decisions |
| 9 | Discount Factor | $DF_t = (1+r)^{-t}$ | Pure number $\in (0,1]$ | Multiplying future cash flow |
| 10 | Effective Annual Rate | $EAR = (1+\dfrac{r_{nom}}{m})^m - 1$ | $m$ = compounding freq./year | Convert nominal to effective rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Condition:** For perpetuity and growing perpetuity formulas, the denominator $r-g$ **must** be strictly positive. If $r \leq g$, the present value is **undefined** or **infinite** — a frequent KTU examiner trap!

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Economics

For KTU B.Tech students, Present Value is not abstract theory — it powers:

- **Capital Budgeting:** Should your firm buy a ₹50 lakh CNC machine or a ₹30 lakh one? Compare NPVs.
- **Equipment Replacement Decisions:** When is it economically optimal to replace machinery?
- **Project Appraisal:** Evaluating long-term infrastructure (bridges, power plants, software systems).
- **Loan Amortization:** Calculating equated monthly installments (EMIs) on home/car loans.
- **Valuation of Firms:** Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) models used in mergers and IPOs.

> [!NOTE]
> A 2024 KTU Module 1 typical question often links PV to an **engineering project** — e.g., "A solar plant generates ₹X for $n$ years; find its present worth at discount rate $r$." Always identify whether cash flows are an *annuity* or *uneven stream* before selecting a formula.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Single Cash Flow PV Formula

We begin with the compound interest identity (the **Future Value** formula), which is itself a recursive identity proven by induction:

$$
FV = PV \cdot (1+r)^n
$$

**Step 1 — Start with the simplest case:** $n = 1$ (one period)
$$
FV_1 = PV \cdot (1+r)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply recursively:** $n = 2$
$$
FV_2 = FV_1 \cdot (1+r) = PV \cdot (1+r) \cdot (1+r) = PV \cdot (1+r)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Generalize to $n$ periods by induction:** Assume $FV_{n-1} = PV \cdot (1+r)^{n-1}$. Then
$$
FV_n = FV_{n-1} \cdot (1+r) = PV \cdot (1+r)^{n-1} \cdot (1+r) = PV \cdot (1+r)^n
$$

**Step 4 — Invert the relationship** to obtain Present Value:
$$
PV = \frac{FV}{(1+r)^n}
$$

This is the **Single Cash Flow Present Value Formula**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Ordinary Annuity PV Formula

An **ordinary annuity** is a sequence of $n$ equal payments $PMT$ made at the **end** of each period. We discount each payment back to time 0 and sum:

$$
PV = \frac{PMT}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{PMT}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{PMT}{(1+r)^3} + \cdots + \frac{PMT}{(1+r)^n}
$$

**Step 1 — Factor out $PMT$:**
$$
PV = PMT \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{n} (1+r)^{-t}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize this as a finite geometric series** with first term $a = (1+r)^{-1}$ and common ratio $q = (1+r)^{-1}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the geometric series summation formula:**
$$
S_n = a \cdot \frac{1 - q^n}{1 - q}
$$

Substituting $a$ and $q$:
$$
S_n = (1+r)^{-1} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{1 - (1+r)^{-1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator:**
$$
1 - (1+r)^{-1} = \frac{(1+r) - 1}{1+r} = \frac{r}{1+r}
$$

**Step 5 — Invert and multiply:**
$$
S_n = \frac{1}{(1+r)} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{\frac{r}{1+r}} = \frac{1}{(1+r)} \cdot \frac{1+r}{r} \cdot \left[1 - (1+r)^{-n}\right]
$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $(1+r)$:**
$$
S_n = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}
$$

**Step 7 — Multiply by $PMT$** to obtain the final annuity PV formula:
$$
PV_{\text{ordinary annuity}} = PMT \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Perpetuity Formula

A **perpetuity** is an annuity with $n \to \infty$. Take the limit of the annuity formula as $n \to \infty$:

$$
PV = \lim_{n \to \infty} PMT \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}
$$

**Step 1 — Examine the term $(1+r)^{-n}$ as $n \to \infty$:** Since $\vert 1+r \vert > 1$ for any $r > 0$, we have $(1+r)^{-n} \to 0$.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$
PV = PMT \cdot \frac{1 - 0}{r} = \frac{PMT}{r}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation of the Growing Perpetuity Formula

A growing perpetuity pays $PMT$ in period 1, $PMT \cdot (1+g)$ in period 2, and so on, forever. The cash flow at time $t$ is $PMT \cdot (1+g)^{t-1}$.

**Step 1 — Write the PV as an infinite series:**
$$
PV = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{PMT \cdot (1+g)^{t-1}}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 2 — Factor:**
$$
PV = \frac{PMT}{1+r} \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1+g}{1+r}\right)^{t-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the infinite geometric series** with ratio $q = \frac{1+g}{1+r}$, which converges if $\vert q \vert < 1$, i.e., $g < r$.

**Step 4 — Apply the infinite geometric sum formula $S_{\infty} = \frac{a}{1-q}$:**
$$
S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1+g}{1+r}} = \frac{1}{\frac{(1+r)-(1+g)}{1+r}} = \frac{1+r}{r-g}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**
$$
PV = \frac{PMT}{1+r} \cdot \frac{1+r}{r-g} = \frac{PMT}{r-g}
$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix Style)

**Problem:** A construction project requires an initial investment of ₹5,00,000 today and will generate cash inflows of ₹1,50,000 at the end of each year for **5 years**. The discount rate is **10% per annum**. Find the NPV and determine whether the project is acceptable.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables:**
- $C_0 = -5{,}00{,}000$ (initial outflow)
- $PMT = 1{,}50{,}000$
- $r = 0.10$
- $n = 5$

**Step 2 — Calculate the discount factor each year:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_1 &= (1.10)^{-1} = 0.9091 \\
DF_2 &= (1.10)^{-2} = 0.8264 \\
DF_3 &= (1.10)^{-3} = 0.7513 \\
DF_4 &= (1.10)^{-4} = 0.6830 \\
DF_5 &= (1.10)^{-5} = 0.6209
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Discount each cash flow and sum (Uneven Method):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 1{,}36{,}365 \\
PV_2 &= 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 1{,}23{,}960 \\
PV_3 &= 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 1{,}12{,}695 \\
PV_4 &= 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 1{,}02{,}450 \\
PV_5 &= 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6209 = \phantom{0}93{,}135 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the discounted inflows:**
$$
\sum PV_t = 1{,}36{,}365 + 1{,}23{,}960 + 1{,}12{,}695 + 1{,}02{,}450 + 93{,}135 = 5{,}68{,}605
$$

**Step 5 — Compute NPV:**
$$
NPV = -5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}68{,}605 = +68{,}605
$$

**Step 6 — Decision:** Since $NPV = +₹68{,}605 > 0$, the project **should be accepted**.

**Verification using the Annuity Formula directly:**
$$
PV = 1{,}50{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10} = 1{,}50{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.6209}{0.10} = 1{,}50{,}000 \cdot 3.7908 = 5{,}68{,}620
$$

(Tiny difference due to rounding of discount factors.) ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cash Flow Time Line (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the time-value structure of cash flows used in PV calculations. Note that **all node IDs are alphanumeric** and **labels are plain text without markdown formatting**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT0["Time t equals 0 Today PV"] --> nodeT1["Time t equals 1 Discount by factor 1 over 1 plus r"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeT2["Time t equals 2 Discount by factor 1 over 1 plus r squared"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3["Time t equals 3 Discount by factor 1 over 1 plus r cubed"]
    nodeT3 --> nodeTn["Time t equals n Discount by factor 1 over 1 plus r to the n"]
    nodeT0 --> nodeCF0["Cash Flow C0 Initial Outlay Negative"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeCF1["Cash Flow C1 Period 1 Inflow Positive"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeCF2["Cash Flow C2 Period 2 Inflow Positive"]
    nodeT3 --> nodeCF3["Cash Flow C3 Period 3 Inflow Positive"]
    nodeTn --> nodeCFn["Cash Flow Cn Period n Inflow Positive"]
    nodeCF0 --> nodeNPV["Sum all Discounted Cash Flows to get NPV"]
    nodeCF1 --> nodeNPV
    nodeCF2 --> nodeNPV
    nodeCF3 --> nodeNPV
    nodeCFn --> nodeNPV
```

### 4.2 PV Decision Flowchart for KTU Exam Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Identify Cash Flow Pattern"] --> branchA{"Is it a single lump sum?"}
    branchA -- Yes --> pathA1["Use PV equals FV divided by 1 plus r to the n"]
    branchA -- No --> branchB{"Are payments equal each period?"}
    branchB -- Yes --> branchC{"Payments at end or beginning of period?"}
    branchC -- End --> pathB1["Use Ordinary Annuity PV formula"]
    branchC -- Beginning --> pathB2["Use Annuity Due PV equals Ordinary PV times 1 plus r"]
    branchB -- No --> branchD{"Is it an uneven stream with different amounts?"}
    branchD -- Yes --> pathC1["Discount each cash flow separately and sum"]
    branchD -- No --> branchE{"Is there a constant growth rate g?"}
    branchE -- Yes --> pathD1["Use Growing Annuity or Growing Perpetuity"]
    branchE -- No --> pathE1["Re-examine the problem statement carefully"]
```

### 4.3 Relationship Map of PV Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    baseA["Time Value of Money Core Concept"]
    baseA --> childA["Compounding Forward FV"]
    baseA --> childB["Discounting Backward PV"]
    childB --> subA["Single Sum PV"]
    childB --> subB["Annuity PV"]
    childB --> subC["Perpetuity PV"]
    subB --> detailA["Ordinary Annuity End of Period"]
    subB --> detailB["Annuity Due Start of Period"]
    subC --> detailC["Level Perpetuity PMT over r"]
    subC --> detailD["Growing Perpetuity PMT over r minus g"]
    subA --> finaluse["Engineering Project Appraisal NPV IRR Loan EMI Bond Pricing"]
    detailA --> finaluse
    detailB --> finaluse
    detailC --> finaluse
    detailD --> finaluse
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each box represents a concept node; arrows represent logical dependency. When solving a KTU problem, traverse this map from the top to identify the correct formula branch to apply.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

Define the term **Present Value**. Explain why a rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Present Value (PV) is the current worth of a future sum of money or a stream of future cash flows, computed by discounting them back to the present at an appropriate discount rate (often denoted $r$). Mathematically,

$$
PV = \frac{FV}{(1+r)^n}
$$

where $FV$ is the future cash flow, $r$ is the discount rate per period, and $n$ is the number of periods.

**Why a rupee today > a rupee tomorrow (1.5 marks):** This principle arises from three economic realities:
1. **Opportunity to invest** — money received today can earn interest, dividends, or capital gains.
2. **Inflation** — the purchasing power of money falls over time as prices rise.
3. **Risk and uncertainty** — future receipts are not guaranteed; default and delay risks exist.

Hence, to make a rational comparison, future amounts must be **discounted** to their present worth.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary Paper]**

Distinguish between **Present Value** and **Net Present Value**. State the NPV decision rule.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Present Value (PV) | Net Present Value (NPV) |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------------|
| Meaning | Current worth of one or more future cash flows | PV of *all* future cash flows minus initial investment |
| Formula | $PV = \sum_{t} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $NPV = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ |
| Decision Use | Comparing cash flow streams | Accept/reject projects |

**Decision Rule (1.5 marks):**
- If $NPV > 0$: **Accept** the project (it adds value).
- If $NPV < 0$: **Reject** the project (it destroys value).
- If $NPV = 0$: Indifferent — the project breaks even in PV terms.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

A manufacturing firm is evaluating two mutually exclusive machines:

- **Machine X:** Costs ₹8,00,000 today and produces annual savings of ₹2,50,000 for **6 years**. Salvage value at end of year 6 is ₹1,00,000.
- **Machine Y:** Costs ₹10,00,000 today and produces annual savings of ₹3,00,000 for **6 years**. Salvage value at end of year 6 is ₹2,00,000.

The firm's cost of capital is **12% per annum**. Using NPV analysis, determine which machine the firm should purchase.

#### **(a) Compute the NPV of Machine X (7 marks).**

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**
- Initial outflow: $C_0 = -8{,}00{,}000$
- Annual savings: $PMT = 2{,}50{,}000$ for $n = 6$ years (ordinary annuity, end-of-year)
- Salvage at year 6: $SV = 1{,}00{,}000$
- Discount rate: $r = 0.12$

**Step 2 — Calculate PV of the annuity (6 years at 12%):**
$$
PV_{\text{annuity}} = 2{,}50{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-6}}{0.12}
$$

Compute $(1.12)^6$:
$$
(1.12)^6 = 1.97382
$$

So $(1.12)^{-6} = 1/1.97382 = 0.50663$.

$$
PV_{\text{annuity}} = 2{,}50{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.50663}{0.12} = 2{,}50{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.49337}{0.12} = 2{,}50{,}000 \cdot 4.1114 = 10{,}27{,}850
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate PV of salvage value:**
$$
PV_{SV} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^6} = 1{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.50663 = 50{,}663
$$

**Step 4 — Compute NPV of Machine X:**
$$
NPV_X = -8{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}27{,}850 + 50{,}663 = +2{,}78{,}513
$$

> **Result:** $NPV_X = +₹2{,}78{,}513$. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b) Compute the NPV of Machine Y and recommend (7 marks).**

**Step 1 — Variables:**
- $C_0 = -10{,}00{,}000$
- $PMT = 3{,}00{,}000$, $n = 6$, $r = 0.12$
- $SV = 2{,}00{,}000$

**Step 2 — PV of annuity:**
$$
PV_{\text{annuity}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \cdot 4.1114 = 12{,}33{,}420
$$

**Step 3 — PV of salvage:**
$$
PV_{SV} = 2{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.50663 = 1{,}01{,}326
$$

**Step 4 — NPV of Machine Y:**
$$
NPV_Y = -10{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}33{,}420 + 1{,}01{,}326 = +3{,}34{,}746
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

Since both NPVs are positive, both machines are individually acceptable. However, the machines are **mutually exclusive**, so we must compare directly.

$$
NPV_Y - NPV_X = 3{,}34{,}746 - 2{,}78{,}513 = +56{,}233 > 0
$$

> **Recommendation:** Purchase **Machine Y** because it has the higher NPV by ₹56,233, adding more shareholder value. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

A software company is valuing a perpetual licensing stream. The contract will generate ₹5,00,000 in revenue at the end of year 1, and revenues are expected to grow at **4% per annum** forever. The appropriate discount rate is **11% per annum**.

#### **(a) Compute the present value of this growing perpetuity (7 marks).**

**Step 1 — Identify variables for Growing Perpetuity formula:**
- $PMT_1 = 5{,}00{,}000$ (payment at end of year 1)
- $g = 0.04$ (growth rate)
- $r = 0.11$ (discount rate)
- Type: Growing perpetuity (infinite stream)

**Step 2 — Check the validity condition:**
$$
r > g \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.11 > 0.04 \quad \checkmark
$$

The condition holds; the perpetuity is well-defined.

**Step 3 — Apply the Growing Perpetuity PV formula:**
$$
PV = \frac{PMT_1}{r - g} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{0.11 - 0.04} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{0.07}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute final answer:**
$$
PV = 71{,}42{,}857.14 \approx ₹71{,}42{,}857
$$

> **Result:** The contract is worth approximately **₹71.43 lakhs** today. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b) If the discount rate rises to 13%, recompute the PV and interpret. What happens if $g = r$? (7 marks).**

**Step 1 — Recompute at $r = 0.13$:**
$$
PV_{\text{new}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{0.13 - 0.04} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{0.09} = 55{,}55{,}555.56 \approx ₹55{,}55{,}556
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**
- The present value **fell** from ₹71.43 lakhs to ₹55.56 lakhs, a drop of ₹15.87 lakhs.
- This shows that **PV is highly sensitive to the discount rate** — a 2 percentage point increase caused a ~22% decline in PV.
- The intuition: a higher discount rate makes future cash flows less valuable in today's terms.

**Step 3 — Examine the case $g = r$:**
$$
PV = \frac{PMT_1}{r - g} = \frac{PMT_1}{0} \to \infty
$$

**Interpretation:** If growth equals the discount rate, the present value becomes **infinite**. The economic meaning is that the future stream grows fast enough to keep its discounted contribution constant forever — no finite lump sum today can replicate it. This is why we require **strictly $r > g$** for a meaningful PV.

> **Result:** $PV_{r=0.13} = ₹55{,}55{,}556$. The infinite result when $g = r$ is a mathematical singularity; in practice it means the contract has unbounded value relative to its first-year cash flow. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in PV Questions — KTU 2024 Pattern:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to discount the initial outlay correctly:** $C_0$ occurs at time 0, so it is NOT divided by $(1+r)$. Students who write $-C_0/(1+r)$ lose 1–2 marks immediately.
>
> 2. **Mixing annuity-due and ordinary annuity assumptions:** If the problem says "at the *beginning* of each year," you MUST use $PV_{\text{due}} = PV_{\text{ord}} \cdot (1+r)$. This is worth up to **2 marks** in long-answer valuation.
>
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** Cash outflows are **negative**, inflows are **positive**. Mixing these up gives wrong NPV and wrong accept/reject decision — examiners deduct up to **3 marks**.
>
> 4. **Rounding $(1+r)^n$ early:** Carry at least 5 decimal places throughout, only round at the final step. Early rounding can swing NPV by thousands of rupees in long-duration projects.
>
> 5. **Using the perpetuity formula where annuity is needed:** Always check whether $n$ is given. If finite $n$ is provided, use the annuity formula; perpetuity requires $n = \infty$ explicitly.
>
> 6. **Not stating the decision rule explicitly:** When asked "Should you accept the project?", a numerical NPV alone is incomplete — **always write the conclusion in words** ("NPV is positive, hence the project is financially viable and should be accepted") for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Present Value (PV)** = current worth of a future cash flow; reflects the **time value of money**.
- The basic single-cash-flow identity is $PV = FV \cdot (1+r)^{-n}$ — the **discount factor** is $(1+r)^{-n}$.
- An **ordinary annuity** pays at the **end** of each period; an **annuity due** pays at the **beginning**. The latter is $(1+r)$ times larger in PV.
- **Perpetuity** = infinite annuity; PV = $PMT/r$. Requires $r > 0$.
- **Growing perpetuity** = infinite stream with constant growth $g$; PV = $PMT_1 / (r-g)$. Requires $r > g$ strictly.
- **NPV** = sum of all discounted cash flows including the initial outlay. Decision rule: **accept if NPV > 0**.
- **Effective Annual Rate (EAR)** is the true compounded return and is always greater than the nominal rate when compounding is more frequent than annually.
- **Discount rate sensitivity:** A small change in $r$ can dramatically change PV, especially for long-duration cash flows — examiners frequently test this.
- **Units and consistency:** Always ensure $r$, $n$, and compounding frequency refer to the same time unit (months vs. years mismatch is a classic trap).
- **Five core formulas** to memorize for KTU Module 1 PV problems: Single sum, Ordinary annuity, Annuity due, Perpetuity, Growing perpetuity.
- **Geometric series mastery** is essential — every annuity/perpetuity formula is fundamentally a geometric sum.
- **Engineering applications** are favored in KTU papers — frame your answers with reference to project appraisal, equipment replacement, or EMI calculations wherever possible.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Present Value of Annuity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Present Value of Annuity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **annuity** is a sequence of equal payments made at regular intervals over a fixed period of time. The intervals can be monthly, quarterly, semi-annually, or annually, and the payments can be either cash inflows (receipts) or cash outflows (payments). Common real-world examples include **home loan EMIs (Equated Monthly Instalments)**, insurance premiums, pension payments, lease rentals, and bond coupon payments.

The **Present Value (PV) of an Annuity** is the current worth of all future cash flows of the annuity, discounted back to the present at a specified **discount rate (or required rate of return, $r$)**. Mathematically, it represents the single lump-sum amount that, if invested today at rate $r$, would generate exactly the same stream of future cash flows as the annuity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The PV of annuity is a cornerstone concept in Module 1 because it underpins loan amortization, capital budgeting, bond pricing, and retirement planning. Every cash flow problem in KTU exams reduces to either PV of annuity, PV of perpetuity, PV of lump sum, or a combination of these three.

## 1.2 Types of Annuities (KTU Classification)

| Type | Timing of Payment | KTU Exam Frequency |
|------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Ordinary Annuity (Annuity in Arrears)** | End of each period | **Very High** |
| **Annuity Due (Annuity in Advance)** | Beginning of each period | High |
| **Deferred Annuity** | Starts after a deferral period | Moderate |
| **Perpetuity** | Infinite period | High |
| **Growing Annuity** | Payments grow at constant rate $g$ | Low |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Lottery vs. EMI Choice:**
> Imagine your uncle promises to pay you **₹1,00,000 every year for 10 years** starting one year from today. He offers you two options:
> - **Option A:** Receive ₹10,00,000 lump sum today.
> - **Option B:** Receive ₹1,00,000 every year for 10 years.
>
> Which is more valuable? The answer is: **"It depends on the interest rate."** If your bank offers 10% per annum on fixed deposits, ₹1,00,000 received next year is worth only ₹90,909 today. The PV of Annuity formula precisely quantifies this — it converts the future stream of ₹1,00,000 payments into a single *equivalent* present-day lump sum. **If Option B's PV > ₹10,00,000, choose Option B; otherwise, choose Option A.**

This is the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** principle in action: *a rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow* because today's rupee can be invested to earn returns.

## 1.4 Why Present Value of Annuity Matters in Engineering Economics

For B.Tech students, the PV of annuity is essential in:

- **Equipment Replacement Decisions:** Converting a stream of operating costs into a single equivalent cost.
- **Lease vs. Buy Analysis:** Comparing a single purchase price against the PV of lease rentals.
- **Project Appraisal:** Evaluating projects with uniform annual cash inflows (e.g., a power plant with 20 years of ₹50 lakh annual revenue).
- **Loan Valuation:** Determining the fair price of a bond paying fixed coupons.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The PV of an annuity is derived by **summing the present values of individual cash flows**. Let $C$ be the periodic cash flow, $r$ be the discount rate per period, and $n$ be the total number of periods.

### Step 1: Identify Each Future Cash Flow
Each payment of $C$ occurs at the end of period $t$, where $t = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$.

### Step 2: Discount Each Cash Flow to Present (Time $t = 0$)
The PV of the payment at time $t$ is:
$$\frac{C}{(1+r)^t}$$

### Step 3: Sum All Discounted Cash Flows
$$PV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t}$$

### Step 4: Recognize the Geometric Series
This sum is a **finite geometric progression** with first term $a = \frac{C}{1+r}$ and common ratio $q = \frac{1}{1+r}$. Using the geometric series summation formula:
$$S_n = a \cdot \frac{1-q^n}{1-q}$$

### Step 5: Simplify into Closed-Form
After algebraic simplification, we arrive at the standard annuity PV formula.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical problem.**

| Formula Name | Equation | Where Used |
|--------------|----------|-----------|
| PV of Ordinary Annuity | $PV = C \cdot \dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | Payments at **end** of period |
| PV of Annuity Due | $PV_{due} = C \cdot \dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r} \cdot (1+r)$ | Payments at **beginning** of period |
| PV of Deferred Annuity | $PV_{def} = C \cdot \dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r} \cdot (1+r)^{-d}$ | First payment after $d$ periods of delay |
| PV of Perpetuity | $PV = \dfrac{C}{r}$ | Payments continue **forever** ($n \to \infty$) |
| PV of Growing Perpetuity | $PV = \dfrac{C_1}{r-g}$ | Payments grow at rate $g$, $g < r$ |
| PV of Lump Sum | $PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+r)^n}$ | Single future amount |
| Effective Annual Rate (EAR) | $EAR = \left(1+\dfrac{r_{nom}}{m}\right)^m - 1$ | Converting nominal to effective rate |
| Annuity Payment from PV | $C = \dfrac{PV \cdot r}{1-(1+r)^{-n}}$ | EMI / loan amortization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** The expression $\dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ is called the **Present Value Interest Factor of Annuity (PVIFA)** and is denoted as $P(r,n)$. Many KTU problems give this factor directly in the question — use it as $PV = C \cdot P(r,n)$ to save calculation time.

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions & Special Cases

- **When $r \to 0$:** PV approaches $n \cdot C$ (the simple sum, since discounting has no effect).
- **When $n \to \infty$:** Ordinary annuity reduces to perpetuity, $PV = C/r$.
- **When $r$ increases:** PV decreases (inverse relationship — higher discount rate makes future money less valuable today).
- **When $n$ increases:** PV increases (more payments means more total value).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Civil Engineering:** Computing the capitalized cost of a bridge with perpetual maintenance fees.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Evaluating the lifecycle cost of solar panels with uniform annual savings.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Justifying machinery purchase via annual savings comparison.
- **Computer Science:** Lease vs. buy decisions for data center hardware.
- **Financial Engineering:** Pricing mortgage-backed securities and structured annuities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: PV of Ordinary Annuity

Let $C$ = periodic cash flow, $r$ = discount rate per period, $n$ = number of periods.

**Starting Point — Sum of Discounted Cash Flows:**
$$PV = \frac{C}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{C}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{C}{(1+r)^3} + \cdots + \frac{C}{(1+r)^n}$$

**Step 1:** Factor out the common term $C$ from the sum.
$$PV = C \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{1}{(1+r)^3} + \cdots + \frac{1}{(1+r)^n} \right]$$

**Step 2:** Recognize the bracketed expression as a geometric series with:
- First term: $a = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^1}$
- Common ratio: $q = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}$

**Step 3:** Apply the geometric series sum formula $S_n = a \cdot \dfrac{1-q^n}{1-q}$.

$$PV = C \cdot \frac{1}{(1+r)} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1}{(1+r)}\right)^n}{1 - \frac{1}{(1+r)}}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the denominator $1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}$.

$$1 - \frac{1}{(1+r)} = \frac{(1+r) - 1}{(1+r)} = \frac{r}{(1+r)}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into the expression.

$$PV = C \cdot \frac{1}{(1+r)} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{\frac{r}{(1+r)}}$$

**Step 6:** The $(1+r)$ terms cancel out (numerator and denominator).

$$PV = C \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

**Final Result — The KTU Standard Formula:**
$$\boxed{PV = C \cdot \frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}}$$

> This derivation is **scoring gold** in KTU exams — write it step-by-step for full 7 marks.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: PV of Annuity Due

An annuity due has payments at the **beginning** of each period. Therefore, the first payment is at time $t=0$ (no discounting), the second at $t=1$, and so on, with the last payment at $t=n-1$.

**Starting Point:**
$$PV_{due} = C + \frac{C}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{C}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{C}{(1+r)^{n-1}}$$

**Step 1:** Factor out $C$.
$$PV_{due} = C \cdot \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{(1+r)} + \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{(1+r)^{n-1}} \right]$$

**Step 2:** This is a geometric series with $a = 1$ and $q = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}$, having $n$ terms.

$$PV_{due} = C \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1}{(1+r)}\right)^n}{1 - \frac{1}{(1+r)}}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the same simplification as before.

$$PV_{due} = C \cdot \frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r} \cdot (1+r)$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{PV_{due} = C \cdot \frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r} \cdot (1+r)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** PV of annuity due = PV of ordinary annuity $\times (1+r)$. This is because each payment is received one period earlier, so each is worth $(1+r)$ times more.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: PV of Perpetuity

A perpetuity is an annuity with $n \to \infty$.

**Starting Point — Take the limit of the ordinary annuity formula as $n \to \infty$:**

$$PV_{perp} = \lim_{n \to \infty} C \cdot \frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

**Step 1:** Analyze the behavior of $(1+r)^{-n}$ as $n \to \infty$. Since $(1+r) > 1$, the term $(1+r)^{-n} \to 0$.

$$PV_{perp} = C \cdot \frac{1 - 0}{r}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{PV_{perp} = \frac{C}{r}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This elegant formula has been used to value **consol bonds** issued by the British government and to capitalize perpetual maintenance costs of infrastructure like dams and highways.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Numerical Example

**Problem:** Find the PV of an ordinary annuity paying ₹50,000 at the end of each year for 8 years, discounted at 10% per annum.

**Given:**
- $C = 50{,}000$
- $r = 0.10$
- $n = 8$

**Step 1:** Apply the formula.
$$PV = 50000 \cdot \frac{1-(1.10)^{-8}}{0.10}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(1.10)^8$.

$$(1.10)^8 = 2.14358881$$

**Step 3:** Compute the reciprocal.
$$(1.10)^{-8} = \frac{1}{2.14358881} = 0.46650738$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator.
$$1 - 0.46650738 = 0.53349262$$

**Step 5:** Divide by $r$.
$$\frac{0.53349262}{0.10} = 5.3349262$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by $C$.
$$PV = 50000 \times 5.3349262 = 2{,}66{,}746.31$$

**Result:** $PV = ₹2{,}66{,}746.31$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Ordinary Annuity | Annuity Due | Perpetuity |
|---------|-----------------|-------------|------------|
| Timing | End of period | Beginning | End (forever) |
| First Payment | At $t=1$ | At $t=0$ | At $t=1$ |
| Formula | $C \cdot P(r,n)$ | $C \cdot P(r,n) \cdot (1+r)$ | $C / r$ |
| PV Magnitude | Baseline | **Higher** than ordinary | Highest if $n$ large |
| Real Example | Bond coupons | Insurance premium | Preferred stock dividends |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cash Flow Timeline — Ordinary Annuity vs. Annuity Due

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0["Time 0 - Today"]
    T1["Time 1 - End Yr 1"]
    T2["Time 2 - End Yr 2"]
    T3["Time 3 - End Yr 3"]
    TN["Time n - End Yr n"]
    D0["d periods delay"]
    D1["Defer start"]

    T0 -.-> T1
    T1 -.-> T2
    T2 -.-> T3
    T3 -.-> TN

    subgraph OAN["ORDINARY ANNUITY - End of period"]
        O1["Payment C at t=1"]
        O2["Payment C at t=2"]
        O3["Payment C at t=3"]
        ON["Payment C at t=n"]
    end

    subgraph ADU["ANNUITY DUE - Beginning of period"]
        A1["Payment C at t=0"]
        A2["Payment C at t=1"]
        A3["Payment C at t=2"]
        AN["Payment C at t=n-1"]
    end

    subgraph DEF["DEFERRED ANNUITY - Delay d"]
        DF1["No payment during d periods"]
        DF2["First C at t=d+1"]
        DF3["Last C at t=d+n"]
    end
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — PV Annuity Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Identify Annuity Type"] --> B{"Which Type?"}
    B -->|Ordinary| C["Use Formula: C times PVIFA r,n"]
    B -->|Due| D["Use Formula: C times PVIFA r,n times 1+r"]
    B -->|Perpetuity| E["Use Formula: C divided by r"]
    B -->|Deferred| F["Step 1 Compute Ordinary PV"]
    F --> G["Step 2 Discount PV by factor 1+r to power d"]
    B -->|Growing| H["Use Growing Annuity Formula"]
    C --> I["Apply Substituted Values"]
    D --> I
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Compute PVIFA Value"]
    J --> K["Multiply by C and adjustment factors"]
    K --> L["Final PV Answer"]
    L --> M["END: Interpret Decision"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Receive Cash Flow Stream"] --> S1{"Number of Payments?"}
    S1 -->|Finite n| S2{"Payment Timing?"}
    S1 -->|Infinite - Perpetuity| SP1["Use PV equals C divided by r"]
    S2 -->|End of period| SO1["Ordinary Annuity Formula"]
    S2 -->|Start of period| SO2["Multiply Ordinary PV by 1+r"]
    S2 -->|After delay d| SO3["Discount Ordinary PV by 1+r to power d"]
    S1 -->|Growing at rate g| SG1["Use Growing Annuity Formula"]
    SO1 --> SOUT["Apply and Compute Final PV"]
    SO2 --> SOUT
    SO3 --> SOUT
    SG1 --> SOUT
    SP1 --> SOUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define an ordinary annuity. How does it differ from an annuity due?**

**Model Answer:**

An **ordinary annuity** is a series of equal payments made at the **end of each period** for a specified number of periods. Examples include bond coupon payments and loan EMIs paid in arrears.

An **annuity due** is a series of equal payments made at the **beginning of each period**. Examples include rent paid in advance and insurance premiums.

| Feature | Ordinary Annuity | Annuity Due |
|---------|------------------|-------------|
| Timing | End of period | Beginning of period |
| First Payment | At $t=1$ | At $t=0$ |
| PV Magnitude | Lower | Higher by factor $(1+r)$ |

**[3 Marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark timing + 1 mark comparison]**

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the formula for the present value of a perpetuity and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

$$PV_{perpetuity} = \frac{C}{r}$$

where $C$ is the periodic cash flow and $r$ is the discount rate.

**Significance:** The perpetuity formula represents the present value of an infinite stream of cash flows. It is used in **financial economics** to value perpetual bonds (consols), preferred stocks with no maturity, and capitalized costs of perpetual infrastructure maintenance. The model assumes that the discount rate $r > 0$ strictly; otherwise the formula is undefined.

**[3 Marks: 1 mark formula + 1 mark variable meaning + 1 mark significance]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question A:** *(a)* Define the time value of money and derive the formula for the present value of an ordinary annuity. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A company receives ₹2,00,000 at the end of each year for 10 years. If the discount rate is 12% per annum, calculate the present value of this annuity. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition:** The Time Value of Money (TVM) states that **a rupee available today is worth more than the same rupee received in the future**, because today's rupee can be invested to earn returns.

**Derivation:** Let $C$ be the periodic cash flow at end of each period for $n$ periods, and $r$ the discount rate.

$$PV = \frac{C}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{C}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{C}{(1+r)^n}$$

This is a geometric series with first term $\dfrac{C}{(1+r)}$ and common ratio $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)}$. Using the sum formula:

$$PV = \frac{C}{(1+r)} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1}{1+r}\right)^n}{1 - \frac{1}{1+r}}$$

Simplifying the denominator: $1 - \dfrac{1}{1+r} = \dfrac{r}{1+r}$

$$PV = \frac{C}{(1+r)} \cdot \frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{\frac{r}{1+r}} = C \cdot \frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

$$\boxed{PV = C \cdot \frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}}$$

**[7 marks: 1 mark TVM definition + 6 marks step-by-step derivation]**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $C = ₹2{,}00{,}000$, $r = 12\% = 0.12$, $n = 10$ years.

**Apply Formula:**
$$PV = 200000 \cdot \frac{1-(1.12)^{-10}}{0.12}$$

**Step 1:** Compute $(1.12)^{10}$.
$$(1.12)^{10} = 3.105848$$

**Step 2:** Compute the reciprocal.
$$(1.12)^{-10} = \frac{1}{3.105848} = 0.321973$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator.
$$1 - 0.321973 = 0.678027$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $r$.
$$\frac{0.678027}{0.12} = 5.650225$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by $C$.
$$PV = 200000 \times 5.650225 = ₹11{,}30{,}045$$

**Answer:** $PV = ₹11{,}30{,}045$ (approximately)

**[7 marks: 1 mark formula statement + 5 marks computation + 1 mark final answer]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question B:** *(a)* Explain the concept of a perpetuity with a suitable example. Derive the present value of a perpetuity. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A perpetual bond pays ₹5,000 every year forever. If the required rate of return is 8% per annum, calculate the present value. If the rate rises to 10%, what is the new present value? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Concept:** A perpetuity is an annuity that continues indefinitely — there is no end date. The British government's *consol bonds* are classic examples. Preferred stocks paying fixed dividends forever are also perpetuities.

**Derivation:** Starting with the ordinary annuity formula and taking the limit as $n \to \infty$:

$$PV_{perp} = \lim_{n \to \infty} C \cdot \frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

Since $(1+r) > 1$, as $n \to \infty$ we have $(1+r)^{-n} \to 0$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{PV_{perp} = \frac{C}{r}}$$

The formula is valid only when $r > 0$.

**[7 marks: 3 marks concept + example + 4 marks derivation]**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Case 1:** $C = ₹5{,}000$, $r = 8\% = 0.08$

$$PV_1 = \frac{5000}{0.08} = ₹62{,}500$$

**Case 2:** $C = ₹5{,}000$, $r = 10\% = 0.10$

$$PV_2 = \frac{5000}{0.10} = ₹50{,}000$$

**Interpretation:** When the discount rate rises from 8% to 10%, the present value of the perpetuity **falls from ₹62,500 to ₹50,000**, a decrease of ₹12,500. This illustrates the **inverse relationship** between discount rate and present value.

**Percentage decrease:** $\dfrac{62500 - 50000}{62500} \times 100 = 20\%$

**[7 marks: 2 marks formula application + 2 marks Case 1 + 2 marks Case 2 + 1 mark interpretation]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong timing assumption:** Always check whether payments are at the beginning or end of the period. Applying the ordinary annuity formula to an annuity due (or vice versa) costs full 7 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to invert the fraction:** When asked for "payment given PV," many students forget to invert $PVIFA$ and write $C = PV \cdot r / (1-(1+r)^{-n})$ incorrectly. The correct form is shown in Section 2.
>
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** Cash inflows are positive, outflows are negative. Inconsistent sign usage leads to wrong NPV in multi-part problems.
>
> 4. **Misinterpreting the deferral period $d$:** If first payment is at year 5, then $d = 4$ (not 5), because payments are at the **end** of each year after the deferral.
>
> 5. **Rounding too early:** Carry at least 6 decimal places until the final answer to avoid compounding errors.
>
> 6. **Ignoring effective vs. nominal rate:** If compounding is monthly but payments are annual, convert using $EAR = (1 + r_{nom}/m)^m - 1$ first.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PV of Annuity**
>
> ✅ **Core Formula:** $PV = C \cdot \dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ — ordinary annuity, **end-of-period** payments.
>
> ✅ **Annuity Due:** Multiply ordinary annuity PV by $(1+r)$.
>
> ✅ **Deferred Annuity:** Compute ordinary annuity PV, then discount by $(1+r)^{-d}$ where $d$ is the deferral period before first payment.
>
> ✅ **Perpetuity:** $PV = C/r$ — applies only when cash flows are **infinite and constant**.
>
> ✅ **Growing Perpetuity:** $PV = \dfrac{C_1}{r-g}$ — only valid when $r > g$.
>
> ✅ **Inverse Relationships:** Higher $r$ → Lower PV; Higher $n$ → Higher PV.
>
> ✅ **Loan EMI:** $C = \dfrac{PV \cdot r}{1-(1+r)^{-n}}$ where $PV$ is the loan principal.
>
> ✅ **PVIFA Notation:** $\dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ is often tabulated as $P(r,n)$ in KTU question papers — use it directly.
>
> ✅ **Boundary Check:** When $r = 0$, PV equals $n \cdot C$ (no discounting).
>
> ✅ **Time Line Drawing:** Always draw a timeline marking time $0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$ with cash flows at each point — KTU examiners reward this with at least 1 mark in 14-mark problems.
>
> ✅ **Engineering Applications:** Equipment replacement, lease vs. buy, bond pricing, capital budgeting — always identify which annuity type applies.
>
> ✅ **Examiner's Pet Question Pattern:** "Compute EMI for a loan of ₹X at $r$% for $n$ years" → directly uses $C = PV \cdot r / (1-(1+r)^{-n})$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Present Rate of Perpetuity. Investment Criteria: Net Present Value (NPV)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Present Rate of Perpetuity. Investment Criteria: Net Present Value (NPV)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Concept of Perpetuity

A **Perpetuity** is a financial instrument or cash flow stream that pays a fixed amount of money at regular intervals *forever* — that is, for an **infinite number of periods**. There is no maturity date.

The **Present Rate (Present Value) of Perpetuity** is the lump-sum equivalent today of an infinite series of equal periodic cash flows, discounted back to the present at a required rate of return.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A perpetuity is an annuity whose payments continue indefinitely. The present value of a perpetuity paying a constant cash flow $C$ at the end of each period, discounted at rate $r$ per period, is given by:
> $$PV_{\text{perpetuity}} = \dfrac{C}{r}$$
> provided that $r > 0$.

### Real-World Analogy — "The Eternal Scholarship"

Imagine a wealthy alumnus donates ₹1,00,000 *every year, forever*, to a college scholarship fund. The college doesn't receive a lump sum today — instead, it receives an infinite stream of ₹1,00,000 payments stretching into the distant future. If the college wants to fund this scholarship right now by depositing a single amount in a bank earning **8% annual interest**, how much must they deposit? The answer is the *Present Value of the Perpetuity*. The bank will pay ₹1,00,000 in interest each year *in perpetuity* simply by holding the principal — the principal never depletes because only the interest is paid out. That principal (₹12,50,000 at 8%) is the present value.

Common real-world examples of perpetuities:
- **Consol bonds** issued by the British Government (perpetual bonds).
- **Preferred stocks** with fixed dividend obligations.
- **Endowment funds** at universities (e.g., a named professorship).
- **Royalty streams** on certain intellectual property.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Perpetuities Matter in Engineering Economics:**
> Engineers and project managers often evaluate assets with very long (effectively indefinite) service lives — bridges, dams, pipelines, power transmission infrastructure, software platforms. While these are not literally perpetual, the perpetuity formula provides a *first-order approximation* for assets with very long horizons and helps in **capital budgeting** decisions under the NPV framework.

---

## 1.2 Investment Criteria: Net Present Value (NPV)

**Net Present Value (NPV)** is the standard *scientifically optimal* investment criterion in financial economics. It measures the **net wealth created** (in today's currency) by undertaking a project or investment.

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}$$

where:
- $CF_t$ = Net cash flow at time $t$ (negative for outflows, positive for inflows).
- $r$ = Discount rate (often the cost of capital or required rate of return).
- $n$ = Project life in periods.

The initial investment is conventionally taken as $CF_0 < 0$, so NPV may also be written as:

$$NPV = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}$$

### Decision Rule (The Three Cases)

| NPV Value | Decision | Interpretation |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $NPV > 0$ | **ACCEPT** | Project adds value; wealth increases. |
| $NPV = 0$ | **INDIFFERENT** | Project breaks even in present-value terms. |
| $NPV < 0$ | **REJECT** | Project destroys value; wealth decreases. |

### Real-World Analogy — "The Coffee Cart Decision"

Suppose you invest ₹5,00,000 today to buy a coffee cart. It generates ₹1,20,000 in *additional* profit each year for several years. The question: is this a good deal? If you simply summed the ₹1,20,000s, you'd ignore that ₹1,20,000 *next year* is worth less than ₹1,20,000 *today* (because of inflation and the opportunity cost of tying up capital). **NPV** solves this by discounting every future rupee back to today's purchasing power and comparing the total discounted value to today's outflow. If the discounted future profits *exceed* the ₹5,00,000 outlay, you should buy the cart. If not, you'd be better off depositing the money in a fixed deposit.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for Engineers:**
> Think of NPV as the project's *contribution to the firm's market value*. A positive NPV project is one whose discounted future cash inflows are larger than the resources consumed today. Among all mutually exclusive projects, the one with the highest NPV maximizes shareholder wealth.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deriving the Present Value of a Perpetuity

A perpetuity pays a constant cash flow $C$ at the end of each period, forever. The Present Value (PV) is the sum of the PVs of every individual cash flow:

$$PV = \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^1} + \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^2} + \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^3} + \dots \quad \text{(infinite sum)}$$

This is an **infinite geometric series** with:
- First term $a = \dfrac{C}{1+r}$
- Common ratio $q = \dfrac{1}{1+r}$, where $\vert q \vert < 1$ since $r > 0$.

The sum of an infinite geometric series is:

$$S_{\infty} = \dfrac{a}{1 - q} = \dfrac{\dfrac{C}{1+r}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+r}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{C}{1+r}}{\dfrac{r}{1+r}} = \dfrac{C}{r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY RESULT:** $\boxed{PV_{\text{perpetuity}} = \dfrac{C}{r}}$
> The result is independent of any time horizon — a beautiful consequence of exponential discounting.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Cash flow assumption**: Constant $C$ per period, starting at $t = 1$.
2. **Discount each cash flow** back to $t = 0$ using factor $(1+r)^{-t}$.
3. **Recognize** the resulting sum as an infinite geometric progression.
4. **Apply the sum formula** for convergent infinite series ($\vert q \vert < 1$).
5. **Simplify** to obtain the closed-form $\dfrac{C}{r}$.

---

## 2.2 Extension: Growing Perpetuity (Gordon Growth Model)

When cash flows grow at a constant rate $g$ per period forever, with $g < r$:

$$PV_{\text{growing perpetuity}} = \dfrac{C_1}{r - g}$$

where $C_1$ is the cash flow in period 1. This formula is famously used in **stock valuation** (the Gordon Dividend Model).

> [!WARNING]
> The condition $g < r$ is *strictly necessary*. If $g \geq r$, the series diverges and no finite present value exists. KTU examiners frequently test this boundary condition.

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive into NPV

### Step-by-Step NPV Logic

1. **Identify** all cash flows $CF_t$ over the project's life $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n$.
2. **Select** an appropriate discount rate $r$ — typically the *Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)* for the firm, or the project's *opportunity cost of capital*.
3. **Discount** each future cash flow back to time 0: $PV_t = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$.
4. **Sum** all discounted cash flows.
5. **Compare** the sum to zero (or subtract the initial investment explicitly).
6. **Decide**: accept iff NPV $\geq 0$.

### Assumptions of the NPV Method

- Cash flows are **identifiable** and can be reliably estimated.
- The discount rate $r$ is **known and constant** over the project life.
- Cash flows occur at **discrete, regular intervals**.
- **No capital rationing** (firm can raise unlimited capital at rate $r$).
- Investments are **independent** (one project does not affect another's cash flows).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary Conditions | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | PV of Perpetuity | $PV = \dfrac{C}{r}$ | $r > 0$, constant $C$ | Currency (₹, \$, etc.) |
| 2 | PV of Growing Perpetuity | $PV = \dfrac{C_1}{r - g}$ | $0 \leq g < r$ | Currency |
| 3 | Present Worth Factor (Perpetuity) | $PWF_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{r}$ | $r > 0$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | NPV (Discrete) | $NPV = \displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $r > -1$ | Currency |
| 5 | NPV (with Initial Outlay) | $NPV = -I_0 + \displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $r > 0$ | Currency |
| 6 | Net Present Worth | Same as NPV | — | Currency |
| 7 | Decision Rule | Accept if $NPV \geq 0$ | — | — |
| 8 | Present Value Factor | $PVF(t,r) = (1+r)^{-t}$ | $r > 0$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Field Utility:** The NPV criterion is used in *capital budgeting* across infrastructure, manufacturing, software, and energy sectors. Engineers rely on NPV to justify equipment purchases, plant expansions, R&D investments, and replacement-vs-repair decisions.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Present Value of Perpetuity

We start from the foundational definition: the PV of a perpetuity is the sum of the PVs of an infinite sequence of equal cash flows.

### Step 1: Write the Cash Flow Series

A perpetuity pays cash flow $C$ at the end of each period, indefinitely. The first payment is at $t = 1$ (one period from now), the second at $t = 2$, and so on, ad infinitum.

$$PV = \dfrac{C}{1+r} + \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^2} + \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^3} + \dots + \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^n} + \dots \quad \text{as } n \to \infty$$

### Step 2: Identify the Structure as a Geometric Series

Each term is obtained by multiplying the previous term by $\dfrac{1}{1+r}$. Therefore, this is a **geometric progression** with:

- First term $a = \dfrac{C}{1+r}$
- Common ratio $q = \dfrac{1}{1+r}$

### Step 3: Recall the Infinite Geometric Series Formula

For $\vert q \vert < 1$:

$$S_{\infty} = \dfrac{a}{1 - q}$$

### Step 4: Substitute and Simplify

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \dfrac{\dfrac{C}{1+r}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+r}} \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{\dfrac{C}{1+r}}{\dfrac{(1+r) - 1}{1+r}} \quad \text{[Common denominator: } 1+r \text{]}\\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{\dfrac{C}{1+r}}{\dfrac{r}{1+r}} \quad \text{[Simplify numerator: } (1+r) - 1 = r \text{]}\\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{C}{1+r} \times \dfrac{1+r}{r} \quad \text{[Division by a fraction]}\\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{C}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Final Result

$$\boxed{PV_{\text{perpetuity}} = \dfrac{C}{r}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Perpetuity Valuation

**Problem:** A perpetuity pays ₹5,000 at the end of each year forever. The discount rate is 10% per annum. Find the present value.

**Solution:**

- $C = 5{,}000$
- $r = 0.10$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \dfrac{C}{r} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{5{,}000}{0.10} \\[6pt]
&= 50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

The present value is **₹50,000**. Interpretation: ₹50,000 invested today at 10% would generate exactly ₹5,000 of interest each year, forever, without depleting the principal.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Growing Perpetuity

**Problem:** A perpetuity pays ₹1,000 at the end of year 1, with cash flows growing at 5% annually thereafter. Discount rate is 12%. Find PV.

**Solution:**

- $C_1 = 1{,}000$, $r = 0.12$, $g = 0.05$. Note $g < r$ ✓

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \dfrac{C_1}{r - g} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1{,}000}{0.12 - 0.05} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1{,}000}{0.07} \\[6pt]
&= 14{,}285.71
\end{aligned}
$$

The present value is **₹14,285.71**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Net Present Value of an Engineering Project

**Problem:** A firm is considering buying a CNC machine for ₹10,00,000. It is expected to generate net cash inflows of ₹3,00,000 per year for 5 years. The discount rate is 12%. Determine whether the project should be accepted using the NPV criterion.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify cash flows:
- $CF_0 = -10{,}00{,}000$
- $CF_1 = CF_2 = \dots = CF_5 = +3{,}00{,}000$
- $r = 0.12$, $n = 5$

Step 2 — Compute the PV of each cash flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.12^1} = 2{,}67{,}857.14 \\[4pt]
PV_2 &= \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.12^2} = 2{,}39{,}158.16 \\[4pt]
PV_3 &= \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.12^3} = 2{,}13{,}534.07 \\[4pt]
PV_4 &= \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.12^4} = 1{,}90{,}655.42 \\[4pt]
PV_5 &= \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.12^5} = 1{,}70{,}228.05
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Sum and subtract the initial outlay:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{t=1}^{5} PV_t &= 2{,}67{,}857.14 + 2{,}39{,}158.16 + 2{,}13{,}534.07 + 1{,}90{,}655.42 + 1{,}70{,}228.05 \\[4pt]
&= 10{,}81{,}432.84
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Compute NPV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -10{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}81{,}432.84 \\[4pt]
&= +81{,}432.84
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Decision:

Since $NPV = +81{,}432.84 > 0$, the project **should be accepted**. It creates wealth.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — NPV with Non-Uniform Cash Flows

**Problem:** A software firm is evaluating a 4-year project with the following cash flows: $CF_0 = -5{,}00{,}000$; $CF_1 = 1{,}50{,}000$; $CF_2 = 2{,}00{,}000$; $CF_3 = 2{,}50{,}000$; $CF_4 = 1{,}00{,}000$. Discount rate is 10%. Compute NPV.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -5{,}00{,}000 + \dfrac{1{,}50{,}000}{1.10} + \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.10^2} + \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.10^3} + \dfrac{1{,}00{,}000}{1.10^4} \\[6pt]
&= -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}36{,}363.64 + 1{,}65{,}289.26 + 1{,}87{,}828.70 + 68{,}301.35 \\[6pt]
&= 57{,}782.95
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $NPV = +57{,}782.95 > 0$, accept.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Situation | Recommended Formula | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Infinite, constant cash flows | $PV = \dfrac{C}{r}$ | Pure perpetuity |
| Infinite, growing cash flows | $PV = \dfrac{C_1}{r - g}$ | Gordon Growth Model |
| Finite, constant cash flows | $PV = C \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | Ordinary annuity |
| Finite, variable cash flows | $NPV = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Standard NPV |
| Initial outlay required | Subtract $I_0$ explicitly | NPV convention |
| Continuous discounting | $PV = \int_0^{\infty} C e^{-rt}\, dt$ | Continuous cash flows |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cash Flow Timeline — Perpetuity vs. Annuity vs. NPV

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes how cash flows differ across the three fundamental financial structures.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStart([Time t = 0 Today]) --> CF0A[/CF at t = 0/]
    nodeStart --> CF0B[/Initial Investment I0/]
    CF0A --> CF1A([C Period 1])
    CF1A --> CF2A([C Period 2])
    CF2A --> CF3A([C Period 3])
    CF3A --> CFdotsA([... Continues forever])
    CFdotsA --> CFnA([C infinity])

    CF0B --> CF1B([CF1 Year 1])
    CF1B --> CF2B([CF2 Year 2])
    CF2B --> CF3B([CF3 Year 3])
    CF3B --> CFnB([CFn Year n STOP])
    CFnB --> CFzero([No more flows])
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **upper branch** shows a *perpetuity* — equal payments $C$ extending indefinitely.
- The **lower branch** shows a *finite NPV project* — cash flows stop at year $n$ after an initial outlay $I_0$.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the NPV Decision Engine

This block diagram maps how the inputs (cash flows, discount rate, horizon) flow through the NPV engine and produce an investment decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA([Cash Flow Series CFt]) --> engineA[Discount Engine: divide by 1+rt]
    inputB([Discount Rate r]) --> engineA
    inputC([Project Life n]) --> engineA
    engineA --> sumNode[Sum of Discounted Cash Flows]
    sumNode --> npvCalc[NPV equals Sum minus I0]
    npvCalc --> decisionNode{NPV greater than zero?}
    decisionNode -- Yes --> acceptOut([ACCEPT PROJECT Add to firm value])
    decisionNode -- No --> rejectOut([REJECT PROJECT Wealth destroying])
    decisionNode -- Equal --> indOut([INDIFFERENT Breakeven])
```

**Key components:**
- **Input Block:** Three inputs — Cash Flow Series $CF_t$, Discount Rate $r$, Project Life $n$.
- **Discount Engine:** Applies $(1+r)^{-t}$ to each cash flow.
- **Summing Junction:** Aggregates all discounted cash flows.
- **Decision Node:** Performs the $\geq 0$ test and routes to one of three outputs.

---

## 4.3 Process Flow: From Cash Flow Identification to Decision

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1 Identify all incremental cash flows] --> stepB[Step 2 Choose appropriate discount rate r]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Apply discount factor to each CFt]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Sum discounted cash flows]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Subtract initial investment if not in CF0]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Apply decision rule NPV >= 0]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7 Sensitivity and risk analysis]
```

This flowchart captures the **operational workflow** a financial analyst follows when applying NPV in practice. Step 7 (sensitivity analysis) is often added in real engineering projects where input parameters are uncertain.

---

## 4.4 Comparison Schematic — Perpetuity vs. Annuity vs. Lump Sum

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef perpetuity fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    classDef annuity fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af
    classDef lump fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534

    perp[Perpetuity C forever]:::perpetuity
    annu[Annuity C for n periods]:::annuity
    lump[Lump Sum at one point]:::lump

    perp --> formulaPerp[Formula C divided by r]
    annu --> formulaAnnu[Formula C times 1 minus 1+rtor over r]
    lump --> formulaLump[Formula FV times 1+rtopower minus t]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each coloured block corresponds to a different cash flow pattern, with the appropriate KTU-style formula attached.
