# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define a *perpetuity*. Derive the formula for the present value of a perpetuity paying a constant cash flow $C$ per period at discount rate $r$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Definition (1 Mark):* A perpetuity is a stream of equal cash flows that continues indefinitely. There is no maturity date, and payments are made at regular intervals forever.

*Derivation (2 Marks):* The PV is the sum of an infinite geometric series:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \dfrac{C}{1+r} + \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^2} + \dots \quad \text{(infinite sum)} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{C}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

The series converges because the common ratio $\dfrac{1}{1+r} < 1$ for $r > 0$.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the **Net Present Value (NPV) decision rule**. When is a project accepted, rejected, or treated as indifferent?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

- **Accept** if $NPV > 0$ (project creates wealth).
- **Reject** if $NPV < 0$ (project destroys wealth).
- **Indifferent** if $NPV = 0$ (project breaks even in PV terms).

Among mutually exclusive projects, select the one with the **highest NPV**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B Module 1 carries 14 marks. The student must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** A perpetuity bond pays ₹10,000 at the end of every year forever. If the required rate of return is **8%** per annum, calculate the present value of the bond. If the discount rate rises to **10%**, what will be the new present value? Comment on the relationship between discount rate and present value of a perpetuity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company is evaluating a project requiring an initial investment of ₹8,00,000. The project is expected to generate cash inflows of ₹2,50,000 per year for 4 years. Using a discount rate of **10%**, determine the NPV. Should the company accept the project? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (a)

*Step 1 — PV at r = 8%:* Using the perpetuity formula with $C = 10{,}000$ and $r = 0.08$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \dfrac{C}{r} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{0.08} = 1{,}25{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — PV at r = 10%:* Using $C = 10{,}000$ and $r = 0.10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \dfrac{10{,}000}{0.10} = 1{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Comment:* As $r$ increases, the PV of a perpetuity **decreases inversely**. There is an **inverse, non-linear relationship** between $r$ and $PV$ — specifically, doubling $r$ roughly halves $PV$ (compare: $1{,}25{,}000 \to 1{,}00{,}000$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write $PV = C \times r$ instead of $\dfrac{C}{r}$. Always remember: present value is *inversely* proportional to the discount rate.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (b)

*Step 1 — Identify cash flows:* $CF_0 = -8{,}00{,}000$; $CF_1 = CF_2 = CF_3 = CF_4 = +2{,}50{,}000$; $r = 0.10$; $n = 4$.

*Step 2 — Discount each cash flow:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.10} = 2{,}27{,}272.73 \\[4pt]
PV_2 &= \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.10^2} = 2{,}06{,}611.57 \\[4pt]
PV_3 &= \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.10^3} = 1{,}87{,}828.70 \\[4pt]
PV_4 &= \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.10^4} = 1{,}70{,}753.36
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Sum the PVs:*

$$\sum PV_t = 2{,}27{,}272.73 + 2{,}06{,}611.57 + 1{,}87{,}828.70 + 1{,}70{,}753.36 = 7{,}92{,}466.36$$

*Step 4 — Compute NPV:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -8{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}92{,}466.36 \\[4pt]
&= -7{,}533.64
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5 — Decision:* Since $NPV < 0$, the company should **reject** the project.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common error is forgetting the *minus sign* on the initial investment. If a student writes $NPV = +7{,}92{,}466.36$ without subtracting the outlay, full credit is denied. Always state the **sign convention** explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **growing perpetuity**. Derive the formula $\dfrac{C_1}{r - g}$ and state the conditions under which it is valid. A growing perpetuity pays ₹2,000 in year 1 with cash flows growing at **4% per annum**. The discount rate is **11%**. Find the present value. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A firm is considering two mutually exclusive projects:

| Year | Project X (₹) | Project Y (₹) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | $-5{,}00{,}000$ | $-5{,}00{,}000$ |
| 1 | $+2{,}00{,}000$ | $+1{,}00{,}000$ |
| 2 | $+2{,}00{,}000$ | $+2{,}50{,}000$ |
| 3 | $+2{,}00{,}000$ | $+3{,}00{,}000$ |

Using a discount rate of **10%**, compute the NPV of each project and recommend which one the firm should choose. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

*Step 1 — Concept:* A growing perpetuity is a perpetual cash flow stream in which each payment grows by a constant rate $g$ relative to the previous one.

*Step 2 — Derivation:* If $C_1$ is the year-1 cash flow, then $C_2 = C_1(1+g)$, $C_3 = C_1(1+g)^2$, etc. The PV is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \dfrac{C_1}{1+r} + \dfrac{C_1(1+g)}{(1+r)^2} + \dfrac{C_1(1+g)^2}{(1+r)^3} + \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a geometric series with first term $a = \dfrac{C_1}{1+r}$ and common ratio $q = \dfrac{1+g}{1+r}$. For convergence, $\vert q \vert < 1$, i.e., $g < r$. Applying the sum formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \dfrac{a}{1 - q} = \dfrac{\dfrac{C_1}{1+r}}{1 - \dfrac{1+g}{1+r}} = \dfrac{C_1}{r - g}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Validity Conditions:*
- $0 \leq g < r$
- Constant growth rate forever
- Discount rate $r > 0$ is constant

*Step 4 — Numerical Computation:* $C_1 = 2{,}000$, $r = 0.11$, $g = 0.04$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \dfrac{2{,}000}{0.11 - 0.04} = \dfrac{2{,}000}{0.07} = 28{,}571.43
\end{aligned}
$$

The PV of the growing perpetuity is **₹28,571.43**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A classic mistake is to write $PV = \dfrac{C_1}{r + g}$ instead of $\dfrac{C_1}{r - g}$. Always remember: **minus** in the denominator, and ensure $g < r$ is checked.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

*Project X:* Constant cash flows of ₹2,00,000 for 3 years.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_X &= -5{,}00{,}000 + \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.10} + \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.10^2} + \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.10^3} \\[6pt]
&= -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}81{,}818.18 + 1{,}65{,}289.26 + 1{,}50{,}262.96 \\[6pt]
&= -2{,}629.60
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $NPV_X < 0$, Project X should be **rejected**.

*Project Y:* Variable cash flows.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_Y &= -5{,}00{,}000 + \dfrac{1{,}00{,}000}{1.10} + \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.10^2} + \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.10^3} \\[6pt]
&= -5{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}909.09 + 2{,}06{,}611.57 + 2{,}25{,}394.44 \\[6pt]
&= 22{,}915.10
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $NPV_Y > 0$, Project Y should be **accepted**.

*Recommendation:* The firm should choose **Project Y**, as it has a positive NPV whereas Project X has a slightly negative NPV. Among mutually exclusive alternatives, the project with the higher (and positive) NPV is preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often compare *percentage returns* (e.g., IRR) when comparing mutually exclusive projects. KTU examiners stress that **NPV is the gold standard** for ranking, especially when project sizes or lifetimes differ. IRR can give misleading rankings due to scale and reinvestment-rate issues.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Perpetuity** = infinite stream of equal cash flows.
- **PV of Perpetuity** formula: $PV = \dfrac{C}{r}$ — derived from infinite geometric series; valid for $r > 0$.
- **PV of Growing Perpetuity**: $PV = \dfrac{C_1}{r - g}$ — valid only when $g < r$ (strictly).
- **NPV** = sum of all discounted cash flows (inflows minus outflows) over a project's life.
- **Decision Rule**: Accept if $NPV > 0$, Reject if $NPV < 0$, Indifferent if $NPV = 0$.
- **Among mutually exclusive projects**, choose the one with the **highest NPV**.
- **NPV is theoretically superior** to IRR, payback period, and profitability index because it directly measures wealth creation in monetary units.
- **Discount rate** $r$ should reflect the *opportunity cost of capital* — typically the firm's WACC or project's required rate of return.
- **Sign convention**: Initial outlay is negative; subsequent inflows are positive. Always subtract the initial investment explicitly if it is not already in $CF_0$.
- **Boundary conditions matter**: For growing perpetuity, $g < r$ is mandatory; for any NPV formula, $r > 0$ ensures convergence.
- **Real-world applications**: Endowments, consol bonds, preferred stock dividends, long-lived infrastructure, software subscription revenue streams, and Gordon Growth Model for stock valuation.
- **Common student errors** to avoid:
  1. Using $C \times r$ instead of $C / r$ for perpetuity PV.
  2. Forgetting to subtract the initial investment.
  3. Confusing PV of perpetuity with PV of annuity ($n$ finite).
  4. Using $r + g$ instead of $r - g$ in growing perpetuity.
  5. Comparing projects by IRR instead of NPV.
  6. Ignoring the sign of cash flows (treat all as positive).
  7. Failing to check the boundary condition $g < r$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benefit Cost Ratio (BCR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Benefit Cost Ratio (BCR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Benefit Cost Ratio (BCR)** is a fundamental capital budgeting and project evaluation metric used in engineering economics that quantifies the relationship between the present value (or sometimes annual equivalent) of a project's **benefits** (positive cash inflows) and the present value of its **costs** (negative cash outflows). It expresses the **rupees (or monetary units) of benefit received per rupee invested**.

The universally accepted KTU Board formula is:

$$BCR = \frac{\text{Present Value of Benefits (PVB)}}{\text{Present Value of Costs (PVC)}}$$

### Decision Rule (KTU Standard)

| Condition | Decision | Interpretation |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **BCR > 1** | **ACCEPT** the project | Benefits outweigh costs; wealth is created |
| **BCR < 1** | **REJECT** the project | Costs outweigh benefits; wealth is destroyed |
| **BCR = 1** | **INDIFFERENT** | The investor is exactly at break-even (analogous to IRR = MARR) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Discount Rate Used:** All future benefits and costs are discounted to present value using the **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)**, often denoted as **i = 10%** or **i = 12%** in standard KTU numerical problems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Street Food Stall" Intuition

Imagine you, as a B.Tech student, decide to set up a small tea stall near campus for your final-year project. The "cost" is the money you spend on:
- Rent of the cart (**₹20,000**)
- Initial stock of milk, sugar, cups (**₹10,000**)

The "benefit" is the money you collect from selling tea for 1 year (**₹45,000** projected).

Before you invest, you want to know: **"For every ₹1 I put in, how many rupees come back?"**

- If you calculate: BCR = 45,000 / 30,000 = **1.5** → For every ₹1 invested, you get ₹1.50 back. **GO!**
- If BCR = 0.8 → For every ₹1 invested, you get only ₹0.80 back. **DON'T GO!**

This intuitive "₹1 in, ₹? out" logic is exactly what **Benefit Cost Ratio** captures at a corporate/engineering project scale.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** Think of BCR as a **"return-per-rupee"** thermometer. A healthy project must score above 1.0 on this thermometer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation

The BCR method is a refinement of the **Net Present Value (NPV)** method. While NPV gives the absolute rupee value of wealth creation, BCR gives the *relative efficiency* of capital use. The underlying logic rests on three pillars:

1. **Time Value of Money:** A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow. Hence, all future benefits and costs are discounted back to **time t = 0** using the MARR.
2. **Consistent Discounting:** Both numerator (benefits) and denominator (costs) MUST be discounted at the **same rate (i)** to ensure mathematical consistency.
3. **Mutually Exclusive vs. Independent Projects:** BCR is especially powerful for ranking **mutually exclusive projects** where NPV alone may mislead due to scale differences.

---

## 2.2 The KTU Master Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **complete, exam-ready formula set** for any BCR problem in the KTU 2024 scheme.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | When to Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Conventional BCR** | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{B_t}{(1+i)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}$ | Standard project evaluation with multiple inflows/outflows |
| 2 | **Net BCR (NBCR)** | $NBCR = BCR - 1 = \dfrac{PVB - PVC}{PVC}$ | When you want to express "excess benefit per ₹1 invested" |
| 3 | **Modified BCR** | $BCR_{mod} = \dfrac{PV(\text{Operating Benefits})}{PV(\text{Capital Costs only})}$ | Public sector / Govt. projects with separate O&M costs |
| 4 | **Annual Worth BCR** | $BCR = \dfrac{AW(\text{Benefits})}{AW(\text{Costs})}$ | When cash flows are uniform annual series |
| 5 | **NPV ↔ BCR Linkage** | $NPV = PVC \times (BCR - 1)$ | The core mathematical relationship between NPV and BCR |

### Boundary Conditions & Units
- BCR is a **dimensionless ratio** (₹ / ₹).
- Discount rate **i** must be expressed as a decimal (e.g., 10% → 0.10).
- If cash flows are given as a uniform series, use $P/A$ factor: $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$.

---

## 2.3 Types of BCR — The KTU Examiner's Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners LOVE to test the difference between **Conventional BCR** and **Modified BCR**. Confusing these will cost you 3–4 marks instantly.

### (a) Conventional (Unmodified) BCR
Both numerator and denominator include ALL cash flows. The investment and the O&M (Operating & Maintenance) costs are all in the denominator. This is the **default KTU method**.

### (b) Modified BCR
Used in **public sector / government / BCR is not a measure of profitability but of economic efficiency.**

> BCR is **not a measure of profitability** but of **economic efficiency**. A BCR of 1.4 means the project generates ₹1.40 of social/economic value for every ₹1 spent — useful for welfare economics and government feasibility reports (e.g., NHAI highway projects, irrigation dams).

### (c) Gross BCR vs Net BCR
- **Gross BCR:** Total benefits ÷ Total costs (no subtraction).
- **Net BCR:** (Benefits − Costs) ÷ Costs = BCR − 1.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From NPV to BCR

The mathematical equivalence between BCR and NPV is a **favourite KTU derivation question (7-mark)**. Let us derive it step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Define Net Present Value (NPV)

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

This is the **present value of net cash flows** (benefits minus costs) over the project life $n$.

### Step 2 — Split the Single Sum into Two Sums

Using the algebraic property of summation $\sum (X - Y) = \sum X - \sum Y$:

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{B_t}{(1+i)^t} - \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

### Step 3 — Identify PVB and PVC

By definition, the two summations are the **Present Value of Benefits (PVB)** and **Present Value of Costs (PVC)**:

$$NPV = PVB - PVC$$

### Step 4 — Factor Out PVC

We factor $PVC$ from both terms by multiplying and dividing PVB by PVC:

$$NPV = PVC \left( \frac{PVB}{PVC} - 1 \right)$$

### Step 5 — Recognize the Ratio as BCR

The term $\dfrac{PVB}{PVC}$ is, by definition, the **Benefit Cost Ratio**:

$$\boxed{NPV = PVC \times (BCR - 1)}$$

### Step 6 — Establish the Decision Equivalence

Since $PVC > 0$ always (you cannot have a project with zero cost), the **sign of NPV depends entirely on (BCR − 1)**:

$$NPV > 0 \iff BCR > 1 \quad (\text{ACCEPT})$$
$$NPV = 0 \iff BCR = 1 \quad (\text{INDIFFERENT})$$
$$NPV < 0 \iff BCR < 1 \quad (\text{REJECT})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation proves that **BCR and NPV will always give identical accept/reject decisions** for independent projects. The difference arises only when ranking **mutually exclusive projects** of different scales.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Why |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Single independent project | BCR or NPV | Both give the same answer |
| Mutually exclusive projects (different scales) | BCR or Incremental BCR | NPV favors large projects; BCR gives fair efficiency ranking |
| Government / Public welfare project | Modified BCR | Separates O&M from capital cost |
| Project with uniform annual cash flows | Annual Worth BCR | Simpler computation using $P/A$ factor |
| Comparing two projects with same cost | NPV | BCR is redundant if costs are equal |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BCR Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify all cash inflows B_t and outflows C_t] --> B[Choose MARR / Discount Rate i]
    B --> C[Compute PVB using P/F or P/A factors]
    C --> D[Compute PVC using P/F or P/A factors]
    D --> E[Calculate BCR = PVB / PVC]
    E --> F{BCR greater than 1?}
    F -- Yes --> G[ACCEPT Project]
    F -- No --> H{BCR less than 1?}
    H -- Yes --> I[REJECT Project]
    H -- No --> J[INDIFFERENT BCR = 1]
```

## 4.2 BCR Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Cash Flow Series] --> B[Time Value Adjustment via MARR]
    B --> C[PVB Block]
    B --> D[PVC Block]
    C --> E[BCR Engine: PVB / PVC]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Decision Output Layer]
    F --> G[Accept: BCR greater than 1]
    F --> H[Reject: BCR less than 1]
    F --> I[Indifferent: BCR equals 1]
```

## 4.3 Conventional BCR vs Modified BCR — Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Conv[CONVENTIONAL BCR]
        A1[All Benefits in PVB] --> A2[PVB / PVC]
        A3[All Costs incl. O&M in PVC] --> A2
    end
    subgraph Mod[MODIFIED BCR]
        B1[Operating Benefits in PVB] --> B2[PVB / PV of Capital Cost]
        B3[Capital Cost only in PVC] --> B2
        B4[O&M Cost kept separate] --> B2
    end
    Conv --> C[Used in: Private Corporate Projects]
    Mod --> D[Used in: Public / Govt Welfare Projects]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define Benefit Cost Ratio (BCR). State the decision rule for accepting or rejecting a project based on BCR.**

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **BCR** is the ratio of the present value of benefits to the present value of costs of a project, both discounted at the MARR. Mathematically, $BCR = \dfrac{PVB}{PVC}$.
>
> **Decision Rule:** If BCR ≥ 1, accept the project; if BCR < 1, reject the project. A BCR of exactly 1 implies indifference (no wealth creation).

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between Conventional BCR and Modified BCR.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Conventional BCR | Modified BCR |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Denominator** | All costs (capital + O&M) | Only capital costs |
| **Numerator** | All benefits | Only operating benefits (excluding capital recovery portion) |
| **O&M Cost Treatment** | Included in denominator | Subtracted from numerator |
| **Application** | Private sector | Public sector / government projects |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Model Question**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**

> A company is evaluating two mutually exclusive projects **X** and **Y**. Both require an initial investment of **₹5,00,000** at $t = 0$. Project X generates annual benefits of **₹1,50,000** for **5 years**. Project Y generates annual benefits of **₹1,80,000** for **5 years**. Annual O&M cost for X is **₹20,000** and for Y is **₹50,000**. The MARR is **10%**.
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the Conventional BCR for both projects and identify which is preferred.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the NPV of both projects and verify your answer using the NPV–BCR relationship $NPV = PVC \times (BCR - 1)$.

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

**Given Data:**
- Initial Investment (Capital Cost) for both = ₹5,00,000
- Annual benefits (X) = ₹1,50,000; (Y) = ₹1,80,000
- Annual O&M (X) = ₹20,000; (Y) = ₹50,000
- $n = 5$ years; $i = 10\% = 0.10$

**Required Factor:** $P/A, 10\%, 5$

$$\frac{P}{A} = \frac{(1+0.10)^5 - 1}{0.10(1+0.10)^5} = \frac{1.61051 - 1}{0.10 \times 1.61051} = \frac{0.61051}{0.161051} = 3.7908$$

---

### **Part (a) — Conventional BCR Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Net Annual Benefit (Project X):**
$$\text{Net Benefit}_X = 1{,}50{,}000 - 20{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}30{,}000$$

**Net Annual Benefit (Project Y):**
$$\text{Net Benefit}_Y = 1{,}80{,}000 - 50{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}30{,}000$$

**Present Value of Benefits (Project X):**
$$PVB_X = 1{,}30{,}000 \times 3.7908 = \text{₹}4{,}92{,}804$$

**Present Value of Benefits (Project Y):**
$$PVB_Y = 1{,}30{,}000 \times 3.7908 = \text{₹}4{,}92{,}804$$

**Present Value of Costs (Project X):**
The cost is the initial investment of ₹5,00,000 at $t = 0$. (O&M is already netted out in conventional BCR when net benefits are taken; here we treat costs as initial investment only for clarity.)

$$PVC_X = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000$$

**Present Value of Costs (Project Y):**
$$PVC_Y = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000$$

**Conventional BCR:**

$$BCR_X = \frac{4{,}92{,}804}{5{,}00{,}000} = 0.9856$$

$$BCR_Y = \frac{4{,}92{,}804}{5{,}00{,}000} = 0.9856$$

**Decision:** Since both BCRs are **less than 1**, **both projects are rejected** under conventional BCR.

---

### **Part (b) — NPV Calculation & Verification [7 Marks]**

**NPV of Project X:**
$$NPV_X = PVB_X - PVC_X = 4{,}92{,}804 - 5{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}(-7{,}196)$$

**NPV of Project Y:**
$$NPV_Y = PVB_Y - PVC_Y = 4{,}92{,}804 - 5{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}(-7{,}196)$$

**Verification using $NPV = PVC \times (BCR - 1)$:**

$$NPV_X = 5{,}00{,}000 \times (0.9856 - 1) = 5{,}00{,}000 \times (-0.0144) = \text{₹}(-7{,}200) \approx -7{,}196 \;\; \checkmark$$

$$NPV_Y = 5{,}00{,}000 \times (-0.0144) = \text{₹}(-7{,}200) \approx -7{,}196 \;\; \checkmark$$

(Small rounding difference is acceptable.)

**Conclusion:** NPV is negative for both projects, confirming the BCR-based rejection. BCR and NPV methods are **mathematically consistent**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Retest Model]**

> A municipal corporation is evaluating a public welfare project with an initial capital cost of **₹10,00,000** and an annual operating & maintenance cost of **₹1,50,000**. The project yields annual social benefits of **₹4,00,000** for **6 years**. The social discount rate is **8%**.
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the **Modified BCR** and state the decision.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the result with the **Conventional BCR** approach. Which is more appropriate for a public project, and why?

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

**Given Data:**
- Capital Cost = ₹10,00,000 (at $t=0$)
- Annual O&M Cost = ₹1,50,000
- Annual Benefits = ₹4,00,000
- $n = 6$ years; $i = 8\% = 0.08$

**Required Factor:** $P/A, 8\%, 6$

$$\frac{P}{A} = \frac{(1.08)^6 - 1}{0.08(1.08)^6} = \frac{1.58687 - 1}{0.08 \times 1.58687} = \frac{0.58687}{0.12695} = 4.6229$$

---

### **Part (a) — Modified BCR [7 Marks]**

In Modified BCR:
- Numerator = PV of **Benefits − O&M Costs** (i.e., net operating benefits)
- Denominator = PV of **Capital Cost only**

**PV of Net Operating Benefits:**
$$PVB_{mod} = (4{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000) \times 4.6229 = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 4.6229 = \text{₹}11{,}55{,}725$$

**PV of Capital Cost:**
$$PVC_{mod} = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(since it is at } t=0\text{)}$$

**Modified BCR:**
$$BCR_{mod} = \frac{11{,}55{,}725}{10{,}00{,}000} = 1.1557$$

**Decision:** Since **BCR = 1.16 > 1**, the project is **ACCEPTED**.

---

### **Part (b) — Comparison with Conventional BCR [7 Marks]**

**Conventional BCR Calculation:**

**PV of All Benefits:**
$$PVB_{conv} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 4.6229 = \text{₹}18{,}49{,}160$$

**PV of All Costs (Capital + O&M):**
$$PVC_{conv} = 10{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 4.6229$$
$$PVC_{conv} = 10{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}93{,}435 = \text{₹}16{,}93{,}435$$

**Conventional BCR:**
$$BCR_{conv} = \frac{18{,}49{,}160}{16{,}93{,}435} = 1.0920$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Conventional BCR | Modified BCR |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Value** | 1.0920 | 1.1557 |
| **Decision** | Accept (BCR > 1) | Accept (BCR > 1) |
| **Interpretation** | ₹1.09 return per ₹1 cost | ₹1.16 return per ₹1 capital cost |
| **More appropriate for public projects?** | No | **Yes** |

**Why Modified BCR is more appropriate for public projects:**

1. It **isolates the capital efficiency** — answering the question: *"Does the capital investment yield enough surplus to cover O&M and still generate a return?"*
2. Government projects must show that **operating revenues (or social benefits) can sustain O&M** independently, which Modified BCR reveals clearly.
3. Modified BCR prevents the **"masking effect"** of high capital cost being offset by low recurring costs (or vice versa) in the conventional method.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in BCR Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $P/A$ factor** — many students write BCR = (Annual Benefit) / (Annual Cost) directly. This is wrong unless you are using **Annual Worth BCR** with a clear mention. Default method is **Present Worth BCR**.
> 2. **Mixing up the discount rate** — using a different $i$ for benefits and costs. Both MUST use the same MARR.
> 3. **Conventional vs Modified confusion** — always re-read the question. If it says "government project" or "welfare project", default to Modified BCR.
> 4. **Sign of NPV check** — if your BCR > 1 but NPV < 0 (or vice versa), you have made an arithmetic error. They MUST be consistent.
> 5. **Not stating the decision rule** — even if your calculation is correct, KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if the final accept/reject statement is missing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Benefit Cost Ratio (BCR)**

- **BCR** = $PVB / PVC$ — a dimensionless efficiency ratio.
- **Decision Rule:** $BCR > 1 \Rightarrow$ Accept; $BCR < 1 \Rightarrow$ Reject; $BCR = 1 \Rightarrow$ Indifferent.
- **Core Link:** $NPV = PVC \times (BCR - 1)$ — BCR and NPV are mathematically consistent.
- **Conventional BCR:** All benefits in numerator; all costs (capital + O&M) in denominator.
- **Modified BCR:** Net operating benefits (Benefits − O&M) in numerator; only capital cost in denominator. Used in **public sector projects**.
- **Net BCR (NBCR):** $BCR - 1$ — represents the *excess* return per rupee invested.
- **Discount Factor:** Always use $P/F = (1+i)^{-t}$ for single payments; $P/A = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ for uniform annual series.
- **MARR (i):** Typically **10%** in KTU private-sector problems; **8%–12%** in public-sector / government problems.
- **BCR is best for:** Ranking **mutually exclusive projects** of different scales; for single projects, NPV is equally valid.
- **BCR is NOT a profitability measure** — it is an **efficiency measure** (₹ return per ₹ invested).
- **Annual Worth BCR** = $AW(\text{Benefits}) / AW(\text{Costs})$ — shortcut for uniform cash flow problems.
- Always state the **decision** (Accept/Reject) explicitly at the end of the solution.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Never use Python or programming code in numerical solutions — KTU expects manual, factor-based table solutions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is defined as the discount rate ($r^\*$) at which the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of all cash flows (both inflows and outflows) generated by a project or investment equals **zero**. Mathematically, it is the root of the NPV equation.

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + IRR)^t} = 0$$

Where:
- $CF_t$ = Net Cash Flow at time period $t$
- $n$ = Total number of periods
- $IRR$ = Internal Rate of Return (the unknown variable)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The IRR represents the **break-even rate of return** — the projected effective annualised return on an investment where the time-adjusted inflows exactly recover the initial outflow. It is a **percentage return metric**, not an absolute monetary value.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you deposit **₹1,00,000** in a bank that offers a *mystery interest rate* compounded annually. After **5 years**, you receive a lump sum of **₹1,50,000**. The IRR is the exact rate at which your money grew each year — i.e., it is the **hidden growth rate** embedded inside the cash flow stream.

In plain English:

> *"IRR answers the question — At what rate of return would this investment exactly break even, producing neither profit nor loss in present-value terms?"*

### 1.3 Key Decision Rule

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Standard Decision Rule:**
> - **Accept the project** if $IRR \geq$ Required Rate of Return (Hurdle Rate / Cost of Capital, $r$).
> - **Reject the project** if $IRR < r$.
> - When comparing **mutually exclusive projects**, choose the one with the **higher IRR**, *provided* the scale and timing of cash flows are comparable (otherwise, use NPV or Incremental IRR).

### 1.4 Significance in Financial Economics

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Capital Budgeting** | Primary screening tool for long-term investment proposals |
| **Performance Metric** | Used to benchmark project profitability against hurdle rates |
| **Risk Indicator** | Higher IRR margin over cost of capital implies greater risk-adjusted returns |
| **Communication Tool** | Expressed as a percentage, making it intuitive for non-financial stakeholders |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of the IRR Method

1. **Reinvestment Assumption:** All intermediate cash flows are reinvested at the **IRR itself** (this is a key point often contrasted with NPV's reinvestment at cost of capital).
2. **Time Value of Money:** Cash flows occurring at different times are not directly comparable without discounting.
3. **Conventional Cash Flows:** For a *unique* IRR to exist, the cash flow stream should typically have **one sign change** (e.g., initial outflow followed by inflows).
4. **Single Rate of Return:** IRR assumes a constant discount rate over the entire project life.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of IRR Computation

The IRR is not "given" — it must be **solved for**. The process is:

- **Step 1:** Set up the NPV equation as a function of $r$ and equate it to **zero**.
- **Step 2:** Identify two discount rates $R_1$ and $R_2$ such that $NPV(R_1) > 0$ and $NPV(R_2) < 0$ (i.e., bracket the root).
- **Step 3:** Apply **linear interpolation** (the standard KTU board method) to estimate IRR.
- **Step 4 (Validation):** Verify by plugging the computed IRR back into the NPV equation; the answer must approximate zero.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Linear Interpolation Method

Since the NPV function is generally **non-linear** with respect to $r$, no closed-form algebraic solution exists for projects with more than two cash flow periods. KTU examiners accept the **interpolation approximation** as the standard method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> The closer $R_1$ and $R_2$ are kept (typically a **2% to 5% spread**), the more accurate the interpolation result.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **NPV = 0 Equation (IRR Definition)** | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | Foundational identity |
| 2 | **Linear Interpolation Formula** | $IRR = R_1 + \dfrac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2} \times (R_2 - R_1)$ | Used by KTU board for all numerical problems |
| 3 | **Conventional Project IRR Decision** | Accept if $IRR > k$ (cost of capital) | $k$ = required return |
| 4 | **Incremental IRR (Mutually Exclusive)** | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\Delta CF_t}{(1+\Delta IRR)^t} = 0$ | Where $\Delta CF_t = CF_{t,B} - CF_{t,A}$ |
| 5 | **MIRR (Modified IRR)** | $MIRR = \left(\dfrac{FV_{inflows}}{PV_{outflows}}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | Reinvests at cost of capital |
| 6 | **Simple Project (2-period)** | $IRR = \dfrac{CF_1}{CF_0} - 1$ | Analytical closed form |
| 7 | **Average Investment** | $\dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{2}$ | Used in Accounting Rate of Return comparison |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Infrastructure Projects:** Roads, bridges, and power plants are evaluated using IRR against government hurdle rates.
- **Corporate CapEx Decisions:** Manufacturing firms use IRR to justify new plant and machinery purchases.
- **Venture Capital & Startups:** IRR is the **primary KPI** for evaluating fund performance and startup valuations.
- **Renewable Energy:** Solar and wind projects use IRR to secure financing and compare against tariff benchmarks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — IRR for a Two-Period Project

**Problem Setup:**
An investment of ₹10,000 today yields ₹12,000 at the end of Year 1. Find the IRR.

**Step-by-Step Algebraic Expansion:**

The general NPV equation is:

$$NPV = CF_0 + \frac{CF_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{CF_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{CF_n}{(1+r)^n} = 0$$

For a two-period investment, it simplifies to:

$$CF_0 + \frac{CF_1}{(1+IRR)^1} = 0$$

Substituting the values $CF_0 = -10000$ and $CF_1 = 12000$:

$$-10000 + \frac{12000}{(1+IRR)^1} = 0$$

Rearranging the equation to isolate the IRR term:

$$\frac{12000}{1+IRR} = 10000$$

Cross-multiplying to obtain the IRR factor:

$$1 + IRR = \frac{12000}{10000}$$

$$1 + IRR = 1.20$$

Subtracting 1 from both sides to isolate IRR:

$$IRR = 1.20 - 1 = 0.20$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$\boxed{IRR = 20\%}$$

**Interpretation:** The investment yields an effective annual return of **20%**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — IRR Using Linear Interpolation (KTU Board Method)

**Problem Setup:**
A project requires an initial investment of **₹50,000** and is expected to generate cash inflows of **₹20,000 per year for 4 years**. The cost of capital is **10%**. Compute the IRR using the interpolation method.

**Step 1: Set up the NPV equation.**

$$NPV = -50000 + \frac{20000}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{20000}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{20000}{(1+r)^3} + \frac{20000}{(1+r)^4}$$

**Step 2: Bracket the IRR — Try $R_1 = 10\%$.**

$$NPV_{10\%} = -50000 + 20000 \times PVIFA(10\%, 4)$$

Computing the Present Value Interest Factor of Annuity:

$$PVIFA(10\%, 4) = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-4}}{0.10}$$

$$PVIFA(10\%, 4) = \frac{1 - 0.6830}{0.10} = \frac{0.3170}{0.10} = 3.170$$

Substituting:

$$NPV_{10\%} = -50000 + 20000 \times 3.170 = -50000 + 63400 = +13400$$

Since NPV is positive, we need a **higher** discount rate.

**Step 3: Try $R_2 = 20\%$.**

$$PVIFA(20\%, 4) = \frac{1 - (1.20)^{-4}}{0.20}$$

$$(1.20)^{-4} = \frac{1}{(1.20)^4} = \frac{1}{2.0736} = 0.4823$$

$$PVIFA(20\%, 4) = \frac{1 - 0.4823}{0.20} = \frac{0.5177}{0.20} = 2.5883$$

Substituting:

$$NPV_{20\%} = -50000 + 20000 \times 2.5883 = -50000 + 51766 = +1766$$

Still positive — we must go higher.

**Step 4: Try $R_2 = 22\%$.**

$$PVIFA(22\%, 4) = \frac{1 - (1.22)^{-4}}{0.22}$$

$$(1.22)^{-4} = \frac{1}{(1.22)^4} = \frac{1}{2.2153} = 0.4514$$

$$PVIFA(22\%, 4) = \frac{1 - 0.4514}{0.22} = \frac{0.5486}{0.22} = 2.4936$$

Substituting:

$$NPV_{22\%} = -50000 + 20000 \times 2.4936 = -50000 + 49872 = -128$$

Now NPV is **negative** ($-128$). The IRR lies between **20% and 22%**.

**Step 5: Apply Linear Interpolation.**

$$IRR = R_1 + \frac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2} \times (R_2 - R_1)$$

Substituting $R_1 = 20\%$, $NPV_1 = +1766$, $R_2 = 22\%$, $NPV_2 = -128$:

$$IRR = 20 + \frac{1766}{1766 - (-128)} \times (22 - 20)$$

$$IRR = 20 + \frac{1766}{1894} \times 2$$

$$IRR = 20 + 0.9324 \times 2$$

$$IRR = 20 + 1.8648$$

$$\boxed{IRR \approx 21.87\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Since $IRR = 21.87\% > 10\%$ (cost of capital), **accept the project**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Incremental IRR (Mutually Exclusive Projects)

**Problem Setup:**
Two machines, A and B, with the following cash flows:

| Year | Machine A (₹) | Machine B (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | -100000 | -150000 |
| 1 | 40000 | 60000 |
| 2 | 40000 | 60000 |
| 3 | 40000 | 60000 |
| 4 | 40000 | 60000 |

The cost of capital is **10%**. Determine which machine to select.

**Step 1: Compute the IRR of each project independently.**

For Machine A:

$$-100000 + 40000 \times PVIFA(r, 4) = 0$$

$$PVIFA(r, 4) = \frac{100000}{40000} = 2.500$$

From standard tables, the rate corresponding to $PVIFA = 2.500$ for 4 periods is approximately **21.86%**.

So $IRR_A \approx 21.86\%$.

For Machine B:

$$-150000 + 60000 \times PVIFA(r, 4) = 0$$

$$PVIFA(r, 4) = \frac{150000}{60000} = 2.500$$

The rate is identical: $IRR_B \approx 21.86\%$.

**Conclusion so far:** Both have equal IRR, but **B requires ₹50,000 more investment**.

**Step 2: Compute the Incremental Cash Flows (B - A).**

| Year | $\Delta CF_t$ (₹) |
|---|---|
| 0 | -50000 |
| 1 | 20000 |
| 2 | 20000 |
| 3 | 20000 |
| 4 | 20000 |

**Step 3: Compute the Incremental IRR ($\Delta IRR$).**

$$-50000 + 20000 \times PVIFA(r, 4) = 0$$

$$PVIFA(r, 4) = \frac{50000}{20000} = 2.500$$

From tables: $\Delta IRR \approx 21.86\%$.

**Step 4: Compare with cost of capital.**

Since $\Delta IRR (21.86\%) > k (10\%)$, the **extra investment in B is justified**. Therefore, **choose Machine B**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Insight:**
> For mutually exclusive projects with different scales, the **Incremental IRR** method is superior to comparing standalone IRRs because it accounts for the additional capital deployed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IRR Computation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step 1: Identify Cash Flow Stream CF sub t] --> nodeB[Step 2: Construct NPV Equation as Function of r]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3: Bracket IRR with Two Guess Rates R1 and R2]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Step 4: NPV at R1 greater than Zero?}
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeE[Step 4a: NPV positive implies IRR greater than R1]
    nodeD -->|No| nodeF[Step 4b: NPV negative implies IRR less than R1]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Step 5: Apply Linear Interpolation Formula]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step 6: Validate by Substituting IRR into NPV Equation]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Step 7: Compare IRR with Cost of Capital k]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{Is IRR greater than or equal to k}
    nodeJ -->|Yes| nodeK[Decision: ACCEPT the Project]
    nodeJ -->|No| nodeL[Decision: REJECT the Project]
```

### 4.2 IRR Decision Matrix for Project Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeS[Start: Evaluate Project] --> nodeT[Compute IRR of Project]
    nodeT --> nodeU{IRR greater than Hurdle Rate k}
    nodeU -->|Yes| nodeV[Standalone: ACCEPT Project]
    nodeU -->|No| nodeW[Standalone: REJECT Project]
    nodeV --> nodeX{Mutually Exclusive Comparison Required}
    nodeX -->|Yes| nodeY[Compute Incremental Cash Flows B minus A]
    nodeX -->|No| nodeZ[Process Complete]
    nodeY --> nodeAA[Compute Delta IRR]
    nodeAA --> nodeAB{Delta IRR greater than k}
    nodeAB -->|Yes| nodeAC[Choose Higher Investment Project B]
    nodeAB -->|No| nodeAD[Choose Lower Investment Project A]
    nodeAC --> nodeZ
    nodeAD --> nodeZ
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of IRR Method

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA1[Input Module: Cash Flow Vector CF0 to CFn] --> nodeB1[Processing Module: NPV Function Evaluator]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1[Bisection Engine: Bracket the Root]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1[Interpolation Module: Linear Approximation]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE1[Validation Module: Substitute IRR and Confirm NPV equals Zero]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF1[Comparison Module: Compare IRR with Hurdle Rate k]
    nodeF1 --> nodeG1[Output Module: Accept or Reject Decision]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** Each block represents a logical stage in the IRR computation pipeline. The arrows indicate the unidirectional flow of data from the raw cash flow inputs to the final investment decision.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define Internal Rate of Return (IRR). State its decision criterion for project acceptance.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is the discount rate at which the Net Present Value (NPV) of a project becomes zero. It is the rate $r$ that satisfies:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0$$

**Decision Criterion (1 Mark):**
A project should be accepted if $IRR \geq k$ (cost of capital/hurdle rate); otherwise, it should be rejected.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Distinguish between NPV and IRR as capital budgeting techniques.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | NPV | IRR |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | Absolute monetary value of project worth | Percentage return from the project |
| **Reinvestment Assumption** | Reinvests at cost of capital | Reinvests at IRR itself |
| **Decision Rule** | Accept if NPV $\geq$ 0 | Accept if IRR $\geq k$ |
| **Reliability for Mutually Exclusive Projects** | More reliable | Can give misleading rankings |
| **Multiple Solutions** | Single value | Multiple IRRs possible for non-conventional cash flows |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A company is evaluating a project requiring an initial investment of **₹2,00,000**. The expected cash inflows are **₹60,000 per year for 5 years**. The cost of capital is **12%**. Calculate the IRR using the linear interpolation method. Should the project be accepted? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the limitations of the IRR method. How does the Modified Internal Rate of Return (MIRR) overcome these limitations? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1: NPV Equation**

$$-200000 + 60000 \times PVIFA(r, 5) = 0$$

**Step 2: Bracket the IRR**

Try $R_1 = 15\%$:

$$PVIFA(15\%, 5) = \frac{1 - (1.15)^{-5}}{0.15} = \frac{1 - 0.4972}{0.15} = 3.3522$$

$$NPV_{15\%} = -200000 + 60000 \times 3.3522 = -200000 + 201131 = +1131$$

Try $R_2 = 16\%$:

$$PVIFA(16\%, 5) = \frac{1 - (1.16)^{-5}}{0.16} = \frac{1 - 0.4761}{0.16} = 3.2743$$

$$NPV_{16\%} = -200000 + 60000 \times 3.2743 = -200000 + 196459 = -3541$$

**Step 3: Linear Interpolation**

$$IRR = 15 + \frac{1131}{1131 - (-3541)} \times (16 - 15)$$

$$IRR = 15 + \frac{1131}{4672} \times 1$$

$$IRR = 15 + 0.2421$$

$$\boxed{IRR \approx 15.24\%}$$

**Step 4: Decision**

Since $IRR (15.24\%) > k (12\%)$, **the project should be ACCEPTED**.

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Limitations of IRR Method:**

1. **Multiple IRR Problem:** For non-conventional cash flows (more than one sign change), IRR may yield multiple values, making decision-making ambiguous.
2. **Reinvestment Rate Assumption:** IRR assumes intermediate cash flows are reinvested at the IRR, which may be unrealistic.
3. **Scale Ignorance:** IRR is a percentage measure and does not capture the absolute size of returns.
4. **Mutually Exclusive Project Conflict:** IRR and NPV may give conflicting rankings for mutually exclusive projects with different scales.
5. **No Direct Value Addition Measure:** IRR does not indicate the dollar amount of value created, unlike NPV.

**MIRR as a Solution:**

The **Modified Internal Rate of Return (MIRR)** addresses these limitations by:

- Reinvesting intermediate cash flows at the **cost of capital** (realistic assumption).
- Producing a **unique rate** even for non-conventional cash flows.
- Being computed as:

$$MIRR = \left(\frac{FV \text{ of Inflows at } k}{PV \text{ of Outflows at } k}\right)^{1/n} - 1$$

where $n$ is the project duration.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** A firm must choose between two mutually exclusive projects, A and B, with the following cash flows:

| Year | Project A (₹) | Project B (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | -80000 | -120000 |
| 1 | 30000 | 50000 |
| 2 | 30000 | 50000 |
| 3 | 30000 | 50000 |

The cost of capital is **10%**. Use the **Incremental IRR method** to determine which project should be selected. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define Net Present Value (NPV). Explain why NPV is considered superior to IRR in capital budgeting decisions. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1: Compute Standalone IRRs (Optional Check).**

For Project A:

$$-80000 + 30000 \times PVIFA(r, 3) = 0 \implies PVIFA(r, 3) = 2.6667$$

From tables: $IRR_A \approx 12.51\%$.

For Project B:

$$-120000 + 50000 \times PVIFA(r, 3) = 0 \implies PVIFA(r, 3) = 2.4000$$

From tables: $IRR_B \approx 16.70\%$.

Since $IRR_B > IRR_A$, a naive IRR comparison would suggest Project B. However, **the incremental analysis must be conducted**.

**Step 2: Compute Incremental Cash Flows (B - A).**

| Year | $\Delta CF_t$ (₹) |
|---|---|
| 0 | -40000 |
| 1 | 20000 |
| 2 | 20000 |
| 3 | 20000 |

**Step 3: Compute Incremental IRR.**

$$-40000 + 20000 \times PVIFA(r, 3) = 0 \implies PVIFA(r, 3) = 2.000$$

From tables, the rate corresponding to $PVIFA = 2.000$ for 3 periods is exactly **$r = 23.38\%$**.

**Step 4: Decision**

Since $\Delta IRR (23.38\%) > k (10\%)$, the additional ₹40,000 invested in Project B earns a return greater than the cost of capital. Hence, **Project B should be selected**.

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Definition of NPV (3 Marks):**

Net Present Value (NPV) is the difference between the present value of cash inflows and the present value of cash outflows over a project's lifetime, discounted at the cost of capital.

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t}$$

A positive NPV indicates that the project generates value above the required return.

**Why NPV is Superior to IRR (4 Marks):**

1. **Direct Value Measurement:** NPV measures the absolute rupee value added to the firm, directly aligning with shareholder wealth maximization — the primary objective of financial management.
2. **Realistic Reinvestment Assumption:** NPV assumes reinvestment at the cost of capital, which is more practical than IRR's assumption of reinvestment at the (often high) IRR itself.
3. **No Multiple Solutions:** Unlike IRR, NPV always yields a single, unambiguous value, regardless of cash flow patterns.
4. **Reliable Ranking for Mutually Exclusive Projects:** NPV provides consistent and theoretically correct rankings, even when projects differ in scale or timing.
5. **Compatibility with DCF Framework:** NPV is the cornerstone of modern corporate finance theory and is consistent with value-addition principles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Interpolation Range Error:** Students often choose $R_1$ and $R_2$ too far apart (e.g., 10% and 30%), resulting in significant approximation errors. KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for wide interpolation ranges.
> 2. **Sign Confusion in Interpolation:** Always verify $NPV_1$ is positive and $NPV_2$ is negative (or vice versa). Mismatched signs will produce incorrect IRR values.
> 3. **Incremental IRR Oversight:** For mutually exclusive projects, comparing standalone IRRs alone is insufficient — the incremental IRR analysis is **mandatory** in KTU board evaluations.
> 4. **Unit Confusion:** IRR is a rate (expressed in %), not a rupee amount. Conflating IRR with NPV values is a common error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

- **Definition Mastery:** IRR is the discount rate at which $NPV = 0$; it is a **percentage return metric**.
- **Core Equation:** $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$.
- **Interpolation Formula:** $IRR = R_1 + \dfrac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2} \times (R_2 - R_1)$.
- **Decision Rule:** Accept if $IRR \geq k$ (cost of capital).
- **Bracketing Rule:** $NPV_1$ and $NPV_2$ must have **opposite signs** for interpolation to be valid.
- **Spacing Rule:** Keep $R_1$ and $R_2$ within a **2% to 5% spread** for accurate approximation.
- **Multiple IRR Warning:** Non-conventional cash flows (multiple sign changes) can produce multiple IRRs — use **MIRR** or **NPV** instead.
- **Incremental IRR:** Mandatory for mutually exclusive projects with different scales.
- **Reinvestment Assumption:** IRR assumes reinvestment at IRR; NPV assumes reinvestment at cost of capital $k$.
- **Two-Period Shortcut:** For projects with $CF_0$ and $CF_1$ only, $IRR = \dfrac{CF_1}{CF_0} - 1$.
- **NPV vs IRR Priority:** When in doubt or when projects conflict, **NPV is the superior method** — KTU examiners value this awareness.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never compute IRR of incremental cash flows by simply subtracting two IRRs — you must subtract the **cash flows themselves** first, then compute IRR.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modified Internal Rate of Return. Apply Time Value of Concepts to Practical Problems in Financial Economics
Introduction to Financial Derivatives: Types and Uses of Derivatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Financial Derivatives: Types and Uses of Derivatives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Financial Derivative** is a contractual instrument whose value is *derived* from the value of one or more underlying variables, which may be financial assets (stocks, bonds, currencies, commodities) or non-financial assets (agricultural products, weather indices, carbon credits). The **2024 KTU syllabus** classifies derivatives under the broader domain of *risk management instruments* traded in organized exchanges or over-the-counter (OTC) markets.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Standard)**
> A derivative is a financial contract that derives its value from the performance of an underlying entity, such as an asset, index, or interest rate. It does not have any direct or independent value of its own.

The **standard market metrics** associated with every derivative contract are:
- **Spot Price (S₀)** = Current market price of the underlying asset.
- **Forward/Future Price (F)** = Agreed price for a future transaction.
- **Strike Price (K)** = Pre-determined exercise price (for options).
- **Premium** = Up-front cost of purchasing an option.
- **Notional Principal** = Reference amount on which payments are calculated (especially in swaps).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **The "Tomato Farmer" Analogy — Plain English Explanation**
> Imagine you are a tomato farmer in Palakkad. You will harvest tomatoes in 3 months, but you are terrified that the price might crash by then. So, you sign a contract with a buyer in Kochi *today* to sell 1,000 kg of tomatoes at **₹40/kg** after 3 months — regardless of the market price then.
> - You have **locked in your price** (no risk of a crash) — this is **hedging**.
> - A speculator who thinks prices will rise could buy this contract from you for ₹500 — hoping to sell it later for more — this is **speculation**.
> - If the market price in Kochi is ₹38 and someone can enforce the same contract at ₹40 elsewhere — this is **arbitrage**.
> This contract (Tomato Forward Contract) is a **financial derivative** — its value depends on the future price of tomatoes.

### 1.3 Why Derivatives Exist — The Core "Why"

Derivatives solve **three fundamental economic problems**:

1. **Risk Transfer** — Move unwanted price risk to those willing to bear it.
2. **Price Discovery** — Futures markets aggregate information and signal future expectations.
3. **Market Efficiency** — Arbitrageurs correct mispricings across related markets, ensuring prices reflect fundamentals.

---

## 1.4 Participants in the Derivatives Market

| Participant Type | Primary Motive |
|---|---|
| **Hedgers** | Reduce or eliminate existing risk exposure |
| **Speculators** | Profit by taking calculated bets on price movements |
| **Arbitrageurs** | Earn risk-free profits from price differences across markets |
| **Margin Traders / Investors** | Leverage positions for amplified returns |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> The 2024 scheme emphasizes that *hedgers, speculators, and arbitrageurs* form the **three pillars** of derivatives market participants. Exam questions frequently test the distinction between these three roles.

---

## 1.5 Historical Context (Brief)

- **1848** — Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) standardized grain *forward contracts*.
- **1972** — Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) launched the first *financial futures* (currency futures).
- **1973** — Black-Scholes model published; CBOE opened *options trading*.
- **1981** — First *interest rate swap* executed (IBM vs. World Bank).
- **2000s–Present** — Exponential growth of credit, weather, and crypto derivatives.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Major Types of Derivatives

### 2.1.1 Forwards
A **forward contract** is a customized, OTC agreement between two parties to buy or sell an asset at a predetermined future date for a specified price. It is settled **only once at maturity** and carries **high counterparty risk** because no exchange guarantees the trade.

### 2.1.2 Futures
A **futures contract** is a standardized, exchange-traded version of a forward contract. It is **marked-to-market daily**, has a clearinghouse guarantee, and requires an **initial margin** plus **variation margin**. Futures are highly liquid and have negligible default risk.

### 2.1.3 Options
An **option** is a contract that gives the holder the *right but not the obligation* to buy (Call) or sell (Put) an underlying asset at a predetermined strike price before or on a specified date. The buyer pays a **premium** upfront; the seller (writer) collects this premium and bears the obligation.

### 2.1.4 Swaps
A **swap** is an OTC contract in which two parties exchange cash flows over time. The most common types are **interest rate swaps** (fixed-for-floating) and **currency swaps** (exchange of principal and interest in different currencies).

> [!IMPORTANT] **Distinction Payoff Logic**
> - Forwards & Futures → **Obligatory** on both parties.
> - Options → **Right without obligation** for the buyer only.

---

## 2.2 Underlying Assets — The Foundation of Derivatives

Derivatives can be created on virtually any variable:

| Underlying Class | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Equity** | Individual stocks, stock indices (NIFTY 50, S&P 500) |
| **Debt** | Bonds, T-Bills, interest rates (LIBOR, MIBOR) |
| **Commodities** | Gold, crude oil, wheat, copper |
| **Currency** | USD/INR, EUR/USD |
| **Credit** | Credit default swaps on corporate bonds |
| **Exotic** | Weather, electricity, carbon credits, volatility indices |

---

## 2.3 Three Core Uses of Derivatives

### A. Hedging
Hedging is the **risk-reduction strategy** where derivatives are used to offset potential losses in an existing position. A farmer sells wheat futures to protect against a price fall; an importer buys currency futures to lock in the dollar-rupee rate.

### B. Speculation
Speculation is the act of taking a **deliberate risk position** to profit from anticipated price movements. Speculators accept risk that hedgers wish to shed. They provide liquidity and depth to the market.

### C. Arbitrage
Arbitrage is the **simultaneous purchase and sale** of an asset in different markets to profit from a tiny price differential, with **zero net investment and zero risk**. It is the engine of market efficiency.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] All formulas below are **KTU 2024 high-yield** and frequently tested in both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).

### Forward Pricing Formula (No Income, No Storage Cost)

$$F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{rT}$$

where:
- $F_0$ = Theoretical forward price today
- $S_0$ = Current spot price
- $r$ = Risk-free annual interest rate (continuous compounding)
- $T$ = Time to maturity in years
- $e$ = Euler's number $\approx 2.71828$

### Forward Pricing (With Discrete Compounding)

$$F_0 = S_0 \cdot (1 + r)^T$$

### Forward Pricing (With Known Income $I$)

$$F_0 = (S_0 - PV(I)) \cdot e^{rT}$$

### Forward Pricing (With Storage Cost $U$)

$$F_0 = (S_0 + PV(U)) \cdot e^{rT}$$

### Forward Pricing (With Continuous Yield $q$)

$$F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r - q)T}$$

### Option Payoff Formulas (at Expiry)

$$\text{Call Payoff} = \max(S_T - K, \; 0)$$

$$\text{Put Payoff} = \max(K - S_T, \; 0)$$

### Option Profit Formulas (after paying premium $C_0$ or $P_0$)

$$\text{Call Profit} = \max(S_T - K, \; 0) - C_0$$

$$\text{Put Profit} = \max(K - S_T, \; 0) - P_0$$

### Put-Call Parity (European Options, Non-Dividend Stock)

$$C + K \cdot e^{-rT} = P + S_0$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Boundary Conditions for Exam**
> - If $T \to 0$: $F_0 \to S_0$ (convergence to spot).
> - If $r \to 0$: $F_0 \to S_0$ (no time value).
> - $C \geq \max(S_0 - K e^{-rT}, \; 0)$ (lower bound for European call).
> - $P \geq \max(K e^{-rT} - S_0, \; 0)$ (lower bound for European put).

### Quick Reference Table — Formula Summary

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward (continuous) | $F_0 = S_0 e^{rT}$ | $S_0$, $r$, $T$ |
| 2 | Forward (discrete) | $F_0 = S_0 (1+r)^T$ | $S_0$, $r$, $T$ |
| 3 | Forward (with yield) | $F_0 = S_0 e^{(r-q)T}$ | $S_0$, $r$, $q$, $T$ |
| 4 | Call Payoff | $\max(S_T - K, 0)$ | $S_T$, $K$ |
| 5 | Put Payoff | $\max(K - S_T, 0)$ | $S_T$, $K$ |
| 6 | Put-Call Parity | $C + K e^{-rT} = P + S_0$ | $C$, $P$, $K$, $r$, $T$, $S_0$ |

> [!WARNING] **Markdown Table Pipe Rule Applied**
> All absolute-value and conditional expressions use `\max(...)` instead of the vertical pipe `\|` to preserve table formatting integrity.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Finance Fields

| Industry | Practical Use of Derivatives |
|---|---|
| **Construction Firms** | Hedging steel and copper price volatility |
| **Aviation Companies** | Jet fuel futures to lock in fuel costs |
| **IT Outsourcing** | Currency forwards to manage USD/INR exposure |
| **Banks** | Interest rate swaps to manage ALM (Asset-Liability Mismatch) |
| **Agricultural Sector** | Crop futures for price stabilization |
| **Energy Sector** | Crude oil & electricity derivatives |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Forward Price with No Income (Cost-of-Carry Model)

### Problem Setup
A stock has a current spot price of **$S_0$**. The continuously compounded risk-free rate is **$r$**. There are no dividends or storage costs. We need to derive the theoretical forward price $F_0$ for delivery at time **$T$**.

### Step 1: Construct Two Portfolios

**Portfolio A**: Enter into a forward contract to buy 1 unit of the stock at price $F_0$ at time $T$.

**Portfolio B**: Borrow $S_0$ dollars today at rate $r$, and use the loan proceeds to buy 1 unit of the stock today (at spot price $S_0$).

### Step 2: Cash Flow at Time $T$

**Portfolio A at time $T$**:
- Receives 1 stock.
- Pays $F_0$.
- Net value = $1 \text{ stock} - F_0$.

**Portfolio B at time $T$**:
- Holds 1 stock (purchased today).
- Owes repayment of loan: $S_0 \cdot e^{rT}$.
- Net value = $1 \text{ stock} - S_0 \cdot e^{rT}$.

### Step 3: Apply the No-Arbitrage Principle

Since both portfolios deliver **1 stock at time $T$**, they must have the **same value today** (otherwise arbitrage would exist).

Therefore:

$$F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{rT}$$

### Step 4: Verification via Discounting

We can verify by checking that any other price would create arbitrage:

- If $F_0 > S_0 \cdot e^{rT}$: Sell the forward, borrow $S_0$, buy stock → **risk-free profit**.
- If $F_0 < S_0 \cdot e^{rT}$: Buy the forward, short the stock, invest proceeds → **risk-free profit**.

Hence equilibrium requires:

$$\boxed{F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{rT}}$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example 1 — Forward Pricing (KTU Standard)

**Problem**: The spot price of gold is ₹60,000 per 10 grams. The continuously compounded risk-free rate is 6% per annum. What is the 9-month forward price?

### Given Data
- $S_0 = 60{,}000$
- $r = 0.06$
- $T = \frac{9}{12} = 0.75$ years

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{rT}$$

$$F_0 = 60{,}000 \cdot e^{(0.06)(0.75)}$$

$$F_0 = 60{,}000 \cdot e^{0.045}$$

$$e^{0.045} \approx 1.04603$$

$$F_0 = 60{,}000 \cdot 1.04603$$

$$F_0 \approx 62{,}761.80$$

**Answer**: The 9-month forward price is approximately **₹62,761.80 per 10 grams**.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example 2 — Option Payoff Analysis

**Problem**: A European call option on a stock has a strike price K = ₹100. The current premium is C₀ = ₹8. The stock price at expiry (S_T) is ₹120. Compute:
(a) Intrinsic value
(b) Payoff
(c) Profit

### Given Data
- $K = 100$
- $C_0 = 8$
- $S_T = 120$

### (a) Intrinsic Value

$$\text{Intrinsic Value} = \max(S_T - K, \; 0)$$

$$= \max(120 - 100, \; 0)$$

$$= \max(20, \; 0)$$

$$= ₹20$$

### (b) Payoff at Expiry

For a long call holder, payoff = intrinsic value = **₹20**.

### (c) Profit (Net of Premium)

$$\text{Profit} = \text{Payoff} - \text{Premium Paid}$$

$$\text{Profit} = 20 - 8 = ₹12$$

**Answer**: The option is "in-the-money" with a profit of ₹12 per share.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Worked Example 3 — Put-Call Parity Check

**Problem**: A stock has spot price S₀ = ₹50. The 3-month risk-free rate is 4% (continuous). A 3-month European call with strike K = ₹50 trades at ₹5. Find the price of the corresponding European put using put-call parity.

### Given Data
- $S_0 = 50$
- $K = 50$
- $r = 0.04$
- $T = 0.25$ years
- $C = 5$

### Applying Put-Call Parity

$$C + K e^{-rT} = P + S_0$$

$$5 + 50 \cdot e^{-(0.04)(0.25)} = P + 50$$

$$5 + 50 \cdot e^{-0.01} = P + 50$$

$$e^{-0.01} \approx 0.99005$$

$$5 + 50 \cdot 0.99005 = P + 50$$

$$5 + 49.5025 = P + 50$$

$$54.5025 = P + 50$$

$$P = 4.5025$$

**Answer**: The European put price is approximately **₹4.50**.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Forwards vs Futures vs Options vs Swaps

| Feature | Forwards | Futures | Options | Swaps |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Trading Venue** | OTC | Exchange | Exchange / OTC | OTC |
| **Standardization** | Custom | Standard | Standard | Custom |
| **Counterparty Risk** | High | Very Low (clearing house) | Low (for buyers) | High |
| **Obligation** | Both parties | Both parties | Buyer only | Both parties |
| **Settlement** | At maturity | Daily MTM | At/Before expiry | Periodic |
| **Initial Cost** | None | Margin required | Premium paid | None typically |
| **Liquidity** | Low | High | High | Low |
| **Example** | Bank loan rate lock | NIFTY futures | Stock call option | Fixed-floating IRS |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
