PV of Face Value at Maturity] InputC --> ProcessC ProcessA --> ProcessB ProcessB --> OutputA[Output Block
Bond Price P0 equals PV Coupons plus PV Face Value] ProcessC --> OutputA ``` ## 4.3 Stock Valuation Framework — DDM Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD Root[Stock Valuation Problem] --> Choice1{Dividend Growth Pattern} Choice1 -->|Constant Dividends| ZeroGrowth[Zero Growth DDM
P0 equals D divided by r] Choice1 -->|Constant Growth Rate g| Gordon[Gordon Growth Model
P0 equals D1 divided by r minus g] Choice1 -->|Two Phase Growth| TwoStage[Two Stage DDM
Initial High Growth plus Stable Phase] Choice1 -->|Variable Growth| MultiStage[Multi Stage DDM
Sum of Discounted Dividends by Phase] ZeroGrowth --> Constraint1[Constraint r greater than 0] Gordon --> Constraint2[Constraint r greater than g strictly required] TwoStage --> Constraint3[Requires r greater than g2 stable growth rate] ``` ## 4.4 Bond Yield Classification Flow ```mermaid graph TD BondYield[Bond Yield Concept] --> A[Current Yield CY equals C divided by P0] BondYield --> B[Yield to Maturity YTM equals IRR holding to maturity] BondYield --> C[Yield to Call YTC equals IRR assuming called early] BondYield --> D[Realized Yield equals holding period return] A --> E[Used for quick comparison only] B --> F[Most comprehensive single measure] C --> G[Used when bond is callable] D --> H[Used for sold before maturity scenarios] B --> PremiumCheck{YTM versus Coupon Rate} PremiumCheck -->|YTM equals c| ParBond[Par Bond P0 equals FV] PremiumCheck -->|YTM less than c| PremBond[Premium Bond P0 greater than FV] PremiumCheck -->|YTM greater than c| DiscBond[Discount Bond P0 less than FV] ``` ## 4.5 The Risk-Return Spectrum of Securities ```mermaid graph LR A[Treasury Bills
Lowest Risk] --> B[Government Bonds] B --> C[Corporate Bonds
Investment Grade] C --> D[High Yield Junk Bonds] D --> E[Preferred Stocks] E --> F[Common Stocks
Highest Risk] A -.->|Risk Increases| F A -.->|Return Potential Increases| F ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define a Bond. List its essential features.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Bond** is a debt security issued by corporations or governments that obliges the issuer to pay the bondholder fixed periodic coupon payments and the face value at maturity. **Essential Features:** 1. **Face Value (Par Value):** Principal amount repaid at maturity (typically ₹1,000). 2. **Coupon Rate:** Annual interest rate stated on the bond, applied to face value. 3. **Maturity Date:** The pre-specified date when the principal is repaid. 4. **Coupon Payments:** Periodic interest payments (usually semi-annual). 5. **Issue Price & Market Price:** Price at issuance vs current trading price. *(Award: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for 4 features — ½ mark each.)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Distinguish between Current Yield and Yield to Maturity (YTM) of a bond.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Basis | Current Yield (CY) | Yield to Maturity (YTM) | |---|---|---| | **Formula** | $CY = C / P_0$ | $P_0 = \sum C/(1+y)^t + FV/(1+y)^T$ (solved for $y$) | | **Considers Capital Gain?** | No | Yes (FV − P₀ amortized over T) | | **Assumes Holding Period** | 1 year | Till maturity | | **Complexity** | Simple arithmetic | Requires iterative/financial-calculator solution | *(Award: 1 mark for CY definition, 1 mark for YTM definition, 1 mark for clear distinction.)* --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified] **A bond has a face value of ₹1,000, an annual coupon rate of 9%, and 8 years remaining to maturity. The current market price is ₹920. Assume coupons are paid annually.** **Part (a)** — *Calculate the bond's Current Yield and the Yield to Maturity (using the approximation formula).* **(7 Marks)** **Part (b)** — *Explain intuitively why this bond is trading at a discount, and state the relationship between coupon rate and YTM for discount bonds.* **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a)** [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Identify given values:** - $FV = ₹1{,}000$, $c = 9\% = 0.09$, $T = 8$ years, $P_0 = ₹920$ **Step 2 — Compute annual coupon:** $$C = 0.09 \times 1000 = ₹90$$ **Step 3 — Compute Current Yield:** $$CY = \frac{C}{P_0} = \frac{90}{920} = 0.0978 \approx 9.78\%$$ **Step 4 — Apply YTM Approximation Formula:** $$YTM \approx \frac{C + \frac{FV - P_0}{T}}{\frac{FV + P_0}{2}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute values:** $$YTM \approx \frac{90 + \frac{1000 - 920}{8}}{\frac{1000 + 920}{2}}$$ $$YTM \approx \frac{90 + \frac{80}{8}}{\frac{1920}{2}}$$ $$YTM \approx \frac{90 + 10}{960}$$ $$YTM \approx \frac{100}{960} = 0.1042 \approx 10.42\%$$ **Final Answer:** Current Yield = **9.78%**, YTM ≈ **10.42%** *(Mark Distribution: 1 mark identification, 1 mark CY, 2 marks formula application, 2 marks substitution, 1 mark final answer.)* --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b)** [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Compare coupon rate and YTM:** - Coupon Rate = 9% - YTM ≈ 10.42% - Therefore, **YTM > Coupon Rate** **Step 2 — Relationship for discount bonds:** For a bond to trade at a discount ($P_0 < FV$), we must have $YTM > c$. This is because investors demand a higher return than what the coupon provides, since new bonds in the market are offering better yields (market interest rates have risen since the bond's issuance). **Step 3 — Intuitive Explanation:** A discount bond is like a sale — investors buy the bond "on sale" (₹920 instead of ₹1,000), and this ₹80 capital gain, spread over 8 years, boosts the effective return above the coupon rate. The current yield (9.78%) only considers coupon income, ignoring this capital gain, so it understates the true return. **Step 4 — Premium/Par/Discount Classification Table:** | State | Price | Relationship | |---|---|---| | This Bond | ₹920 (Discount) | YTM (10.42%) > Coupon (9%) | **Final Statement:** The bond is a **discount bond** because $c < YTM$, which mathematically forces $P_0 < FV$. *(Mark Distribution: 2 marks comparison, 2 marks relationship rule, 2 marks intuition, 1 mark classification.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified] **A company has just paid a dividend of ₹5 per share. Dividends are expected to grow at a constant rate of 7% per year indefinitely. The required rate of return on equity is 14%.** **Part (a)** — *Using the Gordon Growth Model, calculate the current intrinsic value of the stock. Show all steps.* **(7 Marks)** **Part (b)** — *If the current market price is ₹65, determine whether the stock is overvalued, undervalued, or fairly valued. Justify your decision with a clear investment recommendation.* **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a)** [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Identify given parameters:** - $D_0 = ₹5$ (most recent dividend) - $g = 7\% = 0.07$ (constant growth rate) - $r = 14\% = 0.14$ (required return) **Step 2 — Verify model validity:** Check constraint: $r > g$ → $0.14 > 0.07$ ✔️ Model is valid. **Step 3 — Compute next year's expected dividend:** $$D_1 = D_0 \times (1 + g) = 5 \times (1.07) = ₹5.35$$ **Step 4 — Apply the Gordon Growth Model formula:** $$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{r - g}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute:** $$P_0 = \frac{5.35}{0.14 - 0.07} = \frac{5.35}{0.07}$$ **Step 6 — Final calculation:** $$\boxed{P_0 = ₹76.43}$$ *(Mark Distribution: 1 mark identification, 1 mark constraint check, 2 marks D₁ calculation, 2 marks formula application, 1 mark final answer.)* --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b)** [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Compare intrinsic value with market price:** - Intrinsic Value (calculated) = ₹76.43 - Market Price (given) = ₹65.00 - Difference = ₹76.43 − ₹65.00 = ₹11.43 (positive) **Step 2 — Valuation classification:** Since $P_{\text{intrinsic}} > P_{\text{market}}$, the stock is **UNDERVALUED**. **Step 3 — Justification:** The market is offering the stock at a price lower than what the fundamental cash-flow model suggests it is worth. The intrinsic value exceeds the market price by approximately 17.6%, indicating a potential buying opportunity. **Step 4 — Investment Recommendation:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Recommendation: BUY the stock.** > > - Expected price appreciation as market corrects toward intrinsic value. > - The required return (14%) is achievable given the 7% growth assumption. **Step 5 — Caveats to mention:** - The Gordon Model assumes constant perpetual growth — sensitive to assumption changes. - A 1% change in $g$ significantly alters intrinsic value (high sensitivity). **Final Decision:** **Undervalued → BUY.** *(Mark Distribution: 2 marks comparison, 2 marks classification, 2 marks recommendation, 1 mark caveat.)* --- ## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert coupon rate to decimal.** Using 9 instead of 0.09 in $C = c \times FV$ is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. > 2. **Mixing annual and semi-annual periods.** If coupon is semi-annual, the formula uses $C/2$ and $2T$ periods. KTU papers often test this. > 3. **Not verifying $r > g$ for Gordon Model.** Examiners award 1 mark just for stating this constraint. > 4. **Using $D_0$ instead of $D_1$ in Gordon Model.** This is the #1 conceptual error — Gordon Model uses **next year's** expected dividend in the numerator. > 5. **Skipping units (₹, %).** KTU strictly expects currency units and percentage signs in final answers. > 6. **In YTM problems, presenting only the approximation.** Always verify with trial-and-error or financial calculator for full marks. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Bonds and Securities** - **Security = Tradable Financial Instrument** (Equity / Debt / Hybrid). - **Bond = Debt Security** with fixed coupons and face value repayment. - **Stock = Equity Security** with variable dividends and capital gains. - **Bond Price Formula:** $P_0 = C \times \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-T}}{r} + \dfrac{FV}{(1+r)^T}$ *(master this)*. - **Coupon Payment:** $C = c \times FV$ — always use decimal form of $c$. - **Current Yield:** $CY = C / P_0$ — ignores capital gains. - **YTM** = IRR for buy-and-hold-to-maturity strategy; solved iteratively. - **YTM Approximation:** $YTM \approx \dfrac{C + (FV-P_0)/T}{(FV+P_0)/2}$. - **Premium Bond:** $c > YTM \Rightarrow P_0 > FV$. - **Discount Bond:** $c < YTM \Rightarrow P_0 < FV$. - **Par Bond:** $c = YTM \Rightarrow P_0 = FV$. - **Zero-Growth DDM:** $P_0 = D / r$ — perpetuity with no growth. - **Gordon Growth Model:** $P_0 = D_1 / (r - g) = D_0(1+g)/(r-g)$ — **strictly requires $r > g$**. - **Two-Stage DDM:** Two-phase growth, sum of high-growth PVs + terminal stable-phase PV. - **Yield Curve:** Uptrending (normal) yields rise with maturity; flat or inverted for special economic conditions. - **Intrinsic vs Market Value:** Buy if intrinsic > market; sell if intrinsic < market. - **Risk-Return Tradeoff:** Higher yield ⇒ Higher risk ⇒ Compensates the investor. - **Time Value of Money** is the bedrock of every formula in this module. **Golden Rule:** Every valuation problem reduces to **discounting expected future cash flows back to the present** — the only variation is the nature of the cash flow stream (constant, growing, finite, perpetual).

topics.title: Introduction to Financial Risks and Returns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Financial Risks and Returns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Financial Return?

**Financial Return** is the quantifiable monetary gain or loss generated on an investment over a specific period of time. It is the *reward* that an investor earns for committing capital to a financial asset. Return is the sole motivation behind every investment decision in a market economy.

The formal, textbook-grade definition in the KTU 2024 Scheme context is:

> A return is the total gain or loss experienced on an investment over a given holding period, expressed as a percentage of the initial (or average) invested capital. It encompasses **all cash inflows** — dividends, coupons, capital appreciation (or depreciation), and any ancillary distributions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition Highlight:**
> Return $(R)$ is NOT synonymous with profit. It is a *rate*, not an absolute amount. The return on a ₹1,000 investment that becomes ₹1,150 in one year is **15%**, not ₹150.

### 1.2 What is Financial Risk?

**Financial Risk** refers to the uncertainty surrounding the actual realization of returns relative to the *expected* return. In more rigorous terminology, it is the dispersion (variability) of possible outcomes around the expected (mean) outcome of an investment.

> The probability that the realized return will deviate — favorably or unfavorably — from the originally anticipated return.

Mathematically, risk is captured by the **standard deviation** $(\sigma)$, **variance** $(\sigma^2)$, or in modern portfolio theory, by **beta** $(\beta)$ for systematic risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry of Risk:** Risk is two-sided. Outcomes can deviate *positively* (upside surprise) or *negatively* (downside surprise). However, in behavioral and corporate finance, the term "risk" almost always refers to the *downside deviation* — the possibility of losing capital. This asymmetric interpretation is what investors fear and seek to manage.

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Mountain Hike and the Lottery Ticket"

Imagine two friends — *Arjun* and *Kavya* — each with ₹10,000 to deploy for one year.

- **Arjun** puts his money in a **Government Treasury Bond** yielding a near-certain **6%**. He will almost certainly end up with ₹10,600. There is virtually no chance of more, and virtually no chance of less.
- **Kavya** puts her money into a **small-cap technology startup** whose return could plausibly be **+80%** (₹18,000) or **−60%** (₹4,000). The *average* expected return is around **20%**, but the *uncertainty* is enormous.

| Dimension | Arjun (Bond) | Kavya (Startup) |
|---|---|---|
| Expected Return | **6%** | **20%** |
| Actual Outcome | Predictable | Highly unpredictable |
| **Risk Level** | **Very Low** | **Very High** |
| Investor Profile | Conservative | Aggressive |

This contrast — *higher expected return is offered only when the investor is willing to bear higher risk* — is the central message of the **Risk-Return Tradeoff**, the foundational pillar of financial economics.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Students — "No Free Lunch":** In efficient financial markets, you are never paid extra return *without* accepting extra risk. If someone promises high returns with zero risk, that promise is either a fraud or a misunderstanding.

## 1.4 The Two Fundamental Propositions of Financial Economics

1. **Proposition 1 (The Risk-Return Tradeoff):** Higher expected returns are invariably accompanied by higher risk.
2. **Proposition 2 (Risk Aversion):** Most economic agents prefer a certain rupee to a probabilistic rupee of equal expected value. This is why *risk-free* assets (like T-bills) trade at a lower expected return than risky assets.

These two propositions jointly give rise to the **Risk Premium** — the extra return demanded by investors for bearing risk.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Financial Returns

Returns are classified along two primary axes:

### A. Based on the *Nature* of the Cash Flow

| Return Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Capital Gain (Loss) Yield** | Price appreciation (depreciation) of the asset | Buying ₹100 stock, selling at ₹120 |
| **Dividend / Coupon Yield** | Periodic cash distributions to the owner | ₹5 dividend on a ₹100 share |
| **Total Return** | Sum of capital gain yield and dividend yield | ₹5 dividend + ₹20 price gain = **25%** |

### B. Based on the *Measurement Window*

- **Holding Period Return (HPR):** The actual return earned over the entire duration for which the asset was held.
- **Expected Return $E(R)$:** The probability-weighted average of all possible future returns — a *forward-looking* estimate.
- **Required Return:** The minimum return an investor demands to justify holding a risky asset; it incorporates the **risk-free rate** plus the appropriate **risk premium**.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Financial Risks

Financial risk is not a monolith. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to distinguish at least the following categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Major Classes of Financial Risk (KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus)**

### 2.2.1 Market Risk (Systematic Risk)
- Risk arising from macroeconomic forces that affect *all* securities simultaneously.
- Examples: interest rate movements, exchange rate fluctuations, inflation shocks, recession.
- **Measured by Beta** $(\beta)$ in the CAPM framework.
- **Cannot be eliminated** through diversification.

### 2.2.2 Credit Risk (Default Risk)
- The risk that a borrower or bond issuer fails to make promised payments (interest or principal).
- Most relevant for **corporate bonds**, **bank loans**, and **counterparty transactions**.
- Quantified by **credit ratings** (AAA, AA, A, BBB, etc.) from agencies like CRISIL, ICRA, Moody's, and S&P.

### 2.2.3 Liquidity Risk
- The risk that an asset cannot be bought or sold quickly enough at a fair market price.
- A **liquid** asset trades frequently with minimal price impact (e.g., Reliance Industries shares on NSE).
- An **illiquid** asset (e.g., real estate, unlisted equity) may take months to sell and may require a price concession.

### 2.2.4 Operational Risk
- Risk of loss from failed internal processes, people, systems, or external events.
- Examples: fraud, IT system failure, human error, regulatory non-compliance.
- Of particular importance to **banks and financial institutions**.

### 2.2.5 Inflation Risk (Purchasing Power Risk)
- The risk that inflation erodes the *real* value of returns.
- A 7% nominal return in a 9% inflation environment delivers a **negative real return** of approximately **−1.85%** (computed using the Fisher equation).

### 2.2.6 Interest Rate Risk
- The risk that changes in market interest rates adversely affect asset prices.
- Bond prices and interest rates move in **opposite directions** — when RBI hikes the repo rate, existing bond prices fall.

## 2.3 The Risk-Return Tradeoff — Formal Statement

The **Risk-Return Tradeoff** is the empirical and theoretical principle stating that *potential return is directly proportional to the level of risk undertaken*.

$$
E(R) = R_f + \text{Risk Premium}
$$

Where:
- $E(R)$ = Expected return on the risky asset.
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate (e.g., 91-day T-bill yield).
- Risk Premium = Compensation for bearing risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The risk-free rate is the "rent" you pay to the investor for the use of their money with zero uncertainty. The risk premium is the "insurance premium" they demand as compensation for the possibility of loss. The total — the expected return — is the true *cost of capital* from the investor's perspective.

## 2.4 Risk Aversion — The Behavioral Foundation

A **risk-averse** investor prefers a certain payoff to a probabilistic payoff of identical expected value. The degree of risk aversion is captured by the utility function:

$$
U = E(R) - \tfrac{1}{2} A \sigma^2
$$

Where:
- $U$ = Utility (satisfaction) derived from the investment.
- $E(R)$ = Expected return.
- $A$ = Coefficient of risk aversion ($A > 0$ for a risk-averse investor).
- $\sigma^2$ = Variance of returns.

> A larger $A$ implies *greater* risk aversion — the investor penalizes variance more heavily.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Holding Period Return (HPR) | $\displaystyle \text{HPR} = \frac{P_1 - P_0 + D}{P_0}$ | $P_0$ = initial price, $P_1$ = ending price, $D$ = income received. Expressed as a decimal. |
| 2 | Expected Return | $\displaystyle E(R) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i R_i$ | $p_i$ = probability of state $i$; $R_i$ = return in state $i$. $\sum p_i = 1$. |
| 3 | Variance of Return | $\displaystyle \sigma^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \left[R_i - E(R)\right]^2$ | Units: $(\%)^2$. |
| 4 | Standard Deviation | $\displaystyle \sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Units: $\%$. The principal measure of *total risk*. |
| 5 | Covariance | $\displaystyle \text{Cov}(R_A, R_B) = \sum_i p_i \left[R_{A,i} - E(R_A)\right]\left[R_{B,i} - E(R_B)\right]$ | Measures co-movement between two assets. |
| 6 | Correlation Coefficient | $\displaystyle \rho_{AB} = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_A, R_B)}{\sigma_A \sigma_B}$ | Bounded: $-1 \le \rho \le +1$. |
| 7 | Portfolio Expected Return | $\displaystyle E(R_p) = w_A E(R_A) + w_B E(R_B)$ | $w_A + w_B = 1$. |
| 8 | Portfolio Variance (2 assets) | $\displaystyle \sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$ | Dominant term when $\rho < 1$ is the diversification benefit. |
| 9 | Risk Premium | $\displaystyle \text{RP} = E(R) - R_f$ | The *extra* return demanded for bearing risk. |
| 10 | Utility Function | $\displaystyle U = E(R) - \tfrac{1}{2} A \sigma^2$ | $A > 0$ characterizes risk aversion. |
| 11 | Real Return (Fisher) | $\displaystyle r \approx R - i$ | Approximation; exact form: $(1+r) = (1+R)/(1+i)$. |
| 12 | Sharpe Ratio | $\displaystyle S = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Reward-to-volatility ratio. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Markdown Trap:** In the formulas above, absolute value bars are written as `\vert` or through contextual language (e.g., "$\vert R \vert$") so as not to break markdown table rendering. Do not insert literal `\vert` characters in your KTU answer sheets — use $\vert \cdot \vert$ LaTeX notation.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business

For an engineering student, the relevance of risk-return analysis is profound:

- **Project Appraisal:** Engineers evaluate capital projects (e.g., setting up a new manufacturing plant). The **Net Present Value (NPV)** must be computed using a *risk-adjusted discount rate* — which is precisely $R_f + \text{Risk Premium}$.
- **R&D Investment:** Choosing between a low-risk incremental innovation and a high-risk disruptive innovation requires the risk-return framework.
- **Startups:** Founders must assess whether their target return compensates for the high failure probability.
- **Personal Finance:** From SIPs in mutual funds to home-loan decisions, every rupee is allocated against a backdrop of risk and return.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, step-by-step derivations of the foundational formulas. Every algebraic transition is explicitly justified.

## 3.1 Derivation of Expected Return $E(R)$

### Starting Premise
An asset can end up in one of $n$ possible future "states of the world" (e.g., boom, normal, recession). State $i$ occurs with probability $p_i$ and delivers return $R_i$.

### Step 1 — The Probability-Weighted Mean
By the **Kolmogorov Axioms of Probability**, $\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = 1$. The expected value of a discrete random variable is, by definition, the probability-weighted arithmetic mean:

$$
E(R) = p_1 R_1 + p_2 R_2 + \dots + p_n R_n
$$

### Step 2 — Compact Summation Notation

$$
E(R) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i R_i
$$

### Step 3 — Verification (Sanity Check)
The probabilities must sum to unity. Example: if $p_1 = 0.3$, $p_2 = 0.5$, $p_3 = 0.2$, then $0.3 + 0.5 + 0.2 = 1.00$ ✓.

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose stock ABC has the following return distribution:

| State | Probability $p_i$ | Return $R_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| Boom | 0.25 | +30% |
| Normal | 0.50 | +10% |
| Recession | 0.25 | −20% |

**Step (i):** Verify probabilities sum to one.
$0.25 + 0.50 + 0.25 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step (ii):** Compute each $p_i \times R_i$.
- Boom: $0.25 \times 30 = 7.5$
- Normal: $0.50 \times 10 = 5.0$
- Recession: $0.25 \times (-20) = -5.0$

**Step (iii):** Sum the products.

$$
E(R) = 7.5 + 5.0 + (-5.0) = 7.5\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The investor *anticipates* a 7.5% return on average across the three states. But this number alone hides the risk — the asset can deliver anywhere from **+30% to −20%**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Variance $\sigma^2$

### Step 1 — Concept of Deviation from Mean
For each state $i$, the deviation from the expected return is:

$$
d_i = R_i - E(R)
$$

### Step 2 — Square to Eliminate Sign
Squaring ensures positive and negative deviations do not cancel:

$$
d_i^2 = \left[R_i - E(R)\right]^2
$$

### Step 3 — Probability-Weight
Multiply each squared deviation by its probability and sum:

$$
\sigma^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \left[R_i - E(R)\right]^2
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (Continuing ABC Stock)

We already computed $E(R) = 7.5\%$. Now compute each squared deviation:

**Step (i) — Boom state:**
$(R_1 - E(R))^2 = (30 - 7.5)^2 = (22.5)^2 = 506.25$
Weighted: $0.25 \times 506.25 = 126.5625$

**Step (ii) — Normal state:**
$(R_2 - E(R))^2 = (10 - 7.5)^2 = (2.5)^2 = 6.25$
Weighted: $0.50 \times 6.25 = 3.125$

**Step (iii) — Recession state:**
$(R_3 - E(R))^2 = (-20 - 7.5)^2 = (-27.5)^2 = 756.25$
Weighted: $0.25 \times 756.25 = 189.0625$

**Step (iv) — Sum:**

$$
\sigma^2 = 126.5625 + 3.125 + 189.0625 = 318.75 \;(\%)^2
$$

**Step (v) — Standard Deviation:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{318.75} \approx 17.85\%
$$

**Interpretation:** ABC stock has an expected return of **7.5%** with a standard deviation of **~17.85%**. The standard deviation is more than *double* the expected return — this is a *high-risk, high-uncertainty* asset.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Risk-Return Tradeoff (CAPM Intuition)

The **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** formalizes the linear relationship between risk and required return:

$$
E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \left[E(R_m) - R_f\right]
$$

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Base compensation.**
Every investor deserves at least the risk-free rate $R_f$ for postponing consumption. This is the *time value of money*.

**Step 2 — Market-wide risk premium.**
The market portfolio (e.g., Nifty 50) is expected to earn $E(R_m)$. The difference $E(R_m) - R_f$ is the *average* risk premium for bearing the average level of systematic risk.

**Step 3 — Adjust for the asset's beta.**
Beta $(\beta_i)$ measures how sensitive asset $i$ is to market movements. A $\beta = 1$ implies the asset moves with the market; $\beta > 1$ implies higher sensitivity (aggressive); $\beta < 1$ implies lower sensitivity (defensive).

**Step 4 — Multiply.**
The asset-specific risk premium is $\beta_i \times \left[E(R_m) - R_f\right]$.

**Step 5 — Sum.**
Add the risk-free rate to obtain the required return.

### Worked Example
Suppose $R_f = 6\%$, $E(R_m) = 14\%$, and stock XYZ has $\beta = 1.5$.

**Step (i):** Compute market risk premium: $14 - 6 = 8\%$.

**Step (ii):** Apply beta: $1.5 \times 8 = 12\%$.

**Step (iii):** Add risk-free rate:

$$
E(R_{XYZ}) = 6 + 12 = 18\%
$$

> **Insight:** A stock with $\beta = 1.5$ must offer an expected return of **18%** to attract investors — exactly 1.5 times the market's risk premium.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Portfolio Variance (Two-Asset Case)

For a portfolio $P$ combining asset A (weight $w_A$) and asset B (weight $w_B = 1 - w_A$):

### Step 1 — Portfolio Return in Each State
$R_P = w_A R_A + w_B R_B$

### Step 2 — Deviation from Expected Portfolio Return
$R_P - E(R_P) = w_A [R_A - E(R_A)] + w_B [R_B - E(R_B)]$

### Step 3 — Square and Take Expectation

$$
\sigma_P^2 = E\left[\left\{w_A [R_A - E(R_A)] + w_B [R_B - E(R_B)]\right\}^2\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Expand the Binomial

$$
\sigma_P^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \text{Cov}(R_A, R_B)
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Covariance Identity
Since $\text{Cov}(R_A, R_B) = \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$:

$$
\sigma_P^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B
$$

### Critical Insight — The Diversification Effect
When $\rho_{AB} < 1$, the cross-term $2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$ is **smaller** than it would be at $\rho = 1$, *reducing* total portfolio variance. When $\rho_{AB} \to -1$, the portfolio can theoretically become **risk-free** through clever weighting — the foundation of **modern portfolio theory** by Harry Markowitz (1952).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Risk vs. Return for Common Assets

| Asset Class | Typical $R_f$-Adjusted Expected Return | Typical $\sigma$ (Risk) | Liquidity | Beta Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 91-Day Treasury Bill | 6 – 7% | ~0% | Very High | 0.00 |
| 10-Year Government Bond | 7 – 8% | 5 – 8% | High | 0.05 – 0.20 |
| Large-Cap Equity (Nifty 50) | 12 – 14% | 15 – 20% | Very High | 0.85 – 1.15 |
| Mid-Cap Equity | 14 – 16% | 20 – 28% | Moderate | 1.10 – 1.40 |
| Small-Cap Equity | 16 – 20% | 28 – 40% | Low | 1.30 – 1.80 |
| Real Estate | 9 – 12% | 10 – 18% | Very Low | 0.50 – 0.90 |
| Gold | 8 – 10% | 12 – 16% | High | 0.00 – 0.30 |
| Cryptocurrency (Bitcoin) | Highly variable | 60 – 100% | Moderate | 1.50 – 3.00 |

> [!TIP]
> **Observation for Students:** As you move down the table (toward higher returns), risk *and* volatility both rise — a textbook illustration of the Risk-Return Tradeoff in action.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Risk-Return Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Investor Capital Deployment] --> B{Identify Risk-Return Objective}
    B --> C1[Conservative Profile]
    B --> C2[Moderate Profile]
    B --> C3[Aggressive Profile]

    C1 --> D1[Low Risk Asset Pool]
    D1 --> E1[T-Bills and Bank FDDs]
    D1 --> E2[Government Bonds]
    D1 --> E3[Large Cap Dividend Stocks]

    C2 --> D2[Moderate Risk Asset Pool]
    D2 --> F1[Balanced Mutual Funds]
    D2 --> F2[Corporate Bonds]
    D2 --> F3[Hybrid Equity Debt Portfolio]

    C3 --> D3[High Risk Asset Pool]
    D3 --> G1[Small Cap and Mid Cap Stocks]
    D3 --> G2[Sectoral and Thematic Funds]
    D3 --> G3[Crypto and Startup Equity]

    E1 --> H[Expected Return 6 to 8 percent]
    E2 --> H
    E3 --> H
    F1 --> I[Expected Return 10 to 14 percent]
    F2 --> I
    F3 --> I
    G1 --> J[Expected Return 16 to 25 percent]
    G2 --> J
    G3 --> J

    H --> K[Risk Adjusted Return Ranking via Sharpe Ratio]
    I --> K
    J --> K

    K --> L[Final Portfolio Selection]
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Financial Risks

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Financial Risk] --> R1[Market Risk]
    R --> R2[Credit Risk]
    R --> R3[Liquidity Risk]
    R --> R4[Operational Risk]
    R --> R5[Inflation Risk]
    R --> R6[Interest Rate Risk]

    R1 --> R1a[Equity Market Volatility]
    R1 --> R1b[Currency Fluctuation]
    R1 --> R1c[Recession Impact]

    R2 --> R2a[Default by Issuer]
    R2 --> R2b[Credit Rating Downgrade]

    R3 --> R3a[Inability to Sell Quickly]
    R3 --> R3b[Wide Bid Ask Spread]

    R4 --> R4a[Internal Fraud]
    R4 --> R4b[System Failure]

    R5 --> R5a[Erosion of Real Return]

    R6 --> R6a[Bond Price Decline on Rate Hike]
```

## 4.3 The Risk-Return Tradeoff — Conceptual Graph

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Low Risk Low Return] -->|Increasing Risk| B[Medium Risk Medium Return]
    B -->|Increasing Risk| C[High Risk High Return]
    A -.->|Risk Free Rate| AA[Treasury Bills]
    B -.->|Balanced| BB[Equity Debt Mix]
    C -.->|Aggressive| CC[Small Cap and Crypto]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Movement from left to right along the horizontal axis represents *increasing risk*. The vertical axis represents *expected return*. The Risk-Return Tradeoff is visualized as a *positive-sloping frontier* — you must climb the risk axis to ascend the return axis.

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Investor Action Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Investor Has Surplus Capital] --> S2[Step 1: Define Risk Tolerance]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2: Identify Time Horizon]
    S3 --> S4[Step 3: Estimate Expected Returns for Candidate Assets]
    S4 --> S5[Step 4: Quantify Risk via Standard Deviation or Beta]
    S5 --> S6[Step 5: Compute Risk Adjusted Metric Sharpe Ratio]
    S6 --> S7{Sharpe Ratio Acceptable}
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Step 6: Allocate Capital and Construct Portfolio]
    S7 -->|No| S9[Reconsider Asset Weights or Universe]
    S9 --> S4
    S8 --> S10[Step 7: Monitor and Rebalance Periodically]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define financial risk. Explain the different types of financial risks faced by an investor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Definition (1 mark):* Financial risk is the uncertainty that the actual return on an investment will deviate from its expected return, often measured by the standard deviation or variance of returns.

*Types (2 marks):* The major categories are:
1. **Market Risk** — Risk arising from macroeconomic forces affecting all securities (interest rates, inflation, recession).
2. **Credit Risk** — Risk that a borrower defaults on debt obligations.
3. **Liquidity Risk** — Risk of being unable to sell an asset quickly at a fair price.
4. **Operational Risk** — Risk of loss from failed internal processes, fraud, or system failures.
5. **Inflation Risk** — Risk that inflation erodes the real purchasing power of returns.
6. **Interest Rate Risk** — Risk from adverse movements in market interest rates, particularly affecting bond prices.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is meant by the Risk-Return Tradeoff? Why is it considered the foundational principle of financial economics?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Meaning (1.5 marks):* The Risk-Return Tradeoff is the principle that *higher expected returns can only be obtained by accepting higher risk*. Investors must be compensated with greater expected return for bearing greater uncertainty.

*Why Foundational (1.5 marks):*
1. It governs **all asset pricing** — from the CAPM to option valuation.
2. It explains why **risk-free assets** (T-bills) yield less than **risky assets** (equity).
3. It underpins **portfolio construction** — diversification exploits the tradeoff by reducing risk for a given return.
4. It is the rationale behind the **Risk Premium** — the economic reward for bearing uncertainty.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and must offer **internal choice** between two alternatives. Each alternative typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A stock has the following probability distribution of returns:

| State | Probability | Return |
|---|---|---|
| Boom | 0.20 | +40% |
| Growth | 0.50 | +15% |
| Stagnation | 0.20 | +5% |
| Recession | 0.10 | −25% |

Calculate the **expected return** and the **standard deviation** of the stock. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the concept of the **Risk-Return Tradeoff**. Discuss how a risk-averse investor chooses between a risk-free asset yielding 6% and a risky portfolio with an expected return of 14% and a standard deviation of 18%. Use the Sharpe Ratio framework. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a)

**Step 1 — Verify probabilities.**
$0.20 + 0.50 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 2 — Compute $p_i \times R_i$ for each state.**
- Boom: $0.20 \times 40 = 8.0$
- Growth: $0.50 \times 15 = 7.5$
- Stagnation: $0.20 \times 5 = 1.0$
- Recession: $0.10 \times (-25) = -2.5$

**Step 3 — Expected Return.**

$$
E(R) = 8.0 + 7.5 + 1.0 + (-2.5) = 14.0\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute squared deviations $(R_i - E(R))^2$.**
- Boom: $(40 - 14)^2 = 26^2 = 676$
- Growth: $(15 - 14)^2 = 1^2 = 1$
- Stagnation: $(5 - 14)^2 = (-9)^2 = 81$
- Recession: $(-25 - 14)^2 = (-39)^2 = 1521$

**Step 5 — Probability-weight each squared deviation.**
- Boom: $0.20 \times 676 = 135.20$
- Growth: $0.50 \times 1 = 0.50$
- Stagnation: $0.20 \times 81 = 16.20$
- Recession: $0.10 \times 1521 = 152.10$

**Step 6 — Variance.**

$$
\sigma^2 = 135.20 + 0.50 + 16.20 + 152.10 = 304.00 \;(\%)^2
$$

**Step 7 — Standard Deviation.**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{304.00} \approx 17.44\%
$$

**Final Answer:** $E(R) = 14.0\%$ and $\sigma \approx 17.44\%$. The stock is moderately risky with a positive expected return.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Definition of Risk-Return Tradeoff.**
The Risk-Return Tradeoff is the principle stating that potential return is directly proportional to the level of risk undertaken. No investment can offer high expected return without also offering high variability of outcomes.

**Step 2 — The Risk-Averse Investor's Dilemma.**
A risk-averse investor must compare the *guaranteed* 6% from the risk-free asset with the *expected* 14% from the risky portfolio. The decision is not obvious because the risky portfolio might deliver less than 6% (or even negative returns) due to its 18% volatility.

**Step 3 — Sharpe Ratio Calculation.**

$$
S = \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p} = \frac{14 - 6}{18} = \frac{8}{18} \approx 0.444
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
A Sharpe Ratio of **0.444** means the risky portfolio offers **0.444 units of excess return per unit of total risk**. A positive Sharpe Ratio indicates that the excess return adequately compensates for the volatility.

**Step 5 — Investor Decision Rule.**
- If the investor's *personal* required Sharpe Ratio (reflecting risk aversion) is less than **0.444**, the risky portfolio is attractive.
- If their required Sharpe Ratio exceeds **0.444**, the investor will prefer the risk-free asset.

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**
The Risk-Return Tradeoff, quantified through the Sharpe Ratio, enables the risk-averse investor to make a mathematically defensible, utility-maximizing choice between certainty and chance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**. Derive the expected return on a stock with $\beta = 1.3$, given $R_f = 6\%$ and $E(R_m) = 13\%$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Define **portfolio risk**. Two stocks A and B have the following characteristics: $E(R_A) = 18\%$, $\sigma_A = 22\%$, $E(R_B) = 12\%$, $\sigma_B = 15\%$, and correlation coefficient $\rho_{AB} = 0.40$. If an investor constructs an equal-weighted portfolio ($w_A = w_B = 0.5$), calculate the portfolio's expected return and standard deviation. Comment on the diversification benefit. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Step 1 — CAPM Definition.**
The Capital Asset Pricing Model, developed by William Sharpe (1964), John Lintner, and Jan Mossin, describes the relationship between systematic risk and the expected return of an asset. It states that the expected return on any risky asset equals the risk-free rate plus a risk premium proportional to the asset's beta.

**Step 2 — The CAPM Equation.**

$$
E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \left[E(R_m) - R_f\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Given Values.**
$R_f = 6\%$, $E(R_m) = 13\%$, $\beta_i = 1.3$.

**Step 4 — Compute the Market Risk Premium.**

$$
E(R_m) - R_f = 13 - 6 = 7\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Asset-Specific Risk Premium.**

$$
\beta_i \times (E(R_m) - R_f) = 1.3 \times 7 = 9.1\%
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Expected Return.**

$$
E(R_i) = 6 + 9.1 = 15.1\%
$$

**Final Answer:** The expected return on the stock is **15.1%**, which exceeds the market return of 13% because the stock is *more volatile* than the market ($\beta = 1.3 > 1$).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Step 1 — Definition.**
Portfolio risk is the combined uncertainty (variance or standard deviation) of the returns of all assets held within the portfolio, taking into account both individual volatilities and the pairwise correlations between assets.

**Step 2 — Compute Portfolio Expected Return.**

$$
E(R_p) = w_A E(R_A) + w_B E(R_B)
$$

$$
E(R_p) = 0.5 \times 18 + 0.5 \times 12 = 9 + 6 = 15\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Portfolio Variance Using the Two-Asset Formula.**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values.**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = (0.5)^2 (22)^2 + (0.5)^2 (15)^2 + 2(0.5)(0.5)(0.40)(22)(15)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Each Term.**

- First term: $0.25 \times 484 = 121.00$
- Second term: $0.25 \times 225 = 56.25$
- Third term: $2 \times 0.25 \times 0.40 \times 330 = 0.20 \times 330 = 66.00$

**Step 6 — Sum the Terms.**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = 121.00 + 56.25 + 66.00 = 243.25 \;(\%)^2
$$

**Step 7 — Portfolio Standard Deviation.**

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{243.25} \approx 15.60\%
$$

**Step 8 — Comparison with Weighted-Average Volatility.**
The weighted-average individual standard deviation is $0.5 \times 22 + 0.5 \times 15 = 18.5\%$. The portfolio standard deviation of 15.60% is **lower** than this weighted average because the correlation ($\rho = 0.40$) is significantly less than 1.

**Step 9 — Diversification Benefit.**
Since $\rho_{AB} = 0.40 < 1$, the two stocks do not move perfectly in tandem. Combining them *reduces* total risk relative to the average of their individual risks. This is the **diversification benefit** — the cornerstone of Markowitz's Modern Portfolio Theory. Had the correlation been +1, no diversification would have occurred and $\sigma_p$ would have equaled 18.5%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square the deviations** when computing variance — a single omission costs **3 marks** on a 7-mark sub-part.
> 2. **Mixing up variance and standard deviation** in the final answer — students often report $\sigma^2$ instead of $\sigma$ or vice versa.
> 3. **Neglecting the correlation term** in the portfolio variance formula — examiners specifically test whether students understand that *uncorrelated* assets reduce risk.
> 4. **Sign errors in squared deviations** — particularly when returns are negative. Always square the *deviation from the mean*, not the return itself.
> 5. **Sharpe Ratio interpretation** — students sometimes quote a Sharpe Ratio without explaining whether it is *attractive* or *unattractive* relative to a benchmark. Always contextualize the numerical answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Return** is a *rate* (percentage), not an absolute amount of money.
- **Risk** is the *variability* of returns around the expected return, quantified by $\sigma$ or $\sigma^2$.
- The **Risk-Return Tradeoff** is universal: no high return without high risk in efficient markets.
- **Risk Aversion** is the psychological preference for certainty over a fair gamble.
- **Holding Period Return** $= (P_1 - P_0 + D) / P_0$.
- **Expected Return** $E(R) = \sum p_i R_i$.
- **Variance** $\sigma^2 = \sum p_i [R_i - E(R)]^2$.
- **Standard Deviation** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ is the canonical measure of *total risk*.
- **CAPM** $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$ — the most tested formula in KTU exams.
- **Risk Premium** $= E(R) - R_f$ — the *extra* return for bearing risk.
- **Sharpe Ratio** $= (E(R_p) - R_f) / \sigma_p$ — the *reward per unit of total risk*.
- **Diversification Benefit** arises when $\rho_{AB} < 1$; the lower the correlation, the greater the risk reduction.
- The **Six Major Risks**: Market, Credit, Liquidity, Operational, Inflation, Interest Rate.
- **Real Return** $\approx$ **Nominal Return** − **Inflation Rate** (Fisher approximation).
- Always verify that $\sum p_i = 1$ before computing $E(R)$.
- Always square the deviation $R_i - E(R)$, **not** the return $R_i$ itself.
- The portfolio variance formula has **three** terms — never omit the covariance/correlation cross-term.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra for KTU 2024:** "*Mean first, then deviations squared, weighted by probability, summed, then square-rooted — that is your standard deviation. And remember: $\rho$ is your friend; the smaller it is, the safer your portfolio.*"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Risks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Risks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Financial Risk**
> In the context of **Financial Economics**, **Risk** is formally defined as the quantifiable *uncertainty* concerning the deviation of actual financial outcomes (returns, cash flows, asset values) from the expected (mean) outcome. Mathematically, risk represents the **variability** or **volatility** of returns around their expected value, typically measured using **standard deviation ($\sigma$)**, **variance ($\sigma^2$)**, **beta ($\beta$)**, or **Value at Risk (VaR)**.

The **Types of Risk** taxonomy in KTU Module 3 classifies all financial exposures into two master categories:

| Master Category | Nature | Diversifiable? |
|---|---|---|
| **Systematic Risk** | Market-wide, macro-economic | ❌ **No** — Cannot be eliminated through diversification |
| **Unsystematic Risk** | Firm-specific, micro-economic | ✅ **Yes** — Eliminated via portfolio diversification |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Boat in the Storm"**
> Imagine you are sailing a small boat on a vast ocean. Two dangers exist:
> 1. **The Storm (Systematic Risk)** — A massive cyclone affects the *entire ocean* — every boat rises and falls. You cannot hide from the storm no matter how good your boat is. This represents market-wide events like recessions, interest rate hikes, or wars.
> 2. **The Leaking Hull (Unsystematic Risk)** — Your *specific* boat has a crack, or your engine is faulty. A fleet of 100 boats, each with different cracks, will statistically cancel out individual leaks. This is **diversification** — combining many imperfect assets so that their idiosyncratic problems wash away.
>
> A wise sailor (investor) cannot stop the storm, but **can patch the hull** (diversify) and **read the weather forecast** (hedge using derivatives).

---

### 1.3 Core Risk Categories — The Master Taxonomy

```
FINANCIAL RISK
├── A. SYSTEMATIC (Market / Non-Diversifiable)
│   ├── A1. Market Risk (Equity Risk)
│   ├── A2. Interest Rate Risk
│   ├── A3. Inflation / Purchasing Power Risk
│   ├── A4. Currency / Exchange Rate Risk
│   ├── A5. Political / Regulatory Risk
│   └── A6. Country / Sovereign Risk
│
└── B. UNSYSTEMATIC (Firm-Specific / Diversifiable)
    ├── B1. Business / Operational Risk
    ├── B2. Financial Risk (Leverage)
    ├── B3. Credit / Default Risk
    ├── B4. Liquidity Risk
    ├── B5. Management Risk
    └── B6. Event Risk (M&A, Litigation, Strikes)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Corner:** When asked *"Classify the types of risk"*, students must explicitly use the binary classification **Systematic vs. Unsystematic** as the primary axis, and then list sub-types. Avoid mixing both taxonomies (e.g., writing "Market Risk and Liquidity Risk" as parallel categories — this is structurally incorrect under the KTU 2024 scheme).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Systematic Risk — The "Unavoidable" Exposure

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- **Why it exists:** Driven by *macro-economic forces* — GDP, monetary policy, geopolitical events, global liquidity cycles. These forces affect *all securities simultaneously* in the same direction.
- **How it manifests:** A common **market factor** (e.g., NIFTY 50 index, S&P 500) co-moves with almost every stock. This co-movement is captured by the **Beta ($\beta$)** coefficient.
- **Measurement:** Quantified via the **Single-Index Model** of Sharpe:
  $$R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_m + \varepsilon_i$$
  where $R_i$ is the return on asset $i$, $R_m$ is the market return, and $\varepsilon_i$ is the unsystematic (firm-specific) component.
- **Real-world impact:** During the 2008 Global Financial Crisis and 2020 COVID crash, **systematic risk dominated** — virtually every equity fell, regardless of fundamentals. Diversification provided *zero* shelter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sub-Type Breakdown — Systematic Risk**
> - **A1. Market Risk (Equity Risk):** Loss from adverse movements in stock prices. $\beta > 1$ = aggressive; $\beta < 1$ = defensive.
> - **A2. Interest Rate Risk:** Bond prices and equity valuations fall when RBI/central bank rates rise. Critical for **banks, NBFCs, real estate**.
> - **A3. Inflation / Purchasing Power Risk:** Real return $\approx$ Nominal return $-$ Inflation. Erodes the *future value* of money.
> - **A4. Currency Risk:** Applies to foreign investments. If the rupee depreciates against the dollar, dollar-denominated assets *gain* in rupee terms even if their dollar value is flat.
> - **A5. Political Risk:** Policy shifts (tax changes, expropriation, trade sanctions) — especially relevant in emerging markets.
> - **A6. Country/Sovereign Risk:** Risk that a sovereign government defaults on debt or imposes capital controls (e.g., 1991 India BoP crisis, 1998 Russian default).

---

### 2.2 Unsystematic Risk — The "Diversifiable" Exposure

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- **Why it exists:** Stemming from *firm-level idiosyncratic events* — a product recall, a CEO resignation, a strike, a lawsuit.
- **How it manifests:** Affects **one firm** (or a small group), not the whole market. Returns are *uncorrelated* with the market index.
- **Key Theorem (Markowitz, 1952):** As the number of stocks in a portfolio $N \to \infty$, the **unsystematic variance approaches zero** at a rate of $\frac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2}{N}$. This is the mathematical foundation of diversification.
- **Real-world utility:** Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT) tells us that an investor holding 25–30 well-chosen stocks across sectors can **eliminate ~90% of unsystematic risk**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sub-Type Breakdown — Unsystematic Risk**
> - **B1. Business / Operational Risk:** Inability of the firm to maintain stable revenues due to competition, technology disruption, supply-chain failure.
> - **B2. Financial Risk (Leverage Risk):** Risk arising from the use of debt in the capital structure. Higher D/E ratio ⇒ higher fixed interest burden ⇒ higher probability of insolvency.
> - **B3. Credit / Default Risk:** Counterparty fails to meet contractual obligations (e.g., a bond issuer defaults on coupon). Measured by **Credit Ratings (AAA, AA, A, BBB, BB, B, CCC, D)**.
> - **B4. Liquidity Risk:** Inability to buy/sell an asset quickly at a fair price. Two flavors: **asset liquidity** (can you sell?) and **funding liquidity** (can you pay?).
> - **B5. Management Risk:** Poor strategic decisions, corporate governance failures, fraud.
> - **B6. Event Risk:** Sudden, low-probability, high-impact events — mergers, hostile takeovers, regulatory raids, natural disasters.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory Memorization)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates *all* quantitative tools required by KTU Module 3 for measuring and decomposing risk. **No pipe characters `|` are used inside the table** to preserve markdown integrity — absolute values use $\vert$ notation.

| # | Concept | Formula | Interpretation / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Expected Return** | $E(R) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot R_i$ | Probability-weighted mean return (in %) |
| 2 | **Variance** | $\sigma^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i [R_i - E(R)]^2$ | Average squared deviation (in %²) |
| 3 | **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Total risk of a single asset (in %) |
| 4 | **Covariance** | $Cov(R_i, R_j) = \sum P_k [R_{i,k} - E(R_i)][R_{j,k} - E(R_j)]$ | Co-movement between two assets |
| 5 | **Correlation** | $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{Cov(R_i, R_j)}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}$ | Range: $-1 \le \rho \le +1$, dimensionless |
| 6 | **Beta (CAPM)** | $\beta_i = \dfrac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)}$ | Systematic risk per unit of market risk |
| 7 | **CAPM Equation** | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$ | Required return for asset $i$ |
| 8 | **Sharpe Ratio** | $S = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Risk-adjusted return (per unit of total risk) |
| 9 | **Treynor Ratio** | $T = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\beta_p}$ | Risk-adjusted return (per unit of systematic risk) |
| 10 | **Coefficient of Variation** | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{E(R)}$ | Risk per unit of return (dimensionless) |
| 11 | **Portfolio Variance (2-asset)** | $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ | Total risk of a 2-stock portfolio |
| 12 | **Diversification Effect** | $\sigma_{systematic}^2 = \beta^2 \sigma_m^2$ | Irreducible component of portfolio risk |
| 13 | **Value at Risk (VaR)** | $VaR_{\alpha} = Z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma \cdot V$ | Maximum loss at confidence level $\alpha$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Parallel:** Risk decomposition in finance mirrors **reliability engineering** — *Systematic Risk* is the inherent entropy of the system (unavoidable noise), while *Unsystematic Risk* is component-level variance (reducible via redundancy). Just as a parallel-circuit of 30 resistors has lower variance than any single resistor, a portfolio of 30 stocks has lower unsystematic variance than any single stock.

---

### 2.4 Why "Risk" is Not Synonymous with "Return" — The Risk-Return Tradeoff

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Law of Finance (KTU 2024):**
> $$\text{Expected Return} \;\propto\; \text{Risk (Variance)}$$
> No asset offers higher expected return *without* higher risk in a competitive market. The **Capital Market Line (CML)** and **Security Market Line (SML)** are geometric representations of this tradeoff.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Return, Variance & Standard Deviation (Single Asset)

**Given:** A stock has the following probability distribution of annual returns:

| Scenario | Probability ($P_i$) | Return ($R_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| Boom | **0.25** | **+30%** |
| Normal | **0.50** | **+12%** |
| Recession | **0.25** | **$-8$%** |

**Step 1 — Compute the Expected Return $E(R)$:**

$$E(R) = \sum_{i=1}^{3} P_i \cdot R_i$$

$$E(R) = (0.25)(30) + (0.50)(12) + (0.25)(-8)$$

$$E(R) = 7.5 + 6.0 + (-2.0) = 11.5\%$$

> *The expected return is the probability-weighted average of all possible outcomes.*

**Step 2 — Compute the Deviations $[R_i - E(R)]$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Boom:}     & \quad 30 - 11.5 = +18.5\% \\
\text{Normal:}   & \quad 12 - 11.5 = +0.5\% \\
\text{Recession:} & \quad -8 - 11.5 = -19.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Square the Deviations and Weight by Probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Boom:}      & \quad (0.25)(18.5)^2 = (0.25)(342.25) = 85.5625 \\
\text{Normal:}    & \quad (0.50)(0.5)^2  = (0.50)(0.25)   = 0.1250 \\
\text{Recession:} & \quad (0.25)(-19.5)^2 = (0.25)(380.25) = 95.0625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum to obtain Variance $\sigma^2$:**

$$\sigma^2 = 85.5625 + 0.1250 + 95.0625 = 180.75 \; (\%^2)$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root to obtain Standard Deviation $\sigma$:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{180.75} \approx 13.44\%$$

> **Final Result:** This stock has an expected return of **11.5%** with a risk (total volatility) of **13.44%**. The high $\sigma$ relative to $E(R)$ indicates a volatile, risky investment.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Systematic vs. Unsystematic Risk Decomposition

**Given:** For a stock, we have observed:
- Covariance with market: $Cov(R_i, R_m) = 0.018$
- Market variance: $\sigma_m^2 = 0.0225$
- Total variance of the stock: $\sigma_i^2 = 0.0400$

**Step 1 — Compute Beta ($\beta_i$):**

$$\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{\sigma_m^2} = \frac{0.018}{0.0225} = 0.80$$

> *$\beta = 0.80$ means the stock is 20% less volatile than the market — a defensive stock.*

**Step 2 — Compute Systematic Risk $\sigma_{systematic}^2$:**

$$\sigma_{systematic}^2 = \beta_i^2 \cdot \sigma_m^2 = (0.80)^2 \cdot 0.0225 = 0.64 \cdot 0.0225 = 0.0144$$

**Step 3 — Compute Unsystematic Risk $\sigma_{unsystematic}^2$ (residual):**

$$\sigma_{unsystematic}^2 = \sigma_i^2 - \sigma_{systematic}^2 = 0.0400 - 0.0144 = 0.0256$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Percentage Decomposition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Systematic \%}    & = \frac{0.0144}{0.0400} \times 100 = 36.0\% \\
\text{Unsystematic \%} & = \frac{0.0256}{0.0400} \times 100 = 64.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Insight:** 36% of this stock's total risk is *market-driven* and **cannot be diversified away**, while 64% is *firm-specific* and can theoretically be eliminated by adding ~15–20 more such stocks (with low correlation) to a portfolio.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Portfolio Risk Reduction via Diversification (2-Asset Case)

**Given:** Two stocks, A and B, with the following characteristics:

| Parameter | Stock A | Stock B |
|---|---|---|
| Expected Return $E(R)$ | **20%** | **12%** |
| Standard Deviation $\sigma$ | **30%** | **15%** |
| Correlation $\rho_{AB}$ | — | **+0.20** |

Portfolio weights: $w_A = 0.50$, $w_B = 0.50$.

**Step 1 — Portfolio Expected Return:**

$$E(R_p) = w_A \cdot E(R_A) + w_B \cdot E(R_B) = (0.50)(20) + (0.50)(12) = 16\%$$

**Step 2 — Portfolio Variance (General 2-Asset Formula):**

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 & = (0.50)^2 (30)^2 + (0.50)^2 (15)^2 + 2(0.50)(0.50)(0.20)(30)(15) \\
           & = (0.25)(900) + (0.25)(225) + 2(0.25)(0.20)(450) \\
           & = 225 + 56.25 + 45 \\
           & = 326.25 \; (\%^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Portfolio Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{326.25} \approx 18.06\%$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Weighted-Average Risk (No Diversification Benefit):**

$$\sigma_{weighted} = w_A \sigma_A + w_B \sigma_B = (0.50)(30) + (0.50)(15) = 22.5\%$$

> **Final Result:** The portfolio's actual risk (**18.06%**) is **lower** than the weighted-average of individual risks (**22.5%**). The **diversification benefit = 4.44%**. This is the quantitative proof that *combining assets with $\rho < 1$ reduces total risk*.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Risk Type × Mitigation Tool

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Mapping Table (Exam-Ready):**

| Risk Type | Category | Measurable By | Mitigation Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Market Risk** | Systematic | $\beta$, $\sigma_m$ | Index futures, beta hedging |
| **Interest Rate Risk** | Systematic | Duration, Convexity | Interest rate swaps, IR futures |
| **Inflation Risk** | Systematic | Real vs. nominal returns | TIPS, gold, real estate |
| **Currency Risk** | Systematic | $\sigma_{FX}$, $\beta_{FX}$ | Currency forwards, options |
| **Political Risk** | Systematic | Sovereign ratings | Country diversification |
| **Business Risk** | Unsystematic | $\sigma_{\varepsilon}$ | Diversification (Markowitz) |
| **Financial Risk** | Unsystematic | D/E ratio, Interest coverage | Optimal capital structure |
| **Credit Risk** | Unsystematic | PD, LGD, Credit VaR | CDS, collateral, credit analysis |
| **Liquidity Risk** | Unsystematic | Bid-ask spread, volume | Liquid securities, cash buffer |
| **Management Risk** | Unsystematic | Governance scores | Active ownership, voting |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

---

**Q1. [3 Marks] — Distinguish between Systematic Risk and Unsystematic Risk with suitable examples.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Parameter | Systematic Risk | Unsystematic Risk |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Origin** | Macro-economic / market-wide | Firm-specific / micro-economic |
> | **Diversifiable?** | No (unavoidable) | Yes (through diversification) |
> | **Affects** | Entire market simultaneously | Single firm or industry |
> | **Examples** | Recession, RBI rate hike, war | CEO resignation, product recall, lawsuit |
> | **Measure** | Beta ($\beta$), $\sigma_m$ | Residual variance ($\sigma_\varepsilon^2$) |
> | **Mitigation** | Hedging (futures, options) | Portfolio diversification |
>
> *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for example pair)*

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] — What is Beta ($\beta$) in the context of risk? Explain the interpretation of $\beta = 1.2$.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Beta ($\beta$)** is the statistical measure of *systematic risk* of a security relative to the overall market. It is defined as:
> $$\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)}$$
>
> **Interpretation of $\beta = 1.2$:**
> The stock is **20% more volatile** than the market. If the market moves up by 10%, this stock is *expected* to move up by **12%**; if the market falls 10%, this stock is expected to fall **12%**. Such a stock is termed **aggressive** or **high-beta** and is suitable for bullish (rising) market conditions.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 mark formula + 2 marks interpretation with numerical example)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

## 🔷 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** [7 Marks] — *"A stock has the following returns over 4 years: +25%, −10%, +15%, +10%. Assuming equal probabilities, compute the expected return, variance, and standard deviation. Comment on the risk profile."*

**(b)** [7 Marks] — *"Explain the concept of Value at Risk (VaR). A portfolio has an initial value of ₹50,00,000 with daily volatility ($\sigma_{daily}$) of 2%. Using a 95% confidence level ($Z_{0.05} = 1.645$), calculate the 1-day VaR."*

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1: Compute Expected Return (equal probabilities, $P_i = 0.25$):**

$$E(R) = \frac{1}{4}(25 + (-10) + 15 + 10) = \frac{40}{4} = 10\%$$

**Step 2: Compute Deviations from Mean:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 & = 25 - 10 = +15\% \\
d_2 & = -10 - 10 = -20\% \\
d_3 & = 15 - 10 = +5\% \\
d_4 & = 10 - 10 = 0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Squared Deviations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1^2 & = 225 \\
d_2^2 & = 400 \\
d_3^2 & = 25 \\
d_4^2 & = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Variance $\sigma^2$ (Average of squared deviations):**

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{225 + 400 + 25 + 0}{4} = \frac{650}{4} = 162.5 \; (\%^2)$$

**Step 5: Standard Deviation $\sigma$:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{162.5} \approx 12.75\%$$

**Comment (Required for full 7 marks):** The stock offers a **moderate expected return of 10%** but carries a **high standard deviation of 12.75%** (i.e., $\sigma > E(R)$, coefficient of variation $CV = 1.275$). This indicates a **risky investment** with substantial volatility, suitable only for risk-tolerant investors. The wide swing between +25% and −10% returns reflects exposure to both systematic and unsystematic factors.

#### Part (b) — Solution

**Concept of VaR:**
> Value at Risk (VaR) is a statistical measure that quantifies the *maximum expected loss* of a portfolio over a specified time horizon at a given confidence level. It answers: *"What is the worst loss we can expect with X% confidence?"*

**VaR Formula:**

$$VaR_\alpha = Z_\alpha \cdot \sigma \cdot V$$

where $Z_\alpha$ is the Z-score at confidence level $\alpha$, $\sigma$ is volatility, and $V$ is portfolio value.

**Given Data:**
- Portfolio value: $V = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000$
- Daily volatility: $\sigma_{daily} = 2\% = 0.02$
- Confidence level: $95\%$, hence $Z_{0.05} = 1.645$

**Step-by-Step Calculation:**

$$VaR_{95\%} = 1.645 \times 0.02 \times 50{,}00{,}000$$

$$VaR_{95\%} = 1.645 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}64{,}500$$

**Interpretation:** With **95% confidence**, the maximum expected loss on this ₹50 lakh portfolio over **1 day** will **not exceed ₹1,64,500**. There remains a 5% probability of losses exceeding this amount (tail risk).

---

## 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** [7 Marks] — *"Explain the various types of Systematic Risk in detail. Why is Systematic Risk also called 'Non-Diversifiable Risk'?"*

**(b)** [7 Marks] — *"An investor holds a 2-stock portfolio: Stock X with weight 0.60, $E(R_X) = 18\%$, $\sigma_X = 25\%$; Stock Y with weight 0.40, $E(R_Y) = 10\%$, $\sigma_Y = 15\%$. The correlation coefficient $\rho_{XY} = 0.30$. Compute the portfolio's expected return and standard deviation. What is the diversification benefit?"*

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Theory Question

**Systematic Risk** is the portion of total risk caused by macro-economic factors that affect the entire market. It is called **"Non-Diversifiable"** because no matter how many stocks an investor holds, this risk *cannot be eliminated* — it is a property of the *market itself*, not of any individual security.

**Types of Systematic Risk:**

1. **Market Risk (Equity Risk):** Risk of loss due to adverse movements in the overall stock market index (e.g., NIFTY 50 crashing). Measured by **beta ($\beta$)**.

2. **Interest Rate Risk:** Risk that changes in central bank policy rates will reduce the value of fixed-income securities and equity valuations. Critical for banks and bond-heavy portfolios. Measured by **duration and convexity**.

3. **Inflation / Purchasing Power Risk:** Erosion of real returns when inflation exceeds the nominal yield. A bond yielding 6% with 8% inflation gives a *negative real return*.

4. **Currency / Exchange Rate Risk:** Risk from fluctuations in foreign exchange rates affecting international investments and export-import businesses.

5. **Political / Regulatory Risk:** Risk from changes in government policy — taxation, trade tariffs, nationalization, regulatory crackdowns. The 2016 Indian demonetization is a textbook example.

6. **Country / Sovereign Risk:** Risk that a nation will default on its sovereign debt or impose capital controls (e.g., 1998 Russian crisis, 2001 Argentine default).

**Why Non-Diversifiable?** When the *entire market* falls (e.g., 2008 GFC), all stocks fall *together* — their correlation approaches +1. Markowitz diversification works only when assets are imperfectly correlated ($\rho < 1$). When $\rho \to 1$, no combination reduces risk.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $w_X = 0.60$, $E(R_X) = 18\%$, $\sigma_X = 25\%$
- $w_Y = 0.40$, $E(R_Y) = 10\%$, $\sigma_Y = 15\%$
- $\rho_{XY} = 0.30$

**Step 1: Portfolio Expected Return:**

$$E(R_p) = w_X E(R_X) + w_Y E(R_Y) = (0.60)(18) + (0.40)(10) = 10.8 + 4.0 = 14.8\%$$

**Step 2: Portfolio Variance:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_X^2 \sigma_X^2 + w_Y^2 \sigma_Y^2 + 2 w_X w_Y \rho_{XY} \sigma_X \sigma_Y$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 & = (0.60)^2 (25)^2 + (0.40)^2 (15)^2 + 2(0.60)(0.40)(0.30)(25)(15) \\
           & = (0.36)(625) + (0.16)(225) + 2(0.24)(0.30)(375) \\
           & = 225.00 + 36.00 + 54.00 \\
           & = 315.00 \; (\%^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Portfolio Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{315.00} \approx 17.75\%$$

**Step 4: Weighted-Average Risk (No Diversification):**

$$\sigma_{weighted} = w_X \sigma_X + w_Y \sigma_Y = (0.60)(25) + (0.40)(15) = 15.0 + 6.0 = 21.0\%$$

**Step 5: Diversification Benefit:**

$$\text{Benefit} = 21.0\% - 17.75\% = 3.25\%$$

**Conclusion:** The combined portfolio has a **lower risk (17.75%)** than the simple weighted average (**21.0%**), yielding a **diversification benefit of 3.25%**. The expected return of 14.8% is preserved while risk is reduced — the core principle of Markowitz portfolio theory.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 2w₁w₂ covariance term** in 2-asset portfolio variance — a single missing factor costs **3 full marks**.
> 2. **Confusing "Standard Deviation" with "Variance"** in the final answer — verify the question asks for $\sigma$ or $\sigma^2$.
> 3. **Using sample variance ($n-1$) instead of population variance ($n$)** when probabilities are given — *if probabilities are explicit, divide by $n$, not $n-1$*.
> 4. **Mixing up Beta and Standard Deviation** in conceptual answers — $\beta$ is *systematic only*; $\sigma$ is *total risk*.
> 5. **Failing to state units (% vs. %²)** in numerical problems — KTU evaluators deduct **0.5 mark** per missing unit.
> 6. **Not commenting on risk profile** in variance questions — KTU requires qualitative interpretation alongside numerical answers (mandatory for 14-mark questions).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 High-Frequency KTU Module 3 — Risk Types — Rapid Revision**

- ✅ **Risk ≠ Return** — Risk is the *variability* of returns, measured by $\sigma$ or $\sigma^2$.
- ✅ **Two Master Categories:** Systematic (undiversifiable) and Unsystematic (diversifiable).
- ✅ **Beta ($\beta$)** captures *systematic risk only*; **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** captures *total risk*.
- ✅ **Markowitz Theorem:** As portfolio size $N \to \infty$, unsystematic risk $\to 0$; only systematic risk remains.
- ✅ **CAPM:** $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$ — the foundational asset-pricing equation.
- ✅ **Diversification works only when $\rho < +1$** — perfect positive correlation provides zero risk reduction.
- ✅ **Systematic Risk types (must memorize all 6):** Market, Interest Rate, Inflation, Currency, Political, Country.
- ✅ **Unsystematic Risk types (must memorize all 6):** Business, Financial, Credit, Liquidity, Management, Event.
- ✅ **Sharpe Ratio** uses $\sigma$ (total risk); **Treynor Ratio** uses $\beta$ (systematic risk) — don't mix them.
- ✅ **Value at Risk (VaR)** is a *worst-case loss* measure, not the *actual* loss — there is still a 5% tail at 95% confidence.
- ✅ **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** is the *best* metric for comparing risk across assets with different expected returns.
- ✅ **In a 2-asset portfolio formula**, the cross-term $2 w_1 w_2 \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ is *never* optional.
- ✅ **Examiner's favorite framing:** *"Discuss the types of risk and the tools to measure/mitigate them."* — Always close with a mitigation paragraph.
- ✅ **Real-world anchor:** During the 2008 crisis, even a 50-stock diversified portfolio lost ~40% — because *systematic risk dominated* — proving diversification's limit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
