topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Historical Returns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3: HISTORICAL RETURNS — A KTU PREMIER STUDY GUIDE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Returns (Realized Returns)** refer to the **actual, ex-post** percentage gains or losses generated by a financial asset (or portfolio) over a **past, completed time horizon**. They are computed using **observed market prices, dividend distributions, coupon payments, and capital appreciation** documented in historical records.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, historical returns form the **empirical bedrock** of financial economics. They are used by:
- Investors to estimate *expected future returns*.
- Analysts to quantify *risk premiums*.
- Portfolio managers to compute *standard deviations, variances, and Sharpe ratios*.
- Economists to study *long-run equity risk premiums* and *bond term premia*.

Mathematically, the generic gross return over period $t$ is expressed as:

$$R_{t} = \frac{P_{t} + CF_{t} - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}$$

where:
- $P_{t}$ = Asset price at end of period $t$
- $P_{t-1}$ = Asset price at end of previous period
- $CF_{t}$ = Cash flow received during period (dividend, coupon, rent)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!NOTE]
> **"Looking at the rear-view mirror to drive forward"** 🚗
>
> Imagine you are driving from Kochi to Delhi. The rear-view mirror shows you how bumpy the road behind you was. Historical returns are *that rear-view mirror* of finance. They don't tell you what will happen tomorrow, but they tell you **how the asset behaved under different weather conditions** (bull markets 🐂, bear markets 🐻, recessions, inflations). A wise driver uses the mirror + GPS (forward-looking models). A wise investor uses **historical returns + capital market theory**.

### 1.3 Why Historical Returns Matter in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Historical returns are the *quantitative foundation* for:
> - Estimating the **Equity Risk Premium (ERP)**
> - Computing **volatility** of asset classes
> - Deriving the **real (inflation-adjusted) return**
> - Performing **time-series analysis** of asset performance
> - Building **Mean-Variance Optimization** inputs (Markowitz framework)

### 1.4 Key Categories of Historical Returns

| Category | Asset Class | Key Cash Flow |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Equity Returns** | Stocks, Indices | Dividends + Capital Gains |
| **Bond Returns** | Government/Corporate Bonds | Coupon + Capital Gains |
| **Commodity Returns** | Gold, Oil, Silver | No Cash Flow + Spot Price Change |
| **Real Estate Returns** | REITs, Property | Rent + Appreciation |
| **T-Bill Returns** | Treasury Bills | Only Interest (no capital gain) |

### 1.5 The Core Distinction: Expected vs. Historical Returns

> [!NOTE]
> - **Historical (Realized) Return** = What *actually happened* (ex-post). It is **fact**.
> - **Expected Return** = What investors *anticipate* will happen (ex-ante). It is **probability-weighted**.
>
> Historical returns are the **empirical proxy** used to estimate the expected return under the assumption of *stationarity of return distributions* — a debated but pedagogically central assumption in KTU economics modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Building Blocks of Historical Return Calculation

### Step 1 — Identify the Asset Price Trajectory
Capture the *beginning-of-period* price $P_{0}$ and the *end-of-period* price $P_{T}$ from a reliable source (Bloomberg, NSE, BSE, RBI, FRED).

### Step 2 — Catalogue All Cash Flows
Sum up all **intermediate cash flows**: dividends, coupons, stock splits (adjusted), bonuses, rights.

### Step 3 — Apply the Holding Period Return (HPR) Formula
The single-period HPR is the most fundamental unit:

$$HPR = \frac{P_{1} - P_{0} + D_{1}}{P_{0}}$$

where $D_{1}$ is the cash flow (dividend) received during the period.

### Step 4 — Aggregate Multi-Period Returns
To get a return over $T$ periods, you must **chain** the single-period returns. There are two distinct methods:

#### A. Arithmetic Average Return (AAR)
$$AAR = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} R_{t}$$

- **Use case:** Estimating *expected return in a single future period* (e.g., next year).
- **Bias:** *Overstates* compounded wealth because it ignores volatility drag.

#### B. Geometric Average Return (GAR) — Compounded Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)
$$GAR = \left[ \prod_{t=1}^{T} (1 + R_{t}) \right]^{1/T} - 1$$

- **Use case:** Estimating *historical wealth accumulation* over multiple periods.
- **Property:** Always less than or equal to AAR; equality holds only when all $R_t$ are identical.

### Step 5 — Adjust for Inflation (Real Return)
Using the **Fisher Equation** for approximation:

$$1 + r_{real} = \frac{1 + r_{nominal}}{1 + \pi}$$

where $\pi$ is the inflation rate.

### Step 6 — Quantify Risk (Volatility)
The historical standard deviation is the canonical risk measure:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T-1} \sum_{t=1}^{T} (R_{t} - \bar{R})^{2}}$$

---

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE — IT CARRIES 60% OF NUMERICAL MARKS IN MODULE 3**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Holding Period Return (HPR) | $HPR = \dfrac{P_{1} - P_{0} + CF_{1}}{P_{0}}$ | $CF$ = Cash Flow |
| 2 | Arithmetic Average Return | $AAR = \dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} R_{t}$ | Used for single-period forecasts |
| 3 | Geometric Average Return (CAGR) | $GAR = \left[\prod_{t=1}^{T}(1+R_{t})\right]^{1/T} - 1$ | Used for multi-period wealth |
| 4 | Real Return (Fisher Exact) | $1 + r = \dfrac{1 + r_{nominal}}{1 + \pi}$ | $r$ = real, $\pi$ = inflation |
| 5 | Real Return (Fisher Approx.) | $r \approx r_{nominal} - \pi$ | Linear approximation |
| 6 | Variance | $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{T-1}\sum_{t=1}^{T}(R_{t} - \bar{R})^{2}$ | Sample variance |
| 7 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^{2}}$ | Volatility |
| 8 | Equity Risk Premium | $ERP = \bar{R}_{equity} - \bar{R}_{riskfree}$ | Equity minus T-Bill |
| 9 | Bond Return (Total) | $R_{bond} = \dfrac{C + (P_{1}-P_{0})}{P_{0}}$ | $C$ = Coupon |
| 10 | Sharpe Ratio | $S = \dfrac{\bar{R}_{p} - r_{f}}{\sigma_{p}}$ | Risk-adjusted return |
| 11 | Log Return | $r_{log} = \ln(P_{t}/P_{t-1})$ | Time-additive, used in academia |
| 12 | Cumulative Wealth | $W_{T} = W_{0} \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T}(1+R_{t})$ | Ending wealth |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Financial Utility 🏗️

> [!NOTE]
> Although the course is in the B.Tech Open Elective basket, the **discipline of historical return analysis** is foundational for:
> - **Project Appraisal (Engineering Economics):** Estimating the *expected return* of a capital project from historical industry data.
> - **Infrastructure Investment:** Sovereign-wealth-funded Kerala infrastructure projects (e.g., GIFT City, Metro Rail) are evaluated against *historical bond yields*.
> - **Start-up Valuation:** The *Equity Risk Premium* (calculated from Nifty 50 vs. G-Sec historical returns) is the *discount rate add-on* for start-ups.
> - **Risk-Management of Engineering Portfolios:** Construction firms use volatility of historical returns on commodity-linked contracts to hedge currency risk.

---

## 2.4 Why Geometric < Arithmetic: The "Volatility Drag"

The mathematical inequality:

$$GAR \leq AAR$$

with equality **only if** $\sigma = 0$ (zero volatility) or $T = 1$.

**Why?** Because losses of a given *percentage* require a *larger percentage gain* to recover — a phenomenon called **volatility drag** or **variance drain**.

> [!TIP]
> **Example:** Lose 50%, you need a **100% gain** to break even — not a 50% gain.
> This is the empirical reason why the **CAGR is the *true* historical wealth growth rate** and is the metric that should be used for KTU numerical problems involving multi-year portfolios.

---

## 2.5 Historical Returns Across Asset Classes — A Long-Run Snapshot (Illustrative)

| Asset Class | Approx. Nominal CAGR (Long-Run, India/USA) | Typical Volatility |
|-------------|--------------------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Equity (Nifty 50 / S&P 500)** | ~12% – 14% | High (15% – 20%) |
| **Long-Term G-Secs** | ~7% – 9% | Moderate (8% – 12%) |
| **T-Bills (91-Day)** | ~5% – 6% | Very Low (≤ 1%) |
| **Gold** | ~8% – 10% | Moderate (12% – 16%) |
| **Real Estate** | ~7% – 10% | High but Illiquid |
| **Inflation (CPI)** | ~4% – 6% | Low (1% – 3%) |

> [!WARNING]
> These figures are **illustrative magnitudes only**; in KTU exams you are *not* required to memorize exact numbers. Focus on the *methods* and *interpretations*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Geometric Average Return from Compound Wealth

**Starting Premise:** If $W_0$ is initial wealth and $W_T$ is final wealth after $T$ periods, the per-period *compounded* growth rate $g$ satisfies:

$$W_{T} = W_{0}(1 + g)^{T}$$

**Goal:** Find $g$ in terms of the individual period returns $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_T$.

**Step 1:** Write the wealth evolution recursively:

$$W_{1} = W_{0}(1 + R_{1})$$

**Step 2:** Apply to period 2:

$$W_{2} = W_{1}(1 + R_{2}) = W_{0}(1 + R_{1})(1 + R_{2})$$

**Step 3:** Continue to period $T$:

$$W_{T} = W_{0}\prod_{t=1}^{T}(1 + R_{t})$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions for $W_T$:

$$W_{0}(1 + g)^{T} = W_{0}\prod_{t=1}^{T}(1 + R_{t})$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $W_0$ and take the $T$-th root:

$$1 + g = \left[\prod_{t=1}^{T}(1 + R_{t})\right]^{1/T}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $g$:

$$g = \left[\prod_{t=1}^{T}(1 + R_{t})\right]^{1/T} - 1 \equiv GAR$$

This is the **Geometric Average Return (CAGR)** — derived rigorously. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Arithmetic and Geometric Means

For two periods with returns $R_1$ and $R_2$:

$$(1 + AAR)^{2} = (1 + GAR)^{2} + \sigma^{2}$$

A more general form (for small variances):

$$GAR \approx AAR - \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2}$$

**Derivation Sketch:**

Let $\bar{R} = AAR$ and let $R_t = \bar{R} + \epsilon_t$ with $\sum \epsilon_t = 0$.

Then:

$$\prod_{t=1}^{T}(1 + R_{t}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T}(1 + \bar{R} + \epsilon_{t})$$

Taking log:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{T} \ln(1 + \bar{R} + \epsilon_{t}) \approx T \cdot \ln(1 + \bar{R}) + \frac{1}{1 + \bar{R}}\sum \epsilon_{t} - \frac{1}{2(1+\bar{R})^{2}}\sum \epsilon_{t}^{2}$$

The first two terms yield $AAR$-related quantities; the third term gives the variance correction:

$$GAR \approx AAR - \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2(1 + AAR)^{2}} \cdot (1 + AAR) \approx AAR - \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2}$$

Hence the **rule of thumb**: *Geometric mean is approximately the arithmetic mean minus half the variance.* ✅

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** An investor holds an equity stock for 4 years. Year-end prices and dividends are:
>
> | Year | Beginning Price (₹) | End Price (₹) | Dividend (₹) |
> |------|---------------------|----------------|---------------|
> | 1    | 100                 | 110            | 5             |
> | 2    | 110                 | 108            | 6             |
> | 3    | 108                 | 120            | 4             |
> | 4    | 120                 | 135            | 8             |
>
> **Compute: (a) Annual HPRs, (b) Arithmetic Average Return, (c) Geometric Average Return, (d) Real Return if inflation = 5% p.a.**

### Solution (a) — Annual HPRs

Using $HPR_t = \dfrac{P_t - P_{t-1} + D_t}{P_{t-1}}$:

$$HPR_1 = \frac{110 - 100 + 5}{100} = \frac{15}{100} = 0.15 = 15\%$$

$$HPR_2 = \frac{108 - 110 + 6}{110} = \frac{4}{110} \approx 0.03636 = 3.64\%$$

$$HPR_3 = \frac{120 - 108 + 4}{108} = \frac{16}{108} \approx 0.14815 = 14.81\%$$

$$HPR_4 = \frac{135 - 120 + 8}{120} = \frac{23}{120} \approx 0.19167 = 19.17\%$$

### Solution (b) — Arithmetic Average Return

$$AAR = \frac{0.15 + 0.03636 + 0.14815 + 0.19167}{4} = \frac{0.52618}{4} \approx 0.13155 = 13.16\%$$

### Solution (c) — Geometric Average Return (CAGR)

$$GAR = \left[(1.15)(1.03636)(1.14815)(1.19167)\right]^{1/4} - 1$$

$$\prod = 1.15 \times 1.03636 \times 1.14815 \times 1.19167$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$1.15 \times 1.03636 \approx 1.19181$$

$$1.19181 \times 1.14815 \approx 1.36843$$

$$1.36843 \times 1.19167 \approx 1.63076$$

Now take the 4th root:

$$(1.63076)^{0.25} \approx 1.13015$$

Therefore:

$$GAR \approx 0.13015 = 13.02\%$$

### Solution (d) — Real Return (Fisher)

$$1 + r_{real} = \frac{1 + 0.1316}{1 + 0.05} = \frac{1.1316}{1.05} \approx 1.0777$$

$$r_{real} \approx 7.77\%$$

**Interpretation:** The investor earned ~13% nominally but only ~7.77% in *real purchasing power*. ✅

---

## 3.4 The Equity Risk Premium (ERP) — Empirical Framework

The **Historical Equity Risk Premium** is computed as:

$$ERP = \bar{R}_{equity} - \bar{R}_{riskfree}$$

**Standard Empirical Choices for Risk-Free Rate:**
- 3-month T-Bill (US) — average ~3.5% to 4%
- 10-Year G-Sec (India) — average ~7%

**Standard Empirical Estimates (Long-Run):**
- **Damodaran (NYU):** US Equity Premium ≈ 5% – 6%
- **Indian ERP (Damodaran 2024):** ~7% – 8% using INR returns

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU Module 3:** The ERP derived from *historical returns* is the *plug-in value* for CAPM:
> $$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot ERP$$
> This is the most-tested numerical application in KTU 2024.

---

## 3.5 Bond Return Decomposition

A long-term bond's historical return decomposes into:

$$R_{bond} = \underbrace{\frac{C}{P_{0}}}_{\text{Coupon Yield}} + \underbrace{\frac{P_{1} - P_{0}}{P_{0}}}_{\text{Capital Gain Yield}}$$

- The **coupon yield** is approximately the bond's YTM if held to maturity.
- The **capital gain yield** depends on the *change in market interest rates*: when rates rise, bond prices fall → negative capital gain.
- **Historical observation:** Long-term government bonds in India have delivered ~6% – 8% nominal CAGR over 1990–2024.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Historical Return Computation Pipeline


```mermaid
    Q3 -- Yes --> R2[Use Geometric Average Return CAGR]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Distinguish between Arithmetic Average Return and Geometric Average Return. State when each is appropriately used.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Arithmetic Average Return (AAR):** The simple mean of periodic returns, $AAR = \frac{1}{T}\sum R_t$. It is the *unbiased estimator* of the expected return for a **single future period** (1 mark).
- **Geometric Average Return (GAR / CAGR):** The compounded per-period growth rate, $GAR = \left[\prod (1 + R_t)\right]^{1/T} - 1$. It accurately reflects **historical wealth accumulation** over multiple periods (1 mark).
- **Relationship:** $GAR \leq AAR$, with equality only when $\sigma = 0$. The difference is roughly $\frac{\sigma^2}{2}$, the **volatility drag** (1 mark).
- **Use:** AAR for *forecasting*; GAR for *reporting realized multi-year performance*.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional]**
**What is the Equity Risk Premium (ERP)? How is it computed from historical data, and why is it critical for financial decision-making?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition:** The ERP is the *excess return* that equities are expected to deliver over the risk-free rate (1 mark).
- **Computation:** $ERP = \bar{R}_{equity} - \bar{R}_{riskfree}$, where the equity return is the long-run CAGR of a broad index (e.g., Nifty 50) and the risk-free rate is typically the 10-Year G-Sec yield (1 mark).
- **Importance:** ERP is the *plug-in premium* in CAPM, used to discount future cash flows, evaluate projects, and price equities. It quantifies the **compensation investors demand for bearing equity risk** (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-3 Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

> **"The historical return of a financial asset is the most accessible proxy for its expected return." Critically analyse this statement. With reference to Indian equity and bond markets, compute the Arithmetic Average Return, Geometric Average Return, and the standard deviation for the following 5-year price-dividend series of a hypothetical stock and discuss the implications for an engineering graduate evaluating a long-term investment.**

#### **Data Provided:**

| Year | $P_{t-1}$ (₹) | $P_t$ (₹) | Dividend $D_t$ (₹) |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1    | 200           | 220       | 10                  |
| 2    | 220           | 215       | 12                  |
| 3    | 215           | 240       | 8                   |
| 4    | 240           | 235       | 14                  |
| 5    | 235           | 270       | 10                  |

Assume the average inflation rate is **5.5% p.a.** and the 10-year G-Sec yield is **6.8% p.a.**

#### **Part (a) — Compute AAR, GAR, and Standard Deviation. (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Annual HPRs (2 marks)**

$$HPR_1 = \frac{220 - 200 + 10}{200} = \frac{30}{200} = 0.15 = 15.00\%$$

$$HPR_2 = \frac{215 - 220 + 12}{220} = \frac{7}{220} \approx 0.03182 = 3.18\%$$

$$HPR_3 = \frac{240 - 215 + 8}{215} = \frac{33}{215} \approx 0.15349 = 15.35\%$$

$$HPR_4 = \frac{235 - 240 + 14}{240} = \frac{9}{240} = 0.0375 = 3.75\%$$

$$HPR_5 = \frac{270 - 235 + 10}{235} = \frac{45}{235} \approx 0.19149 = 19.15\%$$

**Step 2: Arithmetic Average Return (1 mark)**

$$AAR = \frac{0.15 + 0.03182 + 0.15349 + 0.0375 + 0.19149}{5} = \frac{0.56430}{5} \approx 0.11286 = 11.29\%$$

**Step 3: Geometric Average Return (2 marks)**

$$\prod_{t=1}^{5}(1 + R_t) = 1.15 \times 1.03182 \times 1.15349 \times 1.0375 \times 1.19149$$

Sequential multiplication:
- $1.15 \times 1.03182 = 1.18659$
- $1.18659 \times 1.15349 = 1.36875$
- $1.36875 \times 1.0375 = 1.42008$
- $1.42008 \times 1.19149 = 1.69198$

$$GAR = (1.69198)^{1/5} - 1 = 1.11074 - 1 = 0.11074 = 11.07\%$$

**Step 4: Standard Deviation (2 marks)**

Mean = 11.286%; compute deviations:

| Year | $R_t$ (%) | $R_t - \bar{R}$ | $(R_t - \bar{R})^{2}$ |
|------|-----------|------------------|------------------------|
| 1    | 15.00     | 3.714            | 13.79                  |
| 2    | 3.18      | -8.106           | 65.71                  |
| 3    | 15.35     | 4.064            | 16.52                  |
| 4    | 3.75      | -7.536           | 56.79                  |
| 5    | 19.15     | 7.864            | 61.84                  |

Sum of squared deviations = $13.79 + 65.71 + 16.52 + 56.79 + 61.84 = 214.65$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{214.65}{5 - 1}} = \sqrt{53.66} \approx 7.32\%$$

#### **Part (b) — Critical Analysis & Implications (7 marks)**

**1. Real Return (Fisher Equation) — 2 marks:**

$$1 + r_{real} = \frac{1 + 0.1107}{1 + 0.055} = \frac{1.1107}{1.055} \approx 1.0528$$

$$r_{real} \approx 5.28\%$$

**2. Equity Risk Premium — 1 mark:**

$$ERP = 11.07\% - 6.8\% = 4.27\%$$

**3. Sharpe Ratio (assuming $r_f = 6.8\%$) — 1 mark:**

$$S = \frac{11.29 - 6.8}{7.32} = \frac{4.49}{7.32} \approx 0.61$$

**4. Critical Analysis (3 marks):**
- **Strengths of using historical returns as expected return proxy:** Empirically grounded; uses *all available information*; reflects *actual market behaviour*; supports CAPM and portfolio theory.
- **Limitations:** Past performance ≠ future results; **survivorship bias** in equity indices; *non-stationarity* of return distributions; regime shifts (2008, 2020 COVID); inflation and tax distortions.
- **Implication for engineering graduate:** The stock delivered ~11% nominal / 5.28% real return, with moderate volatility (7.32%). This is a **reasonable long-term bet** for an engineer building a 20-year wealth plan, but only if held via a *diversified index fund* rather than concentrated single-stock positions. The student should also factor in **taxation (LTCG/STCG)** and **inflation expectations** before committing capital.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]**

> **Discuss the role of historical returns in the construction of the Equity Risk Premium (ERP). Using the data below, calculate the historical ERP for the Indian market over a 5-year window and discuss its implications under CAPM. Also comment on the limitations of using historical ERP as a forward-looking estimator.**

#### **Data Provided:**

| Year | Nifty 50 Total Return (%) | 10-Year G-Sec Yield (%) |
|------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1    | 18.5                       | 7.2                      |
| 2    | -7.4                       | 6.9                      |
| 3    | 24.1                       | 7.0                      |
| 4    | 12.6                       | 7.1                      |
| 5    | 9.8                        | 6.8                      |

#### **Part (a) — Compute Equity CAGR, Risk-Free CAGR, and ERP. (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Equity CAGR (3 marks)**

$$\prod (1 + R_{eq,t}) = 1.185 \times 0.926 \times 1.241 \times 1.126 \times 1.098$$

Sequential:
- $1.185 \times 0.926 = 1.09731$
- $1.09731 \times 1.241 = 1.36176$
- $1.36176 \times 1.126 = 1.53334$
- $1.53334 \times 1.098 = 1.68361$

$$CAGR_{equity} = (1.68361)^{1/5} - 1 = 1.10983 - 1 = 0.10983 = 10.98\%$$

**Step 2: Risk-Free CAGR (2 marks)**

Using geometric mean of (1 + yield):

$$\prod (1 + r_{f,t}) = 1.072 \times 1.069 \times 1.070 \times 1.071 \times 1.068$$

- $1.072 \times 1.069 = 1.14597$
- $1.14597 \times 1.070 = 1.22619$
- $1.22619 \times 1.071 = 1.31325$
- $1.31325 \times 1.068 = 1.40255$

$$CAGR_{rf} = (1.40255)^{1/5} - 1 = 1.07011 - 1 = 0.07011 = 7.01\%$$

**Step 3: Historical ERP (1 mark)**

$$ERP = 10.98\% - 7.01\% \approx 3.97\%$$

**Step 4: Arithmetic averages (1 mark, for context):**

- AAR (equity) = $(18.5 - 7.4 + 24.1 + 12.6 + 9.8)/5 = 57.6/5 = 11.52\%$
- AAR (rf) = $(7.2 + 6.9 + 7.0 + 7.1 + 6.8)/5 = 35.0/5 = 7.0\%$
- AAR-based ERP = $11.52 - 7.00 = 4.52\%$

#### **Part (b) — Implications under CAPM & Limitations (7 marks)**

**1. CAPM Implication — 2 marks:**

For a stock with $\beta = 1.2$:

$$E(R_i) = 7.0\% + 1.2 \times 3.97\% = 7.0\% + 4.76\% = 11.76\%$$

**2. Engineering-Economic Application — 1 mark:**

A Kerala-based start-up founder (engineer-turned-entrepreneur) can use this 11.76% as the *discount rate* to evaluate a 5-year infra-project, ensuring NPV calculations reflect the local capital cost.

**3. Limitations of Historical ERP (4 marks):**

- **Survivorship Bias:** Indices drop weak firms; only survivors are reflected — *overstates* true historical average.
- **Regime Non-Stationarity:** The 5-year window (e.g., 2019–2024) includes COVID and Taper Tantrum 2.0; not representative of the long-run.
- **Look-Ahead Bias:** Using *ex-post* realized returns to estimate *ex-ante* expectations is theoretically problematic.
- **Choice of Risk-Free Proxy:** T-Bill vs 10-Year G-Sec yield gives different ERPs; consensus is to use long-duration sovereign yields.
- **Currency / Inflation:** Cross-country ERP comparison needs PPP or USD normalization.
- **Behavioural Component:** ERP is *time-varying* and correlated with sentiment (Shiller's CAPE-based *implied* ERP often differs markedly from the *realized* ERP).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Module 3 — "Historical Returns"**
>
> 1. **Using the wrong average:** AAR is for forecasting; GAR (CAGR) is for realized multi-period wealth. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the dividend/coupon in HPR:** Many students write $HPR = (P_1 - P_0)/P_0$ only — for equity, the dividend term is **non-negotiable** in KTU valuations.
> 3. **Confusing real and nominal returns:** The Fisher Equation (exact form, not the approximation) is required unless the question explicitly allows the linear approximation.
> 4. **Arithmetic error in CAGR:** The 4th or 5th root must be computed; KTU 2024 expects the *exact* formula chain, not a "rough" estimate.
> 5. **Inconsistent sample variance denominator:** Use $T-1$ (Bessel's correction) for historical samples; the population formula with $T$ is only valid when the *entire population* is observed.
> 6. **Forgetting the units:** AAR of 0.13 must be quoted as **13%** (or 13% p.a.) in the final answer.
> 7. **Ignoring survivorship bias:** In the critical-analysis section, failing to mention this is a *guaranteed 1-mark deduction* in KTU Part (b).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — READ BEFORE ENTERING THE EXAM HALL**

- [x] **Historical Return = Realized, Ex-Post Return** based on observed prices and cash flows.
- [x] **HPR Formula:** $HPR = \dfrac{P_1 - P_0 + CF_1}{P_0}$ — *always include cash flow*.
- [x] **AAR** = simple mean of period returns; used for **forecasting** next-period return.
- [x] **GAR / CAGR** = compounded growth; used for **reporting** historical wealth accumulation.
- [x] **Inequality:** $GAR \leq AAR$; gap ≈ $\sigma^2/2$ (volatility drag).
- [x] **Fisher Equation (exact):** $1 + r = (1 + r_{nom}) / (1 + \pi)$.
- [x] **Fisher Approximation:** $r \approx r_{nom} - \pi$ (only when both < 10%).
- [x] **Sample Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum(R_t - \bar{R})^2}{T-1}}$.
- [x] **Equity Risk Premium (ERP):** $ERP = \bar{R}_{equity} - \bar{R}_{riskfree}$.
- [x] **CAPM Plug-in:** $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot ERP$.
- [x] **Sharpe Ratio:** $S = (\bar{R}_p - r_f)/\sigma_p$ — risk-adjusted performance.
- [x] **Bond Return:** $R_{bond} = C/P_0 + (P_1 - P_0)/P_0$ (coupon + capital gain).
- [x] **Log Return:** $r_{log} = \ln(P_t/P_{t-1})$ — time-additive, used in academic econometrics.
- [x] **Key Biases:** Survivorship bias, look-ahead bias, regime non-stationarity, currency bias.
- [x] **Practical Use:** Inputs for CAPM, mean-variance optimization, Sharpe ratio, project appraisal.
- [x] **Real vs Nominal:** Always convert to *real* terms when comparing returns across time horizons with different inflation regimes.
- [x] **Indian Context:** Nifty 50 (equity) vs 10-Year G-Sec (risk-free) is the standard empirical pairing for Indian ERP estimation.
- [x] **Engineering Tie-in:** Historical returns of industry indices inform the *hurdle rate* for capital budgeting of engineering projects.

> 🏁 **END OF MODULE 3 — HISTORICAL RETURNS — KTU PREMIER ENGINE V10**
