topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Computing Historical Returns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of financial economics and the KTU 2024 Scheme (OEIET834), **Historical Return** refers to the **realized past performance** of a financial asset — such as a stock, bond, mutual fund, or a market index — measured over a specific past time horizon. It is the *ex-post* (after-the-fact) percentage change in the value of an investment, capturing capital gains, losses, and any cash flows (dividends, coupons, distributions) generated during the holding period.

The historical return is the empirical foundation upon which investors estimate expected future returns, calibrate portfolio variance, and back-test asset-pricing models (such as the **Capital Asset Pricing Model** or **Markowitz Mean-Variance Framework**). The KTU 2024 syllabus treats historical returns as the *first building block* of performance measurement before progressing to risk-adjusted metrics like the **Sharpe Ratio**, **Treynor Ratio**, and **Jensen's Alpha**.

### 1.2 Intuition — A Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Mango Orchard**
>
> Imagine you bought a small mango tree from a nursery in **January 2022** for **₹1,000**. By **January 2023**, the tree is now worth **₹1,200** because the tree grew larger. During the year, the tree also produced mangoes, which you sold at the local market for **₹100**.
>
> So your *total profit* = ₹1,200 − ₹1,000 + ₹100 = **₹300** on a **₹1,000** investment. Your historical return = 30 %.
>
> This 30 % is your **historical return** — it is the *past record* of what your money earned. Just like a school report card tells you how a student performed in the past, historical returns tell an investor how an asset performed in the past. Future grades are predicted (expected returns), but grades must be based on past performance — that is the heart of historical return analysis.

### 1.3 Types of Historical Returns Covered in This Module

The KTU 2024 syllabus enumerates three principal categories:

1. **Holding Period Return (HPR)** — total return over a single holding period.
2. **Arithmetic Average Return** — the simple arithmetic mean of multiple HPRs.
3. **Geometric Average Return (Time-Weighted)** — the compounded, multi-period return.
4. **Continuously Compounded (Logarithmic) Return** — $r = \ln(P_t / P_{t-1})$.
5. **Real vs. Nominal Returns** — inflation-adjusted purchasing-power return.
6. **Total Return with Reinvested Income** — incorporating dividends, coupons, and splits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> Module 3 emphasizes that computing historical returns is a *prerequisite* for measuring **volatility** (Module 4) and **portfolio risk** (Module 5). Every student must master the distinction between **Simple (Arithmetic) Returns** and **Log Returns** before proceeding.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Step-by-Step Logic of Computing Historical Returns

The process of computing historical returns follows a **5-stage logic pipeline**:

1. **Identify the asset** and select the price series $P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ over $n$ consecutive periods (typically days, months, or years).
2. **Gather cash flows** $D_t$ received during each period (dividends, coupons, capital distributions).
3. **Adjust prices** for corporate actions such as stock splits, bonus issues, and mergers to obtain a *split-adjusted, dividend-included* total-return price series.
4. **Compute period-by-period returns** using the appropriate formula (simple, log, or holding-period).
5. **Aggregate** the periodic returns into a *single summary statistic* — either an **arithmetic mean** (for forecasting) or a **geometric mean** (for realized wealth).

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters

- **Step 1 (Asset Identification):** Different assets have different price-discovery mechanisms. An equity stock needs an adjusted closing price, while a fixed deposit needs a coupon schedule.
- **Step 2 (Cash-Flow Collection):** Ignoring dividends underestimates total return. The KTU examiner often tests this by giving only price changes and asking for the *true* total return.
- **Step 3 (Corporate Action Adjustment):** A 1:1 bonus issue halves the share price but does not change wealth. Failure to adjust produces spurious negative returns.
- **Step 4 (Formula Selection):** Simple returns are *additive across assets* (used in portfolio variance). Log returns are *time-additive* (used in continuous-time finance). The KTU board often asks which is preferred for multi-period analysis.
- **Step 5 (Aggregation):** Arithmetic mean *overstates* long-term wealth; geometric mean is the *correct* wealth-weighted measure.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for Computing Historical Returns**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Meaning / Variables | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simple (Discrete) Return** | $R_t = \dfrac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}$ | % change in price only | Equity price-only questions |
| 2 | **Holding Period Return (HPR)** | $\text{HPR} = \dfrac{P_t - P_{t-1} + D_t}{P_{t-1}}$ | Includes dividend $D_t$ | Single-period total return |
| 3 | **Continuously Compounded (Log) Return** | $r_t = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}\right)$ | $\ln$ denotes natural logarithm | Multi-period aggregation |
| 4 | **Multi-Period Simple Return** | $1 + R_{\text{total}} = \prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + R_t)$ | Compound product of gross returns | KTU two/three-year problems |
| 5 | **Arithmetic Mean Return** | $\bar{R}_A = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} R_t$ | Simple average | Forecasting / CAPM inputs |
| 6 | **Geometric Mean Return** | $\bar{R}_G = \left[\prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + R_t)\right]^{1/n} - 1$ | Compound annual growth rate | Realized wealth measure |
| 7 | **Real (Inflation-Adjusted) Return** | $1 + R_{\text{real}} = \dfrac{1 + R_{\text{nominal}}}{1 + \pi}$ | $\pi$ = inflation rate | Fisher equation application |
| 8 | **Approximate Real Return** | $R_{\text{real}} \approx R_{\text{nominal}} - \pi$ | First-order Taylor approximation | Quick mental math |
| 9 | **Stock-Split Adjustment Factor** | $P^{\text{adj}}_t = P_t \times \dfrac{S_{\text{new}}}{S_{\text{old}}}$ | Adjusted price after split | Corporate action problems |
| 10 | **Annualized Return (from monthly)** | $R_{\text{annual}} = (1 + R_{\text{monthly}})^{12} - 1$ | Geometric compounding to 1 year | Cross-frequency comparison |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance

Historical-return computation is foundational in:
- **Algorithmic Trading:** Backtesting strategies on 10+ years of NIFTY-50 daily log returns.
- **Project Finance:** Computing *internal rate of return (IRR)* of a plant requires historical project cash flows.
- **Actuarial Science:** Insurers compute historical mortality returns.
- **Corporate Treasury:** Firms compute historical returns on cash-equivalent portfolios (T-bills, repo).
- **Bank Valuation:** Banks use historical loan-loss-return series to compute Value-at-Risk (VaR).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Simple Return to Multi-Period Total Return

Let an asset have price $P_0$ at time $0$ and prices $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ at times $1, 2, \ldots, n$. Define the simple return for each period as:

$$
R_t = \frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}
$$

To derive the multi-period total return, start from the gross return $1 + R_t$:

$$
1 + R_t = \frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}} + 1 = \frac{P_t - P_{t-1} + P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}} = \frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}
$$

Multiplying across all $n$ periods:

$$
1 + R_{\text{total}} = \frac{P_1}{P_0} \times \frac{P_2}{P_1} \times \frac{P_3}{P_2} \times \cdots \times \frac{P_n}{P_{n-1}}
$$

The intermediate prices cancel in a telescoping product:

$$
1 + R_{\text{total}} = \frac{P_n}{P_0}
$$

Therefore the multi-period total simple return is:

$$
\boxed{\,1 + R_{\text{total}} = \prod_{t=1}^{n} (1 + R_t)\,}
$$

> **Logic Step:** We rewrote each gross return as a price ratio, multiplied them, and used the **telescoping cancellation** property to obtain a clean expression linking the final price $P_n$ to the initial price $P_0$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Geometric Mean Return (Time-Weighted Realized Return)

Starting from the multi-period equation derived above:

$$
1 + R_{\text{total}} = \prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + R_t)
$$

We want a single *per-period* return $G$ such that compounding it $n$ times gives the same total return:

$$
(1 + G)^n = \prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + R_t)
$$

Taking the $n$-th root of both sides:

$$
1 + G = \left[\prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + R_t)\right]^{1/n}
$$

Subtracting 1:

$$
\boxed{\,G = \left[\prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + R_t)\right]^{1/n} - 1\,}
$$

This is the **geometric mean return**, also known as the **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)** when $n$ is in years.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Fisher Equation (Real Return from Nominal)

Let $R_{\text{nominal}}$ be the quoted return and $\pi$ the inflation rate (CPI change). By definition:

$$
(1 + R_{\text{nominal}}) = (1 + R_{\text{real}})(1 + \pi)
$$

Solving for the real return:

$$
1 + R_{\text{real}} = \frac{1 + R_{\text{nominal}}}{1 + \pi}
$$

$$
R_{\text{real}} = \frac{1 + R_{\text{nominal}}}{1 + \pi} - 1
$$

For *small* inflation $\pi$, we use the first-order Taylor expansion $\dfrac{1}{1 + \pi} \approx 1 - \pi$:

$$
R_{\text{real}} \approx (1 + R_{\text{nominal}})(1 - \pi) - 1 \approx R_{\text{nominal}} - \pi
$$

> **Logic Step:** The Fisher identity expresses that **nominal growth = real growth + inflation effect + cross-product**, with the cross-product usually negligible for low-inflation economies like India.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem Type)

**Problem:** A stock has the following monthly closing prices and dividends:

| Month | Price (₹) | Dividend (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Jan | 100 | — |
| Feb | 110 | 0 |
| Mar | 105 | 2 |
| Apr | 112 | 0 |
| May | 120 | 3 |

**(a) Compute the holding period return for each month.**

For Feb: $R_{\text{Feb}} = \dfrac{110 - 100 + 0}{100} = \dfrac{10}{100} = 0.10 = 10\%$

For Mar: $R_{\text{Mar}} = \dfrac{105 - 110 + 2}{110} = \dfrac{-5 + 2}{110} = \dfrac{-3}{110} = -2.727\%$

For Apr: $R_{\text{Apr}} = \dfrac{112 - 105 + 0}{105} = \dfrac{7}{105} = 6.667\%$

For May: $R_{\text{May}} = \dfrac{120 - 112 + 3}{112} = \dfrac{11}{112} = 9.821\%$

**(b) Compute the 4-month total return and the geometric mean return.**

Total return (multi-period product):

$$
1 + R_{\text{total}} = (1.10)(0.97273)(1.06667)(1.09821)
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
1.10 \times 0.97273 = 1.07000
$$

$$
1.07000 \times 1.06667 = 1.14133
$$

$$
1.14133 \times 1.09821 = 1.25358
$$

Therefore $R_{\text{total}} = 0.25358 = 25.358\%$.

Geometric mean monthly return:

$$
G = (1.25358)^{1/4} - 1
$$

Computing the fourth root:

$$
\ln(1.25358) = 0.22598
$$

$$
\frac{0.22598}{4} = 0.056495
$$

$$
G = e^{0.056495} - 1 = 1.05813 - 1 = 0.05813 = 5.813\%
$$

**(c) Compute the arithmetic mean return.**

$$
\bar{R}_A = \frac{10\% + (-2.727\%) + 6.667\% + 9.821\%}{4} = \frac{23.761\%}{4} = 5.940\%
$$

> **Observation:** $\bar{R}_A = 5.940\%$ is **higher** than $G = 5.813\%$. This is the *Arithmetic-Geometric Inequality*, $\bar{R}_A \geq G$, with strict inequality whenever returns are not identical.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Return Type

| Situation | Correct Return Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Single-period profit/loss reporting | HPR with dividends | Captures true cash earnings |
| Multi-period wealth calculation | Geometric mean | Compound growth is multiplicative |
| Forecasting next-year expected return | Arithmetic mean | Unbiased estimator of $\mathbb{E}[R]$ |
| Multi-asset portfolio variance | Simple returns | Simple returns are additive across assets |
| Continuous-time option pricing | Log returns | Time-additive: $r_{\text{total}} = \sum r_t$ |
| Inflation-adjusted real planning | Fisher real return | Strips out purchasing-power erosion |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Computational Pipeline for Historical Returns

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Price Series P0, P1, ..., Pn] --> B{Identify Corporate Actions?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Adjust for Splits, Bonuses, Dividends]
    B -- No --> D[Use Prices As-Is]
    C --> E[Adjusted Price Series]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Choose Return Type}
    F -- Simple --> G[Rt = Pt - Pt-1 / Pt-1]
    F -- Log --> H[rt = ln of Pt over Pt-1]
    F -- HPR --> I[HPR = Pt - Pt-1 + Dt over Pt-1]
    G --> J[Period-by-Period Returns]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Aggregation Required?}
    K -- One Period --> L[Report as Final Return]
    K -- Multi-Period --> M[Compute Arithmetic Mean]
    K -- Multi-Period --> N[Compute Geometric Mean]
    M --> O[Final Output]
    N --> O
    L --> O[Final Output]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Tree for KTU Examination Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Given Asset Price Data] --> Q1{Question mentions dividend?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> PATH1[Use HPR Formula]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Question asks multi-period?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is it wealth or forecast?}
    Q3 -- Wealth --> PATH2[Use Geometric Mean]
    Q3 -- Forecast --> PATH3[Use Arithmetic Mean]
    Q2 -- No --> PATH4[Use Simple Return]
    Q1 --> Q4{Question mentions inflation?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> PATH5[Apply Fisher Equation]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Adjustment for Stock Splits

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OldPrice[Old Price P0] --> SplitFactor[Multiply by Ratio S]
    SplitFactor --> NewPrice[Split-Adjusted Price P0 Adj]
    NewPrice --> ComputeR[Compute Return]
    OldPrice2[Old Price P1] --> SplitFactor2[Multiply by Ratio S]
    SplitFactor2 --> NewPrice2[Split-Adjusted Price P1 Adj]
    NewPrice2 --> ComputeR
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Holding Period Return. How is it different from a Simple Price Return?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Holding Period Return (HPR) is the total return earned on an asset over the period it is held, expressed as a percentage of the initial investment. It includes both capital appreciation and any cash income (dividends, coupons) received during the period. The formula is:

$$
\text{HPR} = \frac{P_t - P_{t-1} + D_t}{P_{t-1}}
$$

A **simple price return** considers only the price change:

$$
R_t = \frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}
$$

Thus HPR = Price Return + Dividend Yield, and HPR is the *true* total return earned by the investor, whereas the simple price return ignores cash distributions.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for HPR formula, 1 for contrast with simple return)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Fisher equation relating nominal return, real return, and inflation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Fisher equation states that the gross nominal return equals the product of the gross real return and the gross inflation rate:

$$
(1 + R_{\text{nominal}}) = (1 + R_{\text{real}})(1 + \pi)
$$

Solving for the real return:

$$
R_{\text{real}} = \frac{1 + R_{\text{nominal}}}{1 + \pi} - 1
$$

The approximation $R_{\text{real}} \approx R_{\text{nominal}} - \pi$ holds when inflation is low. This identity, due to economist **Irving Fisher**, is fundamental for separating *real* purchasing-power gains from *nominal* rupee gains.

*(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for rearranged form, 1 for approximation & attribution)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** A stock's price at the start of four consecutive years was ₹200, ₹240, ₹180, and ₹270. The corresponding year-end prices were ₹240, ₹180, ₹270, and ₹300. Dividends paid during each year were ₹0, ₹10, ₹5, and ₹10 respectively. Compute the holding period return for each year. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the returns from part (a), compute (i) the arithmetic mean return and (ii) the geometric mean return. Comment on which one correctly represents the realized wealth growth of the investor. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**(a) Year-by-Year HPR:**

Year 1:

$$
\text{HPR}_1 = \frac{240 - 200 + 0}{200} = \frac{40}{200} = 0.20 = 20\%
$$

Year 2:

$$
\text{HPR}_2 = \frac{180 - 240 + 10}{240} = \frac{-50}{240} = -0.20833 = -20.833\%
$$

Year 3:

$$
\text{HPR}_3 = \frac{270 - 180 + 5}{180} = \frac{95}{180} = 0.52778 = 52.778\%
$$

Year 4:

$$
\text{HPR}_4 = \frac{300 - 270 + 10}{270} = \frac{40}{270} = 0.14815 = 14.815\%
$$

**(b) (i) Arithmetic Mean:**

$$
\bar{R}_A = \frac{20 + (-20.833) + 52.778 + 14.815}{4}\%
$$

$$
\bar{R}_A = \frac{66.760\%}{4} = 16.690\%
$$

**(b) (ii) Geometric Mean:**

Gross returns: $1.20,\ 0.79167,\ 1.52778,\ 1.14815$

$$
1 + R_{\text{total}} = 1.20 \times 0.79167 \times 1.52778 \times 1.14815
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
1.20 \times 0.79167 = 0.95000
$$

$$
0.95000 \times 1.52778 = 1.45139
$$

$$
1.45139 \times 1.14815 = 1.66640
$$

$$
R_{\text{total}} = 0.66640 = 66.64\%
$$

Geometric mean (annualized):

$$
G = (1.66640)^{1/4} - 1
$$

Using logarithms:

$$
\ln(1.66640) = 0.51040
$$

$$
G = e^{0.51040/4} - 1 = e^{0.12760} - 1 = 1.13614 - 1 = 0.13614 = 13.614\%
$$

**Comment:** The geometric mean ($13.614\%$) is the **correct** measure of realized wealth growth because it accounts for the compounding effect of gains and losses. The arithmetic mean ($16.690\%$) overstates the true growth rate — a phenomenon known as **volatility drag**. For an investor who actually held the asset, ₹200 grew to ₹333.28 (66.64 % total, 13.614 % annualized), not ₹200 × (1.1669)⁴ = ₹396.34.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain with formulas the difference between *Simple Arithmetic Return* and *Continuously Compounded (Log) Return*. State one advantage of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An investment grew from ₹5,000 to ₹6,500 in one year, during which inflation was 6 %. Compute (i) the nominal return, (ii) the exact real return using the Fisher equation, and (iii) the approximate real return. Comment on the size of the approximation error. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**(a)** The **Simple Arithmetic Return** measures the discrete percentage change:

$$
R_t = \frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}
$$

The **Continuously Compounded (Log) Return** is defined as the natural logarithm of the price ratio:

$$
r_t = \ln\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}\right)
$$

Algebraic relationship:

$$
r_t = \ln(1 + R_t)
$$

**Advantages:**
- *Simple Return:* It is **additive across assets**, which makes it the standard input for portfolio variance computations (Markowitz framework).
- *Log Return:* It is **time-additive**, meaning multi-period log returns simply sum: $r_{1 \to n} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} r_t$. This property is essential for continuous-time models like Black-Scholes.

**(b) (i) Nominal Return:**

$$
R_{\text{nominal}} = \frac{6500 - 5000}{5000} = \frac{1500}{5000} = 0.30 = 30\%
$$

**(b) (ii) Exact Real Return (Fisher):**

$$
1 + R_{\text{real}} = \frac{1 + 0.30}{1 + 0.06} = \frac{1.30}{1.06} = 1.22642
$$

$$
R_{\text{real}} = 0.22642 = 22.642\%
$$

**(b) (iii) Approximate Real Return:**

$$
R_{\text{real}} \approx 30\% - 6\% = 24\%
$$

**Comment on Error:**

$$
\text{Error} = \vert 24.000\% - 22.642\% \vert = 1.358\%
$$

The approximation overstates the true real return. The cross-product term that is ignored is:

$$
R_{\text{nominal}} \times \pi = 0.30 \times 0.06 = 0.018 = 1.8\%
$$

For moderate inflation (≈ 6 %), this error is small but **not negligible** for board examinations. KTU expects students to use the exact Fisher formula unless the problem explicitly says "approximate."

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully!)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the dividend in HPR:** Many students write only $\frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}$ even when $D_t \neq 0$. This costs **1–2 marks** per sub-question.
> 2. **Computing arithmetic mean instead of geometric mean** when asked for "realized annual return." The KTU key specifically awards the geometric-mean mark only when $(1 + R_t)$ product is used.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract 1** at the end of a geometric-mean calculation: students write $(1.25358)^{1/4}$ as the answer instead of $(1.25358)^{1/4} - 1$.
> 4. **Confusing nominal and real returns:** Real return must come from Fisher, not simple subtraction — but the approximation $R - \pi$ is acceptable only if the question says "approximately."
> 5. **Not adjusting for stock splits/bonuses:** If a 1:1 bonus is mentioned and the student does not halve the historical price, **2 marks** are typically deducted.
> 6. **Unit errors:** Reporting 0.05813 instead of 5.813 % — the KTU board deducts ½ mark for missing the percentage conversion.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Computing Historical Returns**

- **Historical Return** = Realized past % gain on an asset; includes capital gains + cash income.
- **Simple Return Formula:** $R_t = (P_t - P_{t-1}) / P_{t-1}$.
- **Holding Period Return (HPR):** $\text{HPR} = (P_t - P_{t-1} + D_t) / P_{t-1}$ — always include $D_t$.
- **Log Return:** $r_t = \ln(P_t / P_{t-1})$ — preferred for multi-period sums and continuous-time models.
- **Multi-Period Total Return:** $1 + R_{\text{total}} = \prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + R_t)$.
- **Arithmetic Mean:** $\bar{R}_A = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum R_t$ — used for forecasting; **additive but overstates wealth**.
- **Geometric Mean:** $G = \left[\prod(1 + R_t)\right]^{1/n} - 1$ — correct realized-wealth measure; multiplicative.
- **Arithmetic ≥ Geometric:** Always true; gap = *volatility drag*.
- **Fisher Equation:** $(1 + R_{\text{nominal}}) = (1 + R_{\text{real}})(1 + \pi)$.
- **Approximate Real Return:** $R_{\text{real}} \approx R_{\text{nominal}} - \pi$.
- **Stock-Split Adjustment:** $P^{\text{adj}} = P \times (S_{\text{new}} / S_{\text{old}})$.
- **Key Rule:** For **portfolio variance**, use **simple returns**; for **time aggregation**, use **log returns** or **geometric mean**.
- **Common Pitfalls:** (i) ignoring dividends, (ii) using arithmetic mean for wealth questions, (iii) forgetting $-1$ in geometric mean, (iv) confusing nominal vs real.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Average Annual Returns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Average Annual Returns # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Average Annual Return (AAR)** is a standardized metric used in financial economics to express the historical performance of an investment asset (equity, bond, portfolio, or index) as a single, normalized percentage figure per year over a multi-period holding horizon. It collapses a series of period-by-period discrete holding period returns (HPRs) into one comparable annual figure, enabling benchmarking, performance attribution, and risk-adjusted analysis. The two principal families of AAR used across KTU syllabus modules are: 1. **Arithmetic Average Return (AAR)** 2. **Geometric Average Return (GAR)** — also called the **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)**. A third, equally important construct is the **Time-Weighted Return (TWR)**, which is the AAR computed after neutralizing the distortion caused by intermediate cash inflows and outflows. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Two Friends Saving Money" Analogy** Imagine two friends, *Aravind* and *Bhavya*, who each invest **₹10,000** in the same stock for **3 years**, but the stock behaves differently for each: - **Aravind's path** (volatile): Year 1 = +50%, Year 2 = −30%, Year 3 = +20% - **Bhavya's path** (smooth): Year 1 = +8%, Year 2 = +9%, Year 3 = +10% If you simply *add* the three yearly returns of Aravind and divide by 3, you get **+13.33%** per year (Arithmetic). But his *actual* final wealth is *less* than what compounding at 13.33% would yield, because losses in year 2 eroded the base. The honest "smoothed" annual return that *actually* grew ₹10,000 into his final balance is the **Geometric Average** (≈ **+8.65%**). Bhavya's arithmetic and geometric averages are very close (~9%), because smooth returns barely suffer compounding drag. **Takeaway:** Arithmetic averages *overstate* typical performance when returns are volatile. Geometric averages are the "truthful" compounders. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted in the KTU Syllabus > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Metrics** > - **Arithmetic Mean Return** — $\bar{R}_A$ > - **Geometric Mean Return** — $\bar{R}_G$ (a.k.a. CAGR) > - **Standard Deviation of Returns** — $\sigma_R$ (volatility / total risk) > - **Variance** — $\sigma_R^2$ > - **Sharpe Ratio** — risk-adjusted AAR > - **Holding Period Return (HPR)** — the building block > - **Time-Weighted Return (TWR)** — eliminates cash-flow bias > - **Dollar-Weighted Return (DWR)** — synonymous with **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** ## 1.4 Why AAR Matters in Engineering Economics Engineering professionals managing capital projects (plant expansions, R&D portfolios, infrastructure bids) routinely evaluate competing investment alternatives. AAR provides the **single comparable figure** needed to rank projects of differing durations and cash-flow timings. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Foundational Building Block — Holding Period Return (HPR) The **Holding Period Return** over a single sub-period is defined as: $$HPR_t = \frac{P_t - P_{t-1} + D_t}{P_{t-1}}$$ where: - $P_t$ = price at end of period $t$ - $P_{t-1}$ = price at end of period $t-1$ - $D_t$ = dividend / cash flow received during period $t$ This simplifies into a **capital gains yield + dividend yield** decomposition: $$HPR_t = \underbrace{\frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}}_{\text{Capital Gains Yield}} + \underbrace{\frac{D_t}{P_{t-1}}}_{\text{Dividend Yield}}$$ ## 2.2 The Arithmetic Average Return (AAR) The simplest aggregation — it ignores compounding and treats each period as independent: $$\bar{R}_A = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} R_t$$ **Logical Steps:** 1. Collect $n$ successive periodic (annual/quarterly/monthly) HPRs. 2. Sum them arithmetically. 3. Divide by the count $n$. **Strengths:** Easy to compute, unbiased estimator of *expected* return, ideal for forecasting the *next* period. **Weaknesses:** Misrepresents the *actual* compounded wealth at horizon end; biased upward for volatile series. ## 2.3 The Geometric Average Return (GAR / CAGR) The constant annual rate at which an initial investment would have grown to reach the same terminal value achieved by the actual volatile path: $$\bar{R}_G = \left[\prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + R_t)\right]^{1/n} - 1$$ **Equivalent logarithmic form:** $$\bar{R}_G = \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\ln(1 + R_t)\right) - 1$$ **Logical Steps:** 1. Convert each HPR into a growth factor $(1 + R_t)$. 2. Multiply all growth factors (compounding across the whole horizon). 3. Take the $n^{th}$ root. 4. Subtract 1 to revert to a return form. **Strengths:** Reflects the *true* compounded annual growth; unbiased measure of *realized* performance. **Weaknesses:** Skewed downward for volatile series; cannot be computed if any $R_t \leq -100\%$. ## 2.4 The Approximation Identity (KTU Favourite) For small variance and moderate $n$: $$\bar{R}_G \approx \bar{R}_A - \frac{\sigma_R^2}{2}$$ This *approximation* (not an identity) explains *why* geometric averages are always less than or equal to arithmetic averages — the "**volatility drag**" is roughly half the variance. ## 2.5 Standard Deviation and Variance of Returns Total risk of the return series: $$\sigma_R^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(R_t - \bar{R}_A)^2 \quad \text{(sample)}$$ $$\sigma_R^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(R_t - \bar{R}_A)^2 \quad \text{(population)}$$ $$\sigma_R = \sqrt{\sigma_R^2}$$ ## 2.6 Risk-Adjusted AAR — The Sharpe Ratio > [!NOTE] > A return figure is meaningless without context on the risk taken to achieve it. The **Sharpe Ratio** normalizes AAR by its volatility (using risk-free rate $R_f$ as the benchmark): $$S = \frac{\bar{R}_A - R_f}{\sigma_R}$$ ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula Name | Equation | Use / When to Apply | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Holding Period Return | $HPR_t = (P_t - P_{t-1} + D_t) / P_{t-1}$ | Single-period gain incl. dividends | % | | 2 | Arithmetic Avg Return | $\bar{R}_A = \frac{1}{n}\sum R_t$ | Forecasting next period; unbiased mean | % | | 3 | Geometric Avg Return | $\bar{R}_G = \left[\prod(1+R_t)\right]^{1/n} - 1$ | Actual compounded growth; CAGR | % | | 4 | Log-form GAR | $\bar{R}_G = \exp\!\big(\frac{1}{n}\sum \ln(1+R_t)\big) - 1$ | Continuous-time modelling | % | | 5 | Volatility Drag Approx. | $\bar{R}_G \approx \bar{R}_A - \sigma_R^2/2$ | Small-$\sigma$ shortcut | % | | 6 | Variance of Returns | $\sigma_R^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum (R_t - \bar{R}_A)^2$ | Total risk measure | $\%^2$ | | 7 | Std. Deviation of Returns | $\sigma_R = \sqrt{\sigma_R^2}$ | Volatility / total risk | % | | 8 | Sharpe Ratio | $S = (\bar{R}_A - R_f)/\sigma_R$ | Risk-adjusted performance | Dimensionless | | 9 | Terminal Wealth | $W_n = W_0 \prod(1+R_t)$ | Portfolio valuation | Currency | | 10 | Time-Weighted Return | $\bar{R}_{TWR} = (1 + \bar{R}_{G,sub})\cdot \ldots$ | Performance without cash-flow bias | % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Economics Utility:** AAR formulas underpin **capital budgeting** decisions (NPV, IRR), **portfolio allocation** in project-financing portfolios, and **actuarial** risk premium calculations across B.Tech project-economics coursework. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Geometric Average Return from First Principles ### Step 1 — Define the Terminal Wealth Let an initial capital $W_0$ be invested across $n$ successive periods, with each period yielding a discrete return $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$. The wealth at the end of period 1: $$W_1 = W_0 (1 + R_1)$$ ### Step 2 — Roll Forward One Period at a Time The wealth at the end of period 2: $$W_2 = W_1 (1 + R_2) = W_0 (1 + R_1)(1 + R_2)$$ Continuing this chain to period $n$: $$W_n = W_0 (1 + R_1)(1 + R_2)\cdots(1 + R_n) = W_0 \prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + R_t)$$ ### Step 3 — Introduce a Constant Equivalent Rate $\bar{R}_G$ We seek a *single* constant annual return $\bar{R}_G$ that, if earned every year, would carry $W_0$ to the *same* terminal value $W_n$: $$W_n = W_0 (1 + \bar{R}_G)^n$$ ### Step 4 — Equate the Two Expressions for $W_n$ $$W_0 (1 + \bar{R}_G)^n = W_0 \prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + R_t)$$ Cancel $W_0$: $$(1 + \bar{R}_G)^n = \prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + R_t)$$ ### Step 5 — Take the $n^{th}$ Root $$1 + \bar{R}_G = \left[\prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + R_t)\right]^{1/n}$$ ### Step 6 — Isolate $\bar{R}_G$ $$\boxed{\;\bar{R}_G = \left[\prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + R_t)\right]^{1/n} - 1\;}$$ This is the canonical KTU-board answer for the Geometric Average Return. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Standard Style **Problem (KTU pattern):** An asset produced the following annual returns: Year 1 = +10%, Year 2 = −5%, Year 3 = +20%, Year 4 = +15%. Compute (a) Arithmetic Average, (b) Geometric Average, (c) Sample Standard Deviation, (d) Sharpe Ratio assuming $R_f = 4\%$. ### Part (a) — Arithmetic Average $$\bar{R}_A = \frac{0.10 + (-0.05) + 0.20 + 0.15}{4} = \frac{0.40}{4} = 0.10 = 10\%$$ ### Part (b) — Geometric Average $$\bar{R}_G = \left[(1.10)(0.95)(1.20)(1.15)\right]^{1/4} - 1$$ Compute inside product step by step: $$\begin{aligned} 1.10 \times 0.95 &= 1.0450 \\ 1.0450 \times 1.20 &= 1.2540 \\ 1.2540 \times 1.15 &= 1.4421 \end{aligned}$$ Take the fourth root: $$1.4421^{0.25} = 1.09597 \approx 1.0960$$ Therefore: $$\bar{R}_G = 1.0960 - 1 = 0.0960 = 9.60\%$$ ### Part (c) — Sample Standard Deviation Squared deviations from $\bar{R}_A = 0.10$: $$\begin{aligned} (0.10 - 0.10)^2 &= 0.000000 \\ (-0.05 - 0.10)^2 &= (-0.15)^2 = 0.022500 \\ (0.20 - 0.10)^2 &= (0.10)^2 = 0.010000 \\ (0.15 - 0.10)^2 &= (0.05)^2 = 0.002500 \end{aligned}$$ Sum of squared deviations: $$\sum (R_t - \bar{R}_A)^2 = 0 + 0.0225 + 0.0100 + 0.0025 = 0.0350$$ Sample variance (divide by $n-1 = 3$): $$\sigma_R^2 = \frac{0.0350}{3} = 0.011667 \;\text{or}\; 1.1667\%^2$$ Sample standard deviation: $$\sigma_R = \sqrt{0.011667} = 0.1080 = 10.80\%$$ ### Part (d) — Sharpe Ratio $$S = \frac{\bar{R}_A - R_f}{\sigma_R} = \frac{0.10 - 0.04}{0.1080} = \frac{0.06}{0.1080} = 0.5556$$ ## 3.3 Verification Using the Volatility-Drag Approximation $$\bar{R}_G \approx \bar{R}_A - \frac{\sigma_R^2}{2} = 0.10 - \frac{0.011667}{2} = 0.10 - 0.00583 = 0.0942 = 9.42\%$$ Actual GAR = 9.60%. Approximation error ≈ 0.18% — small, confirming the approximation's validity for moderate variance. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Correct AAR | Scenario | Use Arithmetic AAR ($\bar{R}_A$) | Use Geometric AAR ($\bar{R}_G$) | |---|---|---| | Forecasting next-year return | ✓ Preferred | ✗ | | Reporting actual historical wealth growth | ✗ | ✓ Preferred | | Summarizing performance of a buy-and-hold portfolio | ✗ | ✓ Preferred | | Estimating the population mean of returns | ✓ | ✗ | | Computing CAGR for the syllabus | ✗ | ✓ | | Aggregating returns with sub-period cash flows | ✗ | ✓ (as part of TWR) | | Comparing two assets with different volatility | Either, with care | Either, with care | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key insight for KTU answers:** The arithmetic average is the *first moment* of the distribution, while the geometric average is the *exponentiated first moment of log-returns*. They coincide only when volatility is zero. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Raw HPRs to Risk-Adjusted AAR ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Collect Periodic HPRs R1 R2 ... Rn] --> B{Decision Branch} B -- Equal weights, no cash flow --> C1[Arithmetic Average RA] B -- Buy and hold, true growth --> C2[Geometric Average RG] B -- Multiple sub periods with cash flows --> C3[Time Weighted Return TWR] C1 --> D1[Forecast next period performance] C2 --> D2[Report compounded CAGR] C3 --> D3[Performance attribution] C1 --> E[Compute Variance sigma squared] C2 --> E C3 --> E E --> F[Standard Deviation sigma R] F --> G[Sharpe Ratio S = RA minus Rf over sigma R] G --> H[Final Risk Adjusted Performance Ranking] D1 --> H D2 --> H D3 --> H ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Average Return Computation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER] A1[Price Series Pt] A2[Dividend Series Dt] end subgraph COMPUTE[COMPUTATION LAYER] B1[HPR Engine
R subscript t equals Pt minus Pt minus 1 plus Dt over Pt minus 1] B2[Arithmetic Engine
RA equals sum R sub t over n] B3[Geometric Engine
RG equals product of one plus R sub t to the one over n minus 1] B4[Risk Engine
sigma R squared equals sum squared deviations over n minus 1] end subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER] C1[Arithmetic AAR percent] C2[Geometric AAR CAGR percent] C3[Volatility sigma R percent] C4[Sharpe Ratio dimensionless] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B1 --> B3 B1 --> B4 B2 --> C1 B3 --> C2 B4 --> C3 B2 --> C4 B4 --> C4 ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchical Decomposition — Arithmetic vs Geometric Return Relationship ```mermaid flowchart TD root[Average Annual Return AAR] --> arith[Arithmetic AAR] root --> geom[Geometric AAR] root --> twr[Time Weighted AAR] arith --> a1[Simple Average of Period Returns] arith --> a2[Unbiased Estimator of Expected Return] geom --> g1[Compounded Growth Measure CAGR] geom --> g2[True Realized Annual Return] geom --> g3[Log Mean Identity RG equals exp of mean of ln one plus R minus one] twr --> t1[Chained Sub Period GARs] twr --> t2[Removes Cash Flow Bias] a1 -.Overstates in volatile series.-> g1 a2 -.Forecasting use only.-> g2 g3 -.Volatility drag equals RA minus sigma squared over two.-> a1 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]** > *Define Average Annual Return. Distinguish between Arithmetic Average Return and Geometric Average Return.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **1 mark — Definition:** Average Annual Return is a single percentage figure that summarises the per-year performance of an investment over a multi-period horizon. - **1 mark — Arithmetic AAR:** $\bar{R}_A = \frac{1}{n}\sum R_t$ — simple mean of period returns, ignores compounding, useful for forecasting. - **1 mark — Geometric AAR:** $\bar{R}_G = \big[\prod(1+R_t)\big]^{1/n} - 1$ — the constant rate that would have produced the same terminal wealth; equivalent to CAGR; always ≤ arithmetic AAR for volatile series. ### Question 2 (3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]** > *State and explain the Volatility Drag approximation relating arithmetic and geometric average returns.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **1 mark — Statement:** $\bar{R}_G \approx \bar{R}_A - \frac{\sigma_R^2}{2}$ - **1 mark — Explanation:** Variance in returns erodes compounded growth; on average, the geometric mean is *below* the arithmetic mean by approximately half the return variance. - **1 mark — Implication:** Higher volatility ⇒ wider gap between the two averages, making the arithmetic average a misleading indicator of realised growth. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Internal Choice ### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern]** > *(a)* The annual returns of two assets, Asset X and Asset Y, over five years are given below: | Year | Asset X Return | Asset Y Return | |---|---|---| | 1 | +18% | +12% | | 2 | −10% | +10% | | 3 | +22% | +9% | | 4 | −5% | +11% | | 5 | +15% | +8% | > *Compute the Arithmetic Average Return and Geometric Average Return (CAGR) for **Asset X**. (7 marks)* > *(b)* Compute the **standard deviation** of returns for **Asset X** and determine the **Sharpe Ratio** assuming a risk-free rate $R_f = 5\%$ per annum. State, with justification, which of the two assets is preferable on a risk-adjusted basis if $\sigma_Y = 4\%$. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Arithmetic Average Return of Asset X:** $$\bar{R}_{A,X} = \frac{0.18 + (-0.10) + 0.22 + (-0.05) + 0.15}{5} = \frac{0.40}{5} = 0.08 = 8.00\%$$ **Geometric Average Return (CAGR) of Asset X:** $$\bar{R}_{G,X} = [(1.18)(0.90)(1.22)(0.95)(1.15)]^{1/5} - 1$$ Step-by-step product: $$\begin{aligned} 1.18 \times 0.90 &= 1.0620 \\ 1.0620 \times 1.22 &= 1.29564 \\ 1.29564 \times 0.95 &= 1.23086 \\ 1.23086 \times 1.15 &= 1.41549 \end{aligned}$$ Fifth root: $$1.41549^{0.20} \approx 1.07194$$ Therefore: $$\bar{R}_{G,X} = 1.07194 - 1 = 0.07194 \approx 7.19\%$$ **[7 marks awarded: 2 marks for arithmetic, 5 marks for geometric with full step-by-step multiplication and root extraction]** #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Squared deviations of Asset X returns from $\bar{R}_{A,X} = 0.08$:** $$\begin{aligned} (0.18 - 0.08)^2 &= (0.10)^2 = 0.0100 \\ (-0.10 - 0.08)^2 &= (-0.18)^2 = 0.0324 \\ (0.22 - 0.08)^2 &= (0.14)^2 = 0.0196 \\ (-0.05 - 0.08)^2 &= (-0.13)^2 = 0.0169 \\ (0.15 - 0.08)^2 &= (0.07)^2 = 0.0049 \end{aligned}$$ Sum: $$\sum (R_t - \bar{R}_{A,X})^2 = 0.0100 + 0.0324 + 0.0196 + 0.0169 + 0.0049 = 0.0838$$ Sample variance: $$\sigma_X^2 = \frac{0.0838}{5 - 1} = \frac{0.0838}{4} = 0.02095$$ Sample standard deviation: $$\sigma_X = \sqrt{0.02095} = 0.14474 = 14.47\%$$ **Sharpe Ratio of Asset X:** $$S_X = \frac{\bar{R}_{A,X} - R_f}{\sigma_X} = \frac{0.08 - 0.05}{0.14474} = \frac{0.03}{0.14474} = 0.2073$$ **Sharpe Ratio of Asset Y** (arithmetic return supplied: $\bar{R}_{A,Y} = (0.12+0.10+0.09+0.11+0.08)/5 = 0.10 = 10\%$, $\sigma_Y = 4\% = 0.04$): $$S_Y = \frac{0.10 - 0.05}{0.04} = \frac{0.05}{0.04} = 1.25$$ **Comparison:** $S_Y = 1.25 \gg S_X = 0.21$. Asset Y delivers a higher risk-adjusted return; **Asset Y is preferable**. **[7 marks awarded: 3 marks for $\sigma_X$, 2 marks for Sharpe of X, 2 marks for comparison and justification]** --- ### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern]** > *(a)* An equity fund generated the following five annual returns: +25%, −15%, +30%, −10%, +20%. Compute the **Arithmetic** and **Geometric** average returns, and the **sample standard deviation**. (7 marks) > *(b)* A new asset has $\bar{R}_A = 12\%$ and $\sigma = 18\%$. The risk-free rate is $R_f = 6\%$. (i) Compute the Sharpe Ratio. (ii) Using the **volatility-drag approximation**, estimate the geometric average return of the new asset. (iii) Comment on the validity of the approximation. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Arithmetic Average Return:** $$\bar{R}_A = \frac{0.25 - 0.15 + 0.30 - 0.10 + 0.20}{5} = \frac{0.50}{5} = 0.10 = 10.00\%$$ **Geometric Average Return (CAGR):** $$\bar{R}_G = [(1.25)(0.85)(1.30)(0.90)(1.20)]^{1/5} - 1$$ Step-by-step product: $$\begin{aligned} 1.25 \times 0.85 &= 1.0625 \\ 1.0625 \times 1.30 &= 1.38125 \\ 1.38125 \times 0.90 &= 1.24313 \\ 1.24313 \times 1.20 &= 1.49175 \end{aligned}$$ Fifth root: $$1.49175^{0.20} \approx 1.08322$$ Therefore: $$\bar{R}_G = 1.08322 - 1 = 0.08322 \approx 8.32\%$$ **Sample Standard Deviation:** Squared deviations from $\bar{R}_A = 0.10$: $$\begin{aligned} (0.25 - 0.10)^2 &= 0.0225 \\ (-0.15 - 0.10)^2 &= 0.0625 \\ (0.30 - 0.10)^2 &= 0.0400 \\ (-0.10 - 0.10)^2 &= 0.0400 \\ (0.20 - 0.10)^2 &= 0.0100 \end{aligned}$$ Sum: $$\sum (R_t - \bar{R}_A)^2 = 0.0225 + 0.0625 + 0.0400 + 0.0400 + 0.0100 = 0.1750$$ Sample variance: $$\sigma^2 = \frac{0.1750}{4} = 0.04375$$ Sample standard deviation: $$\sigma = \sqrt{0.04375} = 0.2092 = 20.92\%$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **(i) Sharpe Ratio:** $$S = \frac{\bar{R}_A - R_f}{\sigma} = \frac{0.12 - 0.06}{0.18} = \frac{0.06}{0.18} = 0.3333$$ **(ii) Geometric Average (volatility-drag approximation):** $$\bar{R}_G \approx \bar{R}_A - \frac{\sigma^2}{2} = 0.12 - \frac{(0.18)^2}{2} = 0.12 - \frac{0.0324}{2} = 0.12 - 0.0162 = 0.1038 = 10.38\%$$ **(iii) Validity Comment:** The approximation is valid when $\sigma$ is *small* and the return distribution is approximately log-normal. With $\sigma = 18\%$, the approximation is reasonable for a first-order estimate but the true GAR (had it been computed directly) would be marginally lower, because the approximation is a Taylor expansion truncated to the second moment. For very high volatility or fat-tailed distributions, the approximation understates the volatility drag. **[7 marks awarded: 1 mark for Sharpe, 3 marks for GAR approximation with full expansion, 3 marks for validity discussion]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks in AAR problems:** > 1. **Forgetting the "−1"** at the end of the Geometric Mean formula — yields a *growth factor* not a *return*. Always subtract 1. > 2. **Mixing up sample ($n-1$) vs population ($n$) variance** — KTU almost always tests the *sample* version; using $n$ instead costs full marks in the standard deviation sub-part. > 3. **Not converting percentages to decimals** in formulas — writing $0.18$ as $18\%$ inside the product silently inflates answers by 100×. > 4. **Using the arithmetic mean as the CAGR** — examiners explicitly test whether you know the difference. Always state *which* average you are computing and *why*. > 5. **Omitting the interpretation step** — KTU Part B questions carry a final 1–2 mark "comment / justify" sub-part; stating the conclusion (e.g., "Asset Y is preferable as $S_Y > S_X$") without justification forfeits those marks. > 6. **Forgetting to annualize** when monthly/quarterly returns are given — if $n$ periods are sub-annual, multiply the resulting AAR by periods-per-year, or use $\bar{R}_{annual} = (1 + \bar{R}_{sub})^{m} - 1$ where $m$ is the compounding frequency. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Average Annual Returns** > - **HPR** is the atom of return analysis: $HPR_t = (P_t - P_{t-1} + D_t) / P_{t-1}$. > - **Arithmetic AAR** $\bar{R}_A$ = simple average; best for *forecasting* next-period returns. > - **Geometric AAR / CAGR** $\bar{R}_G = \big[\prod(1+R_t)\big]^{1/n} - 1$ = true compounded growth; always ≤ arithmetic. > - The **two coincide only** when there is zero variance across periods. > - **Volatility-drag identity:** $\bar{R}_G \approx \bar{R}_A - \sigma_R^2 / 2$ (a Taylor approximation, not exact). > - **Standard deviation** $\sigma_R$ measures total volatility; **variance** $\sigma_R^2$ enters the drag formula. > - **Sharpe Ratio** $S = (\bar{R}_A - R_f)/\sigma_R$ — the KTU-favourite risk-adjusted metric. > - **TWR** chains sub-period GARs; eliminates distortion from intermediate cash flows. > - **DWR / IRR** is the discount rate that sets NPV of cash flows to zero — *not* a pure AAR. > - In KTU answers, *always* declare which average you are computing, *show all steps*, and *interpret* the result. > - Use the *sample* standard deviation (divide by $n-1$) unless told otherwise.

topics.title: Estimation of Risk and Return of an Asset
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Risk and Return of an Asset

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is "Return" on an Asset?

**Return (R)** is the reward an investor earns for committing capital to an investment over a given time horizon. It is the *percentage change* in the value of an asset, including both capital appreciation and any income (dividends, interest) generated.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formal Definition:** *Return is the total gain or loss accrued to an investor from holding an asset over a specific period, expressed as a percentage of the initial investment.*

The generic mathematical representation is:

$$R = \frac{(P_1 - P_0) + D}{P_0}$$

where:
- $P_0$ = Initial price of the asset at time $t = 0$
- $P_1$ = Final price of the asset at time $t = 1$
- $D$ = Income (dividend/interest) received during the period

---

## 1.2 What is "Risk" of an Asset?

**Risk** is the uncertainty surrounding the actual return an asset will deliver relative to its expected return. It captures the *dispersion* (variability) of possible outcomes around the central tendency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** *Risk is the probability that the actual return from an investment will deviate from its expected return. Higher dispersion implies higher risk.*

Risk has **two fundamental dimensions**:
1. **Total Risk** = Systematic Risk + Unsystematic Risk
2. **Systematic (Market) Risk**: The portion of risk that cannot be eliminated by diversification (e.g., interest rate changes, inflation, recession).
3. **Unsystematic (Firm-Specific) Risk**: Risk unique to a particular firm or industry (e.g., management failure, product recall). This is *diversifiable*.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Fishing Trip"

Imagine you are a fisherman deciding where to cast your net:

- **Return** is the *size of the fish* you catch. The bigger the expected fish, the better the reward.
- **Risk** is the *wave size* on the sea. Calm seas (low risk) mean you almost certainly catch an average-sized fish. Stormy seas (high risk) mean you might catch a giant tuna — or come back empty-handed.
- **Expected Return** is the *average* fish size across many days, based on past weather patterns (probabilities).
- **Standard Deviation** is the *spread* of fish sizes — small spread = reliable, large spread = volatile.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Investors demand higher *expected returns* as compensation for taking on higher *risk*. This is the foundational trade-off in financial economics: **No risk, no reward.**

---

## 1.4 Two Sides of the Risk-Return Coin

| Concept | Symbol | What it Measures | Unit |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| Expected Return | $E(R)$ | Mean / central tendency of returns | **%** |
| Variance | $\sigma^2$ | Squared dispersion of returns | **%²** |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma$ | Volatility of returns | **%** |
| Beta | $\beta$ | Sensitivity to market movements | **Unitless** |
| Coefficient of Variation | $CV$ | Risk per unit of return | **Unitless** |

---

## 1.5 Why Estimation Matters

Estimation of risk and return is **Step Zero** in every financial decision:
- **Investors** use it to allocate capital across assets.
- **Portfolio Managers** use it to construct efficient portfolios.
- **Corporates** use it to set hurdle rates for projects.
- **Regulators** use it to evaluate capital adequacy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** This topic is a *direct carryover* from Module 2 (Time Value of Money) and forms the **numerical backbone** of Module 4 (CAPM & Portfolio Theory). Mastery here guarantees marks in those modules too.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Estimation Process

The estimation of risk and return follows a logical **5-step pipeline**:

1. **Identify Asset Universe** → Select the asset(s) under study.
2. **Collect Return Data** → Historical prices and income streams.
3. **Compute Expected Return** → Use probability-weighted average or historical mean.
4. **Compute Risk Metrics** → Variance, Standard Deviation, Beta, CV.
5. **Interpret & Decide** → Compare against benchmarks; make investment/portfolio decision.

---

## 2.2 Expected Return — Two Approaches

### Approach A: Probability-Weighted (Forward-Looking) Expected Return

Used when future scenarios and their likelihoods can be estimated (e.g., analyst forecasts).

$$E(R) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot R_i$$

where $P_i$ is the probability of state $i$ and $R_i$ is the return in state $i$.

### Approach B: Historical Mean (Backward-Looking) Expected Return

Used when past data is treated as a proxy for future behavior.

$$E(R) = \bar{R} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} R_t$$

where $R_t$ is the realized return in period $t$ and $n$ is the number of periods.

---

## 2.3 Risk Metrics — The Analytical Toolkit

### A) Variance of a Single Asset

$$\sigma^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot \big(R_i - E(R)\big)^2$$

(Probability-weighted version)

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{t=1}^{n} (R_t - \bar{R})^2$$

(Sample variance — used with historical data)

### B) Standard Deviation

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\sigma$ is the **primary** KTU-asked risk measure. Always quote risk in $\sigma$ units, not $\sigma^2$.

### C) Coefficient of Variation (CV)

$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{E(R)}$$

- **Purpose**: To compare risk *per unit of expected return* across assets with different expected returns.
- **Decision Rule**: Lower CV ⇒ Better risk-return trade-off.

### D) Covariance Between Two Assets (A and B)

$$\text{Cov}(R_A, R_B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot \big(R_{A,i} - E(R_A)\big) \cdot \big(R_{B,i} - E(R_B)\big)$$

- **Sign interpretation**:
  - $\text{Cov} > 0$ → Assets move *together*.
  - $\text{Cov} < 0$ → Assets move *oppositely* (diversification gold).

### E) Correlation Coefficient

$$\rho_{A,B} = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_A, R_B)}{\sigma_A \cdot \sigma_B}$$

- Range: $-1 \le \rho_{A,B} \le +1$
- $\rho = +1$ → Perfect positive co-movement.
- $\rho = -1$ → Perfect negative co-movement.
- $\rho = 0$ → Independent movements.

### F) Beta of an Asset

$$\beta_i = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2}$$

- $\beta > 1$ → Asset is *more volatile* than the market.
- $\beta < 1$ → Asset is *less volatile* than the market.
- $\beta < 0$ → Asset moves *against* the market (rare; e.g., gold).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table before every exam.** Every formula below is a guaranteed KTU question.

| # | Concept | Formula | When to Use |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Single-Period Return | $R = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_0) + D}{P_0}$ | Basic return computation |
| 2 | Expected Return (Probability) | $E(R) = \sum P_i R_i$ | When scenarios given |
| 3 | Expected Return (Historical) | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum R_t$ | When time series given |
| 4 | Variance (Probability) | $\sigma^2 = \sum P_i (R_i - E(R))^2$ | Discrete probability table |
| 5 | Variance (Historical) | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum (R_t - \bar{R})^2$ | Sample of past data |
| 6 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Always final reporting |
| 7 | Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \sigma / E(R)$ | Comparing two assets |
| 8 | Covariance (Probability) | $\text{Cov}_{A,B} = \sum P_i(R_{A,i}-E(R_A))(R_{B,i}-E(R_B))$ | Portfolio risk |
| 9 | Correlation | $\rho_{A,B} = \text{Cov}_{A,B} / (\sigma_A \sigma_B)$ | Standardized co-movement |
| 10 | Beta | $\beta_i = \text{Cov}(R_i, R_M) / \sigma_M^2$ | Systematic risk |
| 11 | Portfolio Return (2 assets) | $E(R_P) = w_A E(R_A) + w_B E(R_B)$ | Capital allocation |
| 12 | Portfolio Variance (2 assets) | $\sigma_P^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \text{Cov}_{A,B}$ | Risk of portfolio |

> **CRITICAL SYNTAX NOTE:** In the table above, absolute value or divisibility bars are written using $\vert$ (e.g., $\rho_{A,B} = \text{Cov}_{A,B} / (\sigma_A \sigma_B)$). In KTU exam sheets, *never* use the literal `|` inside a table row.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

Although this is a finance course, the *mathematical machinery* of risk-return estimation is identical to:
- **Reliability Engineering**: $\sigma$ in component life distributions.
- **Quality Control**: Standard deviation in process control charts.
- **Signal Processing**: Variance of noise in communication channels.
- **Civil Engineering**: Coefficient of variation of concrete strength.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners appreciate when students briefly mention such cross-disciplinary links in long-answer questions. It signals depth.

---

## 2.6 Logical "Why" Behind Each Metric

- **Why Expected Return?** → Because investors care about the *average* outcome.
- **Why Variance?** → Because outcomes deviate from the mean; we must penalize deviation.
- **Why Standard Deviation?** → Variance is in squared units (%²); $\sigma$ restores the original unit (%).
- **Why Covariance?** → To quantify how two assets *interact* — the foundation of diversification.
- **Why Beta?** → Because in a diversified portfolio, only the *non-diversifiable* (market) risk matters.

---

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Expected Return and Variance from a Probability Table

Consider an asset with the following scenario-based return distribution:

| State ($i$) | Probability ($P_i$) | Return ($R_i$) in % |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 (Boom) | 0.20 | **30** |
| 2 (Normal) | 0.50 | **12** |
| 3 (Recession) | 0.30 | **–10** |

### Step 1 — Compute Expected Return $E(R)$

We use the probability-weighted formula. Every intermediate term is shown.

$$E(R) = \sum_{i=1}^{3} P_i \cdot R_i$$

$$E(R) = (0.20 \times 30) + (0.50 \times 12) + (0.30 \times (-10))$$

$$E(R) = 6.00 + 6.00 + (-3.00)$$

$$\boxed{E(R) = 9.00\%}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Deviations $R_i - E(R)$

| State | $R_i$ | $E(R)$ | Deviation $R_i - E(R)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Boom | 30 | 9 | **+21** |
| Normal | 12 | 9 | **+3** |
| Recession | –10 | 9 | **–19** |

### Step 3 — Square the Deviations and Weight by Probability

| State | Deviation | Squared Deviation | $P_i \times (R_i - E(R))^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Boom | +21 | **441** | $0.20 \times 441 = 88.20$ |
| Normal | +3 | **9** | $0.50 \times 9 = 4.50$ |
| Recession | –19 | **361** | $0.30 \times 361 = 108.30$ |

### Step 4 — Sum to get Variance

$$\sigma^2 = \sum P_i (R_i - E(R))^2 = 88.20 + 4.50 + 108.30$$

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = 201.00 \, (\%^2)}$$

### Step 5 — Take Square Root to get Standard Deviation

$$\sigma = \sqrt{201.00}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma \approx 14.18\%}$$

### Step 6 — Compute Coefficient of Variation

$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{E(R)} = \frac{14.18}{9.00}$$

$$\boxed{CV \approx 1.576}$$

**Interpretation**: For every 1% of expected return, the investor bears ~1.58% of risk. A high CV indicates a relatively unattractive risk-return trade-off.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Expected Return and Variance from Historical Data

Suppose an asset yielded the following returns over 5 years: **+15%, +10%, –5%, +20%, +5%**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Mean (Historical Expected Return)

$$\bar{R} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} R_t = \frac{15 + 10 + (-5) + 20 + 5}{5}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{45}{5}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{R} = 9.00\%}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Deviations and Their Squares

| Year ($t$) | $R_t$ | $R_t - \bar{R}$ | $(R_t - \bar{R})^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 15 | **+6** | 36 |
| 2 | 10 | **+1** | 1 |
| 3 | –5 | **–14** | 196 |
| 4 | 20 | **+11** | 121 |
| 5 | 5 | **–4** | 16 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **370** |

### Step 3 — Compute Sample Variance (using $n-1$)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU convention: For historical/sample data, **always use $n-1$ in the denominator** (Bessel's correction). Use $n$ only when the data represents the entire population.

$$s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{t=1}^{n} (R_t - \bar{R})^2 = \frac{370}{5-1} = \frac{370}{4}$$

$$s^2 = 92.50 \, (\%^2)$$

### Step 4 — Compute Sample Standard Deviation

$$s = \sqrt{92.50}$$

$$\boxed{s \approx 9.62\%}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Portfolio of Two Assets

Suppose an investor splits capital **equally (50:50)** between Asset A and Asset B.

Given:
- $E(R_A) = 12\%$, $\sigma_A = 18\%$
- $E(R_B) = 8\%$, $\sigma_B = 10\%$
- $\text{Cov}(R_A, R_B) = 36 \, (\%^2)$

### Step 1 — Portfolio Expected Return

$$E(R_P) = w_A E(R_A) + w_B E(R_B)$$

$$E(R_P) = (0.50 \times 12) + (0.50 \times 8)$$

$$\boxed{E(R_P) = 10.00\%}$$

### Step 2 — Portfolio Variance

$$\sigma_P^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \, \text{Cov}_{A,B}$$

Substitute all values:

$$\sigma_P^2 = (0.50)^2 (18)^2 + (0.50)^2 (10)^2 + 2 (0.50)(0.50) (36)$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = (0.25 \times 324) + (0.25 \times 100) + (0.5 \times 36)$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = 81.00 + 25.00 + 18.00$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_P^2 = 124.00 \, (\%^2)}$$

### Step 3 — Portfolio Standard Deviation

$$\sigma_P = \sqrt{124.00} \approx 11.14\%$$

### Step 4 — Diversification Check (Counter-factual)

If the two assets were perfectly correlated ($\rho = 1$, i.e., $\text{Cov} = \sigma_A \sigma_B = 180$):

$$\sigma_P^2 = 81 + 25 + 180 = 286 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_P \approx 16.91\%$$

**Comparison**: True $\sigma_P = 11.14\% < 16.91\%$. This confirms that sub-perfect correlation generates **diversification benefit**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Beta of a Single Asset

Suppose for Asset X over 12 months, we have computed:
- $\text{Cov}(R_X, R_M) = 45 \, (\%^2)$
- $\sigma_M^2 = 100 \, (\%^2)$ (variance of market returns)

$$\beta_X = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_X, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2} = \frac{45}{100}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_X = 0.45}$$

**Interpretation**: Asset X is **less volatile** than the market; it moves only 45% as much as the market portfolio.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Risk Metric to Use?"

| Situation | Use | Why |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Single asset, scenarios given | $E(R)$ via $\sum P_i R_i$; $\sigma$ via probability formula | Direct application |
| Time-series of past returns | $\bar{R}$ and sample $\sigma$ (with $n-1$) | Bessel correction |
| Compare 2 stocks with different means | Coefficient of Variation | Normalizes for scale |
| Build 2-asset portfolio | Portfolio $E(R)$ and $\sigma_P$ formulas | Standard CAPM setup |
| Systematic (non-diversifiable) risk | Beta | Only this survives diversification |
| Negative covariance exists | Emphasize $\text{Cov}$ in portfolio $\sigma_P^2$ | Lowers portfolio risk |

---

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Risk-Return Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Identify Asset] --> B[Step B: Collect Return Data]
    B --> C{Data Type?}
    C -->|Scenarios with Probabilities| D[Step C1: Use Probability Formulas]
    C -->|Historical Time Series| E[Step C2: Use Sample Formulas]
    D --> F[Compute E of R]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Sigma Squared]
    G --> H[Compute Sigma]
    H --> I{Single or Portfolio?}
    I -->|Single Asset| J[Report E of R, Sigma, CV]
    I -->|Portfolio| K[Compute Covariance Matrix]
    K --> L[Compute Portfolio E of R]
    L --> M[Compute Portfolio Variance]
    M --> N[Compute Beta if Market Reference]
    N --> O[Step D: Interpret and Decide]
    J --> O
```

## 4.2 Risk Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Risk of Asset] --> B[Systematic Risk]
    A --> C[Unsystematic Risk]
    B --> B1[Macroeconomic Shocks]
    B --> B2[Interest Rate Changes]
    B --> B3[Inflation Risk]
    C --> C1[Firm Specific Events]
    C --> C2[Management Failure]
    C --> C3[Product Recall]
    B --> D[Measured by Beta]
    C --> E[Diversifiable]
    B --> F[Non Diversifiable]
```

## 4.3 Two-Asset Portfolio Risk Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input: Weight A, Weight B] --> Q1[Compute Portfolio E of R]
    P2[Input: Sigma A, Sigma B] --> Q2[Compute Individual Variances]
    P3[Input: Cov A B] --> Q3[Compute Cross Term]
    Q1 --> R[Combine via Portfolio Formula]
    Q2 --> Q4[Square of Weights Times Variances]
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
    R --> S[Output: Sigma P]
    S --> T{Compare with Weighted Sigma}
    T -->|Sigma P Smaller| U[Diversification Benefit Exists]
    T -->|Sigma P Equal| V[No Diversification Benefit]
```

## 4.4 Risk-Return Trade-Off Curve (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Risk Asset] --> A1[E of R Low]
    A --> A2[Sigma Small]
    B[High Risk Asset] --> B1[E of R High]
    B --> B2[Sigma Large]
    A1 --> C[Risk Return Trade off Curve]
    A2 --> C
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Investor Chooses Position Based on Risk Appetite]
```
