# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between Systematic Risk and Unsystematic Risk. Which of the two is diversifiable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Systematic Risk | Unsystematic Risk |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Source | Market-wide (macro) | Firm or industry-specific |
| Examples | Inflation, interest rates, recession | Manager incompetence, product recall |
| Diversifiable? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Measured by | Beta ($\beta$) | Residual standard deviation |
| Affected by diversification | Remains | Eliminated as portfolio grows |

Systematic risk arises from macroeconomic forces that affect *all* firms simultaneously and therefore cannot be removed through diversification. Unsystematic risk is unique to a particular firm or industry and *can* be eliminated by holding a well-diversified portfolio. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the Coefficient of Variation. Why is it preferred over standard deviation when comparing two assets with different expected returns?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** is defined as the ratio of standard deviation to expected return:

$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{E(R)}$$

It is a **dimensionless, scale-free** measure of risk per unit of expected return. When two assets have *different* expected returns, $\sigma$ alone is misleading because it does not account for the "payoff" being earned. CV adjusts for this by normalizing risk with respect to the mean return. Therefore, the asset with the **lower CV** offers a more attractive risk-return trade-off, even if its $\sigma$ is higher in absolute terms. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Provide a full working model answer.

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**The returns of Stock A under three economic conditions are as follows:**

| State | Probability | Return (%) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Boom | 0.25 | 40 |
| Normal | 0.50 | 15 |
| Recession | 0.25 | –10 |

**Similarly, for Stock B:**

| State | Probability | Return (%) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Boom | 0.25 | 25 |
| Normal | 0.50 | 12 |
| Recession | 0.25 | 5 |

**(a)** Compute the **expected return** and **standard deviation** of Stock A. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** If an investor forms a portfolio with **60% in Stock A and 40% in Stock B**, compute the **expected return**, **variance**, and **standard deviation** of the portfolio. (Assume the returns of A and B are independent.) **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Expected Return & Standard Deviation of Stock A

**Step 1: Expected Return of A**

$$E(R_A) = (0.25 \times 40) + (0.50 \times 15) + (0.25 \times (-10))$$

$$E(R_A) = 10 + 7.5 + (-2.5)$$

$$\boxed{E(R_A) = 15.00\%}$$

**Step 2: Deviations from the Mean**

| State | $R_i$ | $R_i - E(R_A)$ | $(R_i - E(R_A))^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Boom | 40 | **+25** | 625 |
| Normal | 15 | **0** | 0 |
| Recession | –10 | **–25** | 625 |

**Step 3: Variance of A**

$$\sigma_A^2 = (0.25 \times 625) + (0.50 \times 0) + (0.25 \times 625)$$

$$\sigma_A^2 = 156.25 + 0 + 156.25$$

$$\sigma_A^2 = 312.50 \, (\%^2)$$

**Step 4: Standard Deviation of A**

$$\sigma_A = \sqrt{312.50}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_A \approx 17.68\%}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Portfolio of A and B

**Step 1: Expected Return of Stock B**

$$E(R_B) = (0.25 \times 25) + (0.50 \times 12) + (0.25 \times 5)$$

$$E(R_B) = 6.25 + 6.00 + 1.25 = 13.50\%$$

**Step 2: Portfolio Expected Return**

$$E(R_P) = w_A E(R_A) + w_B E(R_B) = (0.60 \times 15) + (0.40 \times 13.50)$$

$$E(R_P) = 9.00 + 5.40$$

$$\boxed{E(R_P) = 14.40\%}$$

**Step 3: Variance of Stock B**

| State | $R_i$ | $R_i - E(R_B)$ | $(R_i - E(R_B))^2$ | Weighted |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Boom | 25 | +11.5 | 132.25 | 33.0625 |
| Normal | 12 | –1.5 | 2.25 | 1.1250 |
| Recession | 5 | –8.5 | 72.25 | 18.0625 |

$$\sigma_B^2 = 33.0625 + 1.1250 + 18.0625 = 52.25 \, (\%^2)$$

$$\sigma_B = \sqrt{52.25} \approx 7.23\%$$

**Step 4: Covariance of A and B**

For each state, compute $(R_{A,i} - E(R_A))(R_{B,i} - E(R_B))$, then weight by $P_i$ and sum.

| State | $R_{A,i} - E(R_A)$ | $R_{B,i} - E(R_B)$ | Product | Weight $P_i$ | Weighted |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Boom | +25 | +11.5 | 287.50 | 0.25 | 71.875 |
| Normal | 0 | –1.5 | 0 | 0.50 | 0 |
| Recession | –25 | –8.5 | 212.50 | 0.25 | 53.125 |

$$\text{Cov}(R_A, R_B) = 71.875 + 0 + 53.125 = 125.00$$

**Step 5: Portfolio Variance**

$$\sigma_P^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \text{Cov}_{A,B}$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = (0.60)^2(312.50) + (0.40)^2(52.25) + 2(0.60)(0.40)(125.00)$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = (0.36 \times 312.50) + (0.16 \times 52.25) + (0.48 \times 125.00)$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = 112.50 + 8.36 + 60.00$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_P^2 = 180.86 \, (\%^2)}$$

**Step 6: Portfolio Standard Deviation**

$$\sigma_P = \sqrt{180.86}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_P \approx 13.45\%}$$

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**The following are the monthly returns of two stocks, X and Y, for the last 6 months:**

| Month | Return X (%) | Return Y (%) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 5 | 8 |
| 2 | –3 | 4 |
| 3 | 10 | 6 |
| 4 | 2 | –2 |
| 5 | 6 | 10 |
| 6 | –4 | 5 |

**(a)** Calculate the **expected return** and **standard deviation** of each stock. Use sample formulas ($n-1$). **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the **correlation coefficient** between Stocks X and Y. Interpret the result. If a portfolio is formed with 50% in X and 50% in Y, what is the **portfolio standard deviation**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Mean and Standard Deviation

**Step 1: Mean of X**

$$\bar{R}_X = \frac{5 + (-3) + 10 + 2 + 6 + (-4)}{6} = \frac{16}{6} \approx 2.67\%$$

**Step 2: Deviations and Squared Deviations for X**

| Month | $R_X$ | $R_X - \bar{R}_X$ | $(R_X - \bar{R}_X)^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 5 | 2.33 | 5.4289 |
| 2 | –3 | –5.67 | 32.1489 |
| 3 | 10 | 7.33 | 53.7289 |
| 4 | 2 | –0.67 | 0.4489 |
| 5 | 6 | 3.33 | 11.0889 |
| 6 | –4 | –6.67 | 44.4889 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **147.3334** |

$$s_X^2 = \frac{147.3334}{6-1} = \frac{147.3334}{5} = 29.4667$$

$$\boxed{s_X \approx 5.43\%}$$

**Step 3: Mean of Y**

$$\bar{R}_Y = \frac{8 + 4 + 6 + (-2) + 10 + 5}{6} = \frac{31}{6} \approx 5.17\%$$

**Step 4: Deviations and Squared Deviations for Y**

| Month | $R_Y$ | $R_Y - \bar{R}_Y$ | $(R_Y - \bar{R}_Y)^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 8 | 2.83 | 8.0089 |
| 2 | 4 | –1.17 | 1.3689 |
| 3 | 6 | 0.83 | 0.6889 |
| 4 | –2 | –7.17 | 51.4089 |
| 5 | 10 | 4.83 | 23.3289 |
| 6 | 5 | –0.17 | 0.0289 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **84.8334** |

$$s_Y^2 = \frac{84.8334}{5} = 16.9667$$

$$\boxed{s_Y \approx 4.12\%}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Correlation and Portfolio Risk

**Step 1: Compute Cross-Product Deviations $(R_X - \bar{R}_X)(R_Y - \bar{R}_Y)$**

| Month | $R_X - \bar{R}_X$ | $R_Y - \bar{R}_Y$ | Product |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 2.33 | 2.83 | 6.5939 |
| 2 | –5.67 | –1.17 | 6.6339 |
| 3 | 7.33 | 0.83 | 6.0839 |
| 4 | –0.67 | –7.17 | 4.8039 |
| 5 | 3.33 | 4.83 | 16.0839 |
| 6 | –6.67 | –0.17 | 1.1339 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **41.3334** |

**Step 2: Sample Covariance**

$$\text{Cov}(X,Y) = \frac{41.3334}{6-1} = \frac{41.3334}{5} = 8.2667$$

**Step 3: Correlation Coefficient**

$$\rho_{X,Y} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{s_X \cdot s_Y} = \frac{8.2667}{5.43 \times 4.12}$$

$$\rho_{X,Y} = \frac{8.2667}{22.3716}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{X,Y} \approx 0.3695}$$

**Interpretation**: Stocks X and Y are **positively but weakly correlated**. They tend to move in the same direction, but the relationship is far from perfect. This implies there is *some scope for diversification*, but the benefit is limited.

**Step 4: Portfolio Standard Deviation (50:50)**

With $w_X = w_Y = 0.50$:

$$\sigma_P^2 = w_X^2 s_X^2 + w_Y^2 s_Y^2 + 2 w_X w_Y \text{Cov}(X,Y)$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = (0.25)(29.4667) + (0.25)(16.9667) + 2(0.25)(8.2667)$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = 7.3667 + 4.2417 + 4.1333 = 15.7417$$

$$\sigma_P = \sqrt{15.7417}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_P \approx 3.97\%}$$

**Counter-factual check:** If the two stocks were perfectly correlated ($\rho = 1$, $\text{Cov} = s_X \cdot s_Y = 22.37$):

$$\sigma_P^2 = 7.37 + 4.24 + 2(0.25)(22.37) = 22.79 \Rightarrow \sigma_P \approx 4.77\%$$

Our actual $\sigma_P = 3.97\%$ is lower → **diversification benefit confirmed**.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on This Topic (KTU Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Wrong denominator in variance:** Using $n$ instead of $n-1$ for *historical/sample* data loses 1 mark. Use $n$ *only* when the data represents the *entire population*.
> 2. **Forgetting to take the square root:** Reporting variance instead of standard deviation when the question asks for "risk". $\sigma \neq \sigma^2$.
> 3. **Misapplying probability formulas to time-series data:** Probability tables require $\sum P_i R_i$; time-series requires $\bar{R} = \frac{1}{n}\sum R_t$.
> 4. **Skipping the cross-term in portfolio variance:** Forgetting $2 w_A w_B \text{Cov}_{A,B}$ gives an incomplete and under-estimated portfolio risk.
> 5. **Not interpreting the answer:** KTU's outcome-based education demands a one-line *interpretation* (e.g., "Stock A is more volatile than B" or "There is a diversification benefit"). Skipping it costs 1 mark even if numbers are correct.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Return** = Capital gain + Income, all divided by initial price.
- **Expected Return** = Probability-weighted mean (forward) or sample mean (backward).
- **Variance** = Probability-weighted mean of *squared* deviations.
- **Standard Deviation** = $\sqrt{\text{Variance}}$ — always the *primary* risk measure.
- **CV** = $\sigma / E(R)$ — use when comparing assets with different expected returns.
- **Covariance** measures *direction* of co-movement; can be positive, negative, or zero.
- **Correlation** = Standardized covariance; bounded in $[-1, +1]$.
- **Beta** = $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_M) / \sigma_M^2$ — measures systematic risk only.
- **Total Risk = Systematic + Unsystematic**; only systematic risk is non-diversifiable.
- **Portfolio Expected Return** = Weighted average of individual expected returns.
- **Portfolio Variance** = Weighted variances + twice the weighted covariance (the *cross-term*).
- **Diversification benefit** exists whenever $\rho < 1$; greater when $\rho$ is low or negative.
- **Bessel's correction**: Use $n-1$ in the denominator for *sample* variance.
- **KTU favourite numbers**: $\sigma = 17.68\%$, $\rho = 0.37$, $\beta = 0.45$ — practice computing them.
- **Always end with an interpretation sentence** to satisfy OBE-style evaluation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Risk and Return of a Portfolio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Risk and Return of a Portfolio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Portfolio?

In the formal language of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Introduction to Financial Economics*, a **portfolio** is defined as a **collection of financial assets** (such as stocks, bonds, commodities, or derivatives) held jointly by an individual or institutional investor. The act of combining multiple assets into a single bundle is known as **portfolio construction**, and the analytical task of quantifying the *reward* earned and the *uncertainty* borne by holding that bundle is called the **measurement of portfolio risk and return**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A portfolio is a weighted combination of two or more assets. The weights $w_i$ represent the proportion of total capital invested in asset $i$, and the constraint $\sum w_i = 1$ must always be satisfied (no leverage assumption in the base model).

## 1.2 The Two Fundamental Quantities

| Quantity | Formal Symbol | Plain-English Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Expected Return** | $E(R_p)$ | The *average* or *mean* profit we anticipate from the portfolio, expressed as a percentage per period. |
| **Portfolio Risk** | $\sigma_p$ or $\sigma_p^2$ | The *spread* or *volatility* of possible returns around the expected value, capturing how much the actual outcome can deviate from our forecast. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Measure Them Together?**
> In financial economics, return and risk are inseparable. The famous dictum *"No risk, no reward"* translates mathematically: the higher the $\sigma_p$, the higher the $E(R_p)$ an investor must demand as compensation. KTU questions frequently test the **trade-off** between these two quantities.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Balanced Thali

Imagine a student in Kerala walking into the campus canteen and choosing a *thali* (a platter meal). If the entire thali were just one fiery chilli pickle, the experience would be extreme — either thrilling or disastrous, with little predictability. But the kitchen assembles **rice, dal, sambar, curd, and pickle** in measured proportions.

- The **taste reward** of the thali = the **expected portfolio return**.
- The **uncertainty that the meal upsets your stomach** = the **portfolio risk**.
- The **proportions of each dish** = the **portfolio weights** $w_i$.

Now notice something subtle: even if every single dish is *unpredictable* on its own, *combining* them in the right proportions produces a *predictable, pleasant* experience. This is the magic of **diversification** — the central insight that makes portfolio measurement a distinct topic from single-asset analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Takeaway:** You do not need to predict any single asset perfectly. By holding a *diversified* portfolio, the **average outcome becomes stable** even if individual outcomes are volatile.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Return on a Single Asset — Building Block

The **holding period return (HPR)** on a single asset over one period is composed of two streams:

$$HPR_i = \frac{(P_1 - P_0) + D}{P_0}$$

where $P_0$ and $P_1$ are the beginning and ending prices, and $D$ is any income (dividend or coupon) received during the period.

Because the *future* return is uncertain, we work with the **expected return**:

$$E(R_i) = \sum_{s=1}^{n} P_s \cdot R_{i,s}$$

where $P_s$ is the probability of state $s$ occurring and $R_{i,s}$ is the return on asset $i$ in that state.

## 2.2 Expected Return of a Portfolio

For a portfolio of $N$ assets with weights $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N$, the expected portfolio return is simply the **weighted average of individual expected returns**:

$$E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot E(R_i)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Unlike risk, the *expected return* of a portfolio is a **linear** function of weights. There is *no diversification benefit* on the return side — only on the risk side.

## 2.3 Risk of a Single Asset

The risk of a single asset is quantified by its **variance** $\sigma_i^2$ and **standard deviation** $\sigma_i$:

$$\sigma_i^2 = \sum_{s=1}^{n} P_s \cdot [R_{i,s} - E(R_i)]^2$$

$$\sigma_i = \sqrt{\sigma_i^2}$$

## 2.4 Covariance and Correlation — The "Bridge" Between Assets

When two assets are held together, their price movements *interact*. This interaction is captured by **covariance**:

$$Cov(R_1, R_2) = \sum_{s=1}^{n} P_s \cdot [R_{1,s} - E(R_1)] \cdot [R_{2,s} - E(R_2)]$$

The **standardised** version is the **correlation coefficient**, bounded between $-1$ and $+1$:

$$\rho_{1,2} = \frac{Cov(R_1, R_2)}{\sigma_1 \cdot \sigma_2} \quad \text{where} \quad -1 \le \rho \le +1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of $\rho$:**
> - $\rho = +1$ : assets move perfectly *together* → no diversification benefit.
> - $\rho = -1$ : assets move perfectly *opposite* → risk can be *eliminated* completely.
> - $\rho = 0$ : assets are *uncorrelated* → partial diversification benefit.
> - Most real-world stock pairs have $\rho$ between **0.2 and 0.7**.

The algebraic link between covariance, correlation, and standard deviations is:

$$Cov(R_1, R_2) = \rho_{1,2} \cdot \sigma_1 \cdot \sigma_2$$

## 2.5 Risk of a Two-Asset Portfolio — The Master Equation

For a portfolio consisting of just two assets with weights $w_1$ and $w_2 = 1 - w_1$, the portfolio variance is:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \, Cov(R_1, R_2)$$

Equivalently, using correlation:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \, \rho_{1,2} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$$

The portfolio standard deviation is the square root:

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\sigma_p^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Observation:** Because of the *cross-term* $2 w_1 w_2 \, Cov$, the portfolio standard deviation is *not* a weighted average of the individual standard deviations (unless $\rho = +1$).

## 2.6 Generalised Formula for an N-Asset Portfolio

For more than two assets, the variance generalises to a quadratic form:

$$\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_i w_j \, Cov(R_i, R_j)$$

This compact notation is a **matrix product**:

$$\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$$

where $\mathbf{w}$ is the $N \times 1$ weight vector and $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is the $N \times N$ covariance matrix whose diagonal contains individual variances and off-diagonal entries are pairwise covariances.

## 2.7 The Diversification Principle

The variance formula reveals a powerful engineering-style insight: the **cross-terms** (covariances) can be *positive* (reinforcing) or *negative* (cancelling). By combining assets whose returns are imperfectly correlated, an investor can construct a portfolio whose **total risk is less than the weighted average of the individual risks**. This is the **free lunch** of diversification.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Constraint / Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Holding Period Return | $HPR_i = \frac{(P_1 - P_0) + D}{P_0}$ | Expressed as a decimal or percentage |
| 2 | Expected Return (Asset) | $E(R_i) = \sum P_s \cdot R_{i,s}$ | $\sum P_s = 1$ |
| 3 | Expected Return (Portfolio) | $E(R_p) = \sum w_i E(R_i)$ | $\sum w_i = 1$; output in % |
| 4 | Variance (Asset) | $\sigma_i^2 = \sum P_s [R_{i,s} - E(R_i)]^2$ | Output in $(\%)^2$ |
| 5 | Covariance | $Cov_{1,2} = \sum P_s [R_{1,s} - E(R_1)][R_{2,s} - E(R_2)]$ | Can be positive, negative, or zero |
| 6 | Correlation | $\rho_{1,2} = \frac{Cov_{1,2}}{\sigma_1 \sigma_2}$ | $-1 \le \rho \le +1$ (dimensionless) |
| 7 | Covariance from $\rho$ | $Cov_{1,2} = \rho_{1,2} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ | Re-arrangement of (6) |
| 8 | Two-Asset Portfolio Variance | $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho_{1,2} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ | Requires $\rho$ value |
| 9 | N-Asset Portfolio Variance | $\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_i w_j \, Cov(R_i, R_j)$ | Double summation form |
| 10 | Portfolio Standard Deviation | $\sigma_p = \sqrt{\sigma_p^2}$ | Output in % (same units as $R$) |

## 2.9 Real-World Utility in Engineering Management

For KTU students entering industry, these formulas underpin:
- **Capital budgeting** decisions (which projects to accept in a firm's portfolio).
- **Robotics & reliability engineering** analogy — combining components with *low failure correlation* yields systems with much higher mean-time-to-failure, identical in spirit to financial diversification.
- **IT project portfolio management** — spreading risk across multiple parallel projects.
- **Personal finance** — building an EPF + equity + debt + gold portfolio.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Two-Asset Portfolio Variance from First Principles

We begin with two assets, $A$ and $B$, with random returns $R_A$ and $R_B$, weights $w_A$ and $w_B$ where $w_A + w_B = 1$. The portfolio return in any state is:

$$R_p = w_A R_A + w_B R_B$$

Taking the expected value:

$$E(R_p) = E(w_A R_A + w_B R_B) = w_A E(R_A) + w_B E(R_B)$$

This confirms linearity of the expected operator. Now, the variance of $R_p$ is, by definition:

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\left[ (R_p - E(R_p))^2 \right]$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\left[ \big( w_A R_A + w_B R_B - w_A E(R_A) - w_B E(R_B) \big)^2 \right]$$

Grouping terms by asset:

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\left[ \big( w_A (R_A - E(R_A)) + w_B (R_B - E(R_B)) \big)^2 \right]$$

Expanding the square (binomial expansion):

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\left[ w_A^2 (R_A - E(R_A))^2 + w_B^2 (R_B - E(R_B))^2 + 2 w_A w_B (R_A - E(R_A))(R_B - E(R_B)) \right]$$

By linearity of expectation, the expectation operator distributes over each term:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \, \underbrace{E[(R_A - E(R_A))^2]}_{\sigma_A^2} + w_B^2 \, \underbrace{E[(R_B - E(R_B))^2]}_{\sigma_B^2} + 2 w_A w_B \, \underbrace{E[(R_A - E(R_A))(R_B - E(R_B))]}_{Cov(R_A,R_B)}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \, Cov(R_A, R_B)}$$

Substituting $Cov(R_A, R_B) = \rho_{A,B} \sigma_A \sigma_B$ gives the correlation form stated earlier. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Two-Asset Portfolio (Conceptual Warm-Up)

**Problem Statement:** Consider an investor constructing a portfolio with the following two assets:

| Asset | Weight | Expected Return $E(R_i)$ | Standard Deviation $\sigma_i$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Equity Fund (E) | $0.60$ | $18\%$ | $22\%$ |
| Government Bond (B) | $0.40$ | $8\%$ | $6\%$ |
| **Correlation $\rho_{E,B}$** | | | **$+0.25$** |

**Step 1 — Expected Portfolio Return:**

$$E(R_p) = w_E E(R_E) + w_B E(R_B)$$

$$E(R_p) = (0.60)(18) + (0.40)(8)$$

$$E(R_p) = 10.8 + 3.2 = 14.0\%$$

**Step 2 — Covariance between Equity and Bond:**

$$Cov(R_E, R_B) = \rho_{E,B} \cdot \sigma_E \cdot \sigma_B$$

$$Cov(R_E, R_B) = (0.25)(22)(6) = 33.0 \, (\%^2)$$

**Step 3 — Portfolio Variance:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_E^2 \sigma_E^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_E w_B \, Cov(R_E, R_B)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.60)^2 (22)^2 + (0.40)^2 (6)^2 + 2(0.60)(0.40)(33.0)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.36)(484) + (0.16)(36) + 2(0.24)(33.0)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = 174.24 + 5.76 + 15.84$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = 195.84 \, (\%^2)$$

**Step 4 — Portfolio Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{195.84} \approx 13.99\%$$

**Step 5 — Comparison & Interpretation:**

The *weighted average* of individual risks would have been:
$$\bar{\sigma} = 0.60(22) + 0.40(6) = 13.2 + 2.4 = 15.6\%$$

The actual portfolio risk $\sigma_p = 13.99\%$ is **less than** this weighted average. The **diversification benefit** is:

$$\text{Benefit} = 15.6\% - 13.99\% = 1.61\%$$

This quantifies how much *free* risk reduction the investor gained simply by combining the two assets instead of holding them separately.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Three-Asset Portfolio (Board-Exam Style)

**Problem Statement:** A portfolio manager holds three assets with the following characteristics:

| Asset | Weight $w_i$ | Expected Return $E(R_i)$ | Standard Deviation $\sigma_i$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Asset X | $0.50$ | $20\%$ | $25\%$ |
| Asset Y | $0.30$ | $12\%$ | $15\%$ |
| Asset Z | $0.20$ | $6\%$ | $8\%$ |

Given the **pairwise covariances**:
- $Cov(X, Y) = 75$
- $Cov(X, Z) = 30$
- $Cov(Y, Z) = 20$

**Step 1 — Verify the Weight Constraint:**

$$\sum w_i = 0.50 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Expected Portfolio Return:**

$$E(R_p) = (0.50)(20) + (0.30)(12) + (0.20)(6)$$

$$E(R_p) = 10.0 + 3.6 + 1.2 = 14.8\%$$

**Step 3 — Portfolio Variance (using double-summation form):**

$$\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \sum_{j=1}^{3} w_i w_j \, Cov(R_i, R_j)$$

Unfolding the 9 terms systematically:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 &= w_X^2 \, \sigma_X^2 + w_Y^2 \, \sigma_Y^2 + w_Z^2 \, \sigma_Z^2 \\
&\quad + 2 w_X w_Y \, Cov(X,Y) + 2 w_X w_Z \, Cov(X,Z) + 2 w_Y w_Z \, Cov(Y,Z)
\end{aligned}$$

Note: when $i = j$, the term $Cov(R_i, R_i) = \sigma_i^2$. The factor of 2 in the cross-terms comes from the symmetric pairs $(i,j)$ and $(j,i)$ in the double sum.

**Step 4 — Numerical Substitution of Variances:**

The diagonal (variance) terms:

$$w_X^2 \sigma_X^2 = (0.50)^2 (25)^2 = (0.25)(625) = 156.25$$

$$w_Y^2 \sigma_Y^2 = (0.30)^2 (15)^2 = (0.09)(225) = 20.25$$

$$w_Z^2 \sigma_Z^2 = (0.20)^2 (8)^2 = (0.04)(64) = 2.56$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Substitution of Cross Terms:**

$$2 w_X w_Y \, Cov(X,Y) = 2(0.50)(0.30)(75) = (0.30)(75) = 22.50$$

$$2 w_X w_Z \, Cov(X,Z) = 2(0.50)(0.20)(30) = (0.20)(30) = 6.00$$

$$2 w_Y w_Z \, Cov(Y,Z) = 2(0.30)(0.20)(20) = (0.12)(20) = 2.40$$

**Step 6 — Summation of All Six Unique Terms:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = 156.25 + 20.25 + 2.56 + 22.50 + 6.00 + 2.40$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = 209.96 \, (\%^2)$$

**Step 7 — Portfolio Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{209.96} \approx 14.49\%$$

**Step 8 — Comparison:**

- Expected return: $14.8\%$
- Risk (portfolio $\sigma$): $14.49\%$
- Weighted average of individual standard deviations:
  $$\bar{\sigma} = (0.50)(25) + (0.30)(15) + (0.20)(8) = 12.5 + 4.5 + 1.6 = 18.6\%$$
- **Diversification benefit:** $18.6\% - 14.49\% = 4.11\%$ (a substantial reduction)

## 3.4 Edge-Case Analysis — Special Correlation Values

| Case | Correlation $\rho$ | Effect on $\sigma_p^2$ | Interpretation |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Perfect Positive | $\rho = +1$ | $\sigma_p^2 = (w_1 \sigma_1 + w_2 \sigma_2)^2$ | No diversification; $\sigma_p$ is a true weighted average. |
| Zero Correlation | $\rho = 0$ | $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2$ | Cross-term vanishes; partial benefit. |
| Perfect Negative | $\rho = -1$ | $\sigma_p^2 = (w_1 \sigma_1 - w_2 \sigma_2)^2$ | Risk can drop to **zero** if $w_1 \sigma_1 = w_2 \sigma_2$. |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If a KTU question gives you a correlation of $-1$ or $+1$, do **not** blindly use the general formula. The standard deviation simplifies to a linear expression, and calculations become trivial.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Portfolio Risk & Return Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Portfolio Inputs] --> B[Identify all N assets in the portfolio]
    B --> C[Assign weights w_i such that sum of w_i equals 1]
    C --> D[Compute individual expected returns E of R_i]
    D --> E[Compute individual standard deviations sigma_i]
    E --> F[Compute pairwise covariances Cov R_i R_j]
    F --> G{Choose Formula Type}
    G -->|Two Assets| H[Use Two Asset Variance Equation]
    G -->|N Assets| I[Use Double Summation Form]
    H --> J[Calculate Expected Portfolio Return E of R_p]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Calculate Portfolio Variance sigma_p squared]
    K --> L[Take square root to obtain sigma_p]
    L --> M[Compare with weighted average of individual sigma]
    M --> N{Diversification Benefit Exists?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Quantify benefit as weighted sigma minus sigma_p]
    N -->|No| P[Report that no benefit exists correlation is plus 1]
    O --> Q[Output: Final Risk Return Profile]
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The Diversification Effect Across Correlation Values

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Asset 1: sigma equals 20 percent] --> C[Combine in Portfolio]
    A2[Asset 2: sigma equals 15 percent] --> C
    C --> D1[If rho equals plus 1: portfolio sigma equals weighted sum equals 17.5 percent]
    C --> D2[If rho equals 0: portfolio sigma reduces to about 12.5 percent]
    C --> D3[If rho equals minus 1: portfolio sigma can be as low as 5 percent]
    D1 --> E[No Diversification Benefit]
    D2 --> F[Moderate Diversification]
    D3 --> G[Maximum Diversification Possible]
```

## 4.3 Diagram — Decomposition of Portfolio Variance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Portfolio Variance sigma_p squared] --> B[Diagonal Terms: Sum of w_i squared times sigma_i squared]
    A --> C[Off Diagonal Cross Terms: 2 times w_i w_j Cov i j]
    B --> D[Captures individual asset risk contributions]
    C --> E[Captures interaction effects between pairs]
    D --> F[Total sigma_p squared]
    E --> F
    F --> G[sigma_p equals square root of total]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The three flowcharts above collectively illustrate that portfolio risk is **not** simply the weighted average of individual risks. The cross-terms (covariances) are the *engine* of diversification. When covariances are *low or negative*, the cross-terms shrink, dragging $\sigma_p$ below the weighted average — this is the geometric intuition behind the numerical results of Worked Example 1 and 2.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the terms "expected return of a portfolio" and "portfolio risk". Mention the statistical measures used to quantify them.**

**Model Answer:**

The **expected return of a portfolio** is the weighted average of the expected returns of the individual assets in the portfolio, where the weights represent the proportion of total investment in each asset. Mathematically, $E(R_p) = \sum w_i E(R_i)$ subject to $\sum w_i = 1$.

**Portfolio risk** is the uncertainty associated with the actual portfolio return deviating from its expected value. It is quantified by the **variance** $\sigma_p^2$ or its square root, the **standard deviation** $\sigma_p$.

In the two-asset case, the risk formula incorporates the **covariance** (or equivalently the **correlation coefficient** $\rho$) between the two assets. The full measure is:
$$\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \, Cov(R_1, R_2)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only "standard deviation" and forget that **variance** is the *fundamental* measure. Always mention **both** for full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of "diversification" in the context of portfolio management. How is the diversification benefit quantified?**

**Model Answer:**

**Diversification** is the strategy of combining assets with imperfectly correlated returns into a single portfolio so as to **reduce the total risk** without necessarily sacrificing expected return.

Mathematically, when two assets are combined, the cross-term $2 w_1 w_2 \, Cov(R_1, R_2)$ in the portfolio variance formula is generally **less** than the corresponding term when each asset is held in isolation weighted by its variance. As a result:

$$\sigma_p < w_1 \sigma_1 + w_2 \sigma_2$$

The **diversification benefit** is quantified as the difference between the weighted average of individual standard deviations and the actual portfolio standard deviation:

$$\text{Benefit} = \sum w_i \sigma_i - \sigma_p$$

The benefit is **maximised** when $\rho = -1$ (perfect negative correlation) and is **zero** when $\rho = +1$ (perfect positive correlation).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A portfolio consists of two assets, A and B, with the following data:

| Parameter | Asset A | Asset B |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Weight | $0.40$ | $0.60$ |
| Expected Return | $15\%$ | $9\%$ |
| Standard Deviation | $18\%$ | $10\%$ |

The correlation coefficient between A and B is $\rho_{A,B} = 0.30$. Calculate:
1. The expected return of the portfolio.
2. The covariance between A and B.
3. The variance and standard deviation of the portfolio.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **diversification principle** in detail. Using the above result, demonstrate that the portfolio risk is less than the weighted average of individual risks, and compute the diversification benefit in percentage points.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Quantitative Computation:**

**Step 1: Expected Portfolio Return**

$$E(R_p) = w_A E(R_A) + w_B E(R_B) = (0.40)(15) + (0.60)(9) = 6.0 + 5.4 = 11.4\%$$

**Step 2: Covariance**

$$Cov(R_A, R_B) = \rho_{A,B} \cdot \sigma_A \cdot \sigma_B = (0.30)(18)(10) = 54.0 \, (\%^2)$$

**Step 3: Portfolio Variance**

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \, Cov(R_A, R_B)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.40)^2(18)^2 + (0.60)^2(10)^2 + 2(0.40)(0.60)(54.0)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.16)(324) + (0.36)(100) + (0.48)(54.0)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = 51.84 + 36.00 + 25.92 = 113.76 \, (\%^2)$$

**Step 4: Portfolio Standard Deviation**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{113.76} \approx 10.67\%$$

**Part (b) — Diversification Principle:**

The **diversification principle** states that combining assets whose returns are imperfectly correlated (i.e., $\rho < +1$) into a single portfolio reduces the total risk below the weighted average of individual risks. This is because the cross-term $2 w_A w_B \, Cov(R_A, R_B)$ in the variance equation is smaller than what it would be if the assets were perfectly positively correlated.

**Step 5: Weighted Average of Individual Standard Deviations**

$$\bar{\sigma} = w_A \sigma_A + w_B \sigma_B = (0.40)(18) + (0.60)(10) = 7.2 + 6.0 = 13.2\%$$

**Step 6: Diversification Benefit**

$$\text{Benefit} = \bar{\sigma} - \sigma_p = 13.2\% - 10.67\% = 2.53 \text{ percentage points}$$

**Conclusion:** The portfolio's risk ($10.67\%$) is approximately $2.53$ percentage points **lower** than the weighted average of individual risks ($13.2\%$), even though the expected return ($11.4\%$) is simply the weighted average of individual expected returns. This clearly demonstrates the diversification benefit.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, 2024 Scheme]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** An investor holds three assets X, Y, and Z with the following details:

| Asset | Weight | Expected Return | Standard Deviation |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| X | $0.50$ | $20\%$ | $25\%$ |
| Y | $0.30$ | $12\%$ | $15\%$ |
| Z | $0.20$ | $6\%$ | $8\%$ |

Given covariances: $Cov(X,Y) = 75$, $Cov(X,Z) = 30$, $Cov(Y,Z) = 20$. Compute the expected return, variance, and standard deviation of the portfolio.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define the **covariance matrix** for a three-asset portfolio. Show that the portfolio variance can be expressed as $\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$. Why is this matrix formulation useful in practice?

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Three-Asset Computation:**

**Step 1: Expected Portfolio Return**

$$E(R_p) = w_X E(R_X) + w_Y E(R_Y) + w_Z E(R_Z) = (0.50)(20) + (0.30)(12) + (0.20)(6) = 10.0 + 3.6 + 1.2 = 14.8\%$$

**Step 2: Diagonal Variance Terms**

$$w_X^2 \sigma_X^2 = (0.50)^2 (25)^2 = (0.25)(625) = 156.25$$

$$w_Y^2 \sigma_Y^2 = (0.30)^2 (15)^2 = (0.09)(225) = 20.25$$

$$w_Z^2 \sigma_Z^2 = (0.20)^2 (8)^2 = (0.04)(64) = 2.56$$

**Step 3: Off-Diagonal Cross Terms (multiplied by 2 for symmetry)**

$$2 w_X w_Y \, Cov(X,Y) = 2(0.50)(0.30)(75) = 22.50$$

$$2 w_X w_Z \, Cov(X,Z) = 2(0.50)(0.20)(30) = 6.00$$

$$2 w_Y w_Z \, Cov(Y,Z) = 2(0.30)(0.20)(20) = 2.40$$

**Step 4: Portfolio Variance**

$$\sigma_p^2 = 156.25 + 20.25 + 2.56 + 22.50 + 6.00 + 2.40 = 209.96 \, (\%^2)$$

**Step 5: Portfolio Standard Deviation**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{209.96} \approx 14.49\%$$

**Part (b) — Covariance Matrix Formulation:**

For three assets, the **covariance matrix** $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is a $3 \times 3$ symmetric matrix:

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_X^2 & Cov(X,Y) & Cov(X,Z) \\ Cov(X,Y) & \sigma_Y^2 & Cov(Y,Z) \\ Cov(X,Z) & Cov(Y,Z) & \sigma_Z^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

The weight vector is:

$$\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} w_X \\ w_Y \\ w_Z \end{bmatrix}$$

The matrix product $\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$ expands as:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} &= \begin{bmatrix} w_X & w_Y & w_Z \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_X^2 & Cov(X,Y) & Cov(X,Z) \\ Cov(X,Y) & \sigma_Y^2 & Cov(Y,Z) \\ Cov(X,Z) & Cov(Y,Z) & \sigma_Z^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} w_X \\ w_Y \\ w_Z \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

This, when fully expanded, yields exactly the same expression as the double summation $\sum_{i=1}^{3} \sum_{j=1}^{3} w_i w_j \, Cov(R_i, R_j)$, and hence equals $\sigma_p^2$.

**Practical Utility:** The matrix form is invaluable in computational finance because:
1. It scales seamlessly to portfolios of **hundreds of assets** (modern portfolios have 50–500+ securities).
2. Software libraries (e.g., MATLAB, Python's NumPy) compute $\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$ in a single line, replacing thousands of manual terms.
3. **Optimisation problems** (e.g., Markowitz mean-variance optimisation) become solvable via standard linear-algebra techniques.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in cross-terms. The cross-term is $2 w_i w_j \, Cov(R_i, R_j)$, **not** $w_i w_j \, Cov(R_i, R_j)$. Students routinely lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Mixing up variance and standard deviation.** $\sigma_p^2$ and $\sigma_p$ are different — the question may ask for either or both.
> 3. **Forgetting to verify $\sum w_i = 1$.** If the given weights do not sum to 1, mention it explicitly and *do not* proceed silently.
> 4. **Not squaring the standard deviation** in the variance equation. The variance equation uses $\sigma^2$, not $\sigma$.
> 5. **Reporting units inconsistently.** Use either decimal form ($0.114$) or percentage form ($11.4\%$) but remain consistent.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Portfolio** = weighted combination of assets, with the constraint $\sum w_i = 1$.
- **Expected portfolio return** is a **linear weighted average**: $E(R_p) = \sum w_i E(R_i)$.
- **Portfolio risk** is a **quadratic function** of weights, NOT a simple average — because of covariance cross-terms.
- **Two-asset variance formula:** $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \, \rho_{1,2} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$.
- **N-asset variance formula:** $\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_i w_j \, Cov(R_i, R_j)$, equivalent to $\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$.
- **Covariance** can be **positive, negative, or zero**; **correlation** is bounded $-1 \le \rho \le +1$.
- **Covariance ↔ Correlation link:** $Cov_{1,2} = \rho_{1,2} \cdot \sigma_1 \cdot \sigma_2$.
- **Diversification benefit** = $\sum w_i \sigma_i - \sigma_p$; exists whenever $\rho < +1$.
- **Special cases:** $\rho = +1 \Rightarrow$ no benefit; $\rho = -1 \Rightarrow$ maximum benefit (risk can be zeroed).
- **Always** verify that the portfolio weights sum to 1 before applying formulas.
- **Always** remember the factor of **2** in the cross-term of the two-asset variance equation.
- **The expected return formula is linear, but the risk formula is not** — this asymmetry is the foundation of Modern Portfolio Theory (Markowitz, 1952).
- The portfolio standard deviation is the **square root** of the portfolio variance, not the weighted sum of individual standard deviations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk-Return Trade Off. Effect of Diversification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIIT834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Risk-Return Trade Off. Effect of Diversification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Risk-Return Trade Off — Formal Definition

The **Risk-Return Trade Off** is the fundamental financial economics principle that establishes a direct, monotonic relationship between the level of risk assumed by an investor and the expected (potential) return earned on an investment. In equilibrium, rational, risk-averse investors will only accept higher risk if they are compensated with a proportionally higher expected return.

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \text{Risk Premium}_i$$

Where:
- $E(R_i)$ = Expected return on asset $i$
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate of return (e.g., Government Treasury Bill yield)
- $\text{Risk Premium}_i$ = Additional return demanded for bearing the risk of asset $i$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> "The Risk-Return Trade Off expresses the relationship between the risk of an investment and its expected return. Higher risk is associated with higher expected return, while lower risk corresponds to lower expected return. Investors must evaluate their risk tolerance to optimize portfolio construction."

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Mountain Climbing Story

> [!NOTE] **Conceptual Analogy**
> Imagine two mountain trails. **Trail A** is a flat, paved highway (low risk) — it takes you slowly but safely to a small hilltop (low return). **Trail B** is a steep, rocky, unmarked cliff face (high risk) — if you reach the summit, the view is breathtaking (high return), but a single slip means you fall. A rational climber chooses the trail based on how much risk they can tolerate. **This is exactly how investors behave in financial markets.**

### 1.3 Risk — Definition and Classification

**Risk** is the uncertainty associated with the actual future return of an investment deviating from its expected return. It is mathematically quantified using **standard deviation ($\sigma$)** and **variance ($\sigma^2$)**.

#### Classification of Risk

| Risk Type | Description | Diversifiable? | Example |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|---------|
| **Systematic Risk** (Market Risk) | Affects the entire market; caused by macroeconomic factors | **No** | Interest rate change, GDP contraction, war |
| **Unsystematic Risk** (Specific Risk) | Affects a single firm or industry | **Yes** | Managerial fraud, product recall, factory fire |

**Total Risk Equation:**
$$\sigma_{\text{Total}}^2 = \sigma_{\text{Systematic}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{Unsystematic}}^2$$

### 1.4 Return — Definition and Components

**Return** is the total gain or loss on an investment over a holding period, expressed as a percentage of the initial investment.

**Holding Period Return (HPR):**
$$R = \frac{P_1 - P_0 + D}{P_0} = \frac{\text{Capital Gain} + \text{Dividend}}{\text{Initial Price}}$$

**Expected Return:**
$$E(R) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot R_i$$

Where $p_i$ = probability of state $i$, $R_i$ = return in state $i$, $\sum p_i = 1$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Risk-Return Trade Off — Theoretical Framework

The trade-off rests on three pillars of investor behavior:

1. **Risk Aversion:** Most investors are risk-averse — they prefer certainty over uncertainty with the same expected outcome.
2. **Marginal Utility of Wealth Decreases:** An extra ₹1,000 means less to a rich person than to a poor person; similarly, a ₹10,000 loss hurts more than a ₹10,000 gain pleases.
3. **Compensation Principle:** Risk-averse investors demand a **Risk Premium** to compensate for bearing additional risk.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) Connection**
> The formal quantification of the Risk-Return Trade Off is given by CAPM:
> $$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$$
> Here, $\beta_i$ measures systematic risk, and $[E(R_m) - R_f]$ is the **Market Risk Premium**.

### 2.2 Why Diversification Works — Theoretical Foundation

**Diversification** is the practice of spreading investments across multiple assets, industries, and geographies to reduce portfolio risk without sacrificing expected returns.

**Markowitz's Insight (1952):** Combining assets whose returns are not perfectly positively correlated reduces portfolio variance without proportionally reducing expected return.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $R = (P_1 - P_0 + D) / P_0$ | Holding Period Return | Single-period return calculation |
| 2 | $E(R) = \sum p_i R_i$ | Expected Return | Weighted average of state returns |
| 3 | $\sigma^2 = \sum p_i [R_i - E(R)]^2$ | Variance | Dispersion of returns |
| 4 | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Standard Deviation | Absolute risk measure |
| 5 | $CV = \sigma / E(R)$ | Coefficient of Variation | Relative risk per unit of return |
| 6 | $Cov(i,j) = \rho_{ij} \sigma_i \sigma_j$ | Covariance | Co-movement between assets |
| 7 | $\rho_{ij} \in [-1, +1]$ | Correlation Coefficient | Ranges from $-1$ (perfect negative) to $+1$ (perfect positive) |
| 8 | $E(R_p) = \sum w_i E(R_i)$ | Portfolio Expected Return | Linear combination of asset weights |
| 9 | $\sigma_p^2 = \sum w_i^2 \sigma_i^2 + \sum \sum_{i \neq j} w_i w_j \sigma_i \sigma_j \rho_{ij}$ | Portfolio Variance (Multi-asset) | Includes all pairwise covariances |
| 10 | $\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$ | Two-Asset Portfolio Variance | Most commonly tested form |
| 11 | $\beta_i = Cov(R_i, R_m) / \sigma_m^2$ | Beta | Systematic risk measure |
| 12 | $\sigma_p^2 \to \sigma_{\text{systematic}}^2$ | Limit of Diversification | As $N \to \infty$, only market risk remains |

> [!NOTE] **Critical Exam Tip**
> When correlation $\rho = -1$, complete risk elimination is theoretically possible. When $\rho = +1$, no diversification benefit exists. Most real-world stocks have $\rho \in [0.3, 0.7]$.

### 2.4 Effect of Increasing Number of Securities

As the number of securities $N$ in a portfolio increases:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^2 \sigma_i^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_i w_j \sigma_i \sigma_j \rho_{ij}$$

**Key Observation:**
- For equal weights $w_i = 1/N$, the **variance terms decrease** as $\sum (1/N^2) \sigma_i^2 \to 0$ when $N \to \infty$ (assuming bounded $\sigma_i$).
- The **covariance terms persist** because they represent systematic co-movements.

**Engineering Analogy:** This is analogous to **signal averaging** in electronics — averaging multiple noisy signals (uncorrelated noise) cancels individual fluctuations but retains the common (systematic) signal component.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Two-Asset Portfolio Expected Return

**Given:** Two assets A and B with weights $w_A$ and $w_B$ where $w_A + w_B = 1$.

**Step 1:** Define portfolio return as weighted average of individual returns.
$$R_p = w_A R_A + w_B R_B$$

**Step 2:** Take expectation of both sides (weights are deterministic).
$$E(R_p) = E(w_A R_A + w_B R_B)$$

**Step 3:** Apply linearity of expectation operator.
$$E(R_p) = w_A E(R_A) + w_B E(R_B)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $w_B = 1 - w_A$ (since weights sum to 1).
$$E(R_p) = w_A E(R_A) + (1 - w_A) E(R_B)$$

$$\boxed{E(R_p) = w_A E(R_A) + w_B E(R_B)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Two-Asset Portfolio Variance

**Given:** $R_p = w_A R_A + w_B R_B$ and $\sigma_p^2 = E[R_p - E(R_p)]^2$.

**Step 1:** Express deviation from mean.
$$R_p - E(R_p) = w_A [R_A - E(R_A)] + w_B [R_B - E(R_B)]$$

**Step 2:** Square both sides.
$$\begin{aligned}
[R_p - E(R_p)]^2 = &\ w_A^2 [R_A - E(R_A)]^2 \\
& + w_B^2 [R_B - E(R_B)]^2 \\
& + 2 w_A w_B [R_A - E(R_A)][R_B - E(R_B)]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Take expectation of both sides.
$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 = &\ w_A^2 E\{[R_A - E(R_A)]^2\} \\
& + w_B^2 E\{[R_B - E(R_B)]^2\} \\
& + 2 w_A w_B E\{[R_A - E(R_A)][R_B - E(R_B)]\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Identify each term.
- $E\{[R_A - E(R_A)]^2\} = \sigma_A^2$
- $E\{[R_B - E(R_B)]^2\} = \sigma_B^2$
- $E\{[R_A - E(R_A)][R_B - E(R_B)]\} = Cov(R_A, R_B) = \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$

**Step 5:** Substitute and finalize.
$$\boxed{\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Exam Style

**Problem:** Consider a two-stock portfolio:
- Stock A: $E(R_A) = 12\%$, $\sigma_A = 20\%$, $w_A = 0.60$
- Stock B: $E(R_B) = 18\%$, $\sigma_B = 30\%$, $w_B = 0.40$
- Correlation: $\rho_{AB} = 0.30$

**Find:** (i) Expected portfolio return, (ii) Portfolio standard deviation.

**Solution (i) — Expected Return:**
$$E(R_p) = (0.60)(12\%) + (0.40)(18\%)$$
$$E(R_p) = 7.2\% + 7.2\% = 14.4\%$$

**Solution (ii) — Portfolio Variance:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 = &\ (0.60)^2 (0.20)^2 + (0.40)^2 (0.30)^2 \\
& + 2(0.60)(0.40)(0.30)(0.20)(0.30)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 = &\ (0.36)(0.04) + (0.16)(0.09) \\
& + (0.48)(0.30)(0.06)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 = &\ 0.0144 + 0.0144 + 0.00864 \\
\sigma_p^2 = &\ 0.03744
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.03744} = 0.1935 = 19.35\%$$

**Comparative Analysis:**

| Metric | Stock A alone | Stock B alone | Portfolio |
|--------|---------------|---------------|-----------|
| Expected Return | 12.00% | 18.00% | **14.40%** |
| Standard Deviation | 20.00% | 30.00% | **19.35%** |

**Interpretation:** The portfolio has higher return than Stock A (12%) with lower risk than Stock B (30%). **Diversification benefit realized.**

### 3.4 Effect of Correlation on Portfolio Risk — Boundary Cases

**Case 1: $\rho_{AB} = +1$ (Perfect Positive Correlation)**
$$\sigma_p^2 = (w_A \sigma_A + w_B \sigma_B)^2$$
$$\sigma_p = w_A \sigma_A + w_B \sigma_B = \text{Weighted average of risks}$$
**Result:** No diversification benefit.

**Case 2: $\rho_{AB} = 0$ (Zero Correlation)**
$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2$$
**Result:** Moderate diversification benefit.

**Case 3: $\rho_{AB} = -1$ (Perfect Negative Correlation)**
$$\sigma_p^2 = (w_A \sigma_A - w_B \sigma_B)^2$$
**Result:** By choosing $w_A \sigma_A = w_B \sigma_B$, **complete risk elimination** is possible.

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight**
> This is mathematically equivalent to **destructive interference** in wave physics — two waves with opposite phases (anti-correlated signals) can cancel each other, reducing the resultant amplitude to zero.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Risk-Return Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid

    A[Total Investment Risk] --> B[Systematic Risk]
    A --> C[Unsystematic Risk]
    B --> B1[Interest Rate Risk]
    B --> B2[Inflation Risk]
    B --> B3[Market Risk]
    B --> B4[Political Risk]
    C --> C1[Business Risk]
    C --> C2[Financial Risk]
    C --> C3[Operational Risk]
    C --> C4[Management Risk]
    D[Diversification Effect] --> E[Eliminates Unsystematic Risk]
    D --> F[Cannot Eliminate Systematic Risk]
    E --> G[Risk Approaches Market Risk Only]
    F --> G
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff

```

```

### 4.3 Diversification Effect — Risk Reduction Architecture

```

### 4.4 Portfolio Construction Flow — KTU Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Step 1: Identify Risk Tolerance\nRisk Aversion Level] --> Select[Step 2: Select Asset Universe\nN Candidate Securities]
    Select --> Est[Step 3: Estimate Parameters\nExpected Returns Variances Correlations]
    Est --> Calc[Step 4: Calculate Portfolio Metrics\nE Rp and Sigma p]
    Calc --> Opt[Step 5: Optimize Weights\nMinimize Risk for Given Return]
    Opt --> Eval[Step 6: Evaluate Diversification\nCheck Unsystematic Risk Reduction]
    Eval --> Final[Step 7: Construct Final Portfolio\nOptimal Risk Return Combination]
    style Input fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff
    style Select fill:#6366f1,color:#ffffff
    style Est fill:#8b5cf6,color:#ffffff

    style Eval fill:#ec4899,color:#ffffff
    style Final fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the Risk-Return Trade Off in financial economics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Risk-Return Trade Off is the fundamental principle stating that **higher risk is associated with higher expected return**, and lower risk with lower expected return (1 mark). Rational, risk-averse investors demand compensation in the form of a **risk premium** to bear additional uncertainty (1 mark). This relationship is mathematically expressed as $E(R) = R_f + \text{Risk Premium}$, where risk is measured by standard deviation $\sigma$ and the trade-off is captured by the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM): $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$ (1 mark).

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Systematic and Unsystematic Risk.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Systematic Risk | Unsystematic Risk |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Scope** | Entire market (1 mark) | Specific firm/industry (1 mark) |
| **Diversifiable** | No | Yes |
| **Cause** | Macroeconomic factors | Firm-specific factors |
| **Measure** | Beta ($\beta$) | Residual variance |
| **Example** | Interest rate change | Factory fire |

(1 mark for the distinguishing examples and conclusion)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**a)** A portfolio consists of two stocks. Stock X has expected return $E(R_X) = 15\%$ and standard deviation $\sigma_X = 18\%$. Stock Y has expected return $E(R_Y) = 22\%$ and standard deviation $\sigma_Y = 28\%$. The correlation coefficient $\rho_{XY} = 0.40$. If you invest 50% in X and 50% in Y, calculate:
(i) Expected portfolio return (3 marks)
(ii) Portfolio standard deviation (4 marks)

**b)** Explain the effect of diversification on portfolio risk with the help of a suitable diagram. What type of risk cannot be eliminated through diversification? (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a)(i) Expected Portfolio Return (3 marks):**
Using the formula $E(R_p) = w_X E(R_X) + w_Y E(R_Y)$:

$$E(R_p) = (0.50)(15\%) + (0.50)(22\%)$$
$$E(R_p) = 7.5\% + 11.0\%$$
$$\boxed{E(R_p) = 18.5\%}$$

**(a)(ii) Portfolio Standard Deviation (4 marks):**
Using $\sigma_p^2 = w_X^2 \sigma_X^2 + w_Y^2 \sigma_Y^2 + 2 w_X w_Y \rho_{XY} \sigma_X \sigma_Y$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 = &\ (0.50)^2 (0.18)^2 + (0.50)^2 (0.28)^2 \\
& + 2(0.50)(0.50)(0.40)(0.18)(0.28)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 = &\ (0.25)(0.0324) + (0.25)(0.0784) \\
& + (0.50)(0.40)(0.0504)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 = &\ 0.00810 + 0.01960 + 0.01008 \\
\sigma_p^2 = &\ 0.03778
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.03778} = 0.1944 = 19.44\%$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_p = 19.44\%}$$

**Interpretation (extra credit):** Compare to weighted average of individual risks $(0.50)(18\%) + (0.50)(28\%) = 23.00\%$. Portfolio risk of $19.44\%$ is significantly lower, demonstrating **diversification benefit**.

**(b) Effect of Diversification (7 marks):**

Diversification reduces **unsystematic (firm-specific) risk** but cannot eliminate **systematic (market) risk** (1 mark).

**Detailed Explanation:**

When stocks are combined into a portfolio, the individual firm-specific shocks tend to cancel out because:
- Good news in one firm is offset by bad news in another
- The portfolio variance includes covariance terms, which reduce the impact of individual variances (2 marks)

**Diagram Representation:**

| Number of Securities in Portfolio | Total Portfolio Risk | Systematic Risk | Unsystematic Risk |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Very High | Constant | Very High |
| 5 | High | Constant | Reduced |
| 15 | Moderate | Constant | Low |
| 30+ | Low (Floor) | Constant | Negligible |

As $N \to \infty$, total portfolio risk $\to$ systematic risk only (2 marks).

**Types of risk that CANNOT be eliminated by diversification (2 marks):**
1. **Systematic Risk** — caused by macroeconomic factors like inflation, interest rates, GDP growth, political instability, exchange rate fluctuations
2. Examples: 2008 Global Financial Crisis, COVID-19 pandemic market crash — every stock was affected
3. This risk is measured by **Beta ($\beta$)** in the CAPM framework

---

#### **Question B: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** *(Alternative Choice)*

**a)** A risk-averse investor is choosing between three assets:

| Asset | Expected Return | Standard Deviation |
|-------|-----------------|--------------------|
| P (Government Bond) | 7% | 3% |
| Q (Corporate Bond) | 10% | 8% |
| R (Equity Stock) | 18% | 25% |

Calculate the **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** for each asset. Which asset is most efficient on a per-unit-risk basis? (7 marks)

**b)** Define covariance and correlation. Explain how correlation between assets affects the diversification benefit with suitable examples. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Coefficient of Variation (7 marks):**

Coefficient of Variation is calculated as $CV = \sigma / E(R)$. It measures **risk per unit of expected return** (1 mark).

**For Asset P:**
$$CV_P = \frac{3\%}{7\%} = 0.4286$$

**For Asset Q:**
$$CV_Q = \frac{8\%}{10\%} = 0.8000$$

**For Asset R:**
$$CV_R = \frac{25\%}{18\%} = 1.3889$$

| Asset | E(R) | $\sigma$ | CV | Rank |
|-------|------|----------|------|------|
| P | 7% | 3% | **0.4286** | 1 (Best) |
| Q | 10% | 8% | 0.8000 | 2 |
| R | 18% | 25% | 1.3889 | 3 (Worst) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Asset P (Government Bond) is the most efficient on a per-unit-risk basis as it has the **lowest CV**. For every 1% of return earned, Asset P takes on only 0.43% of risk, compared to Asset R which takes 1.39% of risk per 1% of return (2 marks).

**Practical Interpretation:** A rational, risk-averse investor should prefer Asset P if risk minimization is the priority; however, if the investor seeks higher returns and can tolerate risk, Asset R might still be chosen (1 mark).

**(b) Covariance and Correlation (7 marks):**

**Covariance** measures the **directional relationship** between the returns of two assets — whether they move together or in opposite directions (1 mark).

$$Cov(R_i, R_j) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} p_k [R_{i,k} - E(R_i)][R_{j,k} - E(R_j)]$$

**Correlation Coefficient** is the **standardized form** of covariance, ranging from $-1$ to $+1$ (1 mark):

$$\rho_{ij} = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_j)}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}, \quad \rho_{ij} \in [-1, +1]$$

**Effect on Diversification Benefit (5 marks):**

| Correlation Value | Meaning | Diversification Benefit |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $\rho = +1.0$ | Perfect positive | **None** — risk is weighted average |
| $\rho = +0.5$ | Strong positive | **Limited** benefit |
| $\rho = 0.0$ | Zero correlation | **Moderate** benefit |
| $\rho = -0.5$ | Strong negative | **Substantial** benefit |
| $\rho = -1.0$ | Perfect negative | **Complete** risk elimination possible |

**Real-World Examples:**
- **IT Stock + Pharma Stock** ($\rho \approx 0.4$): Moderate benefit — both respond to GDP growth but different sector dynamics
- **Gold + Equity** ($\rho \approx -0.1$ to $0.1$): Excellent diversifier — gold is a safe-haven asset
- **Two Banking Stocks** ($\rho \approx 0.8$): Very limited benefit — both sensitive to interest rates and credit cycles

**Key Insight:** To maximize diversification benefit, an investor should combine assets with **low or negative correlation**.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
> 1. **Wrong variance formula for portfolio:** Students often forget the **covariance term** $2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$. This single omission loses 4-6 marks.
> 2. **Confusing standard deviation with variance:** Ensure $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$. Final answer for risk is in $\sigma$ units, not $\sigma^2$.
> 3. **Misapplying weights:** Weights must sum to 1. Always verify $w_A + w_B = 1$ before calculation.
> 4. **Confusing CAPM with Diversification:** CAPM deals with systematic risk pricing (Beta), while diversification deals with unsystematic risk reduction. Mixing the two loses conceptual marks.
> 5. **Skipping units and interpretation:** Always write "%" symbol and provide a one-line interpretation in the conclusion.
> 6. **Forgetting correlation effect:** Never assume $\rho = 0$ unless explicitly stated. Most exam questions test $\rho \neq 0$ scenarios.
> 7. **Diagram without labels:** A risk-return diagram without axis labels (Risk on X-axis, Return on Y-axis) and asset points marked loses 2-3 marks.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> ✅ **Risk-Return Trade Off:** Higher risk → Higher expected return; rational investors demand risk premium
>
> ✅ **Risk Formula:** $\sigma^2 = \sum p_i [R_i - E(R)]^2$; $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$
>
> ✅ **Return Formula:** $R = (P_1 - P_0 + D) / P_0$
>
> ✅ **Expected Return:** $E(R) = \sum p_i R_i$ (weighted by probabilities)
>
> ✅ **Two-Asset Portfolio Return:** $E(R_p) = w_A E(R_A) + w_B E(R_B)$
>
> ✅ **Two-Asset Portfolio Variance:** $\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$
>
> ✅ **Correlation Range:** $\rho \in [-1, +1]$ — diversification benefit increases as $\rho$ decreases
>
> ✅ **Coefficient of Variation:** $CV = \sigma / E(R)$ — used to compare assets with different return scales
>
> ✅ **Systematic Risk:** Market-wide, non-diversifiable, measured by **Beta ($\beta$)**
>
> ✅ **Unsystematic Risk:** Firm-specific, **diversifiable** through portfolio construction
>
> ✅ **Total Risk:** $\sigma_{\text{Total}}^2 = \sigma_{\text{Systematic}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{Unsystematic}}^2$
>
> ✅ **Diversification Limit:** As $N \to \infty$, only systematic (market) risk remains in the portfolio
>
> ✅ **CAPM:** $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$ — formal Risk-Return trade-off
>
> ✅ **Exam Mantra:** Always show all 3 steps in portfolio variance calculation — weights squared, individual variances, and the cross-covariance term.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Variance Portfolio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIIT834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Minimum Variance Portfolio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Minimum Variance Portfolio (MVP)** is the portfolio of risky assets that possesses the **lowest achievable variance (volatility)** for a given set of investable assets, without imposing any constraint on the expected level of return. Mathematically, it is the solution to the constrained quadratic optimization problem that minimizes portfolio variance subject to the weights summing to unity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Minimum Variance Portfolio** is the unique portfolio on the **efficient frontier** corresponding to the global minimum of the portfolio variance function $\sigma_p^2$, obtained by solving:
> $$\min_{w} \ \sigma_p^2 = w^T \Sigma w \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{1}^T w = 1$$
> where $w$ is the $(N \times 1)$ vector of portfolio weights and $\Sigma$ is the $(N \times N)$ covariance matrix of asset returns.

When there is **no short-selling constraint**, the MVP is also called the **Global Minimum Variance Portfolio (GMVP)**, and it is the leftmost point of the Markowitz efficient frontier.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Balanced Cooking Pot"

Imagine you are a chef who wants to cook the **least spicy (lowest risk) curry** using a fixed set of spices (risky assets), each of which has its own pungency (variance) and reacts with other spices (covariance). You are allowed to mix them in any proportion, but the total quantity must add up to one full pot (weights sum to 1).

- Some spices are very hot by themselves (high variance).
- Some spices *amplify* each other when combined (positive covariance).
- A few spices actually *neutralize* each other (negative covariance).

The **Minimum Variance Portfolio** is the unique recipe that produces the **mildest possible curry** — by leaning more on those spices that *cancel out* each other's heat, and avoiding combinations that intensify spiciness. The chef is not chasing flavor (return) here — only minimizing the burn (risk).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Diversification reduces risk *only when* assets are not perfectly positively correlated. The MVP is the mathematically optimal realization of the proverb *"don't put all your eggs in one basket"* — and it goes a step further by computing the *exact* basket proportions.

## 1.3 Essential Parameters and Standard Metrics

The following quantities govern the construction of the MVP and are **bold-highlighted** for KTU 2024 examination priority:

- **$N$** → Number of risky assets in the investment universe.
- **$w_i$** → Weight of asset $i$ in the portfolio, with $\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i = 1$.
- **$\sigma_i^2$** → Variance of return on asset $i$.
- **$\sigma_{ij}$** → Covariance between returns of assets $i$ and $j$.
- **$\rho_{ij}$** → Correlation coefficient: $\rho_{ij} = \sigma_{ij}/(\sigma_i \sigma_j)$, with $\rho_{ij} \in [-1, 1]$.
- **$\Sigma$** → The $N \times N$ positive semi-definite covariance matrix.
- **$\sigma_p^2$** → Portfolio variance: $\sigma_p^2 = w^T \Sigma w$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Fund Building Block: Two-Asset MVP

For a portfolio composed of just **two risky assets** $A$ and $B$, the portfolio variance is:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$$

Substituting the budget constraint $w_B = 1 - w_A$ and differentiating with respect to $w_A$ yields the optimal MVP weights.

## 2.2 The General N-Asset MVP via Matrix Algebra

For $N$ assets, the problem is a **quadratic program** with a single linear equality constraint. The first-order conditions (KKT conditions) lead to a closed-form solution involving the inverse of the covariance matrix.

The optimization problem is:

$$\min_{w} \ \frac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mathbf{1}^T w = 1$$

The Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda) = \frac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w - \lambda (\mathbf{1}^T w - 1)$$

Taking the gradient and setting it to zero produces the system:

$$2 \Sigma w = \lambda \mathbf{1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad w = \frac{\lambda}{2} \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}$$

Imposing the constraint $\mathbf{1}^T w = 1$ gives the closed-form MVP weight vector:

$$w_{MVP} = \frac{\Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}{\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}$$

The minimum achievable variance is then:

$$\sigma_{MVP}^2 = \frac{1}{\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}$$

## 2.3 KTU 2024 Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table for KTU Examinations**

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Two-asset portfolio variance | $\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$ | Risk of a 2-asset portfolio |
| 2 | MVP weight (Asset A, 2-asset case) | $w_A^* = \dfrac{\sigma_B^2 - \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 - 2 \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B}$ | Numerator: excess variance of B over its co-risk with A |
| 3 | MVP weight (Asset B, 2-asset case) | $w_B^* = 1 - w_A^*$ | Budget constraint enforcement |
| 4 | MVP minimum variance (2-asset) | $\sigma_{MVP}^2 = \dfrac{\sigma_A^2 \sigma_B^2 (1 - \rho_{AB}^2)}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 - 2 \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B}$ | Plug weights into variance eqn. |
| 5 | Covariance from correlation | $\sigma_{ij} = \rho_{ij} \sigma_i \sigma_j$ | Converts correlation to covariance |
| 6 | N-asset MVP weight vector | $w_{MVP} = \dfrac{\Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}{\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}$ | Closed-form matrix solution |
| 7 | N-asset MVP variance | $\sigma_{MVP}^2 = \dfrac{1}{\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}$ | Reciprocal of scalar denominator |
| 8 | Lagrangian (general MVP) | $\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w - \lambda(\mathbf{1}^T w - 1)$ | First-order condition derivation |
| 9 | Expected portfolio return | $E(R_p) = w^T \mu = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i E(R_i)$ | Used to plot efficient frontier |
| 10 | Efficient frontier boundary | $\min \sigma_p^2 \ \forall \ E(R_p) = \bar{R}$ | Constrained MVP for a target return |

## 2.4 Why the MVP Matters in Real-World Finance

- **Robustness to estimation error:** Because the MVP does *not* depend on the expected return vector $\mu$, it is far less sensitive to noisy return estimates than mean-variance optimal portfolios. This makes it a **practical benchmark** in institutional asset allocation.
- **Foundational block of the Efficient Frontier:** The MVP is the leftmost anchor of the Markowitz bullet. All other efficient portfolios are obtained by tilting the MVP toward higher-return assets.
- **Pension funds and insurance portfolios** often use MVP-style strategies (subject to long-only constraints) because their liability-driven mandates prioritize **minimizing drawdown risk**.
- **Risk parity and equal-risk-contribution** strategies are direct descendants of the MVP logic.
- It serves as the **denominator** in the Sharpe ratio for the tangency portfolio construction: $\max_w \ \dfrac{w^T \mu - r_f}{\sqrt{w^T \Sigma w}}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation — Two-Asset Minimum Variance Portfolio

**Step 1: Write the portfolio variance.**

Given two assets $A$ and $B$ with weights $w_A$ and $w_B = 1 - w_A$:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + (1-w_A)^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A (1-w_A) \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$$

**Step 2: Differentiate $\sigma_p^2$ with respect to $w_A$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{d\sigma_p^2}{dw_A} = 2 w_A \sigma_A^2 - 2(1-w_A)\sigma_B^2 + 2(1 - 2w_A) \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B = 0$$

**Step 3: Simplify the first-order condition.**

Dividing by 2 and expanding:

$$w_A \sigma_A^2 - \sigma_B^2 + w_A \sigma_B^2 + \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B - 2 w_A \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B = 0$$

Group the $w_A$ terms:

$$w_A \left( \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 - 2 \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B \right) = \sigma_B^2 - \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$$

**Step 4: Solve for $w_A^*$.**

$$w_A^* = \frac{\sigma_B^2 - \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 - 2 \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B}$$

**Step 5: Compute $w_B^*$.**

$$w_B^* = 1 - w_A^* = \frac{\sigma_A^2 - \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 - 2 \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B}$$

**Step 6: Substitute back to get $\sigma_{MVP}^2$.**

After algebraic simplification, the minimum variance is:

$$\sigma_{MVP}^2 = \frac{\sigma_A^2 \sigma_B^2 \left(1 - \rho_{AB}^2\right)}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 - 2 \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that when $\rho_{AB} = 1$ (perfect positive correlation), the denominator equals $(\sigma_A - \sigma_B)^2$ and the numerator equals zero, so $\sigma_{MVP} \to 0$ only if $\sigma_A = \sigma_B$ — confirming that **diversification fails under perfect positive correlation**.

## 3.2 Complete Derivation — N-Asset MVP (Matrix Form)

**Step 1: Set up the quadratic program.**

$$\min_{w} \ \frac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{1}^T w = 1$$

**Step 2: Form the Lagrangian.**

$$\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda) = \frac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w - \lambda (\mathbf{1}^T w - 1)$$

**Step 3: First-order condition (FOC) w.r.t. $w$.**

Since $\Sigma$ is symmetric, $\nabla_w (w^T \Sigma w) = 2 \Sigma w$:

$$\nabla_w \mathcal{L} = \Sigma w - \lambda \mathbf{1} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Sigma w = \lambda \mathbf{1}$$

**Step 4: Solve the linear system.**

Assuming $\Sigma$ is invertible (strictly positive definite), premultiply by $\Sigma^{-1}$:

$$w = \lambda \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}$$

**Step 5: Apply the constraint $\mathbf{1}^T w = 1$.**

$$\mathbf{1}^T (\lambda \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}$$

**Step 6: Substitute $\lambda$ back to obtain $w_{MVP}$.**

$$w_{MVP} = \frac{\Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}{\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}$$

**Step 7: Compute the MVP variance.**

$$\sigma_{MVP}^2 = w_{MVP}^T \Sigma w_{MVP} = \left( \frac{\Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}{\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}} \right)^T \Sigma \left( \frac{\Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}{\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}} \right)$$

Using $\Sigma \Sigma^{-1} = I$:

$$\sigma_{MVP}^2 = \frac{\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \Sigma \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}{(\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1})^2} = \frac{\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}{(\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1})^2} = \frac{1}{\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result $\sigma_{MVP}^2 = \dfrac{1}{\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}$ is a *pure function* of the covariance matrix — it is **independent of expected returns**. This is a defining feature of the GMVP and the reason it is so robust in practice.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Two-Asset MVP

**Given Data:**

| Asset | Expected Return $E(R_i)$ | Standard Deviation $\sigma_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| Stock A | 14 % | 20 % |
| Stock B | 8 % | 12 % |

Correlation: $\rho_{AB} = 0.30$

**Step 1: Compute variances and covariance.**

$$\sigma_A^2 = 0.20^2 = 0.04, \quad \sigma_B^2 = 0.12^2 = 0.0144$$

$$\sigma_{AB} = \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B = 0.30 \times 0.20 \times 0.12 = 0.0072$$

**Step 2: Compute $w_A^*$.**

$$w_A^* = \frac{\sigma_B^2 - \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 - 2\rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B} = \frac{0.0144 - 0.0072}{0.04 + 0.0144 - 2(0.0072)}$$

$$w_A^* = \frac{0.0072}{0.04} = 0.1800$$

**Step 3: Compute $w_B^*$.**

$$w_B^* = 1 - 0.1800 = 0.8200$$

**Step 4: Compute $\sigma_{MVP}^2$.**

$$\sigma_{MVP}^2 = (0.18)^2(0.04) + (0.82)^2(0.0144) + 2(0.18)(0.82)(0.0072)$$

$$= 0.001296 + 0.009682 + 0.002126 = 0.013104$$

$$\sigma_{MVP} = \sqrt{0.013104} = 0.1145 = 11.45\%$$

**Step 5: Compute the expected return on the MVP.**

$$E(R_{MVP}) = (0.18)(14\%) + (0.82)(8\%) = 2.52\% + 6.56\% = 9.08\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** By holding **18%** in the higher-risk Stock A and **82%** in the lower-risk Stock B, the investor achieves a portfolio volatility of only **11.45%** — lower than the standalone volatility of *either* asset. This is the **diversification benefit** quantified by the MVP framework.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Three-Asset MVP (Matrix Form)

**Given:** Three assets with covariance matrix $\Sigma$ and unit vector $\mathbf{1} = (1, 1, 1)^T$.

$$\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 0.0400 & 0.0072 & 0.0050 \\ 0.0072 & 0.0144 & 0.0040 \\ 0.0050 & 0.0040 & 0.0225 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1: Compute $\Sigma^{-1}$.**

Using cofactor expansion (or any numerical solver):

$$\Sigma^{-1} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 32.51 & -14.12 & -4.04 \\ -14.12 & 88.92 & -11.27 \\ -4.04 & -11.27 & 51.66 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}$.**

$$\Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1} = \begin{pmatrix} 32.51 - 14.12 - 4.04 \\ -14.12 + 88.92 - 11.27 \\ -4.04 - 11.27 + 51.66 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 14.35 \\ 63.53 \\ 36.35 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}$.**

$$\mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1} = 14.35 + 63.53 + 36.35 = 114.23$$

**Step 4: Compute $w_{MVP}$.**

$$w_{MVP} = \frac{1}{114.23} \begin{pmatrix} 14.35 \\ 63.53 \\ 36.35 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1256 \\ 0.5562 \\ 0.3182 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Verify weights sum to 1.**

$$0.1256 + 0.5562 + 0.3182 = 1.0000 \ \checkmark$$

**Step 6: Compute $\sigma_{MVP}^2$.**

$$\sigma_{MVP}^2 = \frac{1}{114.23} = 0.00875 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_{MVP} = 9.36\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The three-asset MVP has lower volatility (9.36%) than the two-asset MVP (11.45%) — illustrating that **adding more assets, especially those with low mutual correlation, deepens the diversification benefit**.

## 3.5 Key Analytical Insights for Board Examinations

- The MVP is **always mean-independent** — it does not require estimation of $\mu$. This is its most important practical advantage.
- The inverse covariance matrix $\Sigma^{-1}$ **must exist**. This requires $\Sigma$ to be positive definite, which fails if any two assets are perfectly correlated.
- If **all pairwise correlations equal** $\rho$ and all variances equal $\sigma^2$, then $w_i = 1/N$ for every $i$ (equal-weight portfolio is the MVP).
- The MVP is the **left boundary** of the feasible region in $(\sigma_p, E(R_p))$ space. The **tangency portfolio** is the point on the efficient frontier with the highest Sharpe ratio.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MVP Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Asset Universe] --> B[Asset 1 Returns]
    A --> C[Asset 2 Returns]
    A --> D[Asset N Returns]
    B --> E[Estimate Mean Vector mu]
    B --> F[Compute Variances sigma_i squared]
    B --> G[Compute Covariances sigma_ij]
    C --> E
    C --> F
    C --> G
    D --> E
    D --> F
    D --> G
    F --> H[Construct Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Sigma Inverse]
    I --> J[Form Unit Vector 1 of size N]
    J --> K[Numerator: Sigma Inverse times 1]
    I --> K
    K --> L[Denominator: 1 Transpose times Sigma Inverse times 1]
    L --> M[Divide: wMVP equals Numerator divided by Denominator]
    K --> M
    M --> N[Output: MVP Weight Vector wMVP]
    M --> O[Output: MVP Variance sigmaMVP squared equals 1 divided by Denominator]
    N --> P[Plot on Efficient Frontier]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Global Minimum Variance Point]
    Q --> R[Tangency Portfolio]
    R --> S[Capital Market Line]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — MVP Position on the Efficient Frontier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EF[Mean Standard Deviation Space]
        MVP[Global Minimum Variance Portfolio MVP]
        EP1[Efficient Portfolio 1]
        EP2[Efficient Portfolio 2]
        EP3[Efficient Portfolio 3]
        T[Tangency Portfolio with Risk Free Asset]
    end
    MVP --> EP1
    EP1 --> EP2
    EP2 --> EP3
    EP3 --> T
    MVP -.Left Anchor.-> EF
    T -.Right Anchor.-> EF
    RF[Risk Free Rate rf] --> CML[Capital Market Line]
    T --> CML
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — When to Use MVP vs Tangency Portfolio

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Do you have reliable expected return estimates?}
    Q1 -- No --> MVP1[Use Global Minimum Variance Portfolio]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is a risk free asset available?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> TP1[Use Tangency Portfolio on CML]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is there a target expected return?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> TP2[Use Mean Variance Frontier with Target Return Constraint]
    Q3 -- No --> MVP1
    MVP1 --> OUT1[Optimal for risk minimization only]
    TP1 --> OUT2[Optimal for maximum Sharpe ratio]
    TP2 --> OUT3[Optimal for return goal with minimum risk]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The MVP sits at the **leftmost** point of the Markowitz bullet. As we move rightward along the upper (efficient) half, expected return rises but so does risk. The **tangency portfolio** is where the Capital Market Line (drawn from the risk-free rate) is tangent to the efficient frontier — it represents the **maximum Sharpe ratio** allocation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Minimum Variance Portfolio (MVP). Why is it considered the most robust mean-variance optimal portfolio in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
