$$\dfrac{\partial S^2}{\partial w_k} = \dfrac{2 \left(\mathbf{w}^\top \boldsymbol{\mu}\right) \mu_k \, \sigma_p^2 - \left(\mathbf{w}^\top \boldsymbol{\mu}\right)^2 \cdot 2 (\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w})_k}{\sigma_p^4} = 0$$

Simplifying numerator:

$$\left(\mathbf{w}^\top \boldsymbol{\mu}\right) \mu_k = \left(\mathbf{w}^\top \boldsymbol{\mu}\right)^2 \cdot (\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w})_k / \sigma_p^2$$

Dividing across $k$:

$$\dfrac{\mu_k}{(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w})_k} = \dfrac{\mathbf{w}^\top \boldsymbol{\mu}}{\sigma_p^2} = \text{constant} \; \forall k$$

**Solution (Closed Form):**

$$\mathbf{w}_P = \dfrac{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}}{\mathbf{1}^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ is the vector of excess returns $E(R_i) - R_f$, and $\mathbf{1}$ is the unit vector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** The closed-form weight vector $\mathbf{w}_P$ above is rarely asked in full KTU exams, but students must know its *existence and meaning*. Numericals in ESE typically provide 2 assets and ask for $\sigma_p, E(R_p)$, and $y^*$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Optimal Allocation $y^*$ in the Risky Portfolio

**Statement:** Given the tangency portfolio $P$ with Sharpe Ratio $S_p$, find the weight $y^*$ in $P$ that maximizes utility $U = E(R_c) - \tfrac{1}{2} A \sigma_c^2$.

**Step 1:** Write the Complete Portfolio $C$ as a mix of $P$ and the risk-free asset:

$$R_c = y R_p + (1-y) R_f$$

**Step 2:** Compute expected return and variance of $C$:

$$E(R_c) = y E(R_p) + (1-y) R_f = R_f + y \left[E(R_p) - R_f\right]$$

$$\sigma_c^2 = y^2 \sigma_p^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_c = y \, \sigma_p$$

(Note: $\sigma$ of a combination of a risky and risk-free asset is linear in $y$.)

**Step 3:** Substitute into the utility function:

$$U = R_f + y \left[E(R_p) - R_f\right] - \tfrac{1}{2} A \, y^2 \sigma_p^2$$

**Step 4:** Take the first derivative with respect to $y$ and set to zero (FOC):

$$\dfrac{dU}{dy} = \left[E(R_p) - R_f\right] - A \, y \, \sigma_p^2 = 0$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $y^*$:

$$\boxed{\,y^* = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{A \, \sigma_p^2}\,}$$

**Step 6:** Verification — second derivative:

$$\dfrac{d^2 U}{dy^2} = -A \, \sigma_p^2 < 0 \quad (\text{since } A>0)$$

Negative second derivative confirms a **maximum** ✓.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:**
> - Numerator $E(R_p) - R_f$ = Risk premium → the *reward*.
> - Denominator $A \sigma_p^2$ = Risk penalty → *cost* of bearing variance.
> - **Higher $A$ (more risk-averse) ⇒ smaller $y^*$** ⇒ more weight in T-Bills.
> - **Higher risk premium ⇒ larger $y^*$** ⇒ more aggressive.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Two-Fund Separation Theorem (Geometric Derivation)

The **Two-Fund Separation Theorem** states that *all* investors, regardless of risk aversion $A$, should hold the **same tangency portfolio $P$**, differing only in the mix $y^*$ with the risk-free asset.

**Geometric Proof (sketch — frequently tested in ESE Theory):**

Begin with the FOC from §3.2 applied across any two assets $i, j$:

$$\dfrac{E(R_i) - R_f}{\sigma_i} = \dfrac{E(R_j) - R_f}{\sigma_j} = A \sigma_p \, y^*$$

The LHS is the **Sharpe Ratio**, which depends only on the asset (not the investor). At the optimum for *any* risk-averse investor, the Sharpe Ratio must equalize across all assets. Therefore, all investors agree on the **same efficient mix $P$** — only the position $y^*$ along the CAL differs.

$$\boxed{\,y^* = \dfrac{S_p}{A \, \sigma_p}\,}$$

This is the **Two-Fund Separation Theorem**: separation of the investment decision (choosing $P$) from the financing decision (choosing $y^*$).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A risk-free asset offers $R_f = 5\%$. The tangency portfolio $P$ has $E(R_p) = 15\%$ and $\sigma_p = 20\%$. An investor has risk aversion $A = 4$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the Sharpe Ratio:

$$S_p = \dfrac{15\% - 5\%}{20\%} = \dfrac{10}{20} = 0.50$$

**Step 2:** Compute the optimal weight in $P$:

$$y^* = \dfrac{0.10}{4 \times (0.20)^2} = \dfrac{0.10}{4 \times 0.04} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.16} = 0.625$$

**Step 3:** Compute the expected return on the complete portfolio:

$$E(R_c) = 5\% + 0.625 \times (15\% - 5\%) = 5\% + 6.25\% = 11.25\%$$

**Step 4:** Compute the standard deviation of the complete portfolio:

$$\sigma_c = 0.625 \times 20\% = 12.5\%$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation: The investor holds **62.5% in $P$** and **37.5% in T-Bills**, achieving a moderate expected return of 11.25% with half the volatility of $P$.

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** If $A \to \infty$ (extremely risk-averse), $y^* \to 0$ → 100% T-Bills. If $A \to 0$ (risk-neutral), $y^* \to \infty$ → infinite leverage. Both extremes are mathematically consistent with the formula. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A: Block-Level Architecture of the Optimal Portfolio Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Investor Inputs: Risk Aversion A, Wealth W, Horizon T"] --> B["Step 1: Security Analysis"]
    B --> B1["Forecast E of Ri"]
    B --> B2["Estimate Sigma i"]
    B --> B3["Estimate Rho ij"]
    B1 --> C["Step 2: Construct Efficient Frontier"]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D["Identify Tangency Portfolio P"]
    D --> D1["Maximize Sharpe Ratio S of p"]
    D --> D2["Solve w of P from Sigma inverse mu"]
    D1 --> E["Step 3: Draw Capital Allocation Line CAL"]
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1["CAL Equation: E of Rc = Rf + S of p times Sigma of c"]
    E1 --> F["Step 4: Investor-Specific Allocation"]
    F --> F1["Compute y star = E of Rp minus Rf over A times Sigma of p squared"]
    F1 --> G["Step 5: Construct Complete Portfolio C"]
    G --> G1["y star fraction in P"]
    G --> G2["1 minus y star fraction in Risk Free Asset"]
    G1 --> H["Final Output: Optimal Complete Portfolio"]
    G2 --> H
    H --> H1["Expected Return E of Rc star"]
    H --> H2["Standard Deviation Sigma of c star"]
    H --> H3["Maximum Expected Utility U max"]
```

### 4.2 Diagram B: Efficient Frontier, CAL, and Indifference Curves (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EF["EFFICIENT FRONTIER (Risky Assets Only)"]
        A1["Minimum Variance Portfolio"] --- A2["Diversified Mid-Range Portfolios"]
        A2 --- A3["Tangency Portfolio P"]
        A3 --- A4["High Risk High Return Portfolios"]
    end

    subgraph CAL["CAPITAL ALLOCATION LINE (CAL)"]
        B1["Risk Free Asset Rf at y equals 0"] --- B2["Tangency Portfolio P at y equals 1"]
        B2 --- B3["Leveraged Position P star at y greater than 1"]
    end

    subgraph IC["INDIFFERENCE CURVES OF INVESTOR"]
        C1["Low Utility Curve U1"] --- C2["Medium Utility Curve U2"]
        C2 --- C3["High Utility Curve U3 OPTIMAL TANGENT POINT"]
    end

    A3 -. "Same portfolio" .-> B2
    B2 -. "Tangent to" .-> C3
    C3 -. "Highest reachable utility" .-> D["OPTIMAL COMPLETE PORTFOLIO C"]
```

### 4.3 Diagram C: Decision Tree for Optimal Portfolio Position

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Compute y star"] --> Q1{"y star greater than 1?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Aggressive["AGGRESSIVE: Borrow at Rf, Leverage P"]
    Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"y star between 0 and 1?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Balanced["BALANCED: Mix of P and Rf"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"y star less than 0?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> Hedge["HEDGE: Short P, Hold only Rf plus cash"]
    Q3 -- "No: y star equals 0" --> Conservative["CONSERVATIVE: 100 percent in Rf"]
    Aggressive --> Final["Execute Trade"]
    Balanced --> Final
    Hedge --> Final
    Conservative --> Final
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Viva:** Always label three points on the same plot — (i) $R_f$ on the y-axis, (ii) Tangency $P$ on the frontier, (iii) Investor's optimal $C$ on the CAL *above* $P$ (if leveraged) or *between* $R_f$ and $P$ (if conservative). The indifference curve at $C$ must be **tangent** to the CAL.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**Define the Capital Allocation Line (CAL). Why is it a straight line while the efficient frontier is curved?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Capital Allocation Line (CAL)** is the locus of all complete portfolios formed by combining the risk-free asset $R_f$ with the optimal risky portfolio $P$. Its equation is:

$$E(R_c) = R_f + \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p} \cdot \sigma_c$$

Since $R_f$ has zero standard deviation and zero covariance with any risky asset, the variance of the combination is $\sigma_c^2 = y^2 \sigma_p^2$, which is *linear* in the variance (i.e., $\sigma_c = y\sigma_p$). The linearity of $\sigma$ in $y$ makes the CAL **straight**. In contrast, the efficient frontier is built only from risky assets whose variances interact non-linearly through covariance terms, producing the characteristic **hyperbolic curve**.

*(Award 1 mark for CAL definition, 1 mark for straight-line justification, 1 mark for the curved-frontier justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**State and explain the Two-Fund Separation Theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Two-Fund Separation Theorem**, proposed by Tobin (1958), states that *all rational risk-averse investors, regardless of their individual risk preferences, will hold the same tangency portfolio $P$ of risky assets*. They differ only in the proportion $y^*$ allocated between $P$ and the risk-free asset $R_f$.

**Why?** The first-order condition for utility maximization $\dfrac{E(R_i)-R_f}{\sigma_i} = A\sigma_p y^*$ shows that the **Sharpe Ratio** is the same for every asset at the optimum — independent of $A$. Hence $P$ is *common* across investors. Personal risk preference $A$ only determines **position size $y^*$**, not asset selection. This is why passive index funds and ETFs work — the market portfolio $M$ is the canonical tangency portfolio.

*(Award 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for separation of investment vs. financing decision.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) Derive the optimal allocation $y^*$ in the tangency portfolio for an investor with risk-aversion coefficient $A$. Clearly state the assumptions. (7 marks)**

**(b) An investor has $R_f = 6\%$, and the tangency portfolio $P$ has $E(R_p) = 14\%$ and $\sigma_p = 25\%$. The investor's risk aversion is $A = 3$. Compute (i) the Sharpe Ratio, (ii) the optimal weight $y^*$, (iii) the expected return and standard deviation of the complete portfolio. Interpret the result. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumption 1:** Quadratic utility: $U = E(W) - \tfrac{1}{2} A \sigma_W^2$.

**Assumption 2:** Returns are normally distributed; only mean and variance matter.

**Assumption 3:** The tangency portfolio $P$ is given and is mean-variance efficient.

**Step 1:** Complete portfolio return:

$$R_c = y R_p + (1-y) R_f$$

**Step 2:** Mean and variance of $C$:

$$E(R_c) = R_f + y \left[E(R_p) - R_f\right]$$

$$\sigma_c = y \, \sigma_p$$

**Step 3:** Substituting into utility:

$$U = R_f + y \left[E(R_p) - R_f\right] - \tfrac{1}{2} A y^2 \sigma_p^2$$

**Step 4:** FOC: $\dfrac{dU}{dy} = E(R_p) - R_f - A y \sigma_p^2 = 0$.

**Step 5:** Solve:

$$\boxed{\,y^* = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{A \sigma_p^2}\,}$$

**Step 6:** Second-order condition: $\dfrac{d^2 U}{dy^2} = -A\sigma_p^2 < 0 \Rightarrow \text{maximum}$ ✓.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R_f = 0.06$, $E(R_p) = 0.14$, $\sigma_p = 0.25$, $A = 3$.

**(i) Sharpe Ratio:**

$$S_p = \dfrac{0.14 - 0.06}{0.25} = \dfrac{0.08}{0.25} = 0.32$$

**(ii) Optimal weight:**

$$y^* = \dfrac{0.14 - 0.06}{3 \times (0.25)^2} = \dfrac{0.08}{3 \times 0.0625} = \dfrac{0.08}{0.1875} = 0.4267$$

**(iii) Expected return and SD of complete portfolio:**

$$E(R_c^*) = 0.06 + 0.4267 \times (0.14 - 0.06) = 0.06 + 0.0341 = 0.0941 = 9.41\%$$

$$\sigma_c^* = 0.4267 \times 0.25 = 0.1067 = 10.67\%$$

**Interpretation:** The investor holds **42.67% in the tangency portfolio $P$** and **57.33% in the risk-free asset**, achieving a 9.41% expected return with only 10.67% volatility — less than half the risk of holding $P$ alone (25%).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) Explain the concept of an indifference curve in the context of mean-variance analysis. How does it help determine the optimal portfolio? (7 marks)**

**(b) Two risky assets $A$ and $B$ have the following characteristics:**

| Asset | $E(R)$ | $\sigma$ |
|-------|--------|----------|
| $A$ | 12% | 18% |
| $B$ | 20% | 30% |

The correlation $\rho_{AB} = 0.30$ and $R_f = 5\%$. Construct the Capital Allocation Line when the investor holds a portfolio $P$ consisting of **60% in $A$ and 40% in $B$**. What is the Sharpe Ratio of $P$? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Indifference Curve Theory (7 marks):**

An **indifference curve** in mean-standard deviation space is the locus of portfolios that yield the *same expected utility* $U$ to an investor. From the quadratic utility function:

$$U = E(R_p) - \tfrac{1}{2} A \sigma_p^2 = \text{constant}$$

Rearranging:

$$E(R_p) = U + \tfrac{1}{2} A \sigma_p^2$$

This is an **upward-sloping convex curve** in the $(E(R), \sigma)$ plane. Higher curves represent higher utility. The slope $\dfrac{dE(R)}{d\sigma} = A \sigma$ *increases* with $\sigma$, reflecting the investor's rising required risk premium as risk grows.

**How it determines the optimum:** The optimal complete portfolio is found at the point where an indifference curve is **tangent to the Capital Allocation Line (CAL)**. At tangency, the marginal rate of substitution equals the market price of risk, i.e.:

$$A \sigma_c = S_p = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p}$$

This single point yields the maximum utility *achievable* given the available investment opportunity set.

**(b) CAL Construction (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Portfolio expected return:

$$E(R_P) = 0.60 \times 12\% + 0.40 \times 20\% = 7.2\% + 8.0\% = 15.2\%$$

**Step 2:** Portfolio variance:

$$\sigma_P^2 = (0.60)^2 (0.18)^2 + (0.40)^2 (0.30)^2 + 2 (0.60)(0.40)(0.30)(0.18)(0.30)$$

$$= 0.36 \times 0.0324 + 0.16 \times 0.09 + 0.48 \times 0.0162$$

$$= 0.011664 + 0.0144 + 0.007776 = 0.033840$$

$$\sigma_P = \sqrt{0.033840} = 0.18396 \approx 18.40\%$$

**Step 3:** Sharpe Ratio:

$$S_P = \dfrac{15.2\% - 5\%}{18.40\%} = \dfrac{10.2}{18.40} = 0.5543$$

**Step 4:** CAL Equation:

$$E(R_c) = 5\% + 0.5543 \times \sigma_c$$

The investor can choose any point on this line by varying $y$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting that $\sigma_c = y \sigma_P$, not $\sigma_c^2 = y^2 \sigma_P^2$ — make sure to take square root for SD.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Mixing up $\sigma_P^2$ formula signs. The covariance term is always **positive** for the variance computation: $+2 w_1 w_2 \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2$.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Students often compute the Sharpe ratio of *each asset individually* and call it the portfolio's Sharpe Ratio. The portfolio Sharpe Ratio uses the *portfolio's* $E(R_P)$ and $\sigma_P$, not individual asset values.
> - **Mark-loss zone:** Forgetting to write units (%) and rounding prematurely. Keep 4 decimal places throughout.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Optimal Portfolio Choice** = max $U = E(R_p) - \tfrac{1}{2} A \sigma_p^2$ subject to budget constraint.
- ✅ **Three Decision Layers:** Security Analysis → Tangency Portfolio → Investor Allocation.
- ✅ **Sharpe Ratio** $S_p = (E(R_p) - R_f)/\sigma_p$ is the *slope* of the CAL.
- ✅ **Tangency Portfolio** $P$ *maximizes* the Sharpe Ratio.
- ✅ **CAL Equation:** $E(R_c) = R_f + S_p \cdot \sigma_c$ — linear in $\sigma_c$.
- ✅ **Optimal weight formula:** $y^* = (E(R_p) - R_f) / (A \sigma_p^2)$ — **most tested equation**.
- ✅ **Two-Fund Separation:** All investors hold same $P$; only $y^*$ differs.
- ✅ **Indifference Curve:** Convex, upward-sloping, slope $= A \sigma$.
- ✅ **Optimum:** Tangency of CAL with the *highest reachable* indifference curve.
- ✅ **Special cases:** $y^* = 1$ (full risky); $y^* = 0$ (all risk-free); $y^* > 1$ (leverage); $y^* < 0$ (short $P$).
- ✅ **Caveat:** $y^*$ formula assumes the tangency portfolio is *given*. In reality, it must be solved jointly.
- ✅ **Risk aversion** $A > 0$ ⇒ risk averse; $A = 0$ ⇒ risk neutral; $A < 0$ ⇒ risk lover.
- ✅ **Engineering link:** ALM, robo-advisors, pension fund allocation all use this framework.
- ✅ **Remember:** $\sigma_c = y \sigma_p$, not $\sigma_c = y^2 \sigma_p^2$ — the SD scales *linearly*.
- ✅ **Boundary intuition:** As $A \to \infty$, $y^* \to 0$; as $A \to 0$, $y^* \to \infty$ — the formula is **self-consistent**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mean-Variance Frontier of Risky and Risk-Free Assets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mean-Variance Frontier of Risky and Risk-Free Assets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mean-Variance Frontier (MVF):** The set of *minimum-variance portfolios* in the expected return ($\mu$) – standard deviation ($\sigma$) space that dominate all other achievable portfolios. In other words, it is the locus of portfolios that offer the **minimum achievable variance (risk) for every given level of expected return** — equivalently, the **maximum achievable expected return for every given level of risk**.

When a **risk-free asset** (with return $R_f$ and $\sigma_f = 0$) is introduced into the opportunity set, the feasible investment universe expands dramatically. The new efficient boundary is no longer the curved (hyperbolic) risky-asset frontier alone, but a **straight line — the Capital Market Line (CML)** — that is *tangent* to the risky-asset efficient frontier at a single point called the **Tangency Portfolio** (denoted $T$ or $M$ when it represents the market portfolio).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Cliff-Edge Trail":**
> Imagine you are hiking in a mountain range where every point represents a possible investment portfolio. The **horizontal axis** is *risk* (how shaky your legs feel) and the **vertical axis** is *expected reward* (the height you gain). The *risky-asset efficient frontier* is the **steepest, safest cliff edge** — you can't get more height for the same level of shaking, nor less shaking for the same height. Now, suppose a **cable car** (the risk-free asset — government T-bills) is available at the base camp. You can combine walking on the cliff edge with riding the cable car. The new optimal route is a *straight line* from the cable-car station to the exact point of tangency on the cliff — that point is the **tangency portfolio**, and the line is the **Capital Market Line**. Every rational climber (investor) will now pick a point on this straight line, because the curved cliff is strictly *dominated* once the cable car exists.

## 1.3 Why the Frontier Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MVF and CML are the **cornerstone of Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)** by **Harry Markowitz (1952)** and its extension by **James Tobin (1958)**. KTU examiners routinely test:
> - Derivation of the efficient frontier (Lagrangian optimization).
> - Identification of the **tangency portfolio** and its weights.
> - The **Capital Allocation Line (CAL)** / **CML** equation.
> - The **Two-Fund Separation Theorem**.
> - Numerical problems on combining $R_f$ with a tangency portfolio.

Standard key parameters used in this module:
- **Risk-free rate ($R_f$): typically 0.04 – 0.08 in exam problems.**
- **Tangency (market) portfolio standard deviation ($\sigma_M$): portfolio-level dispersion.**
- **Sharpe Ratio ($S_p$): excess return per unit of risk — the slope of the CML.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Setting Up the Risky-Asset Universe

Consider $N$ risky assets with random returns $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N$. A portfolio is a vector of weights $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)^T$ with:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i = 1$$

The portfolio's expected return and variance are:

$$E(R_p) = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu} \qquad \sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ is the $N \times 1$ vector of expected returns and $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is the $N \times N$ covariance matrix.

### 2.1.1 Two-Asset Special Case (Frequently Tested)

For two risky assets $A$ and $B$:

$$E(R_p) = w_A E(R_A) + w_B E(R_B)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$$

where $\rho_{AB}$ is the **correlation coefficient**. The variance simplifies when $\rho_{AB} = -1$ (perfectly negatively correlated), in which case a **zero-variance portfolio** can be constructed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why negative correlation helps:** When one asset zig-zags up, the other zig-zags down, cancelling out the net volatility. The KTU board loves asking the $\rho = -1$ minimum-variance weights.

## 2.2 Deriving the Risky Efficient Frontier — Lagrangian Logic Steps

The minimum-variance frontier in $\mu$-$\sigma$ space is derived by minimizing portfolio variance subject to:
- **Constraint 1:** Target expected return $\mu_p$ is achieved, i.e., $\mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mu_p$.
- **Constraint 2:** Weights sum to unity, i.e., $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{1} = 1$.

The Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda, \gamma) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda (\mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu} - \mu_p) - \gamma (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{1} - 1)$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- **Step 1 — FOC for $\mathbf{w}$:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and set to zero: $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \boldsymbol{\mu} + \gamma \mathbf{1}$.
- **Step 2 — Solve for $\mathbf{w}$:** $\mathbf{w} = \lambda \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu} + \gamma \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{1}$.
- **Step 3 — Apply constraints** to solve for the scalar multipliers $\lambda$ and $\gamma$ in closed form using the auxiliary scalars $A = \mathbf{1}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{1}$, $B = \mathbf{1}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}$, $C = \boldsymbol{\mu}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}$, and the determinant-like discriminant $\Delta = A C - B^2$.
- **Step 4 — Express $\sigma_p^2$ and $\mu_p$** through these scalars to obtain the **two-fund frontier equation**:

$$\sigma_p^2 = \frac{A \mu_p^2 - 2 B \mu_p + C}{\Delta}$$

This is a **hyperbola** in the $(\sigma, \mu)$ plane — the full minimum-variance set. The **upper branch** (higher $\mu$ for given $\sigma$) is the **efficient frontier**.

## 2.3 Adding the Risk-Free Asset — Tobin's Separation

When a risk-free asset is available, every portfolio $P$ is a combination of $R_f$ and *one* (any) risky portfolio $Q$ with weight $w$ in the risky fund:

$$E(R_P) = w E(R_Q) + (1 - w) R_f$$

$$\sigma_P = \vert w \vert \, \sigma_Q$$

Eliminating $w$ gives the **Capital Allocation Line (CAL)** through any risky portfolio $Q$:

$$E(R_P) = R_f + \left[ \frac{E(R_Q) - R_f}{\sigma_Q} \right] \sigma_P$$

The slope $\frac{E(R_Q) - R_f}{\sigma_Q}$ is the **Sharpe Ratio** of portfolio $Q$. Investors prefer the CAL with the **steepest slope** — i.e., the tangency portfolio $T$ that maximizes the Sharpe ratio.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear in 80% of KTU numericals on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | Portfolio expected return | $E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i E(R_i)$ | Weights sum to **1** |
| 2 | Portfolio variance (general) | $\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$ | Bilinear quadratic form |
| 3 | Two-asset variance | $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ | $\rho \in [-1, +1]$ |
| 4 | Covariance | $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = \rho_{ij} \sigma_i \sigma_j$ | $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_i) = \sigma_i^2$ |
| 5 | Correlation from covariance | $\rho_{ij} = \text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) \,/\, \sigma_i \sigma_j$ | Bounded in $[-1, +1]$ |
| 6 | Minimum-variance weight (2 assets) | $w_1^{*} = \dfrac{\sigma_2^2 - \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2 \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2}$ | Min $\sigma_p$ only |
| 7 | Zero-variance weight ($\rho = -1$) | $w_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}$ | $\sigma_p = 0$ possible |
| 8 | Tangency portfolio weight (vector) | $\mathbf{w}_T = \dfrac{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})}{\mathbf{1}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})}$ | Numerator excess-return loading |
| 9 | Sharpe Ratio | $S_p = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Slope of CAL |
| 10 | Capital Market Line (CML) | $E(R_p) = R_f + S_M \, \sigma_p$ | Linear in $\sigma_p$ |
| 11 | Front-end hyperbola (risky only) | $\sigma_p^2 = \dfrac{A \mu_p^2 - 2 B \mu_p + C}{A C - B^2}$ | $A,B,C$ defined above |
| 12 | Two-fund separation | $\mathbf{w}_p = \alpha \mathbf{w}_T + (1 - \alpha) \mathbf{w}_{f}$ | Any efficient $P$ is a mix |
| 13 | Complete-market expected return | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_M) - R_f]$ | CAPM link |
| 14 | Portfolio beta | $\beta_p = \sum w_i \beta_i$ | Linear in weights |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Financial Utility

Although this is a financial-economics course, the underlying **mean-variance optimization** machinery is identical to engineering quadratic programming problems — it appears in:

- **Signal processing:** MMSE (minimum mean square error) filters.
- **Control systems:** LQG (linear-quadratic-Gaussian) regulators.
- **Operations research:** Portfolio rebalancing in robo-advisory engines.
- **Risk management in corporates:** Asset–liability management (ALM) in banks, insurance firms, and pension funds — all use the same Lagrangian trick on a covariance matrix.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Risky Minimum-Variance Frontier (Lagrangian)

We wish to **minimize** $\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$ subject to the linear constraints $\mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mu_p$ and $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{1} = 1$.

**Step 1.** Form the Lagrangian function:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda (\mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu} - \mu_p) - \gamma (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{1} - 1)$$

The factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is a notational convenience so that the first derivative is clean.

**Step 2.** Take the first-order condition (FOC) with respect to $\mathbf{w}$, treating $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ as symmetric and positive-definite:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda \boldsymbol{\mu} - \gamma \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{0}$$

Therefore:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \boldsymbol{\mu} + \gamma \mathbf{1}$$

**Step 3.** Pre-multiply by $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}$ to obtain the optimal weight vector:

$$\mathbf{w} = \lambda \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu} + \gamma \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{1}$$

**Step 4.** Define the three fundamental scalars (these are the building blocks of every KTU problem on this topic):

$$A = \mathbf{1}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{1} \qquad B = \mathbf{1}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu} \qquad C = \boldsymbol{\mu}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}$$

Note that $A$ and $C$ are always positive for a non-singular covariance matrix, and $\Delta = AC - B^2 > 0$.

**Step 5.** Apply the two constraints. Pre-multiplying $\mathbf{w}$ by $\boldsymbol{\mu}^T$ and $\mathbf{1}^T$ respectively:

$$\lambda C + \gamma B = \mu_p \qquad \lambda B + \gamma A = 1$$

**Step 6.** Solve the 2×2 linear system using Cramer's rule:

$$\lambda = \frac{A \mu_p - B}{A C - B^2} = \frac{A \mu_p - B}{\Delta}$$

$$\gamma = \frac{C - B \mu_p}{A C - B^2} = \frac{C - B \mu_p}{\Delta}$$

**Step 7.** Compute $\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$. Substituting $\mathbf{w} = \lambda \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu} + \gamma \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{1}$:

$$\sigma_p^2 = (\lambda \boldsymbol{\mu} + \gamma \mathbf{1})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\lambda \boldsymbol{\mu} + \gamma \mathbf{1})$$

Expanding:

$$\sigma_p^2 = \lambda^2 C + 2 \lambda \gamma B + \gamma^2 A$$

**Step 8.** Plug $\lambda$ and $\gamma$ from Step 6 and simplify. After algebra:

$$\sigma_p^2 = \frac{A \mu_p^2 - 2 B \mu_p + C}{A C - B^2} = \frac{A \mu_p^2 - 2 B \mu_p + C}{\Delta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric interpretation:** This is the equation of a **hyperbola** in the $(\sigma, \mu)$ plane, opening towards higher $\sigma$. The vertex (minimum-variance portfolio) is at $\mu_p = B/A$, $\sigma_p^2 = 1/A$. The *upper* branch of this hyperbola is the **efficient frontier**.

## 3.2 Full Derivation: Tangency Portfolio and the CML

**Step 1.** Maximize the Sharpe ratio $S_p = \frac{\mu_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ over all feasible portfolios. Equivalently, minimize $-\frac{\mu_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ or use the equivalent Lagrangian form.

**Step 2.** The tangency problem minimizes $-\mu_p$ subject to $\sigma_p = 1$ (or maximizes $\mu_p - R_f$ subject to $\sigma_p = 1$). The Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L} = \mathbf{w}^T (\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1}) - \frac{\eta}{2} \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$$

where $\eta$ is a Lagrange multiplier on the unit-variance constraint.

**Step 3.** FOC: $\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1} = \eta \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$, so:

$$\mathbf{w} = \frac{1}{\eta} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})$$

**Step 4.** Apply the unit-variance constraint $\mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = 1$. With $\mathbf{v} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})$:

$$\frac{1}{\eta^2} \mathbf{v}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{v} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta = \sqrt{\mathbf{v}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{v}} = \sqrt{\mathbf{v}^T (\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})}$$

**Step 5.** Impose the budget constraint $\mathbf{1}^T \mathbf{w} = 1$. The scalar $\eta$ is rescaled; denoting the **unnormalised tangency direction** by $\mathbf{v}$:

$$\mathbf{w}_T = \frac{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})}{\mathbf{1}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})}$$

**Step 6.** Plug $\mathbf{w}_T$ back to find $\mu_T$ and $\sigma_T$:

$$\mu_T = \mathbf{w}_T^T \boldsymbol{\mu}, \qquad \sigma_T^2 = \mathbf{w}_T^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}_T$$

The **maximum Sharpe ratio** is:

$$S_T = \frac{\mu_T - R_f}{\sigma_T} = \sqrt{(\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})}$$

**Step 7.** Any efficient portfolio combining the risk-free asset and the tangency portfolio has expected return:

$$E(R_P) = R_f + \alpha (\mu_T - R_f), \qquad \sigma_P = \vert \alpha \vert \, \sigma_T$$

Eliminating $\alpha$:

$$E(R_P) = R_f + S_T \cdot \sigma_P$$

This is the **Capital Market Line (CML)** — a straight line in the $\sigma$-$\mu$ plane, passing through $(0, R_f)$ with slope equal to the maximum Sharpe ratio.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Derivative Example (Two Assets)

Let $R_f = 0.05$, $E(R_A) = 0.12$, $E(R_B) = 0.18$, $\sigma_A = 0.20$, $\sigma_B = 0.30$, $\rho_{AB} = 0.30$.

**Step 1.** Excess returns: $\mu_A - R_f = 0.07$, $\mu_B - R_f = 0.13$.

**Step 2.** Covariance matrix:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.04 & 0.018 \\ 0.018 & 0.09 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3.** Inverse of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$:

$$\det(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = (0.04)(0.09) - (0.018)^2 = 0.0036 - 0.000324 = 0.003276$$

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.003276} \begin{pmatrix} 0.09 & -0.018 \\ -0.018 & 0.04 \end{pmatrix} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 27.47 & -5.495 \\ -5.495 & 12.21 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4.** Tangency numerator $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 27.47 & -5.495 \\ -5.495 & 12.21 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.07 \\ 0.13 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 27.47(0.07) - 5.495(0.13) \\ -5.495(0.07) + 12.21(0.13) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.9229 - 0.7144 \\ -0.3847 + 1.5873 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.2086 \\ 1.2026 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5.** Denominator = sum of components = $1.2086 + 1.2026 = 2.4112$.

**Step 6.** Tangency weights:

$$w_A^T = \frac{1.2086}{2.4112} \approx 0.5012, \qquad w_B^T = \frac{1.2026}{2.4112} \approx 0.4988$$

**Step 7.** Tangency expected return:

$$\mu_T = 0.5012(0.12) + 0.4988(0.18) \approx 0.14998$$

**Step 8.** Tangency variance:

$$\sigma_T^2 = (0.5012)^2(0.04) + (0.4988)^2(0.09) + 2(0.5012)(0.4988)(0.018) \approx 0.04394$$

$$\sigma_T \approx 0.2096$$

**Step 9.** Sharpe ratio:

$$S_T = \frac{0.14998 - 0.05}{0.2096} \approx 0.4770$$

**Step 10.** CML equation: $E(R_P) = 0.05 + 0.4770 \, \sigma_P$. For example, a portfolio with $\sigma_P = 0.15$ has $E(R_P) = 0.05 + 0.4770(0.15) = 0.1216$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Mean-Variance Frontier Construction Pipeline

The following Mermaid **functional architecture flow** maps the chain of operations a portfolio manager (or a KTU numerical solver) follows from raw asset data to the final CML equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: N Asset Returns and Covariance Matrix Sigma] --> B[Compute Excess Return Vector mu minus Rf times 1]
    B --> C[Invert Covariance Matrix Sigma Inverse]
    C --> D[Multiply: Sigma Inverse times Excess Returns]
    D --> E[Compute Tangency Weights via Normalisation]
    E --> F[Compute Tangency Return muT and Risk sigmaT]
    F --> G[Compute Maximum Sharpe Ratio ST]
    G --> H[Form CML: ERp equals Rf plus ST times sigmap]
    H --> I[Plot: Hyperbola becomes Tangent Line from Rf to T]
    I --> J[Output: Two-Fund Separation Universe]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** Each block is a *deterministic transformation*. Notice that the **inversion of the covariance matrix** (highlighted in amber) is the only non-trivial linear-algebra operation; everything else is matrix-vector multiplication. KTU board problems give you $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ explicitly so the inversion is the *only* computation you must show in detail for full marks.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Geometric Comparison — Risky Frontier vs. Risk-Free Augmented Frontier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SF[Standard Risky Universe]
        R1[Risky Asset 1] --> M1[Mean-Variance Hyperbola]
        R2[Risky Asset 2] --> M1
        RN[Risky Asset N] --> M1
        M1 --> EF1[Efficient Frontier Upper Branch]
    end

    subgraph AF[Augmented Universe with Rf]
        RF[Risk-Free Asset at sigma equals 0, return Rf] --> TP[Tangency Portfolio T]
        EF1 --> TP
        TP --> CML1[Capital Market Line: straight line from Rf to T]
    end

    EF1 -.dominated by.-> CML1

    style RF fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
    style TP fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style CML1 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style EF1 fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the dotted arrow "dominated by":** Every point on the curved efficient frontier is *strictly dominated* by a corresponding point on the CML once $R_f$ is available — same risk, higher return. This is precisely **Tobin's separation theorem** in pictorial form, and is a guaranteed 2-mark short-answer in KTU exams.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Two-Fund Separation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Investor Chooses Risk Aversion Level] --> Q2{Place 100 percent in one fund?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q3[Compute alpha: weight in Tangency Portfolio T]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[All in T or All in Rf]
    Q3 --> Q5[Allocate alpha to T and one minus alpha to Rf]
    Q4 --> Q6[Single Asset Choice]
    Q5 --> Q7[Final Portfolio lies on CML]
    Q6 --> Q7

    style Q7 fill:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
    style Q3 fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff
    style Q1 fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-Fund Separation in plain words:** Investors differ only in the *scalar* $\alpha$ (their risk appetite), not in *which* risky assets they hold. They all hold the same **tangency portfolio $T$** in differing proportions. This is the most-tested *conceptual* point in the module.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP, Module 3]**
> **Define the Mean-Variance Frontier. How does the introduction of a risk-free asset alter its shape?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Mean-Variance Frontier (MVF)** is the locus of all minimum-variance portfolios in the $(\sigma, \mu)$ plane — i.e., portfolios offering the *least risk for a given expected return* or equivalently the *highest expected return for a given level of risk*. Without a risk-free asset, the MVF is a **hyperbola** in $\sigma$-$\mu$ space; only the upper branch is efficient. When a risk-free asset is introduced, the efficient set collapses onto a **straight line — the Capital Market Line (CML)** — that is *tangent* to the hyperbola at the **tangency portfolio** $T$. Every point on the curved frontier below the tangent point is *dominated* (higher return for the same risk is now available on the CML). This is **Tobin's Separation Theorem**, and the slope of the CML equals the **maximum Sharpe ratio** of any risky portfolio.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 3]**
> **State and explain the Two-Fund Separation Theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Two-Fund Separation Theorem** states that when a risk-free asset is available, every investor's optimal portfolio is a *linear combination of just two funds*: the **risk-free asset** and a single **risky tangency portfolio** $T$ that maximises the Sharpe ratio. The weight $\alpha$ on $T$ is chosen according to the investor's risk preference, but *the composition of the risky fund is identical for all investors*. Thus, the investment problem *separates* into (i) a *collective* problem of finding the tangency portfolio and (ii) a *personal* problem of mixing it with $R_f$. Mathematically, $\mathbf{w}_p = \alpha \mathbf{w}_T + (1 - \alpha) \mathbf{w}_f$, with the resulting expected return lying on the CML: $E(R_p) = R_f + S_T \sigma_p$.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A — 14 Marks**
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]**
>
> **(a) (7 marks)** A portfolio manager considers two risky assets A and B with $E(R_A) = 14\%$, $E(R_B) = 22\%$, $\sigma_A = 18\%$, $\sigma_B = 28\%$, and correlation $\rho_{AB} = 0.25$. A risk-free asset yields $R_f = 6\%$. Calculate the **weights, expected return, and standard deviation of the tangency portfolio**.
>
> **(b) (7 marks)** An investor desires a portfolio with expected return of **16%**. Determine the weights in $R_f$ and the tangency portfolio, and the resulting portfolio standard deviation using the CML.

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Tangency Portfolio Computation**

**Step 1 — Build the covariance matrix.** Covariance = $\rho \sigma_A \sigma_B = 0.25 \times 0.18 \times 0.28 = 0.0126$.

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.0324 & 0.0126 \\ 0.0126 & 0.0784 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute excess returns.** $\mu_A - R_f = 0.14 - 0.06 = 0.08$, $\mu_B - R_f = 0.22 - 0.06 = 0.16$.

**Step 3 — Invert the covariance matrix.** $\det(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = (0.0324)(0.0784) - (0.0126)^2 = 0.00254016 - 0.00015876 = 0.0023814$.

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.0023814} \begin{pmatrix} 0.0784 & -0.0126 \\ -0.0126 & 0.0324 \end{pmatrix} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 32.92 & -5.291 \\ -5.291 & 13.61 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by excess returns.**

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1}) = \begin{pmatrix} 32.92(0.08) - 5.291(0.16) \\ -5.291(0.08) + 13.61(0.16) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.6336 - 0.8466 \\ -0.4233 + 2.1776 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.7870 \\ 1.7543 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Normalise to obtain tangency weights.** Sum = $1.7870 + 1.7543 = 3.5413$.

$$w_A^T = \frac{1.7870}{3.5413} \approx 0.5046, \qquad w_B^T = \frac{1.7543}{3.5413} \approx 0.4954$$

**Step 6 — Tangency expected return:**

$$\mu_T = 0.5046(0.14) + 0.4954(0.22) = 0.07064 + 0.10899 = 0.17963 \;(\text{17.96\%})$$

**Step 7 — Tangency variance:**

$$\sigma_T^2 = (0.5046)^2(0.0324) + (0.4954)^2(0.0784) + 2(0.5046)(0.4954)(0.0126)$$

$$= 0.008231 + 0.019227 + 0.006300 = 0.033758$$

$$\sigma_T = \sqrt{0.033758} \approx 0.18373 \;(\text{18.37\%})$$

**Step 8 — Sharpe ratio:** $S_T = (0.17963 - 0.06)/0.18373 \approx 0.6510$.

**Marks awarded:** Setup (2), inversion (2), weights (1), $\mu_T$/$\sigma_T$ (2) = **7/7**.

**(b) Building a Target-Return Portfolio on the CML**

**Step 1 — CML equation:** $E(R_p) = 0.06 + 0.6510 \, \sigma_p$.

**Step 2 — Solve for $\sigma_p$ when $E(R_p) = 0.16$:** $0.16 = 0.06 + 0.6510 \, \sigma_p \Rightarrow \sigma_p = 0.10/0.6510 \approx 0.1536$ (15.36%).

**Step 3 — Weight in tangency portfolio:** $\alpha = \sigma_p / \sigma_T = 0.1536 / 0.18373 \approx 0.8361$.

**Step 4 — Weight in risk-free asset:** $1 - \alpha = 0.1639$ (16.39%).

**Step 5 — Final portfolio weights in A and B:**
$w_A = 0.8361 \times 0.5046 \approx 0.4219$ (42.19%),
$w_B = 0.8361 \times 0.4954 \approx 0.4142$ (41.42%),
$w_{R_f} = 0.1639$ (16.39%).

**Verification:** Sum = $0.4219 + 0.4142 + 0.1639 = 1.0000$ ✓.
Expected return = $0.4219(0.14) + 0.4142(0.22) + 0.1639(0.06) = 0.05907 + 0.09112 + 0.00983 = 0.16002 \approx 0.16$ ✓.

**Marks awarded:** CML setup (2), $\sigma_p$ from target return (2), weights $\alpha$ and $1-\alpha$ (2), final allocation breakdown (1) = **7/7**.

---

> ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**
>
> **(a) (7 marks)** Using the Lagrangian approach, derive the **minimum-variance frontier equation** for $N$ risky assets in terms of the scalars $A$, $B$, $C$, and $\Delta$. Clearly state each step and the FOCs.
>
> **(b) (7 marks)** Two risky assets X and Y have $E(R_X) = 10\%$, $E(R_Y) = 20\%$, $\sigma_X = 15\%$, $\sigma_Y = 25\%$, $\rho_{XY} = -0.5$. Show that by choosing appropriate weights, the portfolio variance can be **made equal to zero**. Calculate the **minimum-variance portfolio weights** and the **weights that yield zero variance**.

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Lagrangian Derivation**

The problem is: $\min_{\mathbf{w}} \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$ subject to $\mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mu_p$ and $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{1} = 1$.

**Step 1 — Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda (\mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu} - \mu_p) - \gamma (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{1} - 1)$$

**Step 2 — FOC w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$:** $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda \boldsymbol{\mu} - \gamma \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{0} \Rightarrow \mathbf{w} = \lambda \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu} + \gamma \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{1}$.

**Step 3 — Define scalars:** $A = \mathbf{1}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{1}$, $B = \mathbf{1}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}$, $C = \boldsymbol{\mu}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}$, $\Delta = A C - B^2$.

**Step 4 — Apply constraints** to get the linear system $\lambda C + \gamma B = \mu_p$ and $\lambda B + \gamma A = 1$. Solving:

$$\lambda = \frac{A \mu_p - B}{\Delta}, \qquad \gamma = \frac{C - B \mu_p}{\Delta}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\sigma_p^2$:** $\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda^2 C + 2\lambda \gamma B + \gamma^2 A$.

**Step 6 — Substitute** $\lambda$ and $\gamma$, simplify, and obtain:

$$\sigma_p^2 = \frac{A \mu_p^2 - 2B \mu_p + C}{\Delta}$$

This is the **minimum-variance frontier equation** — a hyperbola in the $(\sigma_p, \mu_p)$ plane. Its vertex is at $\mu_p = B/A$, $\sigma_p^2 = 1/A$. The upper branch ($\mu_p \geq B/A$) is the **efficient frontier**.

**Marks awarded:** Lagrangian formation (1), FOC and weight solution (2), constraint application (2), final frontier equation (1), geometric interpretation (1) = **7/7**.

**(b) Zero-Variance and Minimum-Variance Weights**

**Step 1 — Covariance:** $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = \rho \sigma_X \sigma_Y = (-0.5)(0.15)(0.25) = -0.01875$.

**Step 2 — Zero-variance condition** (only possible when $\rho = -1$; here $\rho = -0.5$ so a true zero is impossible, but we illustrate the *method* by checking the minimum).

Set $\frac{d \sigma_p^2}{d w_X} = 0$:

$$\frac{d}{dw_X}\left[ w_X^2 (0.0225) + (1-w_X)^2 (0.0625) + 2 w_X (1-w_X)(-0.01875) \right] = 0$$

Expanding the bracket:

$$\sigma_p^2 = 0.0225 w_X^2 + 0.0625 (1 - 2 w_X + w_X^2) - 0.0375 w_X (1 - w_X)$$

$$= 0.0225 w_X^2 + 0.0625 - 0.125 w_X + 0.0625 w_X^2 - 0.0375 w_X + 0.0375 w_X^2$$

$$= (0.0225 + 0.0625 + 0.0375) w_X^2 + (-0.125 - 0.0375) w_X + 0.0625$$

$$= 0.1225 w_X^2 - 0.1625 w_X + 0.0625$$

Derivative: $0.245 w_X - 0.1625 = 0 \Rightarrow w_X^{*} = 0.6633$, $w_Y^{*} = 0.3367$.

**Step 3 — Minimum variance:**

$$\sigma_p^{*2} = 0.1225 (0.6633)^2 - 0.1625 (0.6633) + 0.0625 = 0.05389 - 0.10779 + 0.0625 = 0.00860$$

$$\sigma_p^{*} = \sqrt{0.00860} \approx 0.0928 \;(9.28\%)$$

**Step 4 — For the (theoretical) zero-variance case** if $\rho = -1$ instead of $-0.5$:

$$w_X^{ZV} = \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_X + \sigma_Y} = \frac{0.25}{0.15 + 0.25} = \frac{0.25}{0.40} = 0.625$$

$$w_Y^{ZV} = 1 - 0.625 = 0.375$$

With these weights and $\rho = -1$, $\sigma_p = 0$ exactly. Since the given $\rho = -0.5$ is *not* equal to $-1$, a true zero-variance portfolio is **not achievable** — the minimum variance above ($\sigma_p^* \approx 9.28\%$) is the best possible. The student should explicitly state this caveat for full marks.

**Marks awarded:** Covariance (1), derivative setup and FOC (2), min-var weights (2), zero-variance derivation with $\rho = -1$ caveat (2) = **7/7**.

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Missing normalisation of tangency weights:** Students often present the unnormalised vector $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})$ and forget to divide by the sum of its components. **Lose 1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Sign errors in the Lagrangian FOC:** The Lagrangian is *minimised*, so the FOC must yield $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \lambda\boldsymbol{\mu} + \gamma\mathbf{1}$, **not** $-\lambda$ or $-\gamma$. Sign slippage costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing CAL with CML:** The Capital Allocation Line is through *any* risky portfolio. The **Capital Market Line** is specifically through the *tangency* portfolio. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if you call the wrong one.
> 4. **Forgetting the budget constraint $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{1} = 1$:** Tangency weights must sum to 1, not just satisfy the variance condition. Check the sum at the end.
> 5. **Hyperbola vs. parabola:** The minimum-variance frontier is a **hyperbola**, not a parabola. Writing it as $\sigma_p^2 = a \mu_p^2 + b \mu_p + c$ is *technically correct*, but explicitly identifying it as a **hyperbola in $(\sigma, \mu)$-space** and explaining that the upper branch is the efficient frontier scores the *interpretation* marks.
> 6. **Zero-variance caveat:** When $\rho \neq -1$, true $\sigma_p = 0$ is impossible. Students who blindly apply the $\rho = -1$ formula and claim zero variance lose **2 marks**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Mean-Variance Frontier of Risky and Risk-Free Assets:**
>
> - [ ] **Mean-Variance Frontier** = set of *minimum-variance* portfolios in $(\sigma, \mu)$ space; a **hyperbola** whose *upper branch* is the **efficient frontier**.
> - [ ] **Two-asset variance formula:** $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2$. Always state the **correlation range** $[-1, +1]$.
> - [ ] **Lagrangian scalars:** $A = \mathbf{1}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{1}$, $B = \mathbf{1}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}$, $C = \boldsymbol{\mu}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}$, $\Delta = A C - B^2$.
> - [ ] **Frontier equation:** $\sigma_p^2 = (A \mu_p^2 - 2B \mu_p + C)/\Delta$.
> - [ ] **Tangency portfolio formula:** $\mathbf{w}_T = \dfrac{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})}{\mathbf{1}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})}$. Weights must sum to **1**.
> - [ ] **Capital Market Line (CML):** $E(R_p) = R_f + S_T \sigma_p$, where $S_T = (\mu_T - R_f)/\sigma_T$ is the **maximum Sharpe ratio**.
> - [ ] **Two-Fund Separation Theorem:** All investors hold combinations of $R_f$ and the *same* tangency portfolio $T$; only the mixing weight $\alpha$ differs.
> - [ ] **CML slope** = $S_T$; intercept = $R_f$; passes through tangency point $T$.
> - [ ] **Hyperbola $\to$ Straight line:** Adding $R_f$ converts the curved efficient frontier into the *dominant* straight CML.
> - [ ] **Zero-variance portfolio** exists **only** when $\rho_{ij} = -1$ (perfect negative correlation).
> - [ ] **Minimum-variance weights** come from the FOC $d\sigma_p^2/dw = 0$, *not* from the tangency formula (different objective).
> - [ ] **Risk-free asset has $\sigma = 0$ and $\text{Cov}(R_f, R_i) = 0$** — this is what *flattens* the frontier into a line.
> - [ ] **Verify** all numerical answers: weights sum to 1; expected returns lie on the CML; variance is non-negative.
> - [ ] **Examiner's mantra:** "Show the FOC, solve the linear system, normalise the weights, plug back, and *interpret geometrically*." Memorise this 5-step ritual for the 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Advanced Asset Return Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Advanced Asset Return Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Advanced Asset Return Models** are a class of equilibrium and empirical financial frameworks designed to explain the cross-sectional and time-series behaviour of expected returns on risky assets. Unlike the elementary single-factor Capital Market Line (CML) or the naive historical mean-variance analysis, advanced models incorporate **systematic risk factors**, **intertemporal consumption preferences**, **arbitrage restrictions**, or **empirical firm characteristics** to price assets more accurately.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic formally introduces four landmark paradigms:

1. **The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** — Sharpe (1964), Lintner (1965), Mossin (1966).
2. **The Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT)** — Ross (1976).
3. **The Fama-French Three-Factor Model** — Fama & French (1993).
4. **The Fama-MacBeth (1973) Two-Pass Regression Procedure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The objective is *not* to merely state equations but to understand **why markets must reward certain risks and not others**, the **assumptions** that permit each model, and the **empirical weaknesses** that motivated moving from CAPM → APT → Fama-French.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Salary Determinant" Intuition

Imagine an engineer's monthly take-home salary (the **expected return** on a stock). A naive model says: "Salary = Base Pay + Bonus". But that is too simplistic. A more realistic model asks:

- **Base Pay (Risk-Free Rate $R_f$):** What every employee gets regardless of performance — analogous to a Treasury Bill yield.
- **Department Risk Premium ($\beta \cdot MRP$):** Working in a high-risk department (R&D, oil rigs) earns extra compensation — analogous to **market beta**.
- **Size Premium ($s \cdot SMB$):** Smaller teams often get extra allowances to attract talent — analogous to the **Size factor (Small Minus Big)**.
- **Value Premium ($h \cdot HML$):** Employees in "value-add" legacy departments may earn differently than those in "growth" segments — analogous to the **Value factor (High Minus Low book-to-market)**.

Just as a sophisticated HR policy combines *base + risk + size + value*, advanced asset return models combine a **risk-free rate** with multiple **risk premia** multiplied by their respective **factor sensitivities (betas)**.

### 1.3 Core Terminology & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics You Must Memorise:**

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Risk-Free Rate | $R_f$ | Return on a default-free asset (e.g., 10Y G-Sec) |
| Market Risk Premium | $MRP = E(R_m) - R_f$ | Excess return demanded for bearing average market risk |
| Asset Beta | $\beta_i$ | Sensitivity of asset $i$'s return to the market portfolio |
| Firm-Specific (Idiosyncratic) Risk | $\varepsilon_i$ | Diversifiable residual return |
| Alpha (Jensen's) | $\alpha_i$ | Abnormal return unexplained by the model |
| R-squared | $R^2$ | Fraction of return variance explained by factors |

### 1.4 Why Do We Need *Advanced* Models?

The **Single-Index Market Model** (Sharpe, 1963) only decomposes returns into market and noise components. It says *nothing* about **why** expected returns differ across assets. Advanced models add the **equilibrium pricing kernel** — i.e., they explain *which risks are priced* and *by how much*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hierarchical Architecture of Asset Return Models

Models evolved along two parallel tracks — **Theoretical (Equilibrium)** and **Empirical (Reduced-Form)**:

**Track A — Equilibrium Models** (derived from investor optimisation):
- Mean-Variance Optimisation → CML → **CAPM**
- Intertemporal consumption choices → **Consumption-CAPM (C-CAPM)**
- No-arbitrage under multi-factor world → **APT (Ross)**

**Track B — Empirical Models** (statistical fit to data):
- **Market Model** (statistical precursor)
- **Fama-French 3-Factor** (adds Size + Value)
- **Carhart 4-Factor** (adds Momentum)
- **Fama-French 5-Factor** (adds Profitability + Investment)

### 2.2 Assumptions Underlying Each Advanced Model

**CAPM Assumptions (10 standard):**
1. Investors are **mean-variance optimisers** (quadratic utility / Normal returns).
2. **Single-period** horizon.
3. **Homogeneous expectations** about inputs.
4. Existence of a **risk-free asset** with unlimited borrowing/lending.
5. All assets are **infinitely divisible** and marketable.
6. **No taxes, no transaction costs**.
7. **Prices are exogenously given** (price-takers).
8. **Total asset supply is fixed**.
9. Information is **costless and simultaneous**.
10. Markets are in **competitive equilibrium**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Why these matter in KTU exams:** Whenever a question asks *"Critically evaluate CAPM"*, examiners expect you to discuss the violation of *at least three* of these assumptions (homogeneous expectations, single-period, tax-free trading) — this is where marks are lost or gained.

**APT Assumptions (more relaxed):**
1. Returns follow a **factor structure**: $R_i = E(R_i) + \sum_{k} \beta_{ik} F_k + \varepsilon_i$.
2. Factor residuals $\varepsilon_i$ have **finite variance** and zero mean.
3. **Well-diversified portfolios** eliminate idiosyncratic risk.
4. Sufficient **arbitrage activity** keeps markets efficient.

The key insight: APT does **not** require mean-variance optimisation — only that arbitrageurs exploit mispricings.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table. It is the highest-yield summary for Module 4.**

| Model | Core Equation | Premium Term | Factor Sensitivities |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk-Free Asset | $E(R_f)$ | — | — |
| Market Model (statistical) | $R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_m + \varepsilon_i$ | None | $\beta_i$ |
| CAPM (1-Factor Equilibrium) | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \left[ E(R_m) - R_f \right]$ | $MRP$ | $\beta_i$ |
| APT (Multi-Factor No-Arbitrage) | $E(R_i) = R_f + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_{ik} \lambda_k$ | $\lambda_k$ | $\beta_{ik}$ for $k=1...K$ |
| Fama-French 3-Factor | $E(R_i) - R_f = \alpha_i + \beta_i MRP + s_i \cdot SMB + h_i \cdot HML$ | $SMB$, $HML$ | $\beta_i$, $s_i$, $h_i$ |
| Carhart 4-Factor | Adds WML (Momentum) to FF3 | $WML$ | Adds $m_i$ |
| Fama-MacBeth Procedure | Cross-sectional regression each period: $R_{it} = \gamma_{0t} + \gamma_{1t} \hat\beta_{i,t-1} + u_{it}$ | Time-varying $\gamma_{1t}$ | Pre-estimated $\hat\beta$ |

### 2.4 The Real-World Engineering & Finance Utility

- **Portfolio Construction:** Asset managers (BlackRock, Vanguard) use these models to compute **Cost of Equity** for capital budgeting in engineering firms (e.g., WACC for a new power plant).
- **Hedge Fund Risk Budgeting:** APT's multi-factor structure underlies Barra and Axioma risk models — a $500B+ industry.
- **Regulatory Capital:** Banks compute equity capital using CAPM-implied asset correlations (Basel II/III frameworks).
- **Engineering Project Appraisal:** Discount rates for long-gestation infrastructure (metros, smart-grid projects) are anchored on CAPM/APT beta estimates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — The CAPM from the Two-Fund Separation Theorem

**Step 1:** Every investor holds a combination of the **risk-free asset $R_f$** and the **Market Portfolio $M$** (Two-Fund Separation Theorem, Tobin, 1958).

$$w_M \cdot M + (1 - w_M) \cdot R_f = R_p \quad \text{(portfolio return)}$$

**Step 2:** Define the weight $w_M$ chosen by investor $i$ to match their risk tolerance:

$$w_M^{(i)} = \frac{E(R_M) - R_f}{\sigma_M^2 \cdot \theta_i}$$

where $\theta_i$ is the **risk-aversion coefficient** (larger $\theta_i \Rightarrow$ smaller $w_M$).

**Step 3:** In **equilibrium**, the aggregate demand for $M$ equals its **fixed supply** (normalised to 1). Hence, $\sum_i w_M^{(i)} = 1$.

**Step 4:** Express the expected return and beta of any individual asset $i$:

$$\beta_i = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2} \quad \text{(definition of regression beta)}$$

$$\text{Var}(R_M) = \sigma_M^2 \quad \text{(market portfolio variance)}$$

**Step 5:** In equilibrium, the expected excess return on asset $i$ must be **proportional** to its covariance with the market portfolio. Combining market-clearing with the pricing relation:

$$E(R_i) - R_f = \frac{E(R_M) - R_f}{\sigma_M^2} \cdot \text{Cov}(R_i, R_M)$$

**Step 6:** Recognising that $\beta_i = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2}$, we substitute:

$$E(R_i) - R_f = \beta_i \cdot \left[ E(R_M) - R_f \right]$$

**Step 7:** Re-arranging for $E(R_i)$:

$$\boxed{E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \left[ E(R_M) - R_f \right]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of $\beta_i$:**
> - $\beta_i = 1$: Asset moves with the market.
> - $\beta_i > 1$: Aggressive (amplified) asset.
> - $\beta_i < 1$: Defensive asset.
> - $\beta_i < 0$: Hedge / inverse asset (e.g., gold during equity crashes).

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — APT from the Law of One Price (Arbitrage Argument)

**Step 1:** Assume asset returns follow a **K-factor structure**:

$$R_i = E(R_i) + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_{ik} F_k + \varepsilon_i$$

with $E(\varepsilon_i) = 0$, $E(\varepsilon_i \cdot F_k) = 0$, and $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) < \infty$.

**Step 2:** Construct a **well-diversified portfolio** $P$ with weights $w_i$ such that:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i = 1, \qquad \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \beta_{ik} = 0 \quad \forall k = 1, 2, ..., K$$

**Step 3:** Compute the expected return of this portfolio. Idiosyncratic risks $\varepsilon_i$ vanish as $N \to \infty$ by the Law of Large Numbers:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \text{Var}\left( \sum_i w_i \varepsilon_i \right) \to 0$$

Hence:

$$E(R_P) = \sum_i w_i E(R_i)$$

**Step 4:** Apply **No-Arbitrage**: If $E(R_P) > 0$, every arbitrageur shorts the risk-free asset and buys $P$, earning infinite profit; if $E(R_P) < 0$, the reverse. Equilibrium therefore demands:

$$E(R_P) = R_f \cdot \sum_i w_i = R_f$$

$$\therefore \sum_i w_i \left[ E(R_i) - R_f \right] = 0$$

**Step 5:** Since this must hold for *all* valid weight vectors $w_i$, the bracket term must be **decomposable** into the factor structure:

$$E(R_i) - R_f = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_{ik} \lambda_k$$

**Step 6:** The $\lambda_k$ are interpreted as the **risk premium per unit of factor-k exposure**:

$$\boxed{E(R_i) = R_f + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_{ik} \lambda_k}$$

where $\lambda_k$ is determined by market clearing for each factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference from CAPM:** CAPM forces $\beta$ to a *single* market factor (rigorous but restrictive). APT allows *any* number of macroeconomic factors (inflation, term-spread, default-spread, industrial production) without specifying them a priori.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Fama-MacBeth (1973) Two-Pass Regression

This is the **empirical workhorse** used to test whether CAPM/APT holds in real data.

**Pass 1 — Time-Series Regression (for each asset $i$):**

$$R_{i,t} = \hat\gamma_{i0} + \hat\beta_i \cdot R_{M,t} + \hat\varepsilon_{i,t}, \quad t = 1, 2, ..., T$$

Extract the estimated beta $\hat\beta_i$ for each of $N$ assets.

**Pass 2 — Cross-Sectional Regression (for each period $t$):**

$$R_{i,t} = \hat\gamma_{0t} + \hat\gamma_{1t} \cdot \hat\beta_i + \hat u_{i,t}, \quad i = 1, 2, ..., N$$

**Final Aggregation:**

$$\bar\gamma_1 = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \hat\gamma_{1t}, \qquad \sigma(\gamma_1) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T(T-1)} \sum_{t=1}^{T} (\hat\gamma_{1t} - \bar\gamma_1)^2}$$

**t-statistic for CAPM test:**

$$t = \frac{\bar\gamma_1}{\sigma(\gamma_1)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:**
> - If $t \approx 0$ and $\bar\gamma_1 \approx 0$: CAPM rejected (no reward for beta alone).
> - If $\bar\gamma_1 > 0$ significantly: CAPM supported.
> - Fama-MacBeth themselves found a **flat relationship** — a major empirical blow to CAPM, motivating Fama-French.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — CAPM Computation

**Given:**
- $R_f = 6\%$, $E(R_M) = 14\%$, so $MRP = 8\%$.
- Stock A: $\beta_A = 1.5$
- Stock B: $\beta_B = 0.7$

**Step A — Compute $E(R_A)$:**

$$E(R_A) = R_f + \beta_A \cdot MRP = 6 + 1.5 \cdot 8 = 6 + 12 = 18\%$$

**Step B — Compute $E(R_B)$:**

$$E(R_B) = R_f + \beta_B \cdot MRP = 6 + 0.7 \cdot 8 = 6 + 5.6 = 11.6\%$$

**Step C — Verify SML (Security Market Line):**
Plot $(0, 6)$, $(1, 14)$, $(1.5, 18)$, $(0.7, 11.6)$ — all points must lie on the straight line $E(R) = 6 + 8\beta$. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Evolution of Advanced Asset Return Models

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Historical Mean-Variance Analysis] --> A1[Single-Index Market Model 1963]
    A1 --> B0[CAPM 1964 Sharpe Lintner Mossin]
    A1 --> C0[APT 1976 Ross]
    B0 --> D0[Empirical CAPM Tests Roll 1977]
    D0 --> E0[Fama MacBeth 1973 Two-Pass]
    E0 --> F0[Fama French 3-Factor 1993]
    C0 --> F0
    F0 --> G0[Carhart 4-Factor 1997 Momentum]
    F0 --> H0[Fama French 5-Factor 2015 Profitability Investment]
    B0 --> I0[Consumption CAPM Breeden 1979]
    I0 --> J0[Habit Formation Models]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Left branch (Equilibrium):** CAPM → Consumption CAPM → Habit-Formation.
> - **Middle branch (No-Arbitrage):** APT → Multi-Factor Empirical Models.
> - **Right branch (Empirical):** Market Model → Fama-MacBeth → Fama-French → Carhart → Fama-French 5-Factor.
> This evolution shows how each model **fixed a specific failure** of its predecessor.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Fama-French 3-Factor Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input[Asset Return Ri] --> Excess[Excess Return Ri minus Rf]
    Excess --> Regr1[Beta Exposure MRP]
    Excess --> Regr2[Size Exposure SMB]
    Excess --> Regr3[Value Exposure HML]
    Regr1 --> Out1[Expected Return Component]
    Regr2 --> Out1
    Regr3 --> Out1
    Out1 --> Final[E(Ri) = Rf + Beta times MRP + s times SMB + h times HML plus Alpha]
    SMBDef[SMB = Small cap return minus Big cap return] --> Regr2
    HMLDef[HML = High Book to Market return minus Low Book to Market return] --> Regr3
    MRPDef[MRP = E Rm minus Rf] --> Regr1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to *"Draw the Fama-French factor structure"*, examiners expect you to explicitly label the **three betas** ($\beta$ for market, $s$ for size, $h$ for value) and the **three premia** (MRP, $SMB$, $HML$). This functional block diagram is the cleanest representation.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Fama-MacBeth Two-Pass Logic Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    StepA[Step 1 Time Series Regression per Asset] --> StepA1[Obtain Beta Estimate for each Asset]
    StepA1 --> StepB[Step 2 Cross Sectional Regression per Period]
    StepB --> StepB1[Obtain Gamma coefficients for each Period]
    StepB1 --> StepC[Step 3 Average Gamma coefficients across Time]
    StepC --> StepD[Step 4 Compute Standard Error and t statistic]
    StepD --> StepE{Decision t statistic significant and nonzero}
    StepE -- Yes --> StepF[CAPM Supported]
    StepE -- No --> StepG[CAPM Rejected Consider Multi Factor Models]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State and explain the Capital Asset Pricing Model. What does the beta coefficient represent?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **CAPM** (Sharpe, 1964) is an equilibrium model that relates the expected return on an asset to its **systematic (non-diversifiable) risk**:

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \left[ E(R_M) - R_f \right]$$

**Interpretation of $\beta_i$ (1 mark each):**
- $\beta_i$ measures the **sensitivity** of asset $i$'s return to movements in the market portfolio.
- Mathematically: $\beta_i = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2}$.
- $\beta_i = 1$: asset moves in line with market; $\beta_i > 1$: aggressive; $\beta_i < 1$: defensive; $\beta_i < 0$: inverse/hedge asset.
- Only **systematic risk** (captured by $\beta$) is rewarded; idiosyncratic risk is diversifiable and earns no premium.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between the CAPM and the APT with respect to assumptions and factor structure.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Dimension | CAPM | APT |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Factors** | Single factor (market portfolio) | Multiple factors ($K$ factors) |
| **Key Assumption** | Mean-variance optimisation, homogeneous expectations | No-arbitrage; factor structure on returns |
| **Asset Specific Risk** | Fully diversified away (identical to APT in that respect) | Diversified away in well-diversified portfolios |
| **Identification of Factors** | Explicit (market portfolio) | Factors need not be specified — empirical estimation |
| **Empirical Testability** | Difficult (requires market portfolio proxy) | Easier (multiple testable factor specifications) |

CAPM is more **restrictive** but provides a **cleaner closed-form** solution; APT is more **general** but less **operational** without specifying factors.

---

### 📘 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the CAPM starting from the assumption that all investors are mean-variance optimisers with homogeneous expectations. Explain the role of the market portfolio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data is provided for three stocks:

| Stock | Beta | Expected Return |
|---|---|---|
| P | 0.8 | 10% |
| Q | 1.2 | 14% |
| R | 1.6 | 18% |

Given $R_f = 6\%$ and $E(R_M) = 12\%$, test whether these stocks are correctly priced according to CAPM. Identify any mispriced security and construct the arbitrage portfolio. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Two-Fund Separation:* Under mean-variance optimisation and homogeneous expectations, every investor holds a combination of the **risk-free asset $R_f$** and the **tangency portfolio (market portfolio $M$)**. (1 mark)

*Step 2 — Optimal weights:* For investor $i$ with risk-aversion $\theta_i$:

$$w_M^{(i)} = \frac{E(R_M) - R_f}{\theta_i \cdot \sigma_M^2}$$

*Step 3 — Market clearing:* Equilibrium requires $\sum_i w_M^{(i)} = 1$ (since the market portfolio supply is normalised to 1). (1 mark)

*Step 4 — Pricing relation:* The expected excess return on any asset $i$ is proportional to its covariance with $M$:

$$E(R_i) - R_f = \frac{E(R_M) - R_f}{\sigma_M^2} \cdot \text{Cov}(R_i, R_M)$$

(1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the reasoning)

*Step 5 — Substitute $\beta_i$:* Recognising $\beta_i = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2}$:

$$\boxed{E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \left[ E(R_M) - R_f \right]}$$

(1 mark for final substitution)

*Role of Market Portfolio (1 mark):* The market portfolio $M$ is the unique mean-variance-efficient portfolio that includes ALL risky assets in proportion to their market capitalisation. It serves as the **numeraire** against which all systematic risk is measured. In equilibrium, every investor — irrespective of risk preference — holds $M$ (combined with $R_f$).

---

**(b) CAPM Test & Arbitrage Construction (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Compute MRP:*

$$MRP = E(R_M) - R_f = 12 - 6 = 6\%$$

*Step 2 — Compute CAPM-implied returns using $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot MRP$:* (1 mark)

| Stock | Beta | CAPM-Implied $E(R_i)$ | Actual $E(R_i)$ | Alpha |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P | 0.8 | $6 + 0.8 \cdot 6 = 10.8\%$ | 10% | $-0.8\%$ |
| Q | 1.2 | $6 + 1.2 \cdot 6 = 13.2\%$ | 14% | $+0.8\%$ |
| R | 1.6 | $6 + 1.6 \cdot 6 = 15.6\%$ | 18% | $+2.4\%$ |

(2 marks for the table)

*Step 3 — Identify mispricings:*
- **Stock Q** lies **above the SML** → **underpriced** (offers too high a return for its risk) → **BUY**.
- **Stock R** lies even further above SML → **underpriced** → **BUY**.
- **Stock P** lies below SML → **overpriced** → **SELL SHORT**. (1 mark)

*Step 4 — Construct Arbitrage Portfolio:*
Construct a zero-cost, zero-beta, positive-return portfolio:

1. **Buy** $w_Q$ of Q and $w_R$ of R.
2. **Sell short** $w_P$ of P.
3. Choose weights so that $\sum w_i \beta_i = 0$ and $\sum w_i = 0$.

Take a long position in Q and R, short P such that net beta is zero:

$$w_Q \cdot 1.2 + w_R \cdot 1.6 - w_P \cdot 0.8 = 0, \quad w_Q + w_R - w_P = 0$$

Choose $w_P = 2, w_Q = 1, w_R = 0.5$ (verification: $\beta = 1.2 + 0.8 - 1.6 = 0.4 \neq 0$ — adjust to satisfy both constraints).

Proper solution: set $w_Q = w_P$ (so $\beta$-balance): $0.8 w_P + 1.2 w_P = 2 w_P$, and add $w_R = w_P \cdot 0.8/1.6 = 0.5 w_P$. Net investment $= 0$. (2 marks)

*Step 5 — Arbitrage profit:* The portfolio earns the alpha difference with zero net investment and zero systematic risk, which is a **riskless arbitrage profit** — violating CAPM equilibrium. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the Arbitrage Pricing Theory as proposed by Ross (1976). How does it differ from CAPM in terms of factor structure and assumptions? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The Fama-French three-factor model regression for a stock $X$ over 60 months yields:

$$\hat R_X - \hat R_f = 1.5\% + 1.10 \cdot (R_M - R_f) + 0.35 \cdot SMB + 0.20 \cdot HML$$

with an $R^2 = 0.78$. Interpret the coefficients. If the risk-free rate is $5\%$, market risk premium is $6\%$, $E(SMB) = 3\%$, $E(HML) = 4\%$, compute the expected return on Stock $X$ using the Fama-French model. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) APT Explanation (7 marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):* The **Arbitrage Pricing Theory** (Ross, 1976) is a multi-factor asset pricing model in which the expected return of an asset is a linear function of its sensitivities (betas) to a set of **K macroeconomic factors**, plus a risk-free rate:

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_{ik} \lambda_k$$

where $\lambda_k$ is the **risk premium** associated with factor $k$.

*Key Assumptions (2 marks):*
1. Asset returns are generated by a **factor model**: $R_i = E(R_i) + \sum_k \beta_{ik} F_k + \varepsilon_i$.
2. Idiosyncratic residuals have **zero mean, finite variance, and are uncorrelated** with factors and across assets.
3. **Well-diversified portfolios** can be constructed.
4. Sufficient **arbitrage activity** prevents persistent mispricings.
5. Capital markets are competitive.

*Derivation Logic (1 mark):* APT does NOT require mean-variance optimisation. It relies on the **Law of One Price**: in equilibrium, no arbitrage opportunity can exist, so the expected return of a portfolio with zero factor exposure must equal $R_f$.

*Differences from CAPM (2 marks):*

| Aspect | CAPM | APT |
|---|---|---|
| Number of factors | One (market) | Multiple (K) |
| Investor preferences | Mean-variance | Not specified |
| Equilibrium condition | Mean-variance optimisation + homogeneous expectations | No-arbitrage only |
| Specification of factors | Explicit (market portfolio) | Implicit (factors not pre-specified) |
| Restrictiveness | High | Low |

---

**(b) Fama-French Coefficient Interpretation & Expected Return (7 marks):**

*Interpretation of Coefficients (3 marks):*

| Coefficient | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $\hat\alpha$ | $1.5\%$ | Abnormal return not explained by the three factors (positive alpha indicates the stock outperformed its factor-implied return) |
| $\hat\beta$ | $1.10$ | Stock is **10% more volatile** than the market; an aggressive equity |
| $\hat s$ (Size loading) | $0.35$ | Stock behaves more like a **small-cap** stock; positive SMB exposure |
| $\hat h$ (Value loading) | $0.20$ | Stock behaves more like a **value** stock (high book-to-market); positive HML exposure |
| $R^2$ | $0.78$ | **78%** of the variation in excess returns is explained by the three factors jointly |

*Step 1 — Compute Expected Return using Fama-French:*

$$E(R_X) = R_f + \beta \cdot E(R_M - R_f) + s \cdot E(SMB) + h \cdot E(HML)$$

$$E(R_X) = 5 + 1.10 \cdot 6 + 0.35 \cdot 3 + 0.20 \cdot 4$$

(1 mark for formula setup, 1 mark for substitution)

*Step 2 — Compute each term:*

- Risk-free component: $5\%$
- Market contribution: $1.10 \cdot 6 = 6.6\%$
- Size contribution: $0.35 \cdot 3 = 1.05\%$
- Value contribution: $0.20 \cdot 4 = 0.8\%$

*Step 3 — Sum:*

$$E(R_X) = 5 + 6.6 + 1.05 + 0.8 = 13.45\%$$

(1 mark for final answer)

*Step 4 — Add alpha (if total return):*

$$\text{Total expected return} = E(R_X) + \alpha = 13.45 + 1.5 = 14.95\%$$

(1 mark for alpha adjustment)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls to Avoid:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $R_f$:** Many students compute only $\beta \cdot MRP$ and forget to add $R_f$. **Lose 1 mark instantly.**
> 2. **Confusing alpha with expected return:** In Fama-French, $\alpha$ is the **abnormal** component; total $E(R) = R_f + \text{factor terms} + \alpha$. Treat the regression equation's LHS carefully.
> 3. **Not specifying the time period:** Fama-MacBeth uses *monthly* cross-sectional regressions; Fama-French typically uses *monthly or daily*. Always state.
> 4. **Skipping the interpretation step:** Numerical answers without coefficient interpretation lose 1–2 marks. Examiners reward the *meaning*, not just the number.
> 5. **Mixing up $SMB$ and $HML$:** $SMB$ = Small Minus Big (size); $HML$ = High Minus Low (value). Reversing these is a common error.
> 6. **In CAPM test questions:** Always compute the **SML-implied return** first, then compare with actual return, then identify **alpha sign** (positive = underpriced, negative = overpriced).

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4:**

- [x] **CAPM** assumes mean-variance optimisation → Single-factor → $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i(E(R_M) - R_f)$.
- [x] **Beta** = $\dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2}$; only systematic risk is priced.
- [x] **SML** passes through $(0, R_f)$ and $(1, E(R_M))$; slope = MRP.
- [x] **Alpha** = $R_i - E(R_i)_{\text{model}}$; positive alpha ⇒ underpricing.
- [x] **APT** is multi-factor, derived from no-arbitrage; $E(R_i) = R_f + \sum_k \beta_{ik} \lambda_k$.
- [x] **APT** factors are unspecified a priori (macroeconomic or statistical).
- [x] **Fama-French 3-Factor:** adds $SMB$ (size) and $HML$ (value) to CAPM.
- [x] **$SMB$** = return on small-cap portfolio − return on big-cap portfolio.
- [x] **$HML$** = return on high B/M portfolio − return on low B/M portfolio.
- [x] **Fama-MacBeth Procedure:** Two-pass regression — first estimate $\hat\beta_i$ via time-series; second, regress $R_{i,t}$ on $\hat\beta_i$ cross-sectionally each month.
- [x] **Empirical failure of CAPM:** Roll (1977) critique + Fama-MacBeth flat relationship ⇒ motivated multi-factor models.
- [x] **R-squared** in Fama-French typically 0.7–0.85; far higher than CAPM alone (≈0.30).
- [x] **Risk premium sign:** MRP > 0, SMB > 0 (small-cap premium), HML > 0 (value premium).
- [x] **Difference between Market Model and CAPM:** Market Model is *statistical* (no equilibrium); CAPM is *equilibrium* (implies pricing).
- [x] **No-arbitrage condition:** A zero-beta, zero-cost portfolio cannot have positive expected return.

---

*End of Notes — Module 4: Introduction to Advanced Asset Return Models*
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned | Outcome-Based Education*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-Index Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multi-Index Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Multi-Index Model (MIM)** is a multifactor asset pricing framework in which the return on a risky security is expressed as a **linear function of the returns on several macroeconomic or market indices** (factors), plus a random error term that captures firm-specific (idiosyncratic) influences.

$$R_{i} \;=\; \alpha_{i} \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, I_{j} \;+\; e_{i}$$

where the symbols carry the following rigorous meanings (KTU 2024 Scheme notation):

- $R_{i}$ — Return on security $i$ over the holding period.
- $\alpha_{i}$ — The intercept (the expected return on $i$ when all index returns are zero; in practice, the *expected abnormal return*).
- $I_{j}$ — Return on macro-factor (index) $j$, $j = 1, 2, \dots, n$.
- $\beta_{ij}$ — Factor loading (sensitivity coefficient) of security $i$ with respect to index $j$.
- $e_{i}$ — Residual (firm-specific) return with $E(e_{i}) = 0$, $\mathrm{Var}(e_{i}) = \sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$, and $E(e_{i}\, e_{k}) = 0$ for $i \neq k$.
- $n$ — Number of indices in the model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Multi-Index Model is a **direct generalisation of Sharpe's Single Index Model (1963)**. The single-index model is merely the special case $n = 1$. The model is conceptually related to **Ross's Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT, 1976)** but uses observable indices rather than latent factors.
