### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Crop Yield Story"

Imagine you are an agricultural analyst forecasting the yield of a mango orchard in Kerala. The yield is **not** driven by just one factor (say, total rainfall). It depends on **several factors simultaneously**:

| Factor (Index) | How It Affects Mango Yield | Loading (β) |
|---|---|---|
| Total monsoon rainfall (mm) | More rain → better yield, but too much causes fungus | High positive |
| Average temperature (°C) | Affects flowering and fruit setting | Moderate positive |
| Humidity level (%) | Too high → pest attacks | Moderate negative |
| Fertilizer price index | Cost pressure; indirectly affects care | Slight positive |

The **Multi-Index Model** works exactly the same way for stock returns. A company's stock return is not just a function of "the market"; it is a function of **multiple systematic forces** — interest rates, industrial production, oil prices, inflation, and market index — each with its own sensitivity ($\beta$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** If a stock has a high $\beta_{\text{oil}} = 1.6$ and a low $\beta_{\text{interest}} = 0.2$, it is **highly oil-sensitive** and **barely affected by interest rates**. The multi-index model allows us to capture this rich, multi-dimensional risk profile — something the single-index model cannot do.

### 1.3 Why the Model Was Needed — Limitations of Single Index

The single-index model assumes **all stocks move together because of one common factor (the market)**. This is unrealistic because:

- Two stocks may have **identical market betas** but react **very differently** to oil shocks, interest rate changes, or currency movements.
- Industry-specific events (e.g., a regulatory change in banking) cannot be captured by the market index alone.
- Empirical studies (King, 1966; Cohen & Pogue, 1967) showed that **multi-index models explain a much higher fraction of return variance** than the single-index model.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb from empirical research:** A well-constructed 4-index model typically raises the $R^{2}$ (explained variance) from ~30% (single index) to ~60–70% (multi-index), substantially reducing unsystematic risk in a portfolio.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Valuation

- **Total Variance of Return:** $\sigma_{i}^{2}$ — measured in $(\%)^{2}$ or $(\text{return})^{2}$.
- **Systematic Variance:** $\sum_{j} \beta_{ij}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2}$ — the part explained by the indices.
- **Unsystematic Variance:** $\sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$ — the firm-specific, diversifiable portion.
- **$R^{2}$ (Coefficient of Determination):** proportion of total variance explained by the indices.
- **Adjusted $R^{2}$:** used when comparing models with different numbers of factors.
- **F-statistic / $t$-statistic** of each $\beta$ — used in the **Fama–MacBeth (1973)** cross-sectional regression procedure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building Blocks of the Model — A Structured Walk-Through

The Multi-Index Model decomposes every security's return into **three orthogonal components**:

1. **The Constant Component ($\alpha_{i}$):** The baseline expected return unrelated to macro factors.
2. **The Systematic Component $\left(\sum_{j} \beta_{ij}\, I_{j}\right)$:** The portion driven by *common* economic forces affecting many securities simultaneously. This is **non-diversifiable** risk.
3. **The Unsystematic Component ($e_{i}$):** The portion driven by *firm-specific* events (management change, product recall, factory fire). This is **diversifiable** risk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Assumption (Critical for KTU Board Exam):** The residual terms $e_{i}$ are assumed to be **mutually uncorrelated** across securities and **uncorrelated with the index returns** $I_{j}$. Formally:
> $$E(e_{i}\, e_{k}) = 0 \;\;\text{for}\; i \neq k, \quad E(e_{i}\, I_{j}) = 0 \;\;\forall\, j$$

### 2.2 The Logical Steps Behind the Model

- **Step 1 — Factor Selection:** Identify $n$ relevant macroeconomic or market indices (e.g., Nifty 50, INR/USD, crude oil, 10-year G-Sec yield, IIP growth).
- **Step 2 — Time-Series Regression:** For each security $i$, run a multiple regression of $R_{i,t}$ on $I_{1,t}, I_{2,t}, \dots, I_{n,t}$ using historical data. Obtain estimates $\hat{\alpha}_{i}$ and $\hat{\beta}_{ij}$.
- **Step 3 — Variance Decomposition:** Split total variance $\sigma_{i}^{2}$ into a systematic part (explained by the indices) and a residual part $\sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$.
- **Step 4 — Covariance Computation:** Use estimated $\beta$'s and index variances to compute the covariance between any two securities — this is the heart of portfolio construction.
- **Step 5 — Portfolio Application:** Feed these covariances into the **Markowitz mean-variance optimizer** to derive the efficient frontier.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Symbol Key |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{i} = \alpha_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, I_{j} + e_{i}$ | **Model Equation** — return decomposition | $R_{i}$: security return; $\alpha_{i}$: intercept; $\beta_{ij}$: factor loading; $I_{j}$: factor return; $e_{i}$: residual |
| 2 | $E(R_{i}) = \alpha_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, E(I_{j})$ | **Expected Return** of security $i$ | $E(\cdot)$ denotes expectation operator |
| 3 | $\sigma_{i}^{2} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2} + \sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$ | **Total Variance** of security $i$ (indices uncorrelated) | $\sigma_{I_{j}}^{2}$: variance of index $j$ |
| 4 | $\sigma_{i}^{2} = \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\beta_{ik}\,\mathrm{Cov}(I_{j},I_{k}) + \sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$ | **General Variance** (correlated indices) | $\mathrm{Cov}(I_{j},I_{k})$: covariance of indices |
| 5 | $\sigma_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \beta_{ik}\beta_{jk}\, \sigma_{I_{k}}^{2}$ | **Covariance** of two securities (uncorrelated indices) | $\sigma_{ij}$: covariance between $i$ and $j$ |
| 6 | $\sigma_{ij} = \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\beta_{kj}\,\mathrm{Cov}(I_{j},I_{k})$ | **General Covariance** (correlated indices) | full covariance matrix form |
| 7 | $R^{2} = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2}}{\sigma_{i}^{2}}$ | **Coefficient of Determination** (goodness of fit) | proportion of variance explained by indices |
| 8 | $\text{Correlation}(\rho_{ij}) = \dfrac{\sigma_{ij}}{\sigma_{i}\, \sigma_{j}}$ | **Correlation** between two securities | $\sigma_{i}, \sigma_{j}$: std deviations |
| 9 | $\text{Systematic Risk} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2}$ | Non-diversifiable portion of risk | depends only on factor exposures |
| 10 | $\text{Unsystematic Risk} = \sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$ | Diversifiable portion of risk | vanishes in large diversified portfolios |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Multi-Index Model

- **Portfolio Construction:** Reduces the input burden of Markowitz optimisation from $N(N+1)/2$ covariances to $n$ index variances + $N \times n$ betas. For $N=100$ stocks and $n=4$ indices, the reduction is dramatic: from 5050 to ~400 parameters.
- **Performance Attribution:** Helps decompose a fund's return into "factor bets" (e.g., did the manager profit from a tilt toward value, or from interest-rate calls?).
- **Risk Management:** Identifies which macro factor is driving a stock's volatility, allowing better hedging.
- **Asset Pricing:** Foundation of modern **multi-factor models** (Fama–French 3-factor, Carhart 4-factor, etc.).
- **Stress Testing:** Banks use multi-index models to test how portfolios behave under scenarios like "oil shock + interest-rate hike".

### 2.5 Criteria for Selecting Indices (KTU Favourite Sub-Question)

A good index in the MIM must satisfy:

1. **Relevance** — must have a logical/economic link to security returns.
2. **Exogeneity** — must not be heavily influenced by the securities being analysed.
3. **Measurability** — return data must be available and reliable.
4. **Low correlation among themselves** — to avoid multicollinearity.
5. **Statistical significance** — the index must have a significant $\beta$ (high $t$-statistic).
6. **Incremental explanatory power** — adding the index should materially raise $R^{2}$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"REMEMBER-ME"* — **R**elevance, **E**xogeneity, **M**easurability, **E**xplanatory power, **M**ulticollinearity check, **B**eta significance, **E**conomy of factors.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Variance Formula

Starting from the model equation:

$$R_{i} = \alpha_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, I_{j} + e_{i}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the variance operator on both sides.**

Because $\alpha_{i}$ is a constant, $\mathrm{Var}(\alpha_{i}) = 0$.

$$\sigma_{i}^{2} = \mathrm{Var}(R_{i}) = \mathrm{Var}\!\left(\alpha_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, I_{j} + e_{i}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Use the property that the variance of a sum equals the sum of variances plus twice the pairwise covariances.**

For a general sum $X = A + B + C$:
$$\mathrm{Var}(X) = \mathrm{Var}(A) + \mathrm{Var}(B) + \mathrm{Var}(C) + 2\mathrm{Cov}(A,B) + 2\mathrm{Cov}(A,C) + 2\mathrm{Cov}(B,C)$$

Applying this with $A = \sum_{j} \beta_{ij} I_{j}$ and $B = e_{i}$:

$$\sigma_{i}^{2} = \mathrm{Var}\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, I_{j}\right) + \mathrm{Var}(e_{i}) + 2\,\mathrm{Cov}\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, I_{j},\, e_{i}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the assumption that the residual $e_{i}$ is uncorrelated with all index returns $I_{j}$.**

By the MIM assumption: $E(e_{i}\, I_{j}) = 0 \;\;\forall\, j$, hence $\mathrm{Cov}(I_{j}, e_{i}) = 0$. Therefore the cross-term vanishes:

$$\mathrm{Cov}\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, I_{j},\, e_{i}\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, \mathrm{Cov}(I_{j}, e_{i}) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Expand the variance of the systematic part.**

Using the general formula for the variance of a linear combination:

$$\mathrm{Var}\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, I_{j}\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}^{2}\, \mathrm{Var}(I_{j}) + 2 \sum_{1 \le j < k \le n} \beta_{ij}\beta_{ik}\, \mathrm{Cov}(I_{j}, I_{k})$$

**Step 5 — Combine all terms into the final general variance formula.**

$$\sigma_{i}^{2} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2} + 2 \sum_{j<k} \beta_{ij}\beta_{ik}\, \mathrm{Cov}(I_{j}, I_{k}) + \sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify under the common assumption that indices are mutually uncorrelated.**

If the chosen indices are uncorrelated: $\mathrm{Cov}(I_{j}, I_{k}) = 0$ for $j \neq k$. The middle term drops out, leaving the **clean MIM variance formula**:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{i}^{2} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2} + \sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The form $\sum \beta_{ij}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2}$ is mathematically identical to a **sum of squared weighted sinusoids in signal processing**, where each factor is a "frequency" and each $\beta_{ij}^{2}$ is its "power contribution" to the total signal energy (= variance).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Covariance Formula

For two securities $i$ and $k$:

$$R_{i} = \alpha_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, I_{j} + e_{i}, \quad R_{k} = \alpha_{k} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{kj}\, I_{j} + e_{k}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the covariance operator on $R_{i}$ and $R_{k}$.**

$$\sigma_{ik} = \mathrm{Cov}(R_{i}, R_{k}) = \mathrm{Cov}\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, I_{j} + e_{i},\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{kj}\, I_{j} + e_{k}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Expand using bilinearity of covariance.**

$$\sigma_{ik} = \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{l=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\beta_{kl}\, \mathrm{Cov}(I_{j}, I_{l}) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, \mathrm{Cov}(I_{j}, e_{k}) + \sum_{l=1}^{n} \beta_{kl}\, \mathrm{Cov}(e_{i}, I_{l}) + \mathrm{Cov}(e_{i}, e_{k})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the MIM independence assumptions.**

- $\mathrm{Cov}(I_{j}, e_{k}) = 0$ for all $j$ (residuals uncorrelated with indices).
- $\mathrm{Cov}(e_{i}, I_{l}) = 0$ for all $l$.
- $\mathrm{Cov}(e_{i}, e_{k}) = 0$ for $i \neq k$ (residuals of different securities uncorrelated).

The last three terms vanish, leaving:

$$\sigma_{ik} = \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{l=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\beta_{kl}\, \mathrm{Cov}(I_{j}, I_{l})$$

**Step 4 — Simplify under the assumption of uncorrelated indices.**

If indices are uncorrelated: $\mathrm{Cov}(I_{j}, I_{l}) = 0$ when $j \neq l$, and $\mathrm{Cov}(I_{j}, I_{l}) = \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2}$ when $j = l$. The double sum collapses to a single sum:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{ik} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\beta_{kj}\, \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2}\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Consider a **2-index model** with the following parameters for two stocks, A and B:

| Parameter | Stock A | Stock B |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | 0.5 % | 0.8 % |
| $\beta_{1}$ (Index 1: Nifty 50) | 0.9 | 1.2 |
| $\beta_{2}$ (Index 2: Crude Oil) | 0.4 | $-0.3$ |
| $\sigma_{e}^{2}$ (residual variance) | 25 $(\%)^{2}$ | 36 $(\%)^{2}$ |

Index variances: $\sigma_{I_{1}}^{2} = 64 \;(\%)^{2}$, $\sigma_{I_{2}}^{2} = 49 \;(\%)^{2}$. Indices are uncorrelated.

**Step 1 — Compute total variance of Stock A.**

$$\sigma_{A}^{2} = \beta_{A1}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{1}}^{2} + \beta_{A2}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{2}}^{2} + \sigma_{e_{A}}^{2}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\sigma_{A}^{2} = (0.9)^{2} \cdot 64 + (0.4)^{2} \cdot 49 + 25$$

$$= 0.81 \cdot 64 + 0.16 \cdot 49 + 25$$

$$= 51.84 + 7.84 + 25$$

$$\sigma_{A}^{2} = 84.68 \;(\%)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total variance of Stock B.**

$$\sigma_{B}^{2} = (1.2)^{2} \cdot 64 + (-0.3)^{2} \cdot 49 + 36$$

$$= 1.44 \cdot 64 + 0.09 \cdot 49 + 36$$

$$= 92.16 + 4.41 + 36$$

$$\sigma_{B}^{2} = 132.57 \;(\%)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute covariance between Stock A and Stock B.**

$$\sigma_{AB} = \beta_{A1}\beta_{B1}\, \sigma_{I_{1}}^{2} + \beta_{A2}\beta_{B2}\, \sigma_{I_{2}}^{2}$$

$$= (0.9)(1.2)(64) + (0.4)(-0.3)(49)$$

$$= 69.12 + (-5.88)$$

$$\sigma_{AB} = 63.24 \;(\%)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute correlation coefficient.**

$$\rho_{AB} = \frac{\sigma_{AB}}{\sigma_{A}\, \sigma_{B}} = \frac{63.24}{\sqrt{84.68} \cdot \sqrt{132.57}}$$

$$= \frac{63.24}{9.202 \cdot 11.514} = \frac{63.24}{105.95} \approx 0.597$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R^{2}$ (goodness of fit) for Stock A.**

$$R_{A}^{2} = \frac{\beta_{A1}^{2}\sigma_{I_{1}}^{2} + \beta_{A2}^{2}\sigma_{I_{2}}^{2}}{\sigma_{A}^{2}} = \frac{51.84 + 7.84}{84.68} = \frac{59.68}{84.68} \approx 0.7047$$

So the **2-index model explains about 70.47 %** of Stock A's return variance — a substantial improvement over what a single-index model would typically capture.

**Step 6 — Verify systematic and unsystematic risk split for Stock B.**

Systematic risk: $92.16 + 4.41 = 96.57 \;(\%)^{2}$.

Unsystematic risk: $36 \;(\%)^{2}$.

Total: $96.57 + 36 = 132.57 = \sigma_{B}^{2}$ ✓ *(checks out)*

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Always show the *check-sum* (systematic + unsystematic = total) in your final answer — KTU examiners award **partial marks** for the verification step even when the final number has a small arithmetic slip.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*State the multi-index model equation and explain the meaning of each term. How is it different from the single index model?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The multi-index model expresses the return on a security as a linear function of returns on multiple macroeconomic indices plus a residual:

$$R_{i} = \alpha_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, I_{j} + e_{i}$$

- $R_{i}$ = return on security $i$
- $\alpha_{i}$ = expected return when all index returns are zero
- $\beta_{ij}$ = sensitivity of security $i$ to index $j$
- $I_{j}$ = return on index $j$
- $e_{i}$ = firm-specific residual return

**Difference from single index model (SIM):**
- SIM uses only one factor (the market index); MIM uses $n \ge 2$ factors.
- MIM has $n$ betas per stock instead of one.
- MIM captures industry-specific and macro-economic influences that SIM ignores, hence higher explanatory power ($R^{2}$).
- MIM is closer in spirit to Ross's Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT).

*(Word limit: 80–100 words. Marks awarded for equation — 1, definitions — 1, comparison — 1.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Explain the concept of systematic and unsystematic risk in the context of the multi-index model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In the multi-index model:
$$R_{i} = \alpha_{i} + \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}\, I_{j}}_{\text{Systematic}} + \underbrace{e_{i}}_{\text{Unsystematic}}$$

- **Systematic Risk** = $\sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2}$. It arises from common macro-economic factors (market, oil, interest rates) that affect all securities simultaneously. It is **non-diversifiable** — even a fully diversified portfolio cannot eliminate it.

- **Unsystematic Risk** = $\sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$. It arises from firm-specific events (strikes, lawsuits, product launches). It is **diversifiable** — by holding a large number of stocks in a portfolio, this component averages out toward zero.

*(Marks: definition of systematic — 1, unsystematic — 1, diversification implication — 1.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Answer **any one** of the following: **Question A or Question B**. Each carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the assumptions and the structure of the multi-index model. Derive the formula for the total variance of a security's return in the multi-index framework. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 3-index model. The data for Stock X and Stock Y are given below:

| Parameter | Stock X | Stock Y |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ (% per month) | 0.6 | 0.4 |
| $\beta_{1}$ (Industrial Production) | 1.2 | 0.8 |
| $\beta_{2}$ (Crude Oil Price) | 0.5 | $-0.6$ |
| $\beta_{3}$ (10-yr G-Sec Yield) | $-0.3$ | 0.7 |
| $\sigma_{e}^{2}$ $(\%)^{2}$ | 20 | 30 |

Index variances: $\sigma_{I_{1}}^{2} = 36$, $\sigma_{I_{2}}^{2} = 64$, $\sigma_{I_{3}}^{2} = 25$ $(\%)^{2}$. Indices are uncorrelated.

Compute:
(i) Total variance of Stock X and Stock Y.
(ii) Covariance between Stock X and Stock Y.
(iii) Correlation coefficient between the two stocks. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Assumptions, Structure, and Variance Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions of the Multi-Index Model:**
1. Security returns are linearly related to a set of index returns.
2. Residuals $e_{i}$ are mutually uncorrelated: $E(e_{i}\, e_{k}) = 0$ for $i \neq k$.
3. Residuals are uncorrelated with the index returns: $E(e_{i}\, I_{j}) = 0 \;\forall j$.
4. Index returns may be correlated with each other (general case) or assumed uncorrelated (simplified case).

**Structure:** The model postulates
$$R_{i} = \alpha_{i} + \beta_{i1} I_{1} + \beta_{i2} I_{2} + \dots + \beta_{in} I_{n} + e_{i}$$
where the variance has two parts — systematic (driven by indices) and unsystematic (residual).

**Derivation of Variance:**

$$\sigma_{i}^{2} = \mathrm{Var}\!\left(\alpha_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij} I_{j} + e_{i}\right)$$

Using bilinearity of variance and the assumption that $e_{i}$ is independent of all $I_{j}$:

$$\sigma_{i}^{2} = \mathrm{Var}\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij} I_{j}\right) + \mathrm{Var}(e_{i}) + 2\,\mathrm{Cov}\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij} I_{j}, e_{i}\right)$$

The cross-covariance term is zero by assumption. Expanding the systematic variance using the general linear-combination rule:

$$\mathrm{Var}\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij} I_{j}\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2} + 2 \sum_{j<k} \beta_{ij}\beta_{ik}\, \mathrm{Cov}(I_{j}, I_{k})$$

Hence the **general MIM variance formula**:

$$\sigma_{i}^{2} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2} + 2 \sum_{j<k} \beta_{ij}\beta_{ik}\, \mathrm{Cov}(I_{j}, I_{k}) + \sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$$

If indices are mutually uncorrelated (a common simplifying assumption in KTU problems), the middle term vanishes:

$$\sigma_{i}^{2} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2} + \sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$$

*(Marks distribution: assumptions — 2 marks; structure — 1 mark; derivation — 4 marks.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Total variance of Stock X:**

$$\sigma_{X}^{2} = \beta_{X1}^{2}\sigma_{I_{1}}^{2} + \beta_{X2}^{2}\sigma_{I_{2}}^{2} + \beta_{X3}^{2}\sigma_{I_{3}}^{2} + \sigma_{e_{X}}^{2}$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma_{X}^{2} = (1.2)^{2}(36) + (0.5)^{2}(64) + (-0.3)^{2}(25) + 20$$

$$= 1.44 \cdot 36 + 0.25 \cdot 64 + 0.09 \cdot 25 + 20$$

$$= 51.84 + 16.00 + 2.25 + 20$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{X}^{2} = 90.09 \;(\%)^{2}}$$

**Total variance of Stock Y:**

$$\sigma_{Y}^{2} = (0.8)^{2}(36) + (-0.6)^{2}(64) + (0.7)^{2}(25) + 30$$

$$= 0.64 \cdot 36 + 0.36 \cdot 64 + 0.49 \cdot 25 + 30$$

$$= 23.04 + 23.04 + 12.25 + 30$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{Y}^{2} = 88.33 \;(\%)^{2}}$$

**(ii) Covariance between Stock X and Stock Y:**

$$\sigma_{XY} = \beta_{X1}\beta_{Y1}\sigma_{I_{1}}^{2} + \beta_{X2}\beta_{Y2}\sigma_{I_{2}}^{2} + \beta_{X3}\beta_{Y3}\sigma_{I_{3}}^{2}$$

$$= (1.2)(0.8)(36) + (0.5)(-0.6)(64) + (-0.3)(0.7)(25)$$

$$= 34.56 + (-19.20) + (-5.25)$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{XY} = 10.11 \;(\%)^{2}}$$

**(iii) Correlation coefficient:**

$$\sigma_{X} = \sqrt{90.09} \approx 9.491 \;\%$$

$$\sigma_{Y} = \sqrt{88.33} \approx 9.399 \;\%$$

$$\rho_{XY} = \frac{\sigma_{XY}}{\sigma_{X} \cdot \sigma_{Y}} = \frac{10.11}{9.491 \times 9.399} = \frac{10.11}{89.21}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{XY} \approx 0.113}$$

**Interpretation:** Although the two stocks have similar total variances (90.09 vs 88.33), their correlation is very low (~0.11). This is because their factor exposures have offsetting signs (Stock X is hurt by interest rate rises while Stock Y benefits). Such a pair is **excellent for diversification**.

*(Marks distribution: variance X — 2; variance Y — 2; covariance — 2; correlation — 1.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2025, Model Paper]

**(a)** Compare the Single Index Model and the Multi-Index Model. Discuss the criteria for selecting the indices in a multi-index framework. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-index model, the following data are estimated for a portfolio of three stocks A, B, C:

| Stock | $\beta_{1}$ (Market) | $\beta_{2}$ (Industry) | $\sigma_{e}^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1.1 | 0.4 | 30 |
| B | 0.7 | 1.2 | 20 |
| C | 1.4 | $-0.2$ | 25 |

Index variances: $\sigma_{I_{1}}^{2} = 100$, $\sigma_{I_{2}}^{2} = 64 \;(\%)^{2}$. Indices are uncorrelated.

Compute:
(i) The systematic and unsystematic variance of each stock.
(ii) The covariance between every pair of stocks.
(iii) Comment on which pair offers the best diversification benefit. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Comparison and Index Selection (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Single Index Model | Multi-Index Model |
|---|---|---|
| Number of factors | 1 (market index) | $n \ge 2$ macro/factor indices |
| Beta per stock | One $\beta$ | $n$ betas ($\beta_{i1}, \beta_{i2}, \dots, \beta_{in}$) |
| Variance formula | $\sigma_{i}^{2} = \beta_{i}^{2}\sigma_{M}^{2} + \sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$ | $\sigma_{i}^{2} = \sum_{j} \beta_{ij}^{2}\sigma_{I_{j}}^{2} + \sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$ |
| Covariance | $\sigma_{ij} = \beta_{i}\beta_{j}\sigma_{M}^{2}$ | $\sigma_{ij} = \sum_{k} \beta_{ik}\beta_{jk}\sigma_{I_{k}}^{2}$ |
| $R^{2}$ | Usually 25–35% | Usually 50–75% |
| Data requirement | Moderate | High |
| Conceptual link | Sharpe (1963) | Ross APT (1976) extension |

**Criteria for Selecting Indices:**

1. **Economic Justification:** There must be a theoretical reason to expect the index to influence security returns.
2. **Statistical Significance:** The estimated $\beta$ should have a high $t$-statistic (typically $> 2$).
3. **Incremental Explanatory Power:** Adding the index should noticeably raise the $R^{2}$.
4. **Low Multicollinearity:** Indices should not be highly correlated with each other.
5. **Measurability & Availability:** Index data should be easily obtainable over a long period.
6. **Parsimony (Occam's Razor):** Add factors only as long as the marginal $R^{2}$ improvement justifies the added complexity.
7. **Exogeneity:** The index should be largely unaffected by the securities being modelled.

*(Marks: comparison — 4, criteria — 3.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Systematic and unsystematic variance of each stock:**

| Stock | Systematic Variance $\sum \beta_{ij}^{2}\sigma_{I_{j}}^{2}$ | Unsystematic $\sigma_{e}^{2}$ | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | $(1.1)^{2}(100) + (0.4)^{2}(64) = 121 + 10.24 = 131.24$ | 30 | 161.24 |
| B | $(0.7)^{2}(100) + (1.2)^{2}(64) = 49 + 92.16 = 141.16$ | 20 | 161.16 |
| C | $(1.4)^{2}(100) + (-0.2)^{2}(64) = 196 + 2.56 = 198.56$ | 25 | 223.56 |

**(ii) Covariance between every pair:**

$\sigma_{AB} = \beta_{A1}\beta_{B1}\sigma_{I_{1}}^{2} + \beta_{A2}\beta_{B2}\sigma_{I_{2}}^{2} = (1.1)(0.7)(100) + (0.4)(1.2)(64)$

$$= 77.0 + 30.72 = 107.72 \;(\%)^{2}$$

$\sigma_{AC} = (1.1)(1.4)(100) + (0.4)(-0.2)(64) = 154.0 - 5.12 = 148.88 \;(\%)^{2}$

$\sigma_{BC} = (0.7)(1.4)(100) + (1.2)(-0.2)(64) = 98.0 - 15.36 = 82.64 \;(\%)^{2}$

**(iii) Correlation coefficients:**

$$\rho_{AB} = \frac{107.72}{\sqrt{161.24} \cdot \sqrt{161.16}} = \frac{107.72}{12.698 \cdot 12.695} \approx \frac{107.72}{161.21} \approx 0.668$$

$$\rho_{AC} = \frac{148.88}{\sqrt{161.24} \cdot \sqrt{223.56}} = \frac{148.88}{12.698 \cdot 14.952} \approx \frac{148.88}{189.86} \approx 0.784$$

$$\rho_{BC} = \frac{82.64}{\sqrt{161.16} \cdot \sqrt{223.56}} = \frac{82.64}{12.695 \cdot 14.952} \approx \frac{82.64}{189.81} \approx 0.435$$

**Comment on Diversification:**

The pair **(B, C)** offers the **best diversification benefit** because their correlation is the lowest ($\rho_{BC} \approx 0.435$). This is driven by the offsetting industry-factor exposures: Stock B has high positive industry loading (+1.2) while Stock C has a negative industry loading (−0.2). When the industry factor moves, the two stocks move in opposite directions, reducing aggregate portfolio volatility.

In contrast, Stocks A and C are most highly correlated (≈ 0.784) because they share strong positive market exposure — they tend to move together during market swings, providing limited diversification.

*(Marks: variance table — 2; covariances — 2; correlations — 1.5; interpretation — 1.5.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Sign errors on $\beta$:** A negative $\beta$ must be cubed (or squared) correctly. When squaring, the sign disappears; when computing covariance (product of two betas), the sign matters!
> - **Forgetting the unsystematic variance:** Students often compute only the systematic part and forget to add $\sigma_{e}^{2}$ for the total variance.
> - **Mixing up the formula for $\sigma_{ij}$ and $\sigma_{i}^{2}$:** The covariance has $\beta_{ik}\beta_{jk}$ (no square), while variance has $\beta_{ij}^{2}$ (one beta squared).
> - **Unit confusion:** $\sigma^{2}$ is in $(\%)^{2}$, $\sigma$ is in $\%$. Do not equate them.
> - **Stopping at one step:** Always present the final boxed answer with units. KTU examiners often refuse partial credit if units are missing.
> - **In Q1 Part A:** Many students write the single-index equation by mistake. Read "multi-index" carefully!

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Core Equation:** $R_{i} = \alpha_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij} I_{j} + e_{i}$.
- **Three Components:** Constant ($\alpha_{i}$), Systematic (factor-driven), Unsystematic (firm-specific).
- **Variance Formula (uncorrelated indices):** $\sigma_{i}^{2} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{ij}^{2}\, \sigma_{I_{j}}^{2} + \sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$.
- **Variance Formula (correlated indices):** $\sigma_{i}^{2} = \sum_{j,k} \beta_{ij}\beta_{ik}\,\mathrm{Cov}(I_{j},I_{k}) + \sigma_{e_{i}}^{2}$.
- **Covariance Formula (uncorrelated indices):** $\sigma_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \beta_{ik}\beta_{jk}\, \sigma_{I_{k}}^{2}$.
- **Coefficient of Determination:** $R^{2} = \dfrac{\text{Systematic Variance}}{\text{Total Variance}}$.
- **Correlation:** $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ij}}{\sigma_{i}\, \sigma_{j}}$.
- **Key Assumptions:** Residuals mutually uncorrelated, residuals uncorrelated with indices, indices may or may not be mutually correlated.
- **Systematic vs Unsystematic Risk:** Systematic = non-diversifiable; Unsystematic = diversifiable in large portfolios.
- **Index Selection Criteria:** Economic relevance, statistical significance, incremental $R^{2}$, low multicollinearity, measurability, parsimony, exogeneity.
- **Single → Multi Index:** SIM is the special case $n=1$ of MIM.
- **Relation to APT:** MIM is a *practical, observable-index version* of the more general Arbitrage Pricing Theory.
- **Why multi-index?** Captures industry, macro, and policy-related risks that a single market index misses; substantially raises $R^{2}$.
- **Memory Hook for KTU:** *"ALPHA, BETAs, INDICES, RESIDUAL → VARIANCE, COVARIANCE, R-SQUARE, PORTFOLIO."*
- **Pitfalls to avoid:** Sign errors, missing $\sigma_{e}^{2}$, missing units, forgetting cross-terms in correlated-index case.
- **Examiner's expectation:** Always present a check-sum (systematic + unsystematic = total) and a clear, boxed final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Index Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Single Index Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Single Index Model (SIM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Index Model (SIM):** A statistical model, originally proposed by **William F. Sharpe (1963)**, that decomposes the return of any individual risky asset into three components — (i) a constant expected return (`α`), (ii) a systematic component driven by the return of a common **market index** (`R_M`) scaled by a sensitivity coefficient (**β**), and (iii) a **firm-specific (idiosyncratic) residual** (`e_i`) that is uncorrelated with the market.

In equation form:

$$R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i \, R_M + e_i$$

where $R_i$ and $R_M$ are expressed as **excess returns** (returns in excess of the risk‑free rate $R_f$) under the standard CAPM‑linked formulation.

The model is formally called the **Market Model** in its regression form, and the **Single Index Model** when used for portfolio simplification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain‑English Analogy — "The Sea and the Boats"**
>
> Imagine a vast sea (the **market**). Every boat on the sea (each **stock**) rises and falls partly because of the sea itself (the tide, waves — these are the **systematic / market‑wide forces**) and partly because of its own weight, the number of passengers, holes in its hull, etc. (the **firm‑specific** factors).
>
> - The **tide** is the same for every boat at a given moment → this is `R_M`.
> - Some boats (e.g., small fishing boats) react strongly to a small tide; others (large cargo ships) barely move → this reaction is **β**.
> - The little wobble of a particular boat *after* the tide is accounted for is `e_i`.
>
> Therefore, instead of tracking *every boat's wobble relative to every other boat* (which would require thousands of calculations in a Markowitz framework), we only need to know how each boat responds to the common tide. This is the **massive computational simplification** that the SIM provides.

### 1.3 Salient Features of the SIM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Highlights (Board‑Favourite Points):**
>
> 1. Reduces the number of input estimates from $\dfrac{N(N+1)}{2}$ (Markowitz) to roughly **3N + 1**.
> 2. Uses a **single common factor** — the return on a broad market index (e.g., NIFTY 50, S&P 500, BSE Sensex).
> 3. Splits total risk (`σ_i²`) into **Systematic Risk** ($\beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2$) and **Unsystematic Risk** ($\sigma_{ei}^2$).
> 4. Enables construction of an **efficient portfolio** using only $\beta$ and $\sigma_e$.
> 5. Forms the statistical backbone of the **Treynor Index, Sharpe Index, Jensen's Alpha, and the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**.

### 1.4 Standard Terminology & Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $R_i$ | Excess return on stock $i$ | — |
| $\alpha_i$ | Intercept / abnormal return of stock $i$ | Small in equilibrium |
| $\beta_i$ | Sensitivity of $R_i$ to market movements | $0 \le \beta_i \le 2$ (typical) |
| $R_M$ | Excess return on the market index | — |
| $e_i$ | Residual / firm‑specific return | $E(e_i)=0$ |
| $\sigma_{ei}^2$ | Residual variance of stock $i$ | Always $> 0$ |
| $\sigma_M^2$ | Variance of market index returns | — |
| $\sigma_i^2$ | Total variance of stock $i$ | $\sigma_{ei}^2 + \beta_i^2\sigma_M^2$ |
| $\rho_i^2$ / $R_i^2$ | Coefficient of determination | $0 \le R_i^2 \le 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High‑Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Assumptions

The Single Index Model rests on **four classical assumptions**, all of which are routinely tested in KTU examination questions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of the SIM:**
>
> 1. **Linearity:** $E(R_i \mid R_M)$ is a linear function of $R_M$.
> 2. **Zero‑mean residuals:** $E(e_i) = 0$ for every $i$.
> 3. **Residuals uncorrelated with the market:** $\text{Cov}(e_i, R_M) = 0$.
> 4. **Cross‑independence of residuals:** $\text{Cov}(e_i, e_j) = 0$ for all $i \ne j$.

The fourth assumption is what produces the **drastic reduction** in the number of covariances that must be estimated. It implies that **all covariance between any two stocks is transmitted solely through their common dependence on $R_M$.**

## 2.2 Variance Decomposition — The Heart of SIM

Starting from $R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_M + e_i$ and taking variance:

$$\sigma_i^2 = \beta_i^2 \, \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ei}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - **$\beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2$** = Systematic (Market) Risk — *cannot* be diversified away.
> - **$\sigma_{ei}^2$** = Unsystematic (Firm‑Specific) Risk — *can* be eliminated through diversification.

## 2.3 Covariance and Correlation Between Two Stocks

Because $e_i$ and $e_j$ are independent:

$$\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = \beta_i \, \beta_j \, \sigma_M^2$$

$$\rho_{ij} = \frac{\beta_i \, \beta_j \, \sigma_M^2}{\sigma_i \, \sigma_j}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board‑Tip:** Two stocks are correlated **only because they respond to the same market index**. If $\beta_i = 0$ or $\beta_j = 0$, then $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = 0$ even if both are risky.

## 2.4 Coefficient of Determination ($R_i^2$)

The fraction of total risk that is **systematic**:

$$R_i^2 = \frac{\beta_i^2 \, \sigma_M^2}{\sigma_i^2} = \frac{\beta_i^2 \, \sigma_M^2}{\beta_i^2 \, \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ei}^2}$$

The fraction that is **diversifiable**:

$$1 - R_i^2 = \frac{\sigma_{ei}^2}{\sigma_i^2}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_M + e_i$ | Basic regression / market model |
| 2 | $\beta_i = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2}$ | Slope of regression |
| 3 | $\sigma_i^2 = \beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ei}^2$ | Total variance split |
| 4 | $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = \beta_i \beta_j \sigma_M^2$ | Pairwise covariance |
| 5 | $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{\beta_i \beta_j \sigma_M^2}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}$ | Pairwise correlation |
| 6 | $R_i^2 = \dfrac{\beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2}{\sigma_i^2}$ | % systematic risk |
| 7 | $1 - R_i^2 = \dfrac{\sigma_{ei}^2}{\sigma_i^2}$ | % diversifiable risk |
| 8 | $E(R_i) = \alpha_i + \beta_i \, E(R_M)$ | Expected excess return |
| 9 | $E(R_p) = \alpha_p + \beta_p \, E(R_M)$ | Portfolio expected return |
| 10 | $\beta_p = \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i \beta_i$ | Portfolio beta |
| 11 | $\sigma_{ep}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 \sigma_{ei}^2$ | Portfolio residual variance |
| 12 | $\sigma_p^2 = \beta_p^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ep}^2$ | Portfolio total variance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic — "S‑I‑M = Systematic In Market":**
> The **first term** of every variance/covariance formula is the **Systematic part** (always involves $\sigma_M^2$); the **second term** is the **Idiosyncratic part** (always involves $\sigma_{ei}^2$).

## 2.6 Computational Advantage over Markowitz

For a universe of $N$ securities:

| Model | Variances to Estimate | Covariances to Estimate | Total Inputs |
|-------|-----------------------|-------------------------|---------------|
| Markowitz | $N$ | $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | $\dfrac{N(N+1)}{2}$ |
| **SIM** | $N+1$ ($\sigma_M^2$ + $N$ $\sigma_{ei}^2$) | $0$ (derived) | **$3N + 1$** (incl. $N$ betas) |

> [!NOTE]
> For $N = 100$ securities: Markowitz needs **5,050** inputs, while SIM needs only **301**. This is why SIM is the workhorse of large‑scale empirical portfolio construction and the foundation of the **Sharpe‑Lintner CAPM**.

## 2.7 Real‑World Utility

- **Portfolio Construction:** Practitioners use SIM to build the efficient frontier with $O(N)$ inputs instead of $O(N^2)$.
- **Performance Evaluation:** Jensen's $\alpha$, Treynor ratio, Appraisal ratio all rely on SIM regressions.
- **Index Tracking & ETF Design:** Tracker funds minimise $\sigma_e^2$ against a chosen index.
- **Risk Management:** VaR (Value at Risk) decomposition splits exposure into market vs. idiosyncratic components using $\beta$ and $\sigma_e$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step‑by‑Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Variance Decomposition

We begin with the regression form of the model:

$$R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_M + e_i$$

**Step 1 — Compute the expectation** (using $E(e_i)=0$ and $E(\alpha_i)=\alpha_i$):

$$E(R_i) = \alpha_i + \beta_i \, E(R_M)$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the expectation from both sides** to obtain deviations:

$$R_i - E(R_i) = \beta_i \, [R_M - E(R_M)] + e_i$$

Let $u_i = R_i - E(R_i)$, $u_M = R_M - E(R_M)$, and $u_{ei} = e_i - E(e_i) = e_i$. Then:

$$u_i = \beta_i \, u_M + u_{ei}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the variance operator** to both sides:

$$\text{Var}(u_i) = \text{Var}(\beta_i u_M + u_{ei})$$

**Step 4 — Expand using the variance of a sum.** Since $u_{ei}$ is uncorrelated with $u_M$ by assumption 3, the cross term vanishes:

$$\text{Var}(u_i) = \text{Var}(\beta_i u_M) + \text{Var}(u_{ei}) + 2 \, \text{Cov}(\beta_i u_M, u_{ei})$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.** The cross covariance is zero:

$$\sigma_i^2 = \beta_i^2 \, \text{Var}(u_M) + \text{Var}(u_{ei})$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_i^2 = \beta_i^2 \, \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ei}^2}$$

This is the **fundamental variance decomposition** of the SIM.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Covariance Between Two Stocks

Starting from the deviation form for two stocks $i$ and $j$:

$$u_i = \beta_i u_M + u_{ei}$$

$$u_j = \beta_j u_M + u_{ej}$$

**Step 1 — Take the covariance:**

$$\text{Cov}(u_i, u_j) = \text{Cov}(\beta_i u_M + u_{ei},\; \beta_j u_M + u_{ej})$$

**Step 2 — Expand bilinearly** into four terms:

$$\text{Cov}(u_i, u_j) = \beta_i \beta_j \, \text{Cov}(u_M, u_M) + \beta_i \, \text{Cov}(u_M, u_{ej}) + \beta_j \, \text{Cov}(u_{ei}, u_M) + \text{Cov}(u_{ei}, u_{ej})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the SIM assumptions:**
- $\text{Cov}(u_M, u_{ej}) = 0$ (residual uncorrelated with market).
- $\text{Cov}(u_{ei}, u_M) = 0$ (same reason).
- $\text{Cov}(u_{ei}, u_{ej}) = 0$ (cross‑independence of residuals).
- $\text{Cov}(u_M, u_M) = \sigma_M^2$.

**Step 4 — Only the first term survives:**

$$\boxed{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = \beta_i \, \beta_j \, \sigma_M^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** All pairwise covariances depend only on the **two betas and the single market variance**. No $N(N-1)/2$ individual covariances are required.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coefficient of Determination ($R_i^2$)

**Step 1 — Start from the definition** of $R_i^2$ as the ratio of explained variance to total variance:

$$R_i^2 = \frac{\text{Variance explained by market}}{\text{Total variance}} = \frac{\beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2}{\sigma_i^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the variance decomposition** $\sigma_i^2 = \beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ei}^2$:

$$\boxed{R_i^2 = \frac{\beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2}{\beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ei}^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Range check:** As $\sigma_{ei}^2 \to 0$ (the stock is purely market‑driven), $R_i^2 \to 1$. As $\sigma_{ei}^2 \to \infty$ (firm‑specific noise dominates), $R_i^2 \to 0$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Portfolio Beta and Residual Variance

For a portfolio with weights $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_N$ where $\sum X_i = 1$:

**Step 1 — Portfolio return:**

$$R_p = \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i R_i = \sum X_i (\alpha_i + \beta_i R_M + e_i)$$

**Step 2 — Group the terms:**

$$R_p = \underbrace{\sum X_i \alpha_i}_{\alpha_p} + \underbrace{\left(\sum X_i \beta_i\right)}_{\beta_p} R_M + \underbrace{\sum X_i e_i}_{e_p}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the portfolio parameters:**

$$\alpha_p = \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i \alpha_i$$

$$\beta_p = \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i \beta_i$$

$$e_p = \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i e_i$$

**Step 4 — Residual variance of the portfolio.** Because residuals are cross‑independent:

$$\sigma_{ep}^2 = \text{Var}(e_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 \sigma_{ei}^2$$

**Step 5 — Total portfolio variance** (applying derivation 1 to the portfolio):

$$\boxed{\sigma_p^2 = \beta_p^2 \, \sigma_M^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^2 \, \sigma_{ei}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asymptotic Property:** As $N \to \infty$ with equal weights $X_i = 1/N$, the second term $\sum (1/N^2) \sigma_{ei}^2 \to 0$. Therefore **all unsystematic risk is diversified away**, and only systematic risk $\beta_p^2 \sigma_M^2$ survives. This is the empirical rationale behind **broad‑based index investing**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Question.** Consider three stocks with the following SIM parameters:

| Stock | $\alpha_i$ | $\beta_i$ | $\sigma_{ei}^2$ |
|-------|-----------|----------|----------------|
| A | 2 % | 1.20 | 0.0040 |
| B | 1 % | 0.90 | 0.0025 |
| C | 3 % | 1.50 | 0.0064 |

The market variance is $\sigma_M^2 = 0.0225$. An investor forms an equally‑weighted portfolio ($X_A = X_B = X_C = 1/3$). Compute:
(a) Total variance of each stock.
(b) Pairwise covariance of each pair.
(c) Portfolio beta and residual variance.
(d) Portfolio total variance.

### Solution

**(a) Total variance of each stock** — use $\sigma_i^2 = \beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ei}^2$:

Stock A:
$$\sigma_A^2 = (1.20)^2 (0.0225) + 0.0040 = 0.0324 + 0.0040 = 0.0364$$

Stock B:
$$\sigma_B^2 = (0.90)^2 (0.0225) + 0.0025 = 0.018225 + 0.0025 = 0.020725$$

Stock C:
$$\sigma_C^2 = (1.50)^2 (0.0225) + 0.0064 = 0.050625 + 0.0064 = 0.057025$$

**(b) Pairwise covariances** — use $\text{Cov}(i,j) = \beta_i \beta_j \sigma_M^2$:

$$\text{Cov}(A,B) = (1.20)(0.90)(0.0225) = 0.0243$$

$$\text{Cov}(A,C) = (1.20)(1.50)(0.0225) = 0.0405$$

$$\text{Cov}(B,C) = (0.90)(1.50)(0.0225) = 0.030375$$

**(c) Portfolio beta and residual variance:**

$$\beta_p = \tfrac{1}{3}(1.20 + 0.90 + 1.50) = \tfrac{1}{3}(3.60) = 1.20$$

$$\sigma_{ep}^2 = \sum X_i^2 \sigma_{ei}^2 = \tfrac{1}{9}(0.0040 + 0.0025 + 0.0064) = \tfrac{1}{9}(0.0129) = 0.001433$$

**(d) Portfolio total variance:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = \beta_p^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ep}^2 = (1.20)^2 (0.0225) + 0.001433 = 0.0324 + 0.001433 = 0.033833$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Although each stock has a sizeable $\sigma_{ei}^2$, the portfolio's residual variance (0.001433) is much smaller than the average individual residual variance (~0.0043). Diversification at work.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High‑Level Conceptual Map (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT LAYER"]
        market["Market Index Return RM"]
        stockA["Stock A Return RA"]
        stockB["Stock B Return RB"]
        stockN["Stock N Return RN"]
    end

    subgraph REGRESSION["REGRESSION / ESTIMATION LAYER"]
        regA["Regression of RA on RM yields alpha_A, beta_A, sigma_eA2"]
        regB["Regression of RB on RM yields alpha_B, beta_B, sigma_eB2"]
        regN["Regression of RN on RM yields alpha_N, beta_N, sigma_eN2"]
    end

    subgraph PARAMETERS["PARAMETER OUTPUT LAYER"]
        betaSet["Vector of Betas (beta_1 ... beta_N)"]
        resVarSet["Vector of Residual Variances (sigma_e1^2 ... sigma_eN^2)"]
        marketVar["Market Variance sigma_M^2"]
    end

    subgraph RISK["RISK DECOMPOSITION LAYER"]
        sysRisk["Systematic Risk = beta_p^2 x sigma_M^2"]
        unsysRisk["Unsystematic Risk = sum of X_i^2 x sigma_ei^2"]
        totalRisk["Total Portfolio Risk = Systematic + Unsystematic"]
    end

    market --> regA
    market --> regB
    market --> regN
    stockA --> regA
    stockB --> regB
    stockN --> regN

    regA --> betaSet
    regB --> betaSet
    regN --> betaSet

    regA --> resVarSet
    regB --> resVarSet
    regN --> resVarSet

    market --> marketVar

    betaSet --> sysRisk
    marketVar --> sysRisk
    resVarSet --> unsysRisk

    sysRisk --> totalRisk
    unsysRisk --> totalRisk
```

## 4.2 Block‑Level Functional Architecture of the SIM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC["SOURCE BLOCK"]
        data["Historical Returns\nRM, RA, RB, ..., RN"]
    end

    subgraph EST["ESTIMATION BLOCK"]
        cov["Covariance Engine:\nCov(Ri, RM) and sigma_M^2"]
        beta["Beta Calculator:\nbeta_i = Cov(Ri, RM) / sigma_M^2"]
        alphaCalc["Alpha Calculator:\nalpha_i = E(Ri) - beta_i x E(RM)"]
        resVar["Residual Variance:\nsigma_ei^2 = sigma_i^2 - beta_i^2 x sigma_M^2"]
    end

    subgraph PORT["PORTFOLIO BLOCK"]
        weights["Weights X1, X2, ..., XN"]
        betaP["Portfolio Beta:\nbeta_p = sum X_i x beta_i"]
        resVarP["Portfolio Residual Variance:\nsigma_ep^2 = sum X_i^2 x sigma_ei^2"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT BLOCK"]
        eret["Expected Portfolio Return:\nE(Rp) = alpha_p + beta_p x E(RM)"]
        tvar["Total Portfolio Variance:\nsigma_p^2 = beta_p^2 x sigma_M^2 + sigma_ep^2"]
        sharpe["Sharpe Ratio:\nSR = E(Rp) / sigma_p"]
    end

    data --> cov
    cov --> beta
    cov --> alphaCalc
    cov --> resVar
    beta --> betaP
    resVar --> resVarP
    weights --> betaP
    weights --> resVarP
    alphaCalc --> eret
    betaP --> eret
    betaP --> tvar
    resVarP --> tvar
    eret --> sharpe
    tvar --> sharpe
```

## 4.3 Risk Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    totalRisk["Total Portfolio Risk sigma_p^2"]
    totalRisk --> systematic["Systematic Risk\nbeta_p^2 x sigma_M^2"]
    totalRisk --> unsystematic["Unsystematic Risk\nsigma_ep^2"]

    systematic --> noteSys["Cannot be diversified.\nDepends only on market moves."]

    unsystematic --> noteUnsys["Reducible via diversification.\nTends to 0 as N increases."]

    systematic --> examplesSys["Examples:\nInterest rate changes\nInflation shocks\nRecessions"]

    unsystematic --> examplesUnsys["Examples:\nLabour strikes\nProduct recalls\nEarnings surprises"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read These Diagrams in an Exam:** Use Block 4.2 as the canonical answer skeleton for any 7‑mark derivation question. The arrows justify each formula; examiners reward the *logical flow* between estimation → aggregation → output.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
**State the Single Index Model. List its four key assumptions.**

**Model Answer:**

The Single Index Model, proposed by **William Sharpe**, expresses the return of a security as a linear function of the return on a common market index:

$$R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_M + e_i$$

The four key assumptions are:
1. $E(e_i) = 0$ (zero‑mean residuals).
2. $\text{Cov}(e_i, R_M) = 0$ (residuals uncorrelated with market).
3. $\text{Cov}(e_i, e_j) = 0$ for $i \ne j$ (cross‑independence of residuals).
4. Returns are linearly related to the market index.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students frequently forget the **fourth assumption (linearity)**. Listing only three assumptions will cost you a full mark.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**Distinguish between systematic and unsystematic risk under the Single Index Model.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Systematic Risk | Unsystematic Risk |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| Symbol | $\beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2$ | $\sigma_{ei}^2$ |
| Source | Market‑wide factors (e.g., GDP, inflation, interest rates) | Firm‑specific factors (e.g., strikes, management failure) |
| Diversifiable? | **No** — affects all firms simultaneously | **Yes** — eliminated by holding a large portfolio |
| Measured by | $\beta$ | $\sigma_e$ |
| Affects | Every stock in the market | Only individual firms |

Total risk in SIM:

$$\sigma_i^2 = \underbrace{\beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2}_{\text{Systematic}} + \underbrace{\sigma_{ei}^2}_{\text{Unsystematic}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14‑Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU Model — Internal Choice Option 1]**

**[A] (a)** Derive the variance and covariance expressions under the Single Index Model, clearly stating the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**[A] (b)** A portfolio consists of three stocks with the following SIM parameters (excess returns in %):

| Stock | $\alpha_i$ (%) | $\beta_i$ | $\sigma_{ei}^2$ |
|-------|----------------|-----------|-----------------|
| P | 1.0 | 0.80 | 0.0016 |
| Q | 0.5 | 1.10 | 0.0036 |
| R | 2.0 | 1.30 | 0.0049 |

The market variance is $\sigma_M^2 = 0.0200$. Weights are $X_P = 0.5$, $X_Q = 0.3$, $X_R = 0.2$. Compute:
(i) Total variance of each stock.
(ii) Pairwise covariances.
(iii) Portfolio beta and residual variance.
(iv) Portfolio total variance and standard deviation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

**[A] (a) Derivation of variance and covariance under SIM:**

The market model for stock $i$ is:

$$R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_M + e_i$$

**Assumption 1:** $E(e_i) = 0$
**Assumption 2:** $\text{Cov}(e_i, R_M) = 0$
**Assumption 3:** $\text{Cov}(e_i, e_j) = 0$ for $i \ne j$

**Step 1 — Take the expectation** of both sides:

$$E(R_i) = \alpha_i + \beta_i \, E(R_M)$$

**Step 2 — Subtract means to get deviations** ($u_i = R_i - E(R_i)$, $u_M = R_M - E(R_M)$):

$$u_i = \beta_i u_M + u_{ei}$$

**Step 3 — Variance of stock $i$:**

$$\sigma_i^2 = \text{Var}(\beta_i u_M + u_{ei})$$

Since $\text{Cov}(u_M, u_{ei}) = 0$:

$$\sigma_i^2 = \beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ei}^2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 4 — Covariance between $i$ and $j$:**

$$\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = \text{Cov}(\beta_i u_M + u_{ei}, \; \beta_j u_M + u_{ej})$$

Expanding (four terms) and applying assumptions 2 and 3:

$$\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = \beta_i \beta_j \sigma_M^2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Conclusion:** Total variance is the sum of systematic and unsystematic parts, while the entire covariance between any two stocks is *transmitted through the single market index*. This produces the computational reduction central to SIM. **(7 marks)**

---

**[A] (b) Numerical computation:**

**(i) Total variance of each stock** — use $\sigma_i^2 = \beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ei}^2$:

Stock P:
$$\sigma_P^2 = (0.80)^2 (0.0200) + 0.0016 = 0.0128 + 0.0016 = 0.0144$$

Stock Q:
$$\sigma_Q^2 = (1.10)^2 (0.0200) + 0.0036 = 0.0242 + 0.0036 = 0.0278$$

Stock R:
$$\sigma_R^2 = (1.30)^2 (0.0200) + 0.0049 = 0.0338 + 0.0049 = 0.0387$$

**(ii) Pairwise covariances** — use $\text{Cov}(i,j) = \beta_i \beta_j \sigma_M^2$:

$$\text{Cov}(P,Q) = (0.80)(1.10)(0.0200) = 0.01760$$

$$\text{Cov}(P,R) = (0.80)(1.30)(0.0200) = 0.02080$$

$$\text{Cov}(Q,R) = (1.10)(1.30)(0.0200) = 0.02860$$

**(iii) Portfolio parameters:**

$$\beta_p = 0.5(0.80) + 0.3(1.10) + 0.2(1.30) = 0.40 + 0.33 + 0.26 = 0.99$$

$$\sigma_{ep}^2 = (0.5)^2(0.0016) + (0.3)^2(0.0036) + (0.2)^2(0.0049)$$
$$= 0.000400 + 0.000324 + 0.000196 = 0.000920$$

**(iv) Portfolio total variance:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.99)^2 (0.0200) + 0.000920 = 0.019602 + 0.000920 = 0.020522$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.020522} = 0.1433 \text{ (i.e., 14.33%)}$$ **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU Model — Internal Choice Option 2]**

**[B] (a)** Explain the concept of the coefficient of determination ($R_i^2$) under the Single Index Model. Discuss its significance in the context of portfolio diversification. **(7 marks)**

**[B] (b)** An analyst collects the following data for two stocks (excess returns in %) and a market index:

| Period | $R_A$ (%) | $R_B$ (%) | $R_M$ (%) |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | 8 | 5 | 6 |
| 2 | 10 | 7 | 8 |
| 3 | -2 | 1 | -1 |
| 4 | 6 | 4 | 5 |
| 5 | 4 | 3 | 3 |

Estimate $\beta_A$ and $\beta_B$ using the SIM regression. Comment on which stock is more volatile relative to the market. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

**[B] (a) Coefficient of Determination ($R_i^2$):**

The coefficient of determination $R_i^2$ measures the proportion of total return variability of stock $i$ that is explained by its linear relationship with the market index.

$$R_i^2 = \frac{\beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2}{\sigma_i^2} = \frac{\beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2}{\beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ei}^2}$$

**Significance:**
- A high $R_i^2$ (close to 1) indicates that **most of the stock's risk is systematic**, leaving little room for diversification benefit.
- A low $R_i^2$ (close to 0) indicates that **most of the stock's risk is unsystematic** and can be largely eliminated by adding it to a diversified portfolio.
- For a well‑diversified portfolio, the *aggregate* $R_p^2$ approaches 1 as $N$ increases, because all $\sigma_{ei}^2$ terms wash out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** $R_i^2$ is a direct measure of **diversification efficiency** at the individual stock level. Investors constructing the optimal risky portfolio under SIM should prefer stocks with **low $R_i^2$** (since they offer the most diversifiable risk) provided their $\alpha_i$ is non‑negative. **(7 marks)**

---

**[B] (b) Estimation of betas:**

**Step 1 — Compute the means:**

$$\bar{R}_A = \frac{8 + 10 + (-2) + 6 + 4}{5} = \frac{26}{5} = 5.2$$

$$\bar{R}_B = \frac{5 + 7 + 1 + 4 + 3}{5} = \frac{20}{5} = 4.0$$

$$\bar{R}_M = \frac{6 + 8 + (-1) + 5 + 3}{5} = \frac{21}{5} = 4.2$$

**Step 2 — Compute deviations** ($u = R - \bar{R}$):

| Period | $u_A$ | $u_B$ | $u_M$ | $u_A \cdot u_M$ | $u_M^2$ |
|--------|-------|-------|-------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | 2.8 | 1.0 | 1.8 | 5.04 | 3.24 |
| 2 | 4.8 | 3.0 | 3.8 | 18.24 | 14.44 |
| 3 | -7.2 | -3.0 | -5.2 | 37.44 | 27.04 |
| 4 | 0.8 | 0.0 | 0.8 | 0.64 | 0.64 |
| 5 | -1.2 | -1.0 | -1.2 | 1.44 | 1.44 |
| **Sum** | **0.0** | **0.0** | **0.0** | **62.80** | **46.80** |

**Step 3 — Apply the beta formula** $\beta_i = \dfrac{\sum u_i u_M}{\sum u_M^2}$:

$$\beta_A = \frac{62.80}{46.80} = 1.3419$$

$$\beta_B = \frac{\sum u_B u_M}{\sum u_M^2} = \frac{24.40}{46.80} = 0.5214$$

(where $\sum u_B u_M = 1.8 + 11.4 + 15.6 + 0.0 + 1.2 = 30.0$... let me recompute: $1.0(1.8) + 3.0(3.8) + (-3.0)(-5.2) + 0.0(0.8) + (-1.0)(-1.2) = 1.8 + 11.4 + 15.6 + 0 + 1.2 = 30.0$; so $\beta_B = 30.0 / 46.80 = 0.6410$.)

$$\beta_B = \frac{30.00}{46.80} = 0.6410$$

**Comment:** Stock A ($\beta_A \approx 1.34$) is **more volatile than the market** and would amplify market movements. Stock B ($\beta_B \approx 0.64$) is **less volatile than the market** and would dampen market movements. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Students often forget to **subtract the means** before forming products — this gives an *intercept‑biased* beta. Always use deviations.
> 2. Do not round intermediate calculations; carry full precision until the final answer.
> 3. The market variance for this dataset is $\sigma_M^2 = 46.80 / (n-1) = 46.80/4 = 11.70$ if you are using the *sample* formula — KTU generally uses the *population* denominator $n$, so $\sigma_M^2 = 46.80/5 = 9.36$. Be consistent with the formula stated in the question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid‑Revision Checklist — Single Index Model**

- **Core equation:** $R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_M + e_i$.
- **Beta** = sensitivity of stock return to market return; $\beta_M = 1$.
- **Alpha** = abnormal return not explained by the market.
- **Total variance:** $\sigma_i^2 = \beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ei}^2$.
- **Covariance (the killer simplification):** $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = \beta_i \beta_j \sigma_M^2$.
- **Correlation:** $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{\beta_i \beta_j \sigma_M^2}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}$.
- **Coefficient of determination:** $R_i^2 = \dfrac{\beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2}{\sigma_i^2}$.
- **Portfolio beta:** $\beta_p = \sum X_i \beta_i$.
- **Portfolio residual variance:** $\sigma_{ep}^2 = \sum X_i^2 \sigma_{ei}^2$.
- **Portfolio total variance:** $\sigma_p^2 = \beta_p^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{ep}^2$.
- **Diversification:** As $N \to \infty$, $\sigma_{ep}^2 \to 0$ → only systematic risk remains.
- **Assumptions (all four!):** $E(e_i)=0$, $\text{Cov}(e_i,R_M)=0$, $\text{Cov}(e_i,e_j)=0$, linearity.
- **Computational edge:** Reduces inputs from $N(N+1)/2$ to $\approx 3N+1$.
- **Connectors:** SIM is the empirical bridge to **CAPM**, **Treynor ratio**, **Jensen's alpha**, and **Sharpe's market model**.
- **Board buzzwords:** "systematic vs unsystematic", "single factor", "Sharpe 1963", "computational simplification".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equilibrium Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Equilibrium Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What Are Equilibrium Models in Financial Economics?

An **Equilibrium Model** in financial economics is a theoretical framework that determines the fair price or expected return of an asset under the condition that **demand equals supply** in a competitive capital market. In equilibrium, no investor can systematically earn abnormal profits, and all available information is already reflected in market prices.

The two most celebrated equilibrium models taught universally in B.Tech (Open Elective) Financial Economics are:

1. **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** — built on mean-variance optimisation by Markowitz.
2. **Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT)** — built on the no-arbitrage principle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Definition (must be memorised verbatim):**
> *"An equilibrium model describes the state of a financial market in which the price of every asset is such that the quantity demanded by all investors equals the quantity supplied, and no participant has an incentive to alter their holdings."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine a **village vegetable market** where 100 farmers bring tomatoes and 500 buyers come to purchase them. The auctioneer shouts a price — if the price is too high, buyers leave and tomatoes rot (supply > demand). If the price is too low, buyers fight each other and farmers lose money (demand > supply). 

The **equilibrium price** is the single price at which *exactly* every tomato is sold to *exactly* one willing buyer — no surplus, no shortage, no regrets.

In the **stock market**, "tomatoes" are stocks, "farmers" are sellers, and "buyers" are investors. The **equilibrium price** is set so that every stock is held by the investor who values it most, and no investor can earn extra profit just by switching stocks — this is the *no-arbitrage condition*.

---

## 1.3 Why Equilibrium Models Matter

| Aspect | Engineering / Real-World Utility |
|---|---|
| **Portfolio Construction** | Engineers-turned-CFOs use CAPM to decide hurdle rates for new projects |
| **Cost of Capital** | WACC calculations for corporate finance rely on CAPM's expected return |
| **Risk Pricing** | Insurance companies use equilibrium beta to price catastrophe bonds |
| **Investment Banking** | IPO valuations use comparable-firm betas from equilibrium pricing |
| **Treasury Management** | Bond desks use APT factor sensitivities to hedge macro risk |
| **Quant Finance** | Algorithmic trading models verify no-arbitrage violations across exchanges |

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Memorise These)

- **Risk-free rate ($R_f$):** Typically the **10-year Government Treasury yield**, treated as the price of money with zero default risk.
- **Market Risk Premium ($E(R_m) - R_f$):** Historically observed to be **6%–8%** in mature equity markets.
- **Beta ($\beta$):** A unit-less number. $\beta = 1$ means the asset moves with the market; $\beta > 1$ is aggressive; $\beta < 1$ is defensive.
- **Sharpe Ratio:** Reward-per-unit of total risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Prerequisite Recap (Module 1–3 background):** Before diving in, you must be comfortable with **expected return**, **variance**, **covariance**, **correlation**, and the concept of an **efficient frontier**. Equilibrium models are simply the *market-wide* extension of Markowitz's portfolio theory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Logic Stack of Equilibrium Models

### Step 1 — Investors Are Rational and Risk-Averse
Investors rank portfolios using **mean-variance preferences**: they love higher expected return and hate higher variance. This is the foundational axiom of CAPM.

### Step 2 — Homogeneous Expectations
All investors use the **same inputs** (same expected returns, variances, covariances). This is the *Single-Period, Single-Index* assumption.

### Step 3 — Existence of a Risk-Free Asset
Investors can borrow or lend at the **same risk-free rate $R_f$** — the "frictionless" assumption central to CAPM.

### Step 4 — Mean-Variance Optimisation (Markowitz)
Every investor constructs the **efficient frontier** — the set of portfolios with the lowest variance for every level of expected return.

### Step 5 — Introduction of $R_f$ Creates the Capital Market Line (CML)
When the risk-free asset is added, the optimal risky portfolio is unique: it is the **Market Portfolio $M$** — the tangency portfolio. The new efficient set is the straight line from $R_f$ tangent to the Markowitz bullet — this is the **Capital Market Line (CML)**.

### Step 6 — Market Portfolio Aggregates All Risky Assets
By aggregating all investors, equilibrium demand equals supply, and the **Market Portfolio $M$** contains every risky asset in proportion to its **market capitalisation**.

### Step 7 — Security Market Line (SML) Prices Individual Assets
Each asset's expected return is linearly related to its **beta** (systematic risk). This linear relationship is the **Security Market Line (SML)** — the equilibrium pricing equation.

---

## 2.2 The CAPM Equation — Step-by-Step Logic

The intuition: in equilibrium, the only risk that earns a premium is **non-diversifiable (systematic) risk**, measured by $\beta$. Idiosyncratic risk can be eliminated by holding a diversified portfolio, so the market does *not* reward it.

$$\boxed{E(R_i) \;=\; R_f \;+\; \beta_i \bigl[E(R_m) - R_f\bigr]}$$

where:
- $E(R_i)$ = Expected return on asset $i$
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate
- $\beta_i$ = Beta of asset $i$
- $E(R_m)$ = Expected return on the market portfolio
- $E(R_m) - R_f$ = **Market risk premium** (reward for bearing average market risk)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `\mid` style pipes inside table cells to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Expected Return of Asset** | $E(R_i)$ | Single-period mean return |
| 2 | **Beta Definition** | $\beta_i = \dfrac{Cov(R_i,\,R_m)}{Var(R_m)}$ | Systematic risk relative to market |
| 3 | **CAPM Equilibrium Equation** | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \bigl[E(R_m) - R_f\bigr]$ | Core pricing relation |
| 4 | **Capital Market Line (CML)** | $E(R_p) = R_f + \dfrac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m}\,\sigma_p$ | Only for *efficient* (fully diversified) portfolios |
| 5 | **Security Market Line (SML)** | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \bigl[E(R_m) - R_f\bigr]$ | Holds for *any* asset or portfolio |
| 6 | **Sharpe Ratio** | $S_p = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Reward per unit total risk |
| 7 | **Market Portfolio Variance** | $\sigma_m^2 = \sum_i w_i \, Cov(R_i,\,R_m)$ | Variance weighted by covariances |
| 8 | **APT (Multi-Factor)** | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_{i1}F_1 + \beta_{i2}F_2 + \dots + \beta_{in}F_n$ | $F_k$ = factor risk premium for factor $k$ |
| 9 | **No-Arbitrage Condition** | Two identical cash-flow streams must have the same price | Foundation of APT and derivative pricing |
| 10 | **Zero-Beta CAPM (Black)** | $E(R_i) = E(R_{Z}) + \beta_i\bigl[E(R_m) - E(R_{Z})\bigr]$ | When no risk-free asset exists |

---

## 2.4 CML vs. SML — A Critical KTU Distinction

| Feature | Capital Market Line (CML) | Security Market Line (SML) |
|---|---|---|
| **X-axis** | Total risk ($\sigma_p$) | Systematic risk ($\beta_i$) |
| **Applies to** | Only **efficient** portfolios | **Any** individual asset or portfolio |
| **Slope** | $\dfrac{E(R_m)-R_f}{\sigma_m}$ (Sharpe ratio of market) | $E(R_m) - R_f$ (market risk premium) |
| **Equation** | $E(R_p) = R_f + \dfrac{E(R_m)-R_f}{\sigma_m}\sigma_p$ | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i\bigl[E(R_m) - R_f\bigr]$ |
| **Graph Shape** | Straight line in $(\sigma, E(R))$ space | Straight line in $(\beta, E(R))$ space |

> [!WARNING]
> **Most common KTU mistake:** Students plot the CML with beta on the X-axis — that is *wrong*. CML uses total risk ($\sigma$), SML uses beta ($\beta$). Examiners frequently give 0 marks for swapping these.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, **every corporate finance decision in a B.Tech engineer's startup** flows through CAPM:
- Choosing the **discount rate** for an NPV calculation.
- Pricing an **infrastructure project** with public-private partnership.
- Setting the **hurdle rate** in a manufacturing capacity expansion.

For multi-factor risks (oil prices, exchange rates, interest rates), the **APT** is more realistic than CAPM, and is widely used by **oil & gas PSUs** and **multinational IT firms** in their treasury risk modelling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Beta from Covariance & Variance

### Statement
Beta of asset $i$ with respect to market $m$ is defined as the sensitivity of asset returns to market returns.

### Derivation

By definition of regression slope in a single-factor linear model:

$$R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_m + \varepsilon_i$$

Take covariance of both sides with $R_m$:

$$Cov(R_i,\,R_m) = Cov(\alpha_i + \beta_i R_m + \varepsilon_i,\,R_m)$$

Expand using linearity of covariance:

$$Cov(R_i,\,R_m) = Cov(\alpha_i,\,R_m) + \beta_i \, Cov(R_m,\,R_m) + Cov(\varepsilon_i,\,R_m)$$

Since $\alpha_i$ is a constant, $Cov(\alpha_i,\,R_m) = 0$. By assumption, the residual is uncorrelated with the market, so $Cov(\varepsilon_i,\,R_m) = 0$. Also, $Cov(R_m,\,R_m) = Var(R_m)$.

Therefore:

$$Cov(R_i,\,R_m) = \beta_i \, Var(R_m)$$

Solving for $\beta_i$:

$$\boxed{\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i,\,R_m)}{Var(R_m)}}$$

> **Interpretation:** If $Var(R_m) = 0.04$ and $Cov(R_i,\,R_m) = 0.06$, then $\beta_i = 1.5$ — the asset moves **1.5 times** as much as the market. KTU students must compute this confidently.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CAPM from the Two-Fund Separation Theorem

### Setup
- Two assets: the **risk-free asset** with return $R_f$, and the **market portfolio $M$** with return $R_m$.
- A portfolio $P$ invests fraction $w$ in $M$ and $(1-w)$ in $R_f$.

### Derivation

Step 1 — Expected return of $P$:

$$E(R_p) = w\,E(R_m) + (1-w)\,R_f$$

Step 2 — Variance of $P$ (risk-free asset has zero variance and zero covariance with $M$):

$$\sigma_p^2 = w^2 \sigma_m^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_p = \vert w \vert \,\sigma_m$$

Step 3 — Since investors choose $w$ freely:

$$w = \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m}$$

Step 4 — Substitute $w$ into the expected return equation:

$$E(R_p) = \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m} E(R_m) + \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m}\right) R_f$$

Step 5 — Rearrange:

$$E(R_p) = R_f + \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m}\,\sigma_p$$

This is the **Capital Market Line (CML)**.

> **Note:** The CML holds only for *efficient* portfolios formed from $R_f$ and $M$. Individual assets lie **below** the CML because their total risk is greater than their systematic risk.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Security Market Line from CML

### Goal
Show that *any* asset $i$ satisfies $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$.

### Derivation

Step 1 — Let asset $i$ have correlation $\rho_{i,m}$ with the market. The expected return of $i$ must be consistent with equilibrium pricing.

Step 2 — The covariance between $i$ and the market portfolio is:

$$Cov(R_i,\,R_m) = \rho_{i,m}\,\sigma_i\,\sigma_m$$

Step 3 — Define beta using the relation from Derivation 1:

$$\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i,\,R_m)}{Var(R_m)} = \frac{\rho_{i,m}\,\sigma_i\,\sigma_m}{\sigma_m^2} = \rho_{i,m}\,\frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_m}$$

Step 4 — In equilibrium, expected return must compensate only for **systematic (non-diversifiable) risk**, because idiosyncratic risk $\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2$ washes out in a large portfolio. The equilibrium condition yields:

$$E(R_i) - R_f = \beta_i \bigl[E(R_m) - R_f\bigr]$$

Step 5 — Final equilibrium pricing relation:

$$\boxed{E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \bigl[E(R_m) - R_f\bigr]}$$

This is the **SML equation** — the single most important formula in the module.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — APT Pricing Relation (Intuitive Sketch)

### Setup
Suppose asset returns follow a **factor model**:

$$R_i = E(R_i) + \beta_{i1} F_1 + \beta_{i2} F_2 + \varepsilon_i$$

where $F_1, F_2$ are zero-mean factor surprises (e.g., inflation surprise, GDP surprise), and $\varepsilon_i$ is idiosyncratic.

### Equilibrium Argument
In equilibrium, an arbitrage portfolio constructed to be **zero-beta** to every factor, **zero-cost**, and **zero-idiosyncratic-risk** must earn **zero expected return** (else an unlimited profit is possible). This no-arbitrage condition implies:

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_{i1} \lambda_1 + \beta_{i2} \lambda_2 + \dots + \beta_{in} \lambda_n$$

where $\lambda_k = E(R_k) - R_f$ is the **risk premium** for factor $k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU caveat:** APT *does not* require mean-variance preferences, homogeneous expectations, or even a market portfolio. It only requires that arbitrage opportunities be eliminated. This makes APT more flexible than CAPM in real-world applications.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example

### Problem (KTU-Style)
A stock has $\beta = 1.4$, the risk-free rate is $R_f = 6\%$, and the expected market return is $E(R_m) = 14\%$.

**(a)** Calculate the expected return of the stock using CAPM.  
**(b)** If the stock actually delivered a 20% return last year, calculate the **alpha** ($\alpha$). Interpret.

### Solution

**Part (a):** Apply the CAPM equation.

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \bigl[E(R_m) - R_f\bigr]$$

Substitute $R_f = 0.06$, $\beta_i = 1.4$, $E(R_m) = 0.14$:

$$E(R_i) = 0.06 + 1.4 \times (0.14 - 0.06)$$

$$E(R_i) = 0.06 + 1.4 \times 0.08$$

$$E(R_i) = 0.06 + 0.112 = 0.172$$

Therefore, the **expected return is 17.2%**.

**Part (b):** Alpha is the deviation between realised and expected return.

$$\alpha = R_{actual} - E(R_i) = 0.20 - 0.172 = 0.028$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = +2.8\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** The stock **outperformed** the CAPM-implied equilibrium return by 2.8%. This is **positive alpha** — in an efficient market, this should not persist. Either the market is temporarily inefficient, or there is news-driven idiosyncratic information.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
