# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT). State any three of its key assumptions.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** APT is a multifactor asset pricing model proposed by **Stephen Ross (1976)** which states that the expected return on an asset is a linear function of the expected values of multiple macroeconomic (systematic) factors that influence asset returns, with the asset's sensitivity to each factor acting as the weight.

**Three Key Assumptions:**

1. **Capital Markets are Competitive:** No single investor can influence security prices; all are price-takers.

2. **K-Factor Structure:** Returns on all assets can be expressed as a linear function of a finite number $K$ of systematic factors plus an idiosyncratic (firm-specific) term.

3. **Arbitrage-Free Equilibrium:** In equilibrium, all arbitrage opportunities are eliminated. If mispricing arises, arbitrageurs act and restore equilibrium prices.

*(Equivalent alternative assumptions such as risk aversion, sufficiently many assets, and homogeneity of expectations are also accepted.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Distinguish between the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) and the Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT) in any four points.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Table:**

| Dimension | CAPM | APT |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Factors** | Single factor (market return) | Multiple factors (macro variables) |
| **Market Portfolio Requirement** | Requires identification of the true market portfolio | No market portfolio required |
| **Underlying Assumption** | Mean-variance optimization by all investors | No-arbitrage equilibrium |
| **Role of Investor Preferences** | Depends on investor utility / risk aversion | Does not depend on individual preferences |
| **Form of Relationship** | Single-beta linear relation | Multi-beta linear relation |
| **Empirical Testability** | Difficult (market portfolio unobservable) | More flexible and easier to test |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — 14 Marks]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the Arbitrage Pricing Theory in detail. State and explain the three conditions that must be satisfied for a true arbitrage opportunity to exist.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
APT is a multifactor model proposed by Ross (1976). It specifies that the expected return of an asset depends linearly on its sensitivity to multiple systematic risk factors. The relationship is:

$$E(r_i) = r_f + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_{ik} \cdot \lambda_k$$

**Mechanism (3 Marks):**
- Factor loadings $\beta_{ik}$ are estimated via regression of asset returns on factor surprises.
- Factor risk premia $\lambda_k$ represent compensation per unit of exposure to factor $k$.
- In equilibrium, no asset should offer a return different from what APT predicts, because arbitrage would otherwise occur.

**Three Conditions for Arbitrage (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Zero Net Investment:** The arbitrage portfolio is self-financing — no capital is required. Mathematically: $\sum_i w_i = 0$, where $w_i$ are weights.

> 2. **Zero Systematic Risk:** The portfolio's exposure to every factor must be zero: $\sum_i w_i \beta_{ik} = 0$ for all $k = 1, 2, \ldots, K$.

> 3. **Positive Profit with Certainty (or Positive Expected Profit):** The portfolio must yield a positive return — i.e. $\sum_i w_i r_i > 0$ — with zero risk.

All three conditions together define a **pure arbitrage** — riskless profit with zero investment. Markets cannot tolerate such opportunities; arbitrageurs exploit them, and prices adjust until no such portfolio remains.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: A portfolio consists of three assets A, B, and C with the following factor sensitivities:**

| Asset | $\beta_{\text{Inflation}}$ | $\beta_{\text{GDP}}$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0.9 | 1.4 |
| B | 1.6 | 0.6 |
| C | 1.1 | 1.0 |

**Given $r_f = 6\%$, $\lambda_{\text{Inf}} = 4\%$, $\lambda_{\text{GDP}} = 5\%$, compute the expected return on each asset using the 2-factor APT model and identify any mispricing if the observed market expected returns are $E(r_A) = 14\%$, $E(r_B) = 16\%$, $E(r_C) = 13\%$.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — APT Expected Return for Asset A (2 Marks):**

$$E(r_A)^{\text{APT}} = 6\% + 0.9 \times 4\% + 1.4 \times 5\%$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= 6\% + 3.6\% + 7.0\% \\
&= 16.6\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — APT Expected Return for Asset B (2 Marks):**

$$E(r_B)^{\text{APT}} = 6\% + 1.6 \times 4\% + 0.6 \times 5\%$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= 6\% + 6.4\% + 3.0\% \\
&= 15.4\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — APT Expected Return for Asset C (2 Marks):**

$$E(r_C)^{\text{APT}} = 6\% + 1.1 \times 4\% + 1.0 \times 5\%$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= 6\% + 4.4\% + 5.0\% \\
&= 15.4\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison Table and Mispricing Identification (1 Mark):**

| Asset | Market $E(r)$ | APT $E(r)$ | Difference | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 14.0% | 16.6% | −2.6% | Underpriced (price too low) |
| B | 16.0% | 15.4% | +0.6% | Overpriced |
| C | 13.0% | 15.4% | −2.4% | Underpriced |

**Arbitrage Strategy:** Buy A and C (underpriced), short-sell B. As arbitrageurs trade, prices of A and C rise (expected returns fall), while price of B falls (expected return rises). Equilibrium restores when all three equal APT-implied returns.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — 14 Marks]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With the help of a suitable example, explain the construction of a well-diversified portfolio under the APT framework. Why is such a portfolio essential to APT?**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Well-Diversified Portfolio (3 Marks):**

A **well-diversified portfolio** contains a large number $N$ of small, equally-weighted positions, with $w_i = 1/N$ for each asset. The key feature is:

$$\text{Var}(R_p) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_{pk}^2 \sigma_{I_k}^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^2 \sigma_{e_i}^2$$

As $N \to \infty$, the second term (idiosyncratic risk) vanishes because:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^2 \sigma_{e_i}^2 = \frac{1}{N} \overline{\sigma_e^2} \to 0$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Var}(R_p) \xrightarrow{N \to \infty} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_{pk}^2 \sigma_{I_k}^2$$

**Numerical Example (2 Marks):**

Consider $N = 100$ assets with average idiosyncratic variance $\overline{\sigma_e^2} = 0.04$. The residual variance contribution is $0.04/100 = 0.0004$, which is negligible compared to typical systematic variances of $0.01$–$0.05$.

**Why It Is Essential to APT (2 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> APT requires well-diversified portfolios because the theory applies to portfolios where **only systematic (factor) risk matters**. By eliminating firm-specific noise, the portfolio return becomes a pure function of factor exposures, allowing the linear APT pricing equation to be derived without distortion from idiosyncratic shocks.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Critically evaluate the limitations of the Arbitrage Pricing Theory. How does APT compare with the Fama-French Three-Factor Model?**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of APT (4 Marks):**

1. **Factor Identification Problem:** APT does not specify which factors to use — it merely states that "some" factors matter. Empirical researchers must guess (inflation, GDP, term spread, etc.).

2. **No Guidance on Number of Factors:** The optimal $K$ is unknown — too few misses risk; too many causes estimation error.

3. **Assumption of Perfect Markets:** Real markets have transaction costs, taxes, short-sale constraints, and limits to arbitrage.

4. **Linearity Assumption:** Returns may have non-linear relationships with factors in reality.

5. **Estimation Error:** Factor betas and risk premia estimated from historical data are subject to sampling error.

6. **Stationarity:** APT assumes factor sensitivities and risk premia are stable over time, which may not hold across regimes.

**APT vs Fama-French Three-Factor Model (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> | Aspect | APT | Fama-French 3-Factor |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Theoretical Basis** | Pure equilibrium arbitrage argument | Empirical extension of CAPM |
> | **Factors** | Any macro factors (flexible) | Market (Mkt-RF), SMB (size), HML (value) |
> | **Type** | Theoretical pricing model | Empirical asset pricing model |
> | **Testable?** | Indirectly, via factor selection | Directly testable with stock returns data |
> | **Relationship** | APT is more general; Fama-French is a specific empirical implementation of multi-factor thinking |
>
> Fama-French can be viewed as a **specific operationalization** of the APT idea using three empirically motivated factors rather than macroeconomic surprises.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Lose Marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Factor Surprise $I_k$ with the Factor Risk Premium $\lambda_k$.** Remember: $I_k$ is a *random variable with mean zero* (this period's surprise); $\lambda_k = E(I_k)$ is the *expected surprise*, i.e. the risk premium. Students often mix these up and lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Verify Arbitrage Conditions.** A simple "the price is wrong, so arbitrage" answer is incomplete. You **must** explicitly check that the portfolio satisfies: (i) zero net investment, (ii) zero factor exposure, (iii) positive profit. Examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically to this.
>
> 3. **Treating APT and CAPM as Identical.** APT is more general. If only one factor (the market) is used, APT collapses to CAPM — but in general APT allows multiple factors. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 4. **Numerical Errors in Multi-Factor Calculations.** A 2-factor problem involves 4–5 multiplications; one arithmetic slip loses the entire 7-mark sub-part. **Always re-verify your multiplications.**
>
> 5. **Omitting the "Equilibrium Adjustment" Step.** After identifying arbitrage, you **must** state how market forces (buying/selling pressure) restore equilibrium. Many students stop at "arbitrage exists" — this loses at least 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Writing APT Equation Without Defining Variables.** Examiners deduct marks if you write $E(r_i) = r_f + \sum \beta_{ik} \lambda_k$ without stating what each symbol represents. **Always define symbols before using them.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for APT — Module 4**
>
> - APT is a **multifactor asset pricing model** proposed by **Stephen Ross (1976)**.
> - Core equation: $E(r_i) = r_f + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_{ik} \lambda_k$.
> - Random return decomposition: $r_i = E(r_i) + \sum \beta_{ik} I_k + e_i$.
> - $\beta_{ik}$ = factor loading (sensitivity) of asset $i$ to factor $k$.
> - $\lambda_k$ = risk premium on factor $k$.
> - $I_k$ = factor surprise with $E(I_k) = 0$.
> - $e_i$ = idiosyncratic firm-specific noise with $E(e_i) = 0$ and zero correlation with factors.
> - **Well-diversified portfolio** eliminates idiosyncratic risk as $N \to \infty$.
> - **True arbitrage requires three conditions:** zero investment, zero factor risk, positive return.
> - APT is more general than CAPM; reduces to CAPM if only the market factor is used.
> - APT does **not** require identification of the market portfolio (key advantage).
> - **Limitation:** APT does not specify the exact factors — this is the *factor identification problem*.
> - **Number of arbitrage opportunities available:** $N - K - 1$ free portfolios.
> - **Fama-French 3-factor model** is an empirical implementation of multi-factor logic.
> - Equilibrium arises because arbitrageurs exploit mispricings until no pure arbitrage remains.
> - Variance of well-diversified portfolio = only systematic factor variance; idiosyncratic term goes to $0$.
> - For 14-mark numericals: always compute APT return for each asset, then compare with market return, then identify arbitrage strategy and explain equilibrium restoration.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capital Market Line
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capital Market Line

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Capital Market Line (CML)** is a graphical representation in modern portfolio theory that depicts the linear relationship between the **expected return** and the **standard deviation (total risk)** of an *efficient portfolio* that combines a **risk-free asset** with a **risky market portfolio**.

Mathematically, the CML is the tangent line drawn from the **risk-free rate of return ($R_f$)** on the expected return axis to the **efficient frontier** of risky assets, where the point of tangency represents the **Market Portfolio (M)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The CML is plotted in **Expected Return vs. Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** space (not variance). It is exclusively applicable to *efficient portfolios* — i.e., portfolios that lie on the Markowitz Efficient Frontier. Individual securities and inefficient portfolios do **NOT** lie on the CML.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pizza Delivery Bike"

Imagine you are a pizza shop owner. You have two delivery options:

1. **A bicycle (Risk-Free Asset):** Predictable, no traffic risk, fixed return of 5% (no matter what). You earn exactly 5%.
2. **A sports bike (Risky Market Portfolio):** Can go fast on highways, but faces traffic, weather, and road risks. Average speed (return) is 12%, but speed (return) varies — sometimes 20%, sometimes 4%.

Now imagine you want to design a hybrid: 50% bicycle + 50% sports bike. Your expected return will be `(0.5 × 5%) + (0.5 × 12%) = 8.5%`. The total risk (variability) will be lower than 100% sports bike.

The **Capital Market Line** is essentially the **"menu" of all such hybrid combinations** — from 100% bicycle (lowest return, zero risk) → 50-50 mix → 100% sports bike (highest return, highest risk) → even borrowing money to buy *more* sports bikes than you can afford (leverage) for super-high returns.

The CML is the **straight line** that connects the bicycle to the sports bike and extends beyond — every point on this line is a possible optimal risk-return combination for a rational investor.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk-free rate of return** | $R_f$ | Typically 10-year government bond yield |
| **Expected return of market portfolio** | $E(R_m)$ | Weighted average return of all risky assets |
| **Standard deviation of market portfolio** | $\sigma_m$ | Total risk of the market portfolio |
| **Sharpe Ratio (Slope of CML)** | $S_m$ | $\frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Callout:** The **slope of the CML** is the **Sharpe Ratio** of the market portfolio, which quantifies the **excess return per unit of total risk**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation

The Capital Market Line emerges from **Markowitz's Modern Portfolio Theory (1952)** combined with **Tobin's Separation Theorem (1958)**. Its derivation is rooted in the following logic chain:

1. **Step 1 — Markowitz Efficient Frontier:** When an investor combines multiple risky assets, the set of portfolios that offer the **highest expected return for a given level of risk** forms the *efficient frontier* (a curved boundary in $E(R)$-$\sigma$ space).

2. **Step 2 — Introduction of Risk-Free Asset:** Once a **risk-free asset** (theoretical 0 variance, 0 default risk, e.g., government T-bills) is introduced, every investor can now construct portfolios that are *combinations* of the risk-free asset and any risky portfolio.

3. **Step 3 — Tobin's Two-Fund Separation Theorem:** All rational investors should hold a combination of:
   - The **risk-free asset ($R_f$)**
   - **One single optimal risky portfolio** (the tangency / market portfolio)

4. **Step 4 — Capital Market Line Emerges:** The line connecting $R_f$ and the Market Portfolio $M$ (the tangency point) is the CML. All investors — regardless of risk appetite — will choose a point on this line.

## 2.2 Two Regions of the CML

The CML is divided into two strategic regions:

- **Lending Region (Below M):** Investors place some money in $R_f$ and the rest in M. This is conservative — they "lend" at $R_f$.
- **Borrowing Region (Above M):** Investors borrow money *at* $R_f$ and invest the borrowed amount **plus** their own capital into M. This is leverage — risk and return are amplified.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values inside markdown tables (NEVER use raw `|`).

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CML Equation (Expected Return)** | $E(R_p) = R_f + \left[ \dfrac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \right] \cdot \sigma_p$ | $E(R_p)$ = Expected portfolio return (%), $R_f$ = Risk-free rate (%), $\sigma_p$ = Portfolio std. dev. (%) |
| 2 | **Sharpe Ratio (CML Slope)** | $S_p = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Excess return per unit of total risk |
| 3 | **Market Portfolio Sharpe Ratio** | $S_m = \dfrac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m}$ | Slope of CML itself |
| 4 | **Standard Deviation on CML** | $\sigma_p = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{(E(R_m) - R_f)/\sigma_m}$ | Risk required to achieve $E(R_p)$ |
| 5 | **Weight in Risky Portfolio (CML)** | $w_m = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{E(R_m) - R_f}$ | Fraction in market portfolio (residual $1-w_m$ in $R_f$) |
| 6 | **Weight in Risk-Free Asset** | $w_f = 1 - w_m$ | Negative $w_f$ implies borrowing |
| 7 | **Portfolio Return Decomposition** | $E(R_p) = w_f \cdot R_f + w_m \cdot E(R_m)$ | Linear combination form |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Economic Utility

- **Asset Pricing Benchmark:** CML serves as the foundation for **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** — a tool used by financial engineers to value stocks, bonds, and derivatives.
- **Portfolio Construction:** Robo-advisors and wealth managers use CML logic to construct optimal portfolios for clients.
- **Capital Budgeting:** Corporate finance teams use CML-derived discount rates (cost of capital) to evaluate infrastructure and R&D projects.
- **Risk Management:** Banks and insurance firms use CML to allocate capital between low-risk government bonds and high-risk equity instruments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CML Equation

### Setup
Consider a portfolio $P$ constructed by combining:
- A **risk-free asset** with return $R_f$ and $\sigma_f = 0$
- The **market portfolio** $M$ with expected return $E(R_m)$ and standard deviation $\sigma_m > 0$

Let $w_m$ be the proportion invested in $M$, and $w_f = 1 - w_m$ in the risk-free asset.

### Step 1: Expected Return of the Combined Portfolio

The expected return of a linear combination is the weighted average of expected returns:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(R_p) &= w_f \cdot R_f + w_m \cdot E(R_m) \\
       &= (1 - w_m) \cdot R_f + w_m \cdot E(R_m) \\
       &= R_f - w_m \cdot R_f + w_m \cdot E(R_m) \\
       &= R_f + w_m \cdot \bigl[ E(R_m) - R_f \bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The portfolio's expected return is the risk-free return plus a *risk premium* proportional to the weight in the market portfolio.

### Step 2: Standard Deviation of the Combined Portfolio

Since the risk-free asset has zero variance, the variance of $P$ is purely due to the risky component:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 &= w_f^2 \cdot \sigma_f^2 + w_m^2 \cdot \sigma_m^2 + 2 \cdot w_f \cdot w_m \cdot \text{Cov}(R_f, R_m) \\
\sigma_p^2 &= 0 + w_m^2 \cdot \sigma_m^2 + 0 \quad \text{(since } \sigma_f = 0 \text{ and Cov}(R_f, R_m) = 0\text{)} \\
\sigma_p   &= \vert w_m \vert \cdot \sigma_m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Because the risk-free asset is uncorrelated with the market, total risk scales *linearly* with the weight in the risky asset.

### Step 3: Eliminate $w_m$ to Get the CML

From Step 2:
$$
w_m = \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m}
$$

Substituting into Step 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(R_p) &= R_f + \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m} \cdot \bigl[ E(R_m) - R_f \bigr] \\
       &= R_f + \left( \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \right) \cdot \sigma_p
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Capital Market Line Equation** — a linear equation in $\sigma_p$ with:
> - **Y-intercept** = $R_f$ (the point where $\sigma_p = 0$)
> - **Slope** = $\dfrac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m}$ = **Sharpe Ratio of the Market Portfolio**

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Given $R_f = 6\%$, $E(R_m) = 14\%$, $\sigma_m = 20\%$. An investor desires a portfolio standard deviation of 15%. Find the expected return, weight in market portfolio, and weight in risk-free asset.

### Solution

**Given:** $R_f = 0.06$, $E(R_m) = 0.14$, $\sigma_m = 0.20$, $\sigma_p = 0.15$

**Step 1: Compute the Sharpe Ratio (Slope) of CML:**

$$
S_m = \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} = \frac{0.14 - 0.06}{0.20} = \frac{0.08}{0.20} = 0.40
$$

**Step 2: Compute Expected Return on the CML:**

$$
E(R_p) = R_f + S_m \cdot \sigma_p = 0.06 + (0.40)(0.15) = 0.06 + 0.06 = 0.12 = 12\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute Weight in Market Portfolio:**

$$
w_m = \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{E(R_m) - R_f} = \frac{0.12 - 0.06}{0.14 - 0.06} = \frac{0.06}{0.08} = 0.75 = 75\%
$$

**Step 4: Compute Weight in Risk-Free Asset:**

$$
w_f = 1 - w_m = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25 = 25\%
$$

**Step 5: Verification (using standard deviation check):**

$$
\sigma_p = w_m \cdot \sigma_m = 0.75 \times 0.20 = 0.15 = 15\% \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Analytical Matrix:** CML is the only line in $E(R)$-$\sigma$ space where every combination of $R_f$ and $M$ provides a *superior* Sharpe Ratio compared to any other risky portfolio on the Markowitz frontier. That is why the CML dominates the curved efficient frontier in the region from $R_f$ to $M$.

## 3.3 Leverage Scenario (Borrowing at $R_f$)

**Problem:** An investor wants a portfolio expected return of 18% using the same data. Find the required $\sigma_p$ and $w_m$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p &= \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{S_m} = \frac{0.18 - 0.06}{0.40} = 0.30 = 30\% \\
w_m &= \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{E(R_m) - R_f} = \frac{0.18 - 0.06}{0.14 - 0.06} = \frac{0.12}{0.08} = 1.50 = 150\% \\
w_f &= 1 - 1.50 = -0.50 = -50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **negative $w_f$** means the investor is **borrowing at the risk-free rate** to invest 150% of their own wealth in the market portfolio. This is the **leverage region** of the CML.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Capital Market Line — Graphical Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["Expected Return vs Total Risk Space"]
        A1["Y-axis: E(R) — Expected Return"]
        A2["X-axis: Sigma — Standard Deviation Total Risk"]
    end

    RfNode["POINT Rf\nRisk Free Asset\nSigma=0 E of R = Rf = 6 percent"]
    MNode["POINT M\nMarket Portfolio\nSigma m = 20 percent E of Rm = 14 percent"]
    P1Node["POINT P1\nLending Region\nSigma p1 = 10 percent E of Rp1 = 10 percent"]
    P2Node["POINT P2\nLending Region\nSigma p2 = 15 percent E of Rp2 = 12 percent"]
    P3Node["POINT P3\nLeverage Region\nSigma p3 = 30 percent E of Rp3 = 18 percent"]
    
    EffFront["Markowitz Efficient Frontier\nCurved boundary of risky portfolios"]
    
    CMLLine["CAPITAL MARKET LINE\nLinear boundary from Rf through M extending to leverage region"]
    
    RfNode --- P1Node
    P1Node --- P2Node
    P2Node --- MNode
    MNode --- P3Node
    
    MNode -. Tangent Point .-> EffFront
    CMLLine -. Dominates .-> EffFront
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CML Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Investor with Capital C"]
    RiskAversion{"Risk Aversion Level"}
    
    Conservative["Conservative Investor\nw f greater than 0\nw m between 0 and 1"]
    Moderate["Moderate Investor\nw f = 0\nw m = 1\nHold only market portfolio"]
    Aggressive["Aggressive Investor\nw f less than 0 BORROW\nw m greater than 1\nLeveraged position in M"]
    
    Combine["Combine Rf Asset + Market Portfolio M"]
    CMLCheck["Point lies on Capital Market Line"]
    Output1["Expected Return E of Rp"]
    Output2["Standard Deviation Sigma p"]
    Output3["Sharpe Ratio of Portfolio equals Sharpe of M"]
    
    Start --> RiskAversion
    RiskAversion -->|Low Risk Tolerance| Conservative
    RiskAversion -->|Medium Risk Tolerance| Moderate
    RiskAversion -->|High Risk Tolerance| Aggressive
    
    Conservative --> Combine
    Moderate --> Combine
    Aggressive --> Combine
    
    Combine --> CMLCheck
    CMLCheck --> Output1
    CMLCheck --> Output2
    CMLCheck --> Output3
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — CML vs SML

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        CML1["X axis: Standard Deviation Total Risk"]
        CML2["Y axis: Expected Return E of R"]

        CML4["Slope: Sharpe Ratio of Market"]
        CML5["Risk Measured: Total Risk Sigma"]
    end
    
    subgraph SMLBlock["SML Block Capital Asset Pricing Model"]
        SML1["X axis: Beta Systematic Risk"]
        SML2["Y axis: Expected Return E of R"]

        SML4["Slope: Market Risk Premium E of Rm minus Rf"]
        SML5["Risk Measured: Systematic Risk Beta only"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: Define Capital Market Line. State its equation.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Capital Market Line (CML)** is a linear graphical representation in Modern Portfolio Theory that depicts the relationship between the **expected return** and the **standard deviation (total risk)** of efficient portfolios formed by combining the risk-free asset with the optimal risky market portfolio. It is the tangent line drawn from the risk-free rate point to the Markowitz efficient frontier.

**Equation of CML:**

$$
E(R_p) = R_f + \left[ \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \right] \cdot \sigma_p
$$

Where $E(R_p)$ is the expected portfolio return, $R_f$ is the risk-free rate, $E(R_m)$ and $\sigma_m$ are the expected return and standard deviation of the market portfolio, and $\sigma_p$ is the standard deviation of the portfolio.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Capital Market Line and Security Market Line.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Capital Market Line (CML) | Security Market Line (SML) |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Measure** | Total risk (Standard Deviation, $\sigma$) | Systematic risk (Beta, $\beta$) |
| **Applicability** | Efficient portfolios only | All securities and portfolios |
| **Plots** | $E(R)$ vs. $\sigma$ | $E(R)$ vs. $\beta$ |
| **Slope** | Sharpe Ratio of market | Market Risk Premium $E(R_m) - R_f$ |
| **Context** | Portfolio theory | Asset pricing (CAPM) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks for: (1) confusing the CML equation with the CAPM/SML equation, (2) using **variance** instead of **standard deviation** in the denominator, (3) forgetting to convert percentages to decimals, and (4) failing to interpret whether the investor is in the **lending** or **borrowing/leverage** region based on the sign of $w_f$.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of the Capital Market Line (CML). Discuss its key assumptions and the significance of the Sharpe Ratio as the slope. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An investor is considering a portfolio that combines a risk-free asset yielding 5% with a market portfolio having an expected return of 13% and a standard deviation of 25%. If the investor desires a portfolio standard deviation of 20%, calculate: (i) the expected return, (ii) the weight in the market portfolio, and (iii) the weight in the risk-free asset. Comment on whether the investor is in the lending or leverage region. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

The **Capital Market Line (CML)** is the locus of all optimal portfolios that combine the risk-free asset ($R_f$) with the market portfolio ($M$) — the tangency portfolio of the Markowitz efficient frontier. It is represented in the **$E(R)$–$\sigma$** plane and its equation is:

$$
E(R_p) = R_f + \left[ \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \right] \cdot \sigma_p
$$

**Key Assumptions of CML:**

1. Investors are **rational** and **risk-averse**, preferring higher returns and lower risk. **[1 Mark]**
2. All investors have a **single-period investment horizon**. **[1 Mark]**
3. There exists a **risk-free asset** with zero default risk and zero variance. **[1 Mark]**
4. There are **no transaction costs, taxes, or restrictions** on borrowing/lending at $R_f$. **[1 Mark]**
5. Asset returns are **normally distributed**, and markets are **perfectly competitive** with **homogeneous expectations**. **[1 Mark]**

**Significance of the Sharpe Ratio as Slope:**

The slope of the CML is the **Sharpe Ratio of the market portfolio**, $S_m = \dfrac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m}$. It represents the **excess return (risk premium) earned per unit of total risk** taken. A higher Sharpe Ratio indicates a steeper CML and superior risk-adjusted performance. **[2 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- $R_f = 5\% = 0.05$
- $E(R_m) = 13\% = 0.13$
- $\sigma_m = 25\% = 0.25$
- $\sigma_p = 20\% = 0.20$

**(i) Expected Return of the Portfolio [3 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(R_p) &= R_f + \left( \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \right) \cdot \sigma_p \\
       &= 0.05 + \left( \frac{0.13 - 0.05}{0.25} \right) \cdot 0.20 \\
       &= 0.05 + \left( \frac{0.08}{0.25} \right) \cdot 0.20 \\
       &= 0.05 + (0.32)(0.20) \\
       &= 0.05 + 0.064 \\
       &= 0.114 = 11.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Weight in Market Portfolio [2 Marks]:**

$$
w_m = \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{E(R_m) - R_f} = \frac{0.114 - 0.05}{0.13 - 0.05} = \frac{0.064}{0.08} = 0.80 = 80\%
$$

**(iii) Weight in Risk-Free Asset [2 Marks]:**

$$
w_f = 1 - w_m = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20 = 20\%
$$

**Commentary:** Since $w_f = +20\% > 0$, the investor is in the **LENDING Region** — placing 20% of wealth in the risk-free asset and 80% in the market portfolio.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the equation of the Capital Market Line from first principles. Show clearly how the linear combination of a risk-free asset and the market portfolio leads to a straight-line relationship. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A financial analyst observes the following data: $R_f = 4\%$, $E(R_m) = 12\%$, $\sigma_m = 16\%$. An investor wants a target expected return of 20%. Determine: (i) the required portfolio standard deviation, (ii) the weight in the market portfolio, (iii) the weight in the risk-free asset, and (iv) state whether the investor is borrowing or lending. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

Let a portfolio $P$ be a combination of a risk-free asset (with return $R_f$, $\sigma_f = 0$) and the market portfolio $M$ (with $E(R_m)$, $\sigma_m$). Let $w_m$ be the weight in $M$ and $w_f = 1 - w_m$ in the risk-free asset.

**Step 1 — Expected Return of P [2 Marks]:**

$$
E(R_p) = w_f R_f + w_m E(R_m) = R_f + w_m [E(R_m) - R_f]
$$

**Step 2 — Standard Deviation of P [2 Marks]:**

Since the risk-free asset has zero variance and zero covariance with the market:

$$
\sigma_p = \vert w_m \vert \cdot \sigma_m \implies w_m = \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Derive CML Equation [3 Marks]:**

$$
E(R_p) = R_f + \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m} \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f] = R_f + \left( \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \right) \sigma_p
$$

This is the CML equation — a **straight line** in $E(R)$–$\sigma$ space with y-intercept $R_f$ and slope equal to the Sharpe Ratio of the market portfolio. The linearity arises because combining an asset with zero variance and zero correlation with a risky asset causes total risk to scale linearly with the weight in the risky asset.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $R_f = 0.04$, $E(R_m) = 0.12$, $\sigma_m = 0.16$, $E(R_p) = 0.20$

**Step 1 — Compute Sharpe Ratio (Slope):** **[1 Mark]**

$$
S_m = \frac{0.12 - 0.04}{0.16} = \frac{0.08}{0.16} = 0.50
$$

**(i) Required Portfolio Standard Deviation [2 Marks]:**

$$
\sigma_p = \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{S_m} = \frac{0.20 - 0.04}{0.50} = \frac{0.16}{0.50} = 0.32 = 32\%
$$

**(ii) Weight in Market Portfolio [2 Marks]:**

$$
w_m = \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{E(R_m) - R_f} = \frac{0.20 - 0.04}{0.12 - 0.04} = \frac{0.16}{0.08} = 2.0 = 200\%
$$

**(iii) Weight in Risk-Free Asset [1 Mark]:**

$$
w_f = 1 - 2.0 = -1.0 = -100\%
$$

**(iv) Borrowing or Lending? [1 Mark]:**

Since $w_f = -100\% < 0$, the investor is in the **BORROWING (LEVERAGE) Region** — they must borrow an amount equal to 100% of their own wealth at the risk-free rate of 4% and invest a total of 200% of their capital into the market portfolio.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Capital Market Line**

- **CML** plots $E(R)$ (y-axis) vs. **Standard Deviation $\sigma$** (x-axis) — *not* variance, *not* beta.
- CML applies **only to efficient portfolios**; individual stocks and inefficient portfolios do **NOT** lie on it.
- The CML is the **tangent line** from $R_f$ to the Markowitz Efficient Frontier at the **Market Portfolio (M)**.
- **Slope of CML = Sharpe Ratio of Market Portfolio** = $\dfrac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m}$.
- **Y-intercept = $R_f$** (risk-free rate).
- **CML Equation:** $E(R_p) = R_f + \dfrac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \cdot \sigma_p$.
- **Lending Region:** $w_f > 0$ (positive allocation to risk-free asset).
- **Leverage Region:** $w_f < 0$ (borrow at $R_f$ and invest more than 100% in M).
- **Weight Formula:** $w_m = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{E(R_m) - R_f}$.
- **Key Difference from SML:** CML uses total risk ($\sigma$); SML uses systematic risk ($\beta$).
- **Tobin's Separation Theorem:** All rational investors hold combinations of $R_f$ and $M$ — *one* optimal risky portfolio is sufficient for *all* investors.
- **Assumptions:** Risk aversion, single-period horizon, no transaction costs, no taxes, homogeneous expectations, existence of risk-free asset, normal distribution of returns.
- **Units:** Always express rates and standard deviations in the **same units** (preferably decimals) for consistent computation.
- **Practical Note:** The CML **dominates** the curved efficient frontier in the region from $R_f$ to $M$ — this dominance is the foundation of passive index investing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Market Line and Estimation Models of Beta for Investors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security Market Line and Estimation Models of Beta for Investors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Security Market Line (SML) — Formal Definition

The **Security Market Line (SML)** is the graphical representation of the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**. It depicts the linear relationship between the **expected return** of a financial security (or portfolio) and its **systematic (non-diversifiable) risk**, measured by the **Beta (β)** coefficient. The SML plots the **expected rate of return** on the vertical (Y) axis and the **beta (β)** on the horizontal (X) axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Definition):**
> The SML is the line of expected return–beta relationship derived from the CAPM equilibrium condition. Mathematically, the equation of the SML is:
> $$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$
> where $E(R_i)$ is the expected return on security $i$, $R_f$ is the risk-free rate, $\beta_i$ is the beta of security $i$, $E(R_m)$ is the expected return on the market portfolio, and $[E(R_m) - R_f]$ is the **Market Risk Premium (MRP)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Risk-Return Highway"

Imagine you are driving on a **highway** where each kilometre marker represents a different level of risk (beta). Toll booths at different markers represent the returns investors demand:

- At **km 0** (β = 0, zero risk), the toll is just the **risk-free rate** $R_f$ (e.g., Government Treasury bill rate ≈ **6% to 7%** in India, 2024).
- As you drive further out (higher β → more risk), the toll (expected return) increases **linearly** with the speed of risk-taking.
- The **slope** of this highway is the **Market Risk Premium** — the extra return the market pays per unit of beta risk.
- Vehicles parked **above the highway** are **underpriced bargains** (earning more than the fair return for their risk).
- Vehicles parked **below the highway** are **overpriced** (earning less than the fair return for their risk).

This highway is the **Security Market Line**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Vocabulary for KTU Module 4:**
>
> | Term | Meaning |
> |------|---------|
> | **Risk-Free Rate ($R_f$)** | Return on a default-free asset (e.g., 10-year Government of India G-Sec yield) |
> | **Market Portfolio ($M$)** | Theoretical portfolio containing **all** risky assets in proportion to their market value |
> | **Market Return ($R_m$)** | Expected return on the market index (e.g., Nifty 50, S&P 500, BSE Sensex) |
> | **Beta ($\beta_i$)** | Sensitivity of security $i$'s return to changes in market return |
> | **Market Risk Premium (MRP)** | Excess expected return of market over risk-free rate: $E(R_m) - R_f$ |
> | **Systematic Risk** | Market-wide, non-diversifiable risk (captured by beta) |
> | **Unsystematic Risk** | Firm-specific, diversifiable risk (averaged out in a portfolio) |

## 1.4 Why is the SML Important for an Investor?

1. **Valuation Tool:** Determines the **required rate of return** for any asset based on its systematic risk.
2. **Performance Evaluation:** A security plotting **above** the SML is delivering positive **alpha (α)** — outperforming the market on a risk-adjusted basis.
3. **Capital Budgeting:** Used as the **discount rate** in NPV calculations for projects with similar systematic risk.
4. **Portfolio Construction:** Helps in identifying mispriced securities and rebalancing portfolios.
5. **WACC Computation:** Inputs into the cost of equity for firms raising capital.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CAPM Equilibrium Condition — Foundation of SML

The CAPM, developed independently by **William Sharpe (1964)**, **John Lintner (1965)**, and **Jan Mossin (1966)**, builds on **Harry Markowitz's** Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT). It states that in **market equilibrium**, all investors hold a combination of the **risk-free asset** and the **market portfolio**. The expected return of any risky asset is determined by its contribution to portfolio risk — measured by **beta**.

The CAPM rests on the following critical assumptions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions of CAPM / SML (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):**
> 1. Investors are **rational**, **risk-averse**, and maximize expected utility of wealth.
> 2. All investors have a **single-period investment horizon**.
> 3. All investors have **homogeneous expectations** about returns, variances, and covariances.
> 4. There exists a **risk-free asset** at which investors can borrow or lend unlimited amounts.
> 5. **No taxes**, **no transaction costs**, and **no restrictions** on short-selling.
> 6. The market is perfectly competitive with many buyers and sellers.
> 7. Asset quantities are **fixed and divisible**.

## 2.2 The SML Equation — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The SML equation can be derived intuitively by recognizing that:

- The line must **pass through the point** $(0, R_f)$, because an asset with zero beta (no systematic risk) should earn exactly the risk-free rate.
- The line must **pass through the point** $(1, E(R_m))$, because the market portfolio has a beta of 1 by definition.
- The line is **linear** in beta.

Using the **two-point form** of a line:
$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$

### 2.2.1 Interpreting the Three Components

| Component | Symbol | Investor Interpretation |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------|
| Reward for waiting (time value of money) | $R_f$ | Minimum return for taking no risk |
| Reward for systematic risk | $\beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$ | Extra return demanded for bearing market risk |
| Total expected return | $E(R_i)$ | Fair compensation for total systematic risk |

## 2.3 SML vs. Capital Market Line (CML) — A Critical KTU Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the SML and CML. They look similar but differ in their **X-axis** and the **types of assets** they describe.

| Feature | CML (Capital Market Line) | SML (Security Market Line) |
|---------|---------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **X-axis** | Total Risk ($\sigma_p$, standard deviation) | Systematic Risk ($\beta$) |
| **Y-axis** | Expected Return $E(R_p)$ | Expected Return $E(R_i)$ |
| **Applies to** | Only **efficient** portfolios | **All** securities and portfolios |
| **Slope** | $\frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m}$ (Sharpe Ratio of Market) | $E(R_m) - R_f$ (Market Risk Premium) |
| **Equation** | $E(R_p) = R_f + \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \cdot \sigma_p$ | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$ |
| **Graphs** | Curved (Markowitz frontier) above CML | Straight line passing through $(0, R_f)$ and $(1, E(R_m))$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the SML in Engineering & Corporate Finance

Although this is a finance course, B.Tech graduates frequently work in **project finance, infrastructure consulting, manufacturing, and tech startups** where the SML is applied:

- **Cost of Equity for DCF Valuation:** A civil engineer evaluating a toll road project uses beta of comparable listed infrastructure firms to determine the discount rate.
- **Start-up Valuation:** A computer science entrepreneur pitching to VCs uses industry beta (e.g., IT services beta ≈ 0.9 to 1.2) to justify valuation.
- **Capital Budgeting:** A mechanical engineer assessing a new factory expansion uses SML-derived cost of equity to compute WACC and NPV.
- **Risk-Adjustment in Infrastructure:** Public-Private Partnership (PPP) models use SML-based discount rates set by the Government of India.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following formulas are exam-critical and must be memorized.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$ | SML equation (CAPM) | Percentage (%) |
| 2 | $\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)}$ | Beta as regression slope | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\beta_i = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_m}$ | Beta in terms of correlation | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $Cov(R_i, R_m) = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \sigma_i \cdot \sigma_m$ | Covariance identity | (%)$^2$ |
| 5 | $\alpha_i = R_i - [R_f + \beta_i(R_m - R_f)]$ | Jensen's Alpha (abnormal return) | Percentage (%) |
| 6 | $R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i \cdot R_m + e_i$ | Single Index (Market) Model | Percentage (%) |
| 7 | $R^2 = \frac{\beta_i^2 \cdot \sigma_m^2}{\sigma_i^2}$ | R-squared (% of risk that is systematic) | 0 to 1 |
| 8 | $\beta_{adj} = \frac{2}{3} \beta_{hist} + \frac{1}{3}(1)$ | Blume's Adjusted Beta | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Sharpe Ratio} = \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Reward per unit of total risk | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Treynor Ratio} = \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\beta_p}$ | Reward per unit of systematic risk | Dimensionless |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The SML Equation from First Principles

We derive the SML by starting from the **two-point form of a line**, using the fact that the SML must pass through two known points.

### Step 1: Identify the Two Reference Points

**Point A** — The Risk-Free Asset:
- Beta of risk-free asset: $\beta_f = 0$ (no correlation with market).
- Expected return: $E(R_f) = R_f$.
- Coordinate: $(0, R_f)$.

**Point B** — The Market Portfolio:
- Beta of market portfolio: $\beta_m = 1$ (by construction, the market is correlated 1:1 with itself).
- Expected return: $E(R_m)$.
- Coordinate: $(1, E(R_m))$.

### Step 2: Apply the Two-Point Form of a Line

For a line passing through $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$:
$$\frac{y - y_1}{x - x_1} = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$

Substituting:
$$\frac{E(R_i) - R_f}{\beta_i - 0} = \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{1 - 0}$$

### Step 3: Simplify

$$\frac{E(R_i) - R_f}{\beta_i} = E(R_m) - R_f$$

$$E(R_i) - R_f = \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$

### Step 4: Final SML Equation

$$\boxed{E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]}$$

This is the **fundamental CAPM equation** and the **equation of the SML**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Beta as the Slope of the Characteristic Line

The **Characteristic Line** is the linear regression of a security's returns against the market's returns. Its slope is the **beta**.

### Step 1: Set Up the Regression

$$R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i \cdot R_m + e_i$$

where:
- $R_i$ = Return on security $i$ in a given period.
- $\alpha_i$ = Intercept (Jensen's alpha).
- $\beta_i$ = Slope = sensitivity to market.
- $R_m$ = Market return in the same period.
- $e_i$ = Residual / error term (unsystematic return, $E[e_i] = 0$).

### Step 2: Apply the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Slope Formula

For the OLS regression of $R_i$ (Y) on $R_m$ (X):
$$\beta_i = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{N}(R_{m,t} - \bar{R_m})(R_{i,t} - \bar{R_i})}{\sum_{t=1}^{N}(R_{m,t} - \bar{R_m})^2}$$

### Step 3: Recognize the Statistical Identities

- **Numerator** = $N \cdot Cov(R_i, R_m)$.
- **Denominator** = $N \cdot Var(R_m)$.

### Step 4: Cancel $N$ and Obtain Beta

$$\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)}$$

### Step 5: Convert Using the Correlation Identity

Since $Cov(R_i, R_m) = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \sigma_i \cdot \sigma_m$ and $Var(R_m) = \sigma_m^2$:

$$\beta_i = \frac{\rho_{i,m} \cdot \sigma_i \cdot \sigma_m}{\sigma_m^2} = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_m}$$

### Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{\sigma_m^2} = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_m}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Blume's Adjusted Beta

**Marshall Blume (1971)** observed that historical betas tend to **regress toward the mean (β = 1)** over time. He proposed an adjusted beta:

### Step 1: Acknowledge the Empirical Tendency

Empirically: $\beta_{future} \approx 0.67 \cdot \beta_{past} + 0.33 \cdot 1$.

### Step 2: Apply the General Formula

For a weighting factor $\alpha$ (where $0 < \alpha < 1$):
$$\beta_{adj} = \alpha \cdot \beta_{hist} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot 1$$

### Step 3: Substitute $\alpha = 2/3$ (the empirical estimate by Blume)

$$\beta_{adj} = \frac{2}{3} \beta_{hist} + \frac{1}{3}(1)$$

### Step 4: Simplify

$$\boxed{\beta_{adj} = \frac{2}{3} \beta_{hist} + \frac{1}{3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** Value Line Investment Survey uses Blume's adjusted beta for all its stock recommendations. Bloomberg, Reuters, and NSE/BSE also use variations of this adjustment.

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Style Problem

> **Problem:** A security has the following statistics over the last 60 months:
> - Standard deviation of security returns: $\sigma_i = 18\%$
> - Standard deviation of market returns: $\sigma_m = 12\%$
> - Correlation coefficient: $\rho_{i,m} = 0.65$
> - Risk-free rate: $R_f = 6.5\%$
> - Expected market return: $E(R_m) = 14\%$
> - Actual average return on the security: $\bar{R_i} = 11\%$
>
> **Find:** (a) Beta, (b) Expected return per CAPM, (c) Jensen's Alpha.

### Solution

**(a) Compute Beta using $\beta_i = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_m}$:**

$$\beta_i = 0.65 \cdot \frac{18\%}{12\%} = 0.65 \cdot 1.5 = 0.975$$

**(b) Compute Expected Return via SML:**

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$

$$E(R_i) = 6.5\% + 0.975 \cdot [14\% - 6.5\%]$$

$$E(R_i) = 6.5\% + 0.975 \cdot 7.5\%$$

$$E(R_i) = 6.5\% + 7.3125\%$$

$$\boxed{E(R_i) = 13.8125\% \approx 13.81\%}$$

**(c) Compute Jensen's Alpha:**

$$\alpha_i = \bar{R_i} - E(R_i)$$

$$\alpha_i = 11\% - 13.81\%$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_i = -2.81\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Since $\alpha_i < 0$, the security **underperformed** the market on a risk-adjusted basis. It is plotted **below the SML** and is considered **overpriced**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Estimating Beta from Historical Data

| Method | Data Required | Formula | Strength | Weakness |
|--------|---------------|---------|----------|----------|
| **Historical / Sample Beta** | 60–120 months of $R_i$, $R_m$ | $\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)}$ | Simple, widely used | Sensitive to sample period; assumes stationarity |
| **Adjusted Beta (Blume)** | Historical beta | $\beta_{adj} = \frac{2}{3}\beta_{hist} + \frac{1}{3}$ | Accounts for mean reversion | Empirical coefficients may not hold across markets |
| **Fundamental Beta** | Accounting data (sales, earnings, leverage, dividend payout) | Regression on fundamentals | Forward-looking, useful for unlisted firms | Requires subjective weighting of factors |
| **Bottom-Up Beta** | Industry betas + firm-specific adjustments | $\beta_{firm} = \beta_{industry} \cdot (1 + (1-t)\frac{D}{E})$ | Reflects current business mix | Sensitive to leverage assumptions |
| **Comparable Firms Beta** | Beta of peer companies | Average or median peer beta | Useful for IPOs and private firms | May not capture firm-specific risk |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Security Market Line (Visual Geometry)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeRF["RF Point Beta 0 Return 6.5 percent"]:::rfNode
    nodeSML["SML Straight Line E Ri equals RF plus Beta times MRP"]:::smlNode
    nodeA["Security A Beta 0.5 Return 9 percent"]:::underNode
    nodeB["Market Beta 1 Return 14 percent"]:::marketNode
    nodeC["Security C Beta 1.3 Return 17 percent"]:::overNode
    nodeD["Security D Beta 0.8 Return 8 percent"]:::aboveNode
    nodeE["Security E Beta 1.5 Return 14 percent"]:::belowNode

    nodeRF --- nodeSML
    nodeSML --- nodeB
    nodeA --- nodeSML
    nodeC --- nodeSML
    nodeD --- nodeSML
    nodeE --- nodeSML

    classDef rfNode fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef smlNode fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef underNode fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef marketNode fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef overNode fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef aboveNode fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef belowNode fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **How to read this chart:**
> - The **green SML line** is the locus of all fairly priced securities.
> - **Security A** (low beta, low return) and **Security C** (high beta, high return) lie **on the SML** — fair return for risk.
> - **Security D** plots **above** the SML → **underpriced** → **positive alpha**.
> - **Security E** plots **below** the SML → **overpriced** → **negative alpha**.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Beta Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Start Investor Needs Beta for Security X"]:::startNode
    nodeStep1["Step 1 Collect 60 to 120 months of monthly returns R X and R M"]:::stepNode
    nodeStep2["Step 2 Compute Mean of R X and Mean of R M"]:::stepNode
    nodeStep3["Step 3 Compute Covariance Cov of R X and R M"]:::stepNode
    nodeStep4["Step 4 Compute Variance of R M"]:::stepNode
    nodeStep5["Step 5 Compute Beta equals Cov divided by Var of R M"]:::computeNode
    nodeStep6["Step 6 Apply Blume Adjustment if needed 2/3 of Beta plus 1/3"]:::stepNode
    nodeStep7["Step 7 Substitute into SML to get Expected Return"]:::computeNode
    nodeStep8["Step 8 Compare with actual return to find Alpha"]:::stepNode
    nodeEnd["End Beta Estimate Ready for Decision"]:::endNode

    nodeStart --> nodeStep1
    nodeStep1 --> nodeStep2
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep4
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep5
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep6
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7
    nodeStep7 --> nodeStep8
    nodeStep8 --> nodeEnd

    classDef startNode fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef stepNode fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef computeNode fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef endNode fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block-Level Architecture: CAPM and SML Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput["INPUT BLOCK: Risk Free Rate Rf and Market Risk Premium"]:::inputNode
    nodeBeta["BETA ESTIMATION BLOCK: Covariance and Variance Engine"]:::betaNode
    nodeSML["SML EQUATION BLOCK: E Ri equals Rf plus Beta times MRP"]:::smlNode
    nodeEval["EVALUATION BLOCK: Jensen Alpha and Security Plot"]:::evalNode
    nodeDec["DECISION BLOCK: Buy Hold or Sell Signal"]:::decNode
    nodeOut["OUTPUT: Required Rate of Return for Discounting"]:::outNode

    nodeInput --> nodeBeta
    nodeBeta --> nodeSML
    nodeSML --> nodeEval
    nodeEval --> nodeDec
    nodeDec --> nodeOut

    classDef inputNode fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef betaNode fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef smlNode fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef evalNode fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef decNode fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef outNode fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Risk Decomposition Visualized

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeTotal["Total Risk of Security Variance of R_i"]:::totalNode
    nodeSys["Systematic Risk Beta Squared times Variance of Market"]:::sysNode
    nodeUnsys["Unsystematic Risk Variance of Error Term"]:::unsysNode
    nodeDiv["Diversifiable Spread Across Portfolio"]:::divNode
    nodeNonDiv["Non Diversifiable Cannot be Eliminated"]:::nondivNode

    nodeTotal --> nodeSys
    nodeTotal --> nodeUnsys
    nodeSys --> nodeNonDiv
    nodeUnsys --> nodeDiv

    classDef totalNode fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef sysNode fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef unsysNode fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef divNode fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef nondivNode fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Key insight:** The SML prices **only systematic risk** because unsystematic risk can be eliminated by holding a diversified portfolio. This is why the SML uses **beta** and not **standard deviation** on the X-axis.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Security Market Line (SML). State its equation and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Security Market Line (SML)** is a graphical representation of the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**, showing the linear equilibrium relationship between the **expected return** of a security and its **systematic risk (beta)**. It applies to all individual securities and portfolios, both efficient and inefficient.

**Equation:**
$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$

**Meaning of terms:**
- $E(R_i)$ = Expected return on security $i$
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate of return
- $\beta_i$ = Beta of security $i$ (sensitivity to market)
- $E(R_m)$ = Expected return on the market portfolio
- $[E(R_m) - R_f]$ = Market Risk Premium (MRP)

The SML passes through the points $(0, R_f)$ and $(1, E(R_m))$ with slope equal to the market risk premium.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the Capital Market Line (CML) and the Security Market Line (SML) in 5 points.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | CML | SML |
|---|-----|-----|
| 1 | X-axis: Total risk ($\sigma_p$) | X-axis: Systematic risk ($\beta$) |
| 2 | Y-axis: Expected return | Y-axis: Expected return |
| 3 | Applies **only to efficient portfolios** | Applies to **all securities and portfolios** |
| 4 | Slope: Sharpe Ratio of the market | Slope: Market Risk Premium |
| 5 | Equation uses standard deviation | Equation uses beta |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the CAPM and derive the equation of the Security Market Line. State all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An equity stock has a beta of **1.2**. The risk-free rate is **7%** and the expected market return is **15%**.
   - (i) Find the expected return on the stock using CAPM.
   - (ii) If the stock actually returns **14%**, calculate Jensen's alpha and interpret the result.
   - (iii) Plot the security's position relative to the SML.

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a): CAPM and SML Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM):**
The CAPM, developed by William Sharpe (1964), describes the relationship between systematic risk and expected return. In equilibrium, all investors hold the market portfolio combined with the risk-free asset. The expected return on any risky asset must compensate for:
1. The time value of money (risk-free rate).
2. The systematic risk assumed (beta times market risk premium).

**Derivation of SML:**

The SML must pass through two points:
- $(0, R_f)$ — risk-free asset with $\beta = 0$.
- $(1, E(R_m))$ — market portfolio with $\beta = 1$.

Using the two-point form:
$$\frac{E(R_i) - R_f}{\beta_i - 0} = \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{1 - 0}$$

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$

**Assumptions of CAPM:**
1. Investors are rational and risk-averse.
2. Single-period investment horizon.
3. Homogeneous expectations.
4. Existence of a risk-free borrowing/lending rate.
5. No taxes, no transaction costs, divisible assets.
6. Markets are competitive and frictionless.

**Part (b): Numerical Problem [7 Marks]**

Given: $\beta = 1.2$, $R_f = 7\%$, $E(R_m) = 15\%$, Actual return = $14\%$.

**(i) Expected return via CAPM:**
$$E(R_i) = 7\% + 1.2 \cdot [15\% - 7\%]$$
$$E(R_i) = 7\% + 1.2 \cdot 8\%$$
$$E(R_i) = 7\% + 9.6\% = 16.6\%$$

**(ii) Jensen's alpha:**
$$\alpha = R_{actual} - E(R_i) = 14\% - 16.6\% = -2.6\%$$

**Interpretation:** Since $\alpha < 0$, the stock **underperformed** the market on a risk-adjusted basis. The stock is **overpriced** and plots **below the SML**. A rational investor should **sell** or **avoid** this stock.

**(iii) SML Plot Description:**

| Point | Beta ($\beta$) | Return (%) | Position |
|-------|----------------|------------|----------|
| Risk-free | 0 | 7.0 | On SML |
| Stock (Expected) | 1.2 | 16.6 | On SML (fair value) |
| Stock (Actual) | 1.2 | 14.0 | **2.6% below SML** (overpriced) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various models used by investors to estimate the beta of a security. Explain Blume's adjustment with formula.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The returns on Stock P and the Market Index over 5 months are as follows:

| Month | Return on P (%) | Return on Market (%) |
|-------|------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 10 | 8 |
| 2 | 12 | 10 |
| 3 | 8 | 6 |
| 4 | 15 | 12 |
| 5 | 5 | 4 |

- (i) Calculate the beta of Stock P.
- (ii) If $R_f = 6\%$ and $E(R_m) = 12\%$, find the required return.
- (iii) If the holding period return was **11.5%**, compute Jensen's alpha.

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a): Beta Estimation Models [7 Marks]**

Beta ($\beta$) measures the sensitivity of a security's returns to market returns. Various estimation models include:

1. **Historical Beta (Sample Beta):** Computed by regressing historical returns of the stock on market returns using OLS.
$$\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)}$$

2. **Blume's Adjusted Beta:** Accounts for the empirical observation that betas regress toward 1 over time.
$$\beta_{adj} = \frac{2}{3} \beta_{hist} + \frac{1}{3}$$

3. **Fundamental Beta:** Estimated by relating historical returns to firm-specific fundamentals (leverage, earnings volatility, dividend payout, growth, etc.). Used for unlisted firms and IPOs.

4. **Bottom-Up Beta (Pure-Play Approach):** Starts with industry betas and adjusts for leverage and firm-specific characteristics. Used by consulting firms and investment banks.

5. **Comparable Firms Beta:** Takes the average or median beta of peer companies. Useful when the target firm has limited historical data.

6. **Bayesian Beta:** Combines prior beliefs (industry beta) with sample data using Bayesian statistics to form posterior beta estimates.

**Part (b): Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Calculate Means**

$$\bar{R_p} = \frac{10 + 12 + 8 + 15 + 5}{5} = \frac{50}{5} = 10\%$$

$$\bar{R_m} = \frac{8 + 10 + 6 + 12 + 4}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8\%$$

**Step 2: Calculate Deviations and Products**

| Month | $R_p - \bar{R_p}$ | $R_m - \bar{R_m}$ | $(R_p - \bar{R_p})(R_m - \bar{R_m})$ | $(R_m - \bar{R_m})^2$ |
|-------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 2 | 2 | 4 | 4 |
| 3 | -2 | -2 | 4 | 4 |
| 4 | 5 | 4 | 20 | 16 |
| 5 | -5 | -4 | 20 | 16 |
| **Sum** | **0** | **0** | **48** | **40** |

**Step 3: Calculate Beta**

$$\beta_p = \frac{\sum (R_p - \bar{R_p})(R_m - \bar{R_m})}{\sum (R_m - \bar{R_m})^2} = \frac{48}{40} = 1.20$$

**Step 4: Required Return (CAPM)**

$$E(R_p) = 6\% + 1.20 \cdot [12\% - 6\%] = 6\% + 1.20 \cdot 6\% = 6\% + 7.2\% = 13.2\%$$

**Step 5: Jensen's Alpha**

$$\alpha = 11.5\% - 13.2\% = -1.7\%$$

**Interpretation:** The stock **underperformed** the CAPM-required return by 1.7%. Jensen's alpha is negative, so Stock P plotted **below the SML** is **overpriced**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing CML and SML:** Memorize the difference — CML uses $\sigma$, SML uses $\beta$. **[-1 to -2 marks]**
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract $R_f$ from $E(R_m)$:** The Market Risk Premium is $E(R_m) - R_f$, not just $E(R_m)$. **[-2 marks]**
> 3. **Using actual return in CAPM formula:** CAPM gives **expected** (required) return, not actual. Actual is used only to compute alpha. **[-2 marks]**
> 4. **Sign errors in covariance calculations:** Be careful with negative deviations in regression tables. **[-1 to -2 marks]**
> 5. **Forgetting to state assumptions of CAPM** when asked to "explain CAPM" — full marks require both derivation AND assumptions. **[-2 to -3 marks]**
> 6. **Using sample (N) instead of (N-1)** in variance/covariance: Either is acceptable, but be consistent. **[-0 to -1 mark]**
> 7. **Not interpreting Jensen's alpha:** Always state whether the security is over/undervalued. **[-1 mark]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points for SML & Beta Estimation:**

- **SML Equation (MUST memorize):** $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$
- **SML uses Beta (systematic risk); CML uses Standard Deviation (total risk).**
- **SML applies to ALL securities; CML applies only to EFFICIENT portfolios.**
- **SML is a straight line; CML is part of a curve (Markowitz frontier above the CML).**
- **Slope of SML = Market Risk Premium = $E(R_m) - R_f$.**
- **Beta = 1** → Security moves with the market; **Beta > 1** → Aggressive; **Beta < 1** → Defensive; **Beta < 0** → Inverse (e.g., gold).
- **Beta Formula:** $\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)} = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_m}$
- **Jensen's Alpha Formula:** $\alpha_i = R_{actual} - [R_f + \beta_i(R_m - R_f)]$
  - $\alpha > 0$ → Outperformed (underpriced, BUY)
  - $\alpha < 0$ → Underperformed (overpriced, SELL)
  - $\alpha = 0$ → Fairly priced (HOLD)
- **Blume's Adjusted Beta:** $\beta_{adj} = \frac{2}{3}\beta_{hist} + \frac{1}{3}$ (accounts for mean reversion toward 1).
- **Single Index Model:** $R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_m + e_i$ (used as the basis for empirical beta estimation).
- **Total Risk = Systematic Risk + Unsystematic Risk** (Unsystematic is diversifiable; systematic is not).
- **CAPM Assumptions to remember:** Rational investors, single period, homogeneous expectations, no taxes, no transaction costs, risk-free borrowing/lending, no short-selling restrictions.
- **Market Risk Premium (MRP)** is the extra return demanded for taking on average market risk.
- **Risk-free rate** in India is typically the 10-year G-Sec yield (around 6.5% to 7% in 2024).
- **Practical Beta Estimation Methods:** Historical, Blume-adjusted, Fundamental, Bottom-up, Comparable firms, Bayesian.
- **For unlisted firms or IPOs**, use **pure-play comparable** or **fundamental beta** methods.
- **SML is a benchmarking tool** — use it to find mispriced securities and to compute cost of equity.
- **Always interpret your numerical answer** — KTU examiners award marks for interpretation, not just calculation.
- **Master the regression table** — KTU problems often give you raw data and expect you to compute mean, deviation, products, and beta step-by-step.
- **R-squared of regression** = $\frac{\beta_i^2 \cdot \sigma_m^2}{\sigma_i^2}$ represents the fraction of total risk that is systematic.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — SML and Beta Estimation Models**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Capital - Debt and Equity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Course Code:** OEIET834
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost of Capital - Debt and Equity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 💰 Cost of Capital — Debt and Equity

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Capital (CoC)** is the **minimum required rate of return** that a firm must earn on its investment projects to maintain its market value and satisfy the expectations of its investors (lenders and owners). It represents the **opportunity cost of funds** employed in the business.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Cost of Capital is treated as the **discount rate** used in capital budgeting decisions and firm valuation. It is a composite (weighted) measure that reflects the cost of **each source of finance** — debt, preference capital, and equity — in the proportion of their use in the capital structure.

### 1.2 Specific Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Cost of Debt ($K_d$)** | The effective after-tax rate a firm pays on its borrowings (debentures, term loans, bonds). |
| **Cost of Equity ($K_e$)** | The rate of return required by equity shareholders in exchange for owning the firm's stock. |
| **Cost of Preference Capital ($K_p$)** | The fixed dividend rate demanded by preference shareholders, with tax treatment similar to debt. |
| **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** | The blended cost of all long-term sources of finance, weighted by their proportion in the capital structure. |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍽️

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Hurdle Rate" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a coffee shop owner, Riya, who wants to open a second branch. To fund it, she borrows ₹5,00,000 from a bank at **10% interest** (the bank wants at least 10% to lend) and invests ₹5,00,000 of her own savings, expecting at least **15% return** (since equity is riskier than debt). The **overall cost** Riya must "beat" to make the new branch worthwhile is the **weighted average** of these two expectations — this is her **Cost of Capital**.
>
> If the second branch is projected to earn only 12% per year, Riya should **reject** the project — it doesn't clear the hurdle. Cost of Capital is essentially the **bar of acceptability** for any new investment.

### 1.4 Why Cost of Capital Matters in Engineering/Firm Decisions

- **Investment Appraisal**: NPV, IRR, and PI all depend on the discount rate.
- **Capital Structuring**: Helps decide the optimal debt–equity mix.
- **Performance Benchmarking**: Actual ROIs are compared against CoC.
- **Valuation**: Enterprise value, share valuation, and M&A decisions use WACC.
- **Risk Communication**: Signals the risk profile of the firm to markets.

### 1.5 Classification of Costs in a Firm's Capital Structure

A typical KTU 2024-scheme firm draws funds from:

1. **Long-term Debt** → Debentures, Bonds, Term Loans
2. **Preference Share Capital** → Fixed-dividend, non-voting
3. **Equity Share Capital** → Common stock / retained earnings
4. **Hybrid Securities** → Convertible debentures (advanced level)

This module focuses on **Debt (1)** and **Equity (3)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Conceptual Flow of Cost of Capital

```
Sources of Capital  →  Cost per Source  →  Weights  →  WACC
        ↓                      ↓                ↓           ↓
   Debt, Equity,         Kd, Ke, Kp      E/V, D/V    Discount Rate
   Preference                                      for Projects
```

## 2.2 Cost of Debt ($K_d$) — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.2.1 Why Cost of Debt is the **Lowest** Cost Source

- **Interest is a tax-deductible expense** → Government effectively shares the cost → **Tax Shield** is created.
- Debt-holders have **priority** over equity-holders in liquidation → lower risk → lower required return.
- Contractual obligation → not subject to firm performance uncertainty.

### 2.2.2 Pre-Tax vs. After-Tax Cost

$$K_{d,\text{pre-tax}} = \frac{\text{Annual Interest Payment}}{\text{Net Proceeds from Debt}}$$

$$K_{d,\text{after-tax}} = K_{d,\text{pre-tax}} \times (1 - t)$$

where $t$ = corporate tax rate (commonly **30%** under Indian tax law, but always given in problems).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The "cost" of debt is **not** the coupon rate alone — it is the **effective after-tax rate** the firm must service. A 12% debenture with 30% tax has an effective cost of $12 \times 0.7 = 8.4\%$.

### 2.2.3 Cost of Redeemable Debt

For a debenture issued at discount/premium and redeemable at par or premium:

$$K_d = \frac{I(1-t) + \frac{(RV - NP)}{n}}{\frac{(RV + NP)}{2}}$$

where:
- $I$ = Annual interest in ₹
- $RV$ = Redeemable Value
- $NP$ = Net Proceeds (issue price)
- $n$ = Years to maturity
- $t$ = Tax rate

## 2.3 Cost of Equity ($K_e$) — Three Methods

Equity is **costlier than debt** because:
- Dividends are **not tax-deductible**
- Equity-holders are **last** in the priority stack
- They bear **residual risk** and **business risk**

### 2.3.1 Method 1: Dividend Growth Model (DGM) — *Gordon's Model*

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$$

where:
- $D_1$ = Expected dividend at end of year 1 ($= D_0(1+g)$)
- $P_0$ = Current market price per share
- $g$ = Constant annual growth rate of dividends

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** This is the **most-tested** formula for $K_e$ in KTU 2024 ESE. Always express $D_1$, not $D_0$.

### 2.3.2 Method 2: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)

$$K_e = R_f + \beta \times (R_m - R_f)$$

where:
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate (typically 10-year G-Sec yield)
- $\beta$ = Systematic risk of the firm's stock
- $(R_m - R_f)$ = Market risk premium

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An investor is compensated for: (1) time value of money ($R_f$), and (2) systematic risk ($\beta \times$ market premium).

### 2.3.3 Method 3: Realised Yield / Historical Approach

$$K_e = \frac{\text{Total Expected Return per Share}}{\text{Market Price per Share}}$$

$$K_e = \frac{D_1 + \frac{(P_n - P_0)}{n}}{P_0}$$

## 2.4 Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)

$$WACC = W_e \times K_e + W_d \times K_d \times (1-t) + W_p \times K_p$$

where:
- $W_e = \dfrac{E}{E + D + P}$ (weight of equity)
- $W_d = \dfrac{D}{E + D + P}$ (weight of debt)
- $W_p = \dfrac{P}{E + D + P}$ (weight of preference capital)
- $E, D, P$ = market values (preferred over book values) of equity, debt, preference capital

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always use **market value weights**, not book value weights, unless explicitly stated otherwise. Market values reflect current investor expectations.

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | After-tax Cost of Debt | $K_d(1-t)$ | Irredeemable debt | % per annum |
| 2 | Cost of Redeemable Debt | $\dfrac{I(1-t)+\frac{(RV-NP)}{n}}{\frac{(RV+NP)}{2}}$ | Redeemable bonds/debentures | % per annum |
| 3 | Cost of Equity (DGM) | $\dfrac{D_1}{P_0}+g$ | Dividend-paying firm with constant growth | % per annum |
| 4 | Cost of Equity (CAPM) | $R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | Risk-returns data given | % per annum |
| 5 | Cost of Equity (Realised) | $\dfrac{D_1+\frac{(P_n-P_0)}{n}}{P_0}$ | Historical data analysis | % per annum |
| 6 | WACC | $W_e K_e + W_d K_d(1-t) + W_p K_p$ | Firm-level discount rate | % per annum |
| 7 | Market Value Weight | $W_s = \dfrac{V_s}{V_E+V_D+V_P}$ | Weighting in WACC | Pure ratio (no units) |
| 8 | Tax Shield on Debt | $K_d \times t$ | Quick benefit calculation | % per annum |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Corporate Finance Departments** of every KTU-engineering-turned-manager's firm use WACC to evaluate new project proposals.
- **Banks** (where many B.Tech graduates work) use cost of debt + risk premium to quote loan rates.
- **Startups**: Founders pitch using Ke based on comparable CAPM betas.
- **Valuation analysts** at firms like Deloitte, EY, KPMG discount free cash flows using WACC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Solved Numerical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost of Equity from Dividend Discount Model (Gordon's Logic)

We start with the **Constant Growth Dividend Discount Model**, which states that the current market price equals the present value of all future dividends growing at a constant rate $g$:

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)^1} + \frac{D_2}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{D_3}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots$$

Since $D_t = D_1 \times (1+g)^{t-1}$, the infinite geometric series sums to:

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g}$$

**Solving for $K_e$** (multiply both sides by $(K_e - g)$):

$$P_0(K_e - g) = D_1$$

$$P_0 \cdot K_e - P_0 \cdot g = D_1$$

$$P_0 \cdot K_e = D_1 + P_0 \cdot g$$

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Conditions:** $K_e > g$, and the firm must pay a dividend. If the firm pays no dividend, DGM fails — use CAPM.

---

## 3.2 Solved Problem 1: Cost of Debt (Irredeemable)

**📘 Question:** A company issues 10,000, 12% debentures of ₹100 each. The debentures are irredeemable. The current market price is ₹96. The tax rate is 30%. Calculate the cost of debt.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute annual interest per debenture.

$$I = 12\% \times 100 = ₹12 \text{ per debenture}$$

**Step 2:** Compute pre-tax cost of debt using net proceeds.

$$K_{d,\text{pre-tax}} = \frac{I}{P_0} = \frac{12}{96} = 0.125 = 12.5\%$$

**Step 3:** Apply the tax shield.

$$K_{d,\text{after-tax}} = K_{d,\text{pre-tax}} \times (1 - t) = 12.5\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 12.5\% \times 0.70 = 8.75\%$$

✅ **Cost of Debt = 8.75% per annum**

---

## 3.3 Solved Problem 2: Cost of Equity using DGM

**📘 Question:** A firm's share currently trades at ₹240. The expected dividend at year-end is ₹18 per share. Dividends have grown historically at 8% per year and are expected to grow at the same rate. Compute $K_e$.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify given values.

$$P_0 = ₹240, \quad D_1 = ₹18, \quad g = 8\% = 0.08$$

**Step 2:** Apply Gordon's growth model.

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{18}{240} + 0.08$$

$$K_e = 0.075 + 0.08 = 0.155$$

✅ **Cost of Equity = 15.5% per annum**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** Check $K_e > g$ → $15.5\% > 8\%$ ✅. Also note: dividend yield $= 7.5\%$, capital gains yield $= 8\%$, total = $15.5\%$.

---

## 3.4 Solved Problem 3: Cost of Equity using CAPM

**📘 Question:** The risk-free rate is 7%, the market return is 14%, and the stock's beta is 1.25. Find the cost of equity.

### Solution:

$$K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f) = 7\% + 1.25 \times (14\% - 7\%)$$

$$K_e = 7\% + 1.25 \times 7\% = 7\% + 8.75\% = 15.75\%$$

✅ **Cost of Equity (CAPM) = 15.75% per annum**

---

## 3.5 Solved Problem 4: WACC (Comprehensive)

**📘 Question:** XYZ Ltd. has the following capital structure (Market Values):

- **Equity:** 1,00,000 shares trading at ₹150 each
- **Debt:** ₹40,00,000 of 11% debentures (redeemable at par in 5 years), current market quote ₹98 per ₹100 face value
- **Tax rate:** 30%
- **Expected dividend:** ₹12 per share, growth = 10%

Compute WACC.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Total Market Value**

$$V_E = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 150 = ₹1{,}50{,}00{,}000$$

$$V_D = 40{,}000 \times 98 = ₹39{,}20{,}000$$

$$V_{\text{Total}} = 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 39{,}20{,}000 = ₹1{,}89{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 2: Weights**

$$W_E = \frac{1{,}50{,}00{,}000}{1{,}89{,}20{,}000} = 0.7928 \; (79.28\%)$$

$$W_D = \frac{39{,}20{,}000}{1{,}89{,}20{,}000} = 0.2072 \; (20.72\%)$$

**Step 3: Cost of Equity (DGM)**

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{12}{150} + 0.10 = 0.08 + 0.10 = 18\%$$

**Step 4: Cost of Debt (Redeemable, at par)**

Since redeemable at par ($RV = 100$, $NP = 98$, $n = 5$):

$$K_d = \frac{I(1-t) + \frac{(RV - NP)}{n}}{\frac{(RV + NP)}{2}} = \frac{11(1-0.30) + \frac{(100-98)}{5}}{\frac{(100+98)}{2}}$$

$$K_d = \frac{7.7 + 0.4}{99} = \frac{8.1}{99} = 0.08182 = 8.18\%$$

**Step 5: Compute WACC**

$$WACC = W_E \cdot K_e + W_D \cdot K_d$$

$$WACC = (0.7928 \times 18\%) + (0.2072 \times 8.18\%)$$

$$WACC = 14.2704\% + 1.6950\% = 15.9654\%$$

✅ **WACC ≈ 15.97% per annum**

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** $K_e (18\%) > WACC (15.97\%) > K_d \text{ after-tax} (8.18\%)$ — exactly the correct ordering. ✔

---

## 3.6 Solved Problem 5: Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Methods

**📘 Question:** Two methods yield different $K_e$ values. Which one should be preferred?

| Method | $K_e$ Computed | Required Data | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| DGM | 15.5% | $D_1, P_0, g$ | Stable, dividend-paying firm |
| CAPM | 15.75% | $R_f, \beta, R_m$ | Listed firm with reliable beta |
| Realised Yield | 14.8% | Historical prices + dividends | Long-term back-testing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analyst's Rule:** Cross-validate using **at least two methods**; differences of 1–2% are normal. A divergence > 3% suggests re-examining input assumptions (especially $\beta$ or $g$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of Cost of Capital (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Firm Capital Requirements] --> B[Identify Sources of Funds]
    B --> C1[Long-term Debt]
    B --> C2[Preference Capital]
    B --> C3[Equity Capital]
    C1 --> D1[Compute Kd after-tax]
    C2 --> D2[Compute Kp]
    C3 --> D3[Compute Ke using DGM or CAPM]
    D1 --> E[Apply Weights based on Market Value]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Weighted Average Cost of Capital WACC]
    F --> G[Use as Discount Rate in Capital Budgeting NPV IRR PI]
    F --> H[Use for Firm Valuation DCF Models]
    F --> I[Use for Performance Benchmarking Actual ROI vs WACC]
```

## 4.2 Methods of Cost of Equity Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
