```mermaid
    A[Cost of Equity Ke] --> B[Dividend Growth Model]
    A --> C[CAPM]
    A --> D[Realised Yield Method]
    A --> E[Earnings Yield Approach]
    B --> B1[Ke equals D1 by P0 plus g]
    C --> C1[Ke equals Rf plus Beta times Market Risk Premium]
    D --> D1[Ke equals D1 plus Capital Gain divided by P0]
    E --> E1[Ke equals EPS divided by P0]
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Selecting Cost of Equity Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Ke Method] --> B{Does the firm pay regular dividends?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is growth constant?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Dividend Growth Model]
    C -- No --> E[Use Multi-stage DGM or Realised Yield]
    B -- No --> F{Is the firm listed with reliable beta?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use CAPM]
    F -- No --> H[Use Comparable Industry Ke or Earnings Yield]
```

## 4.4 WACC Construction Process (Granular Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Determine Market Values of E D P] --> B[Step 2: Compute Weights We Wd Wp]
    B --> C[Step 3: Compute Ke via DGM or CAPM]
    B --> D[Step 4: Compute Kd after-tax]
    B --> E[Step 5: Compute Kp if applicable]
    C --> F[Step 6: WACC equals We Ke plus Wd Kd one minus t plus Wp Kp]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Final Discount Rate for Project Appraisal]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are patterned on actual KTU B.Tech ESE question papers of the 2024 scheme. Marks and Bloom's levels (L1–L6) are indicated.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Differentiate between Cost of Debt and Cost of Equity.**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

| Basis | Cost of Debt | Cost of Equity |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Effective rate paid on borrowings | Expected return demanded by equity-holders |
| Tax Treatment | Tax-deductible (tax shield available) | Not tax-deductible |
| Computation | $K_d(1-t)$ | DGM, CAPM, or Realised Yield |
| Payment | Contractual (interest) | Discretionary (dividend) |
| Risk | Lower (priority claim) | Higher (residual claim) |
| Typical Magnitude | 6%–10% after-tax | 12%–18% |

> **Model Answer (Key Points):** Cost of debt is the rate the firm pays to lenders and is reduced by the tax shield since interest is a deductible expense. Cost of equity is the rate of return required by shareholders and is **not** tax-deductible, making it the **higher** of the two. Debt is cheaper because lenders bear less risk and have a senior claim on assets and earnings.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for tax contrast, 1 for risk/priority)*

---

### **Q2. What is WACC? Why is it used as a discount rate in capital budgeting?**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

> **WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital)** is the composite cost of all long-term sources of finance (equity, debt, preference) weighted by their proportion in the firm's capital structure. Mathematically:
> 
> $$WACC = W_e K_e + W_d K_d(1-t) + W_p K_p$$

**Why WACC is used as a discount rate:**

1. **Reflects Overall Cost:** It represents the **average cost** the firm incurs to finance its assets — a true "hurdle rate."
2. **Risk-Consistent:** It embeds the **business and financial risk** of the firm, making it appropriate for average-risk projects.
3. **Market-Aligned:** Computed using **market values**, it reflects current investor expectations.
4. **Capital Budgeting Use:** Projects with **NPV > 0** at WACC discount rate are accepted (adds to shareholder wealth).
5. **Maximization Principle:** Acceptance of projects returning more than WACC leads to **shareholder wealth maximization** — the core KTU 2024 finance objective.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + formula, 2 for reasons)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 scheme, students answer **one full question (14 marks)** from a choice of two. Each question carries sub-parts of **7 marks + 7 marks**.

---

### ✏️ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**A company has the following capital structure:**

| Source | Book Value (₹) | Market Value (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Equity (10,000 shares) | 10,00,000 (₹100 each) | 15,00,000 (₹150 each) |
| 12% Debentures | 5,00,000 | 4,80,000 |
| Tax Rate | 30% | 30% |

Additional data:
- Expected dividend per share next year: **₹14**
- Historical dividend growth rate: **10%** per annum (constant)
- Debentures are **irredeemable**

**Answer the following:**

#### **(a) Compute the Cost of Equity using the Dividend Growth Model and the after-tax Cost of Debt. (7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper adapted]**

**Step 1: Cost of Equity (DGM)**

Given: $D_1 = 14$, $P_0 = 150$, $g = 0.10$

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{14}{150} + 0.10$$

$$K_e = 0.0933 + 0.10 = 0.1933 = 19.33\%$$

✅ **Cost of Equity = 19.33% per annum**

**Step 2: Cost of Debt (Irredeemable, After-Tax)**

Annual interest = $12\% \times 100 = 12$ per debenture
Market price per debenture = $480{,}000 / 5{,}000 = ₹96$ per ₹100 face

$$K_{d,\text{pre-tax}} = \frac{12}{96} = 12.5\%$$

$$K_{d,\text{after-tax}} = 12.5\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 12.5\% \times 0.70 = 8.75\%$$

✅ **After-tax Cost of Debt = 8.75% per annum**

*(7 marks: 4 for Ke, 3 for Kd)*

---

#### **(b) Compute WACC using Market Value Weights. Comment on whether the firm should accept a project yielding 16% return. (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute Market Value Weights**

$$V_E = 15{,}00{,}000, \quad V_D = 4{,}80{,}000$$

$$V_{\text{Total}} = 15{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000 = 19{,}80{,}000$$

$$W_E = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{19{,}80{,}000} = 0.7576 \;(75.76\%)$$

$$W_D = \frac{4{,}80{,}000}{19{,}80{,}000} = 0.2424 \;(24.24\%)$$

**Step 2: Compute WACC**

$$WACC = (W_E \times K_e) + (W_D \times K_{d,\text{after-tax}})$$

$$WACC = (0.7576 \times 19.33\%) + (0.2424 \times 8.75\%)$$

$$WACC = 14.65\% + 2.12\% = 16.77\%$$

✅ **WACC ≈ 16.77% per annum**

**Step 3: Decision on Project**

- Project Yield = 16%
- WACC (Hurdle Rate) = 16.77%
- Since $16\% < 16.77\%$, the project **does NOT cover the cost of capital**.
- **Decision: REJECT the project**, as it would **reduce shareholder wealth** by 0.77% per year.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Using Book Values** instead of market values → lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $(1-t)$ factor** in cost of debt → lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Not commenting** on accept/reject decision → lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Stating NPV without computing WACC** → not awarded full credit.

*(7 marks: 3 for weights, 2 for WACC, 2 for decision + reasoning)*

---

### ✏️ **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

#### **(a) Explain any two methods of calculating Cost of Equity with formulas and suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**Method 1: Dividend Growth Model (DGM)**

**Formula:** $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$

**Assumptions:**
- Dividends grow at a constant rate $g$ forever.
- The required rate of return $K_e$ must exceed $g$.

**Example:** A share trades at ₹200. The expected dividend next year is ₹16, and the growth rate is 8%.

$$K_e = \frac{16}{200} + 0.08 = 0.08 + 0.08 = 16\%$$

**Method 2: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**

**Formula:** $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$

**Logic:** Investors are compensated for **time value of money** ($R_f$) and **systematic risk** ($\beta \times$ market risk premium).

**Example:** $R_f = 6\%$, $R_m = 13\%$, $\beta = 1.2$

$$K_e = 6\% + 1.2 \times (13\% - 6\%) = 6\% + 8.4\% = 14.4\%$$

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | DGM | CAPM |
|---|---|---|
| Data Need | Dividends, prices, growth | Risk-free rate, beta, market return |
| Suitable For | Mature, stable firms | Listed firms with reliable beta |
| Weakness | Fails for non-dividend firms | Beta instability |

*(7 marks: 2 per method formula, 1 per method example, 2 for comparison)*

---

#### **(b) A firm raises ₹10,00,000 through 11% debentures of ₹100 each, redeemable at par after 6 years. Current market quote is ₹94. Tax rate is 30%. Compute the cost of debt. (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify Values**

- Interest $I = 11\% \times 100 = 11$ per debenture
- Net Proceeds $NP = 94$
- Redeemable Value $RV = 100$
- Maturity $n = 6$ years
- Tax rate $t = 0.30$

**Step 2: Apply the Redeemable Debt Formula**

$$K_d = \frac{I(1-t) + \frac{(RV - NP)}{n}}{\frac{(RV + NP)}{2}}$$

$$K_d = \frac{11(1 - 0.30) + \frac{(100 - 94)}{6}}{\frac{(100 + 94)}{2}}$$

$$K_d = \frac{7.7 + 1.0}{97.0} = \frac{8.7}{97.0} = 0.0897$$

✅ **Cost of Debt = 8.97% per annum**

**Interpretation:** Because the debenture is trading at a discount (₹94 < ₹100 face), the firm receives less cash upfront than it must repay later, increasing the effective cost. The **tax shield** partially offsets this, but the cost still sits in the 8%–9% band.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for substitution, 1 for answer, 1 for interpretation)*

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Triggers in Cost of Capital Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Tax Shield** $(1 - t)$ on debt → **−1 to −2 marks**
> 2. **Using $D_0$ instead of $D_1$** in DGM → answer off by 1–2% → **−1 mark**
> 3. **Book values** instead of market values in WACC → fundamental error → **−2 to −3 marks**
> 4. **Missing units (%)** in final answer → **−0.5 mark** (strict markers)
> 5. **No project accept/reject decision** when WACC is computed → **−1 to −2 marks**
>
> **Bonus Tip:** Always show **substituted values in the formula**, not just the final number. Marks are awarded for *method*, not just the answer.

---

## 5.5 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cost of Capital (Debt and Equity)**

- ✅ **Cost of Capital** = minimum required rate of return to satisfy investors.
- ✅ **Cost of Debt** = pre-tax rate × $(1 - t)$; tax shield makes it the cheapest source.
- ✅ **Cost of Equity** > Cost of Debt (always) — dividends are not tax-deductible, and equity is riskier.
- ✅ **DGM:** $K_e = D_1 / P_0 + g$ — needs $D_1$, not $D_0$.
- ✅ **CAPM:** $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ — needs market return and beta.
- ✅ **WACC** = $W_e K_e + W_d K_d(1 - t) + W_p K_p$ — use **market value weights**.
- ✅ WACC is the **discount rate** for capital budgeting and DCF valuation.
- ✅ Project accepted if its return **exceeds WACC** (positive NPV at WACC).
- ✅ **Redeemable debt** formula: $\frac{I(1-t) + (RV-NP)/n}{(RV+NP)/2}$.
- ✅ **Irredeemable debt** formula: $K_d = I(1-t) / NP$.
- ✅ **CAPM** is preferred for **listed firms**; **DGM** for **dividend-paying stable firms**.
- ✅ Always cross-check $K_e > g$ to validate DGM application.
- ✅ **Order check:** $K_e > WACC > K_d (1-t)$ in a debt–equity firm.
- ✅ **Tax rate** of 30% is the KTU default; always read the problem statement for the actual rate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Debt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost of Debt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Debt (KoD / Kd)** is the effective interest rate that a company pays on its borrowed funds (loans, bonds, debentures) expressed as an annual percentage. It represents the **required rate of return** demanded by lenders and bondholders for extending credit to the firm, considering both the coupon payments and the tax-deductibility of interest expense under corporate tax laws.

In the context of the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** framework, the cost of debt is one of the two principal components (alongside the cost of equity) that determines a firm's blended capital cost. Because interest on debt is a **tax-deductible expense** under most tax jurisdictions (including India under the Income Tax Act, 1961), the **after-tax cost of debt** is the figure used in WACC calculations, not the pre-tax (stated) rate.

**Mathematically, the pre-tax cost of debt is denoted as $K_d$, and the after-tax cost of debt is:**
$$K_d(1-T)$$
where $T$ is the **marginal corporate tax rate**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bank Loan Perspective" Analogy**
> Imagine you borrow ₹5,00,000 from a bank at a 10% interest rate to start a small business. The 10% is your **cost of debt** — it is the price the bank charges you for the privilege of using their money instead of yours.
> 
> However, here's the clever part: when you file your business taxes, the government allows you to **deduct the interest you paid** from your taxable income. If your tax rate is 30%, that ₹50,000 interest payment actually only *costs* you ₹35,000 in real terms (because you save ₹15,000 in taxes).
> 
> Therefore, your **true (after-tax) cost of debt** is not 10%, but rather **7%**. That is precisely what `Kd(1−T)` captures — the actual economic burden of borrowing.

This is why debt financing is often called a **"tax shield"** — the government effectively subsidizes a portion of your borrowing cost, making debt cheaper than equity from an after-tax perspective.

## 1.3 Why Cost of Debt Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Capital Budgeting** decisions (Module 3–4 continuity), the cost of debt serves as the discount rate for debt-financed projects. In **Capital Structure** decisions (Module 4–5), it influences the trade-off between financial risk and tax advantage. KTU examiners frequently test the **after-tax adjustment** because it is where students commit the most errors.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Marginal Corporate Tax Rate (T)** in India: typically **25.17%** for domestic companies (22% base + 10% surcharge + 4% cess, as per the **Corporate Tax Act, 2019** for companies opting for the new regime). For older regimes, it is approximately **30%**.
- **Cost of Debt is ALWAYS less than Cost of Equity (Ke)** in a healthy firm because:
  1. Debt holders have priority in liquidation.
  2. Interest payments are contractual obligations.
  3. Tax shield reduces the effective cost.
- The unit of cost of debt is expressed in **percentage (%)** or **decimal form**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Cost of Debt

The cost of debt is not a single number — it is a **composite concept** that can be measured in several ways depending on the type of instrument:

1. **Stated (Coupon) Rate of Interest** — The nominal rate printed on the bond certificate or agreed in the loan contract.
2. **Current Yield** — The annual coupon payment divided by the *current market price* of the debt instrument.
3. **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** — The internal rate of return (IRR) earned by an investor who buys the bond at the current market price and holds it until maturity. **This is the most theoretically correct measure of cost of debt for KTU exams.**
4. **Yield to Call (YTC)** — The yield computed assuming the bond is called (redeemed early) at the earliest possible call date.
5. **Effective (After-Tax) Cost** — The pre-tax cost adjusted for the corporate tax shield.

## 2.2 The Logic Behind Each Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Why YTM is Preferred:**
> The stated coupon rate is misleading because bonds are rarely issued at par in secondary markets. The current yield ignores capital gains/losses at maturity. **YTM incorporates both the coupon income AND the capital gain (or loss) realized when the bond is redeemed at par**, making it the most comprehensive measure of the true borrowing cost from the company's perspective.

## 2.3 Cost of Debt in Different Scenarios

| **Scenario** | **Cost of Debt Formula** | **When to Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Perpetual Debt | $K_d = \dfrac{I}{N}$ | Irredeemable preference-like debt |
| Redeemable Debt (Approximation) | $K_d \approx \dfrac{I + \dfrac{(RV - NP)}{n}}{\dfrac{(RV + NP)}{2}}$ | KTU standard approximation method |
| Redeemable Debt (YTM / Exact) | $NP = \sum_{t=1}^{n}\dfrac{I}{(1+K_d)^t} + \dfrac{RV}{(1+K_d)^n}$ | When market price ≠ issue price |
| After-Tax Cost | $K_d(1 - T)$ | For WACC calculation |
| Cost of Preference Shares (for comparison) | $K_p = \dfrac{D}{NP}$ | Not debt, but parallel concept |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule:** In the formula table above, parentheses and fractions are used instead of vertical bars (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to prevent breaking the markdown table structure. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` if absolute values must appear inside a table row.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Typical KTU Use** |
|---|---|---|
| $K_d = \dfrac{\text{Annual Interest}}{\text{Net Proceeds}} \times 100$ | Simple cost of debt on perpetual debt | Loan-based problems |
| $K_d = \dfrac{I(1-T)}{NP}$ | After-tax cost (perpetual) | WACC problems |
| $NP = \sum_{t=1}^{n}\dfrac{I}{(1+K_d)^t} + \dfrac{RV}{(1+K_d)^n}$ | YTM equation (present value) | Bond pricing problems |
| $K_d \approx \dfrac{I + \dfrac{(RV-NP)}{n}}{\dfrac{RV+NP}{2}}$ | Approximate YTM (linear interpolation) | Common KTU 14-mark question |
| $K_d(\text{after-tax}) = K_d(\text{before-tax}) \times (1-T)$ | Tax shield adjustment | Always the final step |
| $WACC = W_e K_e + W_d K_d(1-T)$ | Weighted average cost of capital | Module 5 linkage |

**Boundary Conditions & Units:**
- $I$ (Interest) → in currency units (₹)
- $NP$ (Net Proceeds) → in currency units (₹)
- $RV$ (Redemption Value) → in currency units (₹)
- $n$ (number of years to maturity) → dimensionless integer
- $T$ (tax rate) → dimensionless fraction between 0 and 1
- $K_d$ → dimensionless (expressed as % or decimal)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Corporate Finance Utility

- **Project Appraisal**: In infrastructure projects (roads, power plants, telecom), debt typically constitutes 60–80% of capital. Accurate $K_d$ is critical for NPV calculations.
- **Bond Valuation**: Investment bankers use YTM to price new bond issues.
- **Credit Rating Impact**: Higher $K_d$ signals higher risk → lower credit rating → higher borrowing costs (a feedback loop).
- **Refinancing Decisions**: Firms compare current YTM with prevailing market rates to decide whether to refinance existing debt.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cost of Irredeemable (Perpetual) Debt

Consider a company that issues a perpetual bond (no maturity date) with a face value $F$, paying annual interest $I$. The bond is sold at net proceeds $NP$ (after flotation costs and discount/premium).

**The investor's required return must equal the annual coupon payment divided by the price paid:**

$$K_d = \dfrac{I}{NP}$$

**Now apply the tax adjustment:**

$$K_d(\text{after-tax}) = \dfrac{I(1-T)}{NP}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU Style):** A company issues perpetual bonds at ₹96 per ₹100 face value, with an annual coupon of 8%. Tax rate = 30%.
> 
> **Step 1:** $I = 8\% \times 100 = ₹8$
> 
> **Step 2:** $NP = ₹96$
> 
> **Step 3:** Pre-tax cost = $8 / 96 = 0.0833$ or **8.33%**
> 
> **Step 4:** After-tax cost = $8.33\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 8.33\% \times 0.70 =$ **5.83%**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost of Redeemable Debt — The Approximation Formula

When debt is redeemable at a value $RV$ (often equal to face value, but sometimes at a premium), and issued at net proceeds $NP$, we use the **YTM approximation formula**:

$$K_d \approx \dfrac{I + \dfrac{(RV - NP)}{n}}{\dfrac{(RV + NP)}{2}}$$

**Derivation Logic:**

The **numerator** captures the average annual income:
- $I$ = annual interest income (coupon)
- $(RV - NP)/n$ = average annual capital gain/loss amortized over $n$ years
- Sum = average annual total return

The **denominator** captures the average investment:
- $(RV + NP)/2$ = average of the redemption value and the purchase price (simple average instead of time-weighted)

This is the **simple arithmetic average approximation**, used by KTU because computing the exact YTM requires iterative solving of a polynomial equation (trial-and-error or Newton's method).

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A company issued ₹1,000 face value debentures redeemable after 10 years at par. The debentures carry a 12% coupon rate, payable annually. Net proceeds = ₹950. Tax rate = 30%. Compute the cost of debt (after-tax).

**Step 1:** Identify given values
- $F = RV = ₹1{,}000$ (redeemable at par)
- $I = 12\% \times 1{,}000 = ₹120$ per year
- $NP = ₹950$
- $n = 10$ years
- $T = 0.30$

**Step 2:** Compute the average annual capital gain/loss
$$\dfrac{RV - NP}{n} = \dfrac{1{,}000 - 950}{10} = \dfrac{50}{10} = ₹5 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator
$$I + \dfrac{RV - NP}{n} = 120 + 5 = ₹125$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator (average investment)
$$\dfrac{RV + NP}{2} = \dfrac{1{,}000 + 950}{2} = \dfrac{1{,}950}{2} = ₹975$$

**Step 5:** Compute pre-tax cost of debt
$$K_d = \dfrac{125}{975} = 0.12820 \approx \mathbf{12.82\%}$$

**Step 6:** Apply the tax shield
$$K_d(\text{after-tax}) = 12.82\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 12.82\% \times 0.70 = \mathbf{8.97\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Although the company pays 12% on the face value, the **effective cost of debt** (after-tax) is only **8.97%**. This is the figure that should be plugged into the WACC equation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Exact YTM Method (For Conceptual Understanding)

The exact YTM is the discount rate $K_d$ that equates the present value of all future cash flows to the net proceeds:

$$NP = \sum_{t=1}^{n}\dfrac{I}{(1+K_d)^t} + \dfrac{RV}{(1+K_d)^n}$$

**Step-by-Step Simplification Using Annuity Formula:**

The first term is the present value of an annuity of $I$ for $n$ periods:
$$\sum_{t=1}^{n}\dfrac{I}{(1+K_d)^t} = I \times \dfrac{1 - (1+K_d)^{-n}}{K_d}$$

The second term is the present value of a single payment $RV$ at time $n$:
$$\dfrac{RV}{(1+K_d)^n}$$

**Therefore, the exact YTM equation becomes:**

$$NP = I \times \dfrac{1 - (1+K_d)^{-n}}{K_d} + \dfrac{RV}{(1+K_d)^n}$$

This is a polynomial in $K_d$ and must be solved iteratively (trial and error or financial calculator). **KTU typically expects students to use the approximation formula unless specifically asked for "exact YTM."**

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pre-Tax vs After-Tax Cost Comparison

| **Parameter** | **Scenario A** | **Scenario B** |
|---|---|---|
| Face Value | ₹1,000 | ₹1,000 |
| Coupon Rate | 10% | 14% |
| Issue Price | ₹980 | ₹980 |
| Maturity | 5 years | 5 years |
| Tax Rate | 30% | 30% |
| Annual Interest ($I$) | ₹100 | ₹140 |
| Capital Loss/Year | ₹4 | ₹4 |
| Avg. Investment | ₹990 | ₹990 |
| **Pre-tax $K_d$** | **10.51%** | **14.55%** |
| **After-tax $K_d$** | **7.36%** | **10.18%** |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that even though Scenario B has a much higher coupon (14%), the *after-tax* cost (10.18%) is still significantly lower than its pre-tax value (14.55%) due to the tax shield. This illustrates why **debt is the preferred financing source** when tax rates are substantial.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Cost of Debt Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Start: Identify Debt Instrument]) --> nodeType{Is Debt Perpetual?}
    nodeType -- Yes --> nodePerp[Use Perpetual Formula]
    nodePerp --> nodePerp1[Compute I / NP for Pre-tax Kd]
    nodePerp1 --> nodePerp2[Apply Tax Shield Kd x 1-T]
    nodePerp2 --> nodeFinal[After-tax Cost of Debt]
    nodeType -- No --> nodeRedeem[Use Redeemable Debt Formula]
    nodeRedeem --> nodeRe1[Compute Average Annual Capital Gain or Loss]
    nodeRe1 --> nodeRe2[Add Annual Interest to Get Numerator]
    nodeRe2 --> nodeRe3[Compute Average Investment as Denominator]
    nodeRe3 --> nodeRe4[Pre-tax Kd Approximation]
    nodeRe4 --> nodeRe5[Apply Tax Shield]
    nodeRe5 --> nodeFinal
    nodeFinal --> nodeOutput([Output: Plug into WACC])
    
    style nodeStart fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeFinal fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeOutput fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeType fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Cost Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputs["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        nodeF[Face Value F]
        nodeI[Annual Interest I]
        nodeNP[Net Proceeds NP]
        nodeRV[Redemption Value RV]
        nodeN[Maturity Period n]
        nodeT[Tax Rate T]
    end
    
    subgraph processing["CALCULATION ENGINE"]
        nodeA[Average Capital Gain or Loss Per Year]
        nodeB[Average Investment Base]
        nodeC[Pre-tax Cost of Debt Kd]
        nodeD[Tax Shield Factor 1-T]
    end
    
    subgraph outputs["FINAL OUTPUT"]
        nodeE[After-tax Cost of Debt Kd x 1-T]
        nodeG[WACC Component Weight x Cost]
    end
    
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeI --> nodeC
    nodeNP --> nodeB
    nodeNP --> nodeC
    nodeRV --> nodeA
    nodeRV --> nodeB
    nodeN --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeT --> nodeD
    nodeE --> nodeG
    
    style inputs fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,stroke-width:2px
    style processing fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57c00,stroke-width:2px
    style outputs fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Comparison of Debt vs Equity Cost

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeTitle([Capital Sources and Their Costs]) --> nodeDebt[Debt Financing]
    nodeTitle --> nodeEquity[Equity Financing]
    
    nodeDebt --> nodeDebt1[Pre-tax Cost Kd]
    nodeDebt1 --> nodeDebt2[Apply Tax Shield 1-T]
    nodeDebt2 --> nodeDebt3[After-tax Cost of Debt]
    
    nodeEquity --> nodeEq1[Cost of Equity Ke]
    nodeEquity --> nodeEq2[Dividend or CAPM Model]
    nodeEq1 --> nodeEq3[No Tax Adjustment]
    nodeEq2 --> nodeEq3
    
    nodeDebt3 --> nodeCompare{Which is Cheaper?}
    nodeEq3 --> nodeCompare
    nodeCompare -- Yes Debt --> nodeResult1[Optimal: Increase Debt in Capital Structure]
    nodeCompare -- Equal --> nodeResult2[Indifferent]
    
    style nodeTitle fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeDebt3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeEq3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeCompare fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term "Cost of Debt." Why is the after-tax cost of debt preferred over the pre-tax cost for capital budgeting decisions?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Cost of Debt** is the effective rate of interest a firm must pay to its lenders and bondholders, comprising the contractual interest rate adjusted for any discount or premium on issue and the amortization of redemption gains/losses over the life of the debt.

> The **after-tax cost of debt** is preferred because interest paid on borrowed funds is a **tax-deductible expense** under corporate tax law. The tax savings (called the **tax shield**) reduce the effective cost of borrowing. Since capital budgeting decisions must reflect the *true economic cost* to the firm, the after-tax figure — computed as $K_d(1-T)$ — accurately represents the net outflow per rupee of interest paid.

**(Marks Distribution: Definition = 2 marks; Reason for after-tax preference = 1 mark)**

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between Current Yield and Yield to Maturity (YTM) as measures of cost of debt.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Current Yield** | **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Annual coupon ÷ Current market price | IRR of all cash flows till maturity |
| Capital Gain/Loss | Ignored | Included |
| Redemption Value | Not considered | Fully considered |
| Theoretical Accuracy | Less accurate | Most accurate |
| Formula | $I/P_0$ | Solved from $NP = \sum I(1+K_d)^{-t} + RV(1+K_d)^{-n}$ |

> **Current Yield** is a partial measure that considers only the income component, whereas **YTM** is the comprehensive measure that accounts for both coupon income and capital appreciation/depreciation over the bond's life.

**(Marks Distribution: Each distinction = 0.5 marks × 4 = 2 marks; Concluding statement = 1 mark)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A company issues 10,000 12% debentures of ₹100 each at a discount of 5%, redeemable at par after 7 years. The company also pays ₹2 per debenture as underwriting commission. Compute the cost of debt using the YTM approximation method. Assume tax rate is 30%.**

**Sub-part (a): Compute the Pre-tax Cost of Debt [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Face Value $F = ₹100$
- Coupon Rate = 12%
- Annual Interest $I = ₹12$
- Discount on issue = 5% of ₹100 = ₹5
- Underwriting commission = ₹2
- Net Proceeds $NP = ₹100 - ₹5 - ₹2 = ₹93$
- Redemption Value $RV = ₹100$ (at par)
- Maturity $n = 7$ years

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute average annual capital gain
$$\dfrac{RV - NP}{n} = \dfrac{100 - 93}{7} = \dfrac{7}{7} = ₹1 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2:** Numerator (average annual total return)
$$I + \dfrac{RV - NP}{n} = 12 + 1 = ₹13$$

**Step 3:** Denominator (average investment)
$$\dfrac{RV + NP}{2} = \dfrac{100 + 93}{2} = \dfrac{193}{2} = ₹96.5$$

**Step 4:** Pre-tax cost of debt
$$K_d = \dfrac{13}{96.5} = 0.1347 \approx \mathbf{13.47\%}$$

**Sub-part (b): Compute the After-tax Cost of Debt and Comment [7 Marks]**

**Step 5:** Apply the tax shield
$$K_d(\text{after-tax}) = 13.47\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 13.47\% \times 0.70 = \mathbf{9.43\%}$$

**Comment:** Although the company pays a stated 12% on its debentures, the **effective cost of debt** after accounting for flotation costs (discount + commission) and the tax shield is only **9.43%**. This is the figure to be used in WACC calculations and capital budgeting decisions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget to **add the underwriting commission to the discount** while calculating net proceeds. The correct formula is: $NP = \text{Issue Price} - \text{Discount} - \text{Commission}$. Also, **never** compute the percentage on ₹13/100 directly — the denominator must be the **average investment** (RV+NP)/2 = ₹96.5, not the face value. These two errors account for **2 to 3 marks lost** per typical answer script.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**XYZ Ltd. has the following capital structure:**
- **Equity Share Capital:** 5,000 shares of ₹100 each, issued at a premium of 25%. Expected dividend = ₹15 per share, growth rate = 5%.
- **12% Debentures:** ₹2,00,000 issued at par, redeemable after 8 years at a premium of 10%.

**Tax rate = 25%. Compute the cost of debt and the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC).**

**Sub-part (a): Compute the Cost of Debt [7 Marks]**

**Given for Debentures:**
- Face Value = ₹100 (assumed, as total is ₹2,00,000 and standard denomination)
- Coupon Rate = 12%
- Annual Interest $I = ₹12$ per ₹100
- Issue Price $NP = ₹100$ (at par)
- Redemption Value $RV = ₹100 + 10\% = ₹110$
- Maturity $n = 8$ years
- Tax Rate $T = 0.25$

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Average annual capital loss per debenture
$$\dfrac{RV - NP}{n} = \dfrac{110 - 100}{8} = \dfrac{10}{8} = ₹1.25 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2:** Numerator
$$I + \dfrac{RV - NP}{n} = 12 + 1.25 = ₹13.25$$

**Step 3:** Denominator (average investment)
$$\dfrac{RV + NP}{2} = \dfrac{110 + 100}{2} = \dfrac{210}{2} = ₹105$$

**Step 4:** Pre-tax cost of debt
$$K_d = \dfrac{13.25}{105} = 0.1262 \approx \mathbf{12.62\%}$$

**Step 5:** After-tax cost of debt
$$K_d(1-T) = 12.62\% \times (1 - 0.25) = 12.62\% \times 0.75 = \mathbf{9.465\%}$$

**Sub-part (b): Compute the WACC [7 Marks]**

**Step 6:** Cost of Equity (using Dividend Growth Model)
$$K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{NP} + g = \dfrac{15}{125} + 0.05 = 0.12 + 0.05 = 17\%$$

**Step 7:** Total Capital
- Equity = 5,000 × ₹125 = ₹6,25,000
- Debt = ₹2,00,000
- Total = ₹8,25,000

**Step 8:** Weights
- $W_e = 6{,}25{,}000 / 8{,}25{,}000 = 0.7576$
- $W_d = 2{,}00{,}000 / 8{,}25{,}000 = 0.2424$

**Step 9:** WACC
$$WACC = W_e \times K_e + W_d \times K_d(1-T)$$
$$WACC = (0.7576 \times 17\%) + (0.2424 \times 9.465\%)$$
$$WACC = 12.88\% + 2.29\% = \mathbf{15.17\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In Question B, students commonly confuse the **issue price with face value** when premium on issue is mentioned. The equity is issued at ₹100 + 25% premium = **₹125 per share**, and this ₹125 is the **net proceeds** for the equity calculation. For the debentures, redeemable at a 10% premium means **RV = ₹110, NOT ₹100**. Failing to incorporate the redemption premium leads to an **incorrect $K_d$** and cascades into a wrong WACC — typically a **4 to 5 mark loss**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Cost of Debt:**

- ✅ **Cost of Debt** = effective interest rate a firm pays on borrowed funds.
- ✅ Always use the **after-tax cost of debt** $K_d(1-T)$ in WACC.
- ✅ **YTM approximation formula** is the most commonly tested method in KTU exams.
- ✅ **Net Proceeds (NP)** = Issue Price − Discount − Flotation Costs − Underwriting Commission.
- ✅ **Redemption Value (RV)** = Par + Premium OR Par − Discount (depending on terms).
- ✅ The **denominator** in the YTM approximation is the **average of RV and NP**, not face value.
- ✅ The **numerator** = Interest + average annual capital gain/loss.
- ✅ Cost of Debt is typically **less than Cost of Equity** due to tax shield and lower risk.
- ✅ Higher financial leverage → higher financial risk → higher $K_d$ demanded by lenders.
- ✅ In India, the **new corporate tax regime** has a base rate of **22%** (effective ~25.17% with cess); the **old regime** is **~30%** (effective ~34.94% with cess).
- ✅ **Perpetual debt** uses the simple formula $K_d = I/NP$, no redemption adjustment needed.
- ✅ The **exact YTM** requires iterative solving; the **approximate method** is algebraically simpler and KTU-preferred unless stated otherwise.
- ✅ Remember the units: Interest, NP, RV are in **currency**; $K_d$ is a **percentage or decimal**; $n$ is **years**; $T$ is a **fraction between 0 and 1**.
- ✅ For WACC: never use **book value weights** unless the problem explicitly states so; prefer **market value weights**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Preference Capital and Equity Capital
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost of Preference Capital and Equity Capital

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is Cost of Capital?

The **Cost of Capital** is the minimum rate of return that a firm must earn on its investment projects to maintain the market value of its shares and satisfy its investors' expectations. It represents the opportunity cost of funds employed in the business.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Cost of capital is the composite cost of the firm's various sources of long-term financing, weighted by their proportions in the capital structure. It is also known as the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** or **Overall Cost of Capital (OCC)**.

## 1.2 Cost of Preference Capital — The Formal Definition

**Cost of Preference Capital ($K_p$)** is the rate of return that preference shareholders expect from their investment in the company. Since preference dividends are fixed in amount and payable at stated intervals, the cost of preference capital is essentially the discount rate that equates the present value of expected preference dividends with the net proceeds from the issue of preference shares.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Preference capital is a **hybrid** form of financing — it combines features of both debt (fixed dividend) and equity (no maturity obligation, dividends not tax-deductible). The cost of preference capital lies between the cost of debt and the cost of equity.

## 1.3 Cost of Equity Capital — The Formal Definition

**Cost of Equity Capital ($K_e$)** is the rate of return that equity shareholders require for investing their money in the firm's shares. It is the opportunity cost of funds invested by the ordinary shareholders, reflecting the risk they bear and the expected appreciation in share value plus dividends.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Principle:** Unlike debt and preference capital, equity shareholders are the **residual claimants** — they receive dividends only after all prior obligations (debt interest and preference dividend) are met. Therefore, the cost of equity is generally the **highest** among all sources of long-term finance.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

### 🍕 The Pizza Investment Analogy

Imagine you and three friends pool money to open a pizza shop:

- **Debt Holder (Bank)** — Gives you ₹1,00,000 at **9% interest**. Guaranteed payment every month. *Low risk, low return.*
- **Preference Shareholder (Aunt Lucy)** — Gives you ₹1,00,000 with a **fixed 8% dividend per year**, paid before the owner's family gets anything. No voting rights, but gets paid regularly. *Medium risk, medium return.*
- **Equity Holder (You)** — Puts in ₹2,00,000. You earn **whatever is left** after paying the bank and Aunt Lucy. Could be a fortune, could be nothing. *Highest risk, highest expected return.*

The **Cost of Preference Capital (8%)** is what Aunt Lucy demands — fair compensation because she gets paid before you but takes more risk than the bank.

The **Cost of Equity (say 15%)** is what *you* demand — a much higher rate because you wait until everyone else is paid, and you absorb all the business risk.

### 📊 The Risk-Return Ladder

| Source of Capital | Risk Level | Typical Cost Range | Payment Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| Debt (Bank Loan) | **Lowest** | 8% – 10% | 1st (Highest) |
| Preference Shares | **Medium** | 9% – 12% | 2nd |
| Equity Shares | **Highest** | 12% – 20% | 3rd (Lowest) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember the Order:** **"Debt-PE-Equity"** → **D**eutsche **P**ost **E**xpress (just remember: Debt paid first, Preference second, Equity last).

---

## 1.5 Types of Preference Shares (Foundation for $K_p$ Calculation)

Understanding the types of preference shares is essential because each variant has a different cost formula.

| Type | Description | Effect on Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Cumulative** | Unpaid dividends accumulate and must be paid before equity dividend | **Increases** $K_p$ (higher risk of non-payment) |
| **Non-Cumulative** | Arrears of dividend are lost if not paid | **Lower** $K_p$ (less risk to investor) |
| **Participating** | Shareholders get extra share in profits | **Increases** $K_p$ |
| **Non-Participating** | Only fixed dividend | **Lower** $K_p$ |
| **Redeemable** | Repayable after a fixed period | Different formula (uses redemption price) |
| **Irredeemable** | No fixed repayment date | Simple formula: $K_p = D_p / P_p$ |
| **Convertible** | Can be converted into equity | **Increases** $K_p$ due to conversion option |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The cost of preference capital ($K_p$) is calculated **after tax** because preference dividends are paid out of **after-tax profits** — they are **NOT tax-deductible** like interest on debt. This is a critical distinction that often appears in exam questions.

---

## 1.6 Methods of Calculating Cost of Equity

There are **four** principal methods used in financial economics:

1. **Dividend Valuation Model (DVM)** — Also called the Gordon Growth Model
2. **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**
3. **Earnings Yield Approach** (Price-Earnings Ratio Method)
4. **Realized Yield Approach** (Historical Method)

Each method is suited to different scenarios and provides a different perspective on the firm's cost of equity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Cost of Preference Capital — Analytical Framework

The cost of preference capital is conceptually similar to the cost of irredeemable debt, with one critical difference: **preference dividends are NOT tax-deductible**, whereas interest on debt is.

### 2.1.1 Case 1: Irredeemable Preference Shares (No Flotation Cost)

When preference shares are perpetual (no maturity) and issued without any issue expenses:

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_p} \times 100$$

Where:
- $K_p$ = Cost of preference capital (in %)
- $D_p$ = Annual preference dividend per share (in ₹)
- $P_p$ = Net proceeds per preference share (in ₹)

### 2.1.2 Case 2: Irredeemable Preference Shares (With Flotation Cost)

If there are issue expenses or underwriting commissions:

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_p - F} \times 100$$

Where $F$ = Flotation cost (issue expenses) per preference share

### 2.1.3 Case 3: Redeemable Preference Shares

When preference shares are repayable after a fixed period at a premium:

$$K_p = \frac{D_p + \frac{(R_p - P_p)}{n}}{\frac{(R_p + P_p)}{2}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $R_p$ = Redemption price per share
- $P_p$ = Net proceeds per share (after flotation cost)
- $n$ = Number of years to redemption
- $D_p$ = Annual preference dividend

### 2.1.4 Tax Adjustment for Preference Dividend

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU:** Unlike interest on debt, preference dividend is **NOT a charge against profit**. It is paid out of **profit after tax (PAT)**. Therefore, **no tax adjustment** is needed in the cost of preference capital formula. The formulas above give the **after-tax cost of preference capital directly**.

---

## 2.2 Cost of Equity Capital — Analytical Framework

### 2.2.1 Method 1: Dividend Valuation Model (DVM) — Gordon's Model

**Assumption:** Dividends grow at a constant rate $g$ perpetually.

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$$

Where:
- $K_e$ = Cost of equity (%)
- $D_1$ = Expected dividend at the end of year 1 ($D_1 = D_0(1+g)$)
- $P_0$ = Current market price per share
- $g$ = Constant growth rate of dividends

**Under Constant Growth Assumption:**

$$K_e = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{P_0} + g$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation of Gordon's Model:** It is derived from the present value of a perpetuity with growing dividends. $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_0(1+g)^t}{(1+K_e)^t} = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g}$

### 2.2.2 Method 2: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)

$$K_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$$

Where:
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate of return (e.g., Government Securities yield)
- $\beta$ = Beta coefficient (measure of systematic risk)
- $R_m$ = Expected return on the market portfolio
- $(R_m - R_f)$ = Market risk premium

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of Beta:**
> - $\beta = 1$: Stock has the same risk as the market
> - $\beta > 1$: Stock is more volatile than the market (aggressive)
> - $\beta < 1$: Stock is less volatile than the market (defensive)
> - $\beta < 0$: Stock moves opposite to the market (rare, e.g., gold)

### 2.2.3 Method 3: Earning Yield Approach (P/E Ratio Method)

$$K_e = \frac{E_1}{P_0} \times 100$$

Where:
- $E_1$ = Expected earnings per share (EPS)
- $P_0$ = Current market price per share

**With Growth Adjustment:**

$$K_e = \frac{E_1}{P_0} + g$$

Where $g$ = Expected growth rate in earnings

### 2.2.4 Method 4: Realized Yield Approach

This method uses historical data:

$$K_e = \frac{\text{Total Realized Return}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times 100$$

Where Total Realized Return includes dividends received plus capital gains (or losses) over the holding period.

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| # | Source | Formula | Key Assumption | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Irredeemable Pref. (No Flotation) | $K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_p}$ | Perpetual life, no issue cost | Default case |
| 2 | Irredeemable Pref. (With Flotation) | $K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_p - F}$ | Perpetual life, issue cost exists | When underwriting/issue cost given |
| 3 | Redeemable Pref. | $K_p = \frac{D_p + \frac{(R_p - P_p)}{n}}{\frac{(R_p + P_p)}{2}}$ | Fixed maturity at premium | When redemption price given |
| 4 | Equity (DVM — Zero Growth) | $K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0}$ | Constant dividend forever | Mature companies, no growth |
| 5 | Equity (Gordon's Model) | $K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | Constant growth rate $g$ | Growing companies |
| 6 | Equity (CAPM) | $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | Market portfolio exists | Risk-based valuation |
| 7 | Equity (Earnings Yield) | $K_e = \frac{E_1}{P_0}$ | EPS-based valuation | When dividends not stable |
| 8 | Equity (P/E with Growth) | $K_e = \frac{E_1}{P_0} + g$ | Earnings grow at rate $g$ | High-growth companies |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Decision Rule for Exam:** If the question mentions "growth rate" → use DVM/Gordon Model. If "beta" or "risk-free rate" is mentioned → use CAPM. If only "dividend" and "price" are given → use DVM. If "earnings per share" is given → use Earnings Yield.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Project Decisions

### Application in Capital Budgeting

In engineering firms, especially those undertaking large infrastructure projects, the cost of capital is used as the **discount rate** to evaluate Net Present Value (NPV) of projects.

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+K)^t} - C_0$$

Where $K$ is the **WACC** (weighted average cost of capital) — a weighted average of $K_d$, $K_p$, and $K_e$.

### Engineering Industry Examples

| Industry | Typical Capital Structure | Why Cost of Capital Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Generation** | High debt (70%) | Choosing between thermal vs. renewable projects |
| **IT Services** | High equity (60-70%) | Evaluating R&D investments |
| **Construction** | Mixed (debt + equity) | Bid pricing for long-term contracts |
| **Telecom** | High debt initially | Spectrum acquisition decisions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Insight:** In 2023-24, Indian companies like **Reliance Industries** and **Tata Power** have used WACC ranging from **8% to 12%** for evaluating renewable energy projects. The cost of equity component ($K_e$) typically ranges from **13% to 16%**, reflecting India's equity risk premium.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost of Irredeemable Preference Shares

**Starting Point:** The market price of a preference share equals the present value of all future dividends (perpetuity).

Let:
- $D_p$ = Constant annual preference dividend
- $P_0$ = Current market price
- $K_p$ = Cost of preference capital

The share value is a perpetuity:

$$P_0 = \frac{D_p}{K_p}$$

Rearranging for $K_p$:

$$K_p \cdot P_0 = D_p$$

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_0}$$

Expressing as a percentage:

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_0} \times 100$$

This derivation assumes that preference shares have no maturity and the dividend is paid indefinitely, similar to a perpetuity bond.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cost of Redeemable Preference Shares

**Starting Point:** The net proceeds equal the present value of (a) annual dividends for $n$ years and (b) redemption price at year $n$.

$$P_p = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{D_p}{(1+K_p)^t} + \frac{R_p}{(1+K_p)^n}$$

Using the present value of annuity formula:

$$P_p = D_p \cdot \frac{1 - (1+K_p)^{-n}}{K_p} + \frac{R_p}{(1+K_p)^n}$$

For approximation (used in KTU textbooks), the **approximate cost** is calculated using:

$$K_p = \frac{D_p + \frac{(R_p - P_p)}{n}}{\frac{(R_p + P_p)}{2}}$$

**Explanation of Components:**

- **Numerator:**
  - $D_p$ = Annual dividend income (recurring return)
  - $\frac{(R_p - P_p)}{n}$ = Annualized capital gain or loss on redemption (spread over $n$ years)

- **Denominator:**
  - $\frac{(R_p + P_p)}{2}$ = Average value of the share over its life

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Average Denominator?** Because the investor holds the share at different values over its life — starting at $P_p$ and ending at $R_p$. The average represents the typical capital tied up.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Gordon's Dividend Growth Model

**Starting Point:** The market price of an equity share equals the present value of all future dividends growing at a constant rate $g$.

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)^1} + \frac{D_2}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{D_3}{(1+K_e)^3} + \cdots$$

Since $D_t = D_0(1+g)^t$, and assuming $K_e > g$:

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_0(1+g)^t}{(1+K_e)^t}$$

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_1(1+g)^{t-1}}{(1+K_e)^t}$$

Using the infinite geometric series formula:

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g}$$

Rearranging for $K_e$:

$$P_0(K_e - g) = D_1$$

$$K_e - g = \frac{D_1}{P_0}$$

$$\boxed{K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Condition:** This model is valid only when $K_e > g$. If $K_e \leq g$, the denominator becomes zero or negative, leading to a meaningless or infinite price. This is a frequent KTU exam trap!

---

## 3.4 Derivation: CAPM

**Starting Point:** The Capital Asset Pricing Model is derived from the **Capital Market Line (CML)** and **Security Market Line (SML)**.

The SML equation for the expected return of any security is:

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \cdot \sigma_i$$

Where $\sigma$ denotes standard deviation. Replacing $\sigma_i / \sigma_m$ with $\beta$:

$$\beta_i = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$$

We get:

$$\boxed{K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)}$$

Where:
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate (intercept on SML)
- $\beta(R_m - R_f)$ = Risk premium for security $i$

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Numerical Solved Examples

### 📘 Example 1: Cost of Irredeemable Preference Shares

**Problem:** A company issues 10,000 preference shares of ₹100 each at par. The annual dividend rate is 8%. Calculate the cost of preference capital.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Face value ($F$) = ₹100
- Issue price = ₹100 (at par)
- Annual dividend rate = 8%
- Number of shares = 10,000

**Step 1:** Calculate annual dividend per share.

$$D_p = 8\% \text{ of } ₹100 = ₹8 \text{ per share}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the cost of preference capital formula.

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_p} \times 100$$

$$K_p = \frac{8}{100} \times 100 = 8\%$$

**Answer:** Cost of preference capital = **8%**

---

### 📘 Example 2: Cost of Preference Shares with Flotation Cost

**Problem:** A company issues 10,000, 9% preference shares of ₹100 each at a premium of 10%. The issue expenses amount to ₹2 per share. Compute the cost of preference capital.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Face value = ₹100
- Issue price = ₹100 + 10% premium = ₹110
- Flotation cost ($F$) = ₹2 per share
- Dividend rate = 9%

**Step 1:** Calculate annual dividend.

$$D_p = 9\% \text{ of } ₹100 = ₹9$$

**Step 2:** Calculate net proceeds.

$$P_p = ₹110 - ₹2 = ₹108$$

**Step 3:** Apply the formula.

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_p} \times 100 = \frac{9}{108} \times 100$$

$$K_p = 8.33\%$$

**Answer:** Cost of preference capital = **8.33%**

---

### 📘 Example 3: Cost of Redeemable Preference Shares

**Problem:** A company issues 5,000, 10% redeemable preference shares of ₹100 each at par. The shares are redeemable after 10 years at a premium of 10%. Compute the cost of preference capital.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Face value = ₹100
- Issue price ($P_p$) = ₹100 (at par)
- Redemption price ($R_p$) = ₹100 + 10% = ₹110
- Dividend rate = 10%
- Number of years ($n$) = 10

**Step 1:** Calculate annual dividend.

$$D_p = 10\% \text{ of } ₹100 = ₹10$$

**Step 2:** Calculate annualized capital gain.

$$\frac{(R_p - P_p)}{n} = \frac{(110 - 100)}{10} = \frac{10}{10} = ₹1$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the average value.

$$\frac{(R_p + P_p)}{2} = \frac{(110 + 100)}{2} = \frac{210}{2} = ₹105$$

**Step 4:** Apply the formula.

$$K_p = \frac{D_p + \frac{(R_p - P_p)}{n}}{\frac{(R_p + P_p)}{2}} \times 100$$

$$K_p = \frac{10 + 1}{105} \times 100 = \frac{11}{105} \times 100$$

$$K_p = 10.48\%$$

**Answer:** Cost of preference capital = **10.48%**

---

### 📘 Example 4: Cost of Equity using Gordon's Model

**Problem:** A company's equity share is currently selling at ₹120. The company has just paid a dividend of ₹6 per share. The expected growth rate in dividends is 8%. Calculate the cost of equity capital.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Current market price ($P_0$) = ₹120
- Just paid dividend ($D_0$) = ₹6
- Growth rate ($g$) = 8%

**Step 1:** Calculate expected dividend next year.

$$D_1 = D_0(1+g) = 6 \times (1.08) = ₹6.48$$

**Step 2:** Apply Gordon's Model.

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$$

$$K_e = \frac{6.48}{120} + 0.08$$

$$K_e = 0.054 + 0.08 = 0.134$$

**Answer:** Cost of equity capital = **13.4%**

---

### 📘 Example 5: Cost of Equity using CAPM

**Problem:** The risk-free rate is 7%. The expected market return is 14%. The beta coefficient of a company's share is 1.2. Compute the cost of equity.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Risk-free rate ($R_f$) = 7%
- Expected market return ($R_m$) = 14%
- Beta ($\beta$) = 1.2

**Step 1:** Calculate market risk premium.

$$R_m - R_f = 14\% - 7\% = 7\%$$

**Step 2:** Apply CAPM.

$$K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$$

$$K_e = 7 + 1.2 \times 7$$

$$K_e = 7 + 8.4 = 15.4\%$$

**Answer:** Cost of equity capital = **15.4%**

---

### 📘 Example 6: Cost of Equity using Earnings Yield Approach

**Problem:** A company's shares are trading at ₹150. Expected earnings per share (EPS) is ₹15. Expected growth rate is 5%. Compute the cost of equity.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Market price ($P_0$) = ₹150
- Expected EPS ($E_1$) = ₹15
- Growth rate ($g$) = 5%

**Step 1:** Apply Earnings Yield formula with growth adjustment.

$$K_e = \frac{E_1}{P_0} + g$$

$$K_e = \frac{15}{150} + 0.05$$

$$K_e = 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.15$$

**Answer:** Cost of equity capital = **15%**

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — $K_p$ vs $K_e$

| Parameter | Cost of Preference Capital ($K_p$) | Cost of Equity Capital ($K_e$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature of Return** | Fixed dividend | Variable (dividends + capital gains) |
| **Risk to Investor** | Medium | High |
| **Priority of Payment** | Before equity | After preference & debt |
| **Tax Treatment** | Paid from PAT (no tax shield) | Paid from PAT (no tax shield) |
| **Voting Rights** | Usually none | Yes |
| **Maturity** | May be redeemable or perpetual | Perpetual |
| **Volatility** | Low to medium | High |
| **Typical Range** | 8% – 12% | 12% – 20% |
| **Common Models** | Direct dividend yield | DVM, CAPM, Earnings Yield |
| **Cost in Capital Structure** | Lower than $K_e$, higher than $K_d$ | Highest among all sources |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "PEAD":** **P**reference dividend paid **E**ven if no profit? **A**ttempt later — **D**epends on company policy. This helps remember that preference dividends are NOT legally compulsory (unlike debt interest).
