# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📝 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Cost of Preference Capital. Why is it considered a hybrid form of financing?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Cost of preference capital is the rate of return that preference shareholders expect from their investment in the company. It is the discount rate that equates the present value of expected preference dividends with the net proceeds from the issue.

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_p} \times 100$$

**Hybrid Nature (1 Mark):** It combines features of:
- **Debt** — fixed dividend, paid before equity
- **Equity** — no tax deduction, paid from after-tax profits, no maturity obligation (if irredeemable)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Explain the Dividend Valuation Model for computing the cost of equity.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The DVM, also called **Gordon's Growth Model**, assumes dividends grow at a constant rate $g$ forever (2 Marks):

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{P_0} + g$$

Where $D_1$ = next year's expected dividend, $P_0$ = current price, $g$ = growth rate (1 Mark).

The model requires $K_e > g$ for validity.

---

## 📝 PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH)

### ⚙️ INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — Answer ANY ONE

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A company has the following capital structure:
- 5,000, 12% preference shares of ₹100 each, issued at a premium of 5%, redeemable after 8 years at par.
- Flotation cost is ₹3 per share.

Calculate the cost of preference capital. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The equity share of a company is currently selling at ₹250. The company is expected to pay a dividend of ₹15 next year. The dividend growth rate is 6% per annum. Using Gordon's Model, calculate the cost of equity. If the risk-free rate is 7%, market return is 13%, and beta is 1.15, compute the cost of equity using CAPM and comment on which value should be preferred. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Cost of Redeemable Preference Capital (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Number of shares = 5,000
- Face value = ₹100
- Issue price = ₹100 + 5% premium = ₹105
- Flotation cost = ₹3 per share
- Net proceeds $P_p$ = ₹105 − ₹3 = **₹102**
- Redemption price $R_p$ = ₹100 (at par)
- Redemption period $n$ = 8 years
- Dividend rate = 12%

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Calculate annual dividend per share.

$$D_p = 12\% \text{ of } ₹100 = ₹12$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Calculate annualized capital gain/loss.

$$\frac{(R_p - P_p)}{n} = \frac{(100 - 102)}{8} = \frac{-2}{8} = -₹0.25$$

*(Negative because the issue price > redemption price — investor suffers a small capital loss on redemption)*

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Calculate the average value.

$$\frac{(R_p + P_p)}{2} = \frac{(100 + 102)}{2} = ₹101$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Apply the formula.

$$K_p = \frac{D_p + \frac{(R_p - P_p)}{n}}{\frac{(R_p + P_p)}{2}} \times 100$$

$$K_p = \frac{12 + (-0.25)}{101} \times 100$$

$$K_p = \frac{11.75}{101} \times 100$$

$$K_p = 11.63\%$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Final answer with interpretation.

**Answer:** **Cost of preference capital = 11.63%**

The cost is slightly higher than the nominal 12% dividend rate because of the flotation cost and capital loss on redemption.

---

#### Part (b) — Cost of Equity using DVM and CAPM (7 Marks)

**Method 1: Gordon's Model (3 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $P_0$ = ₹250
- $D_1$ = ₹15 (next year dividend)
- $g$ = 6%

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{15}{250} + 0.06$$

$$K_e = 0.06 + 0.06 = 0.12 = 12\%$$

**Method 2: CAPM (3 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $R_f$ = 7%
- $R_m$ = 13%
- $\beta$ = 1.15

$$K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$$

$$K_e = 7 + 1.15 \times (13 - 7)$$

$$K_e = 7 + 1.15 \times 6$$

$$K_e = 7 + 6.9 = 13.9\%$$

**Commentary (1 Mark):**

CAPM gives a higher cost (13.9%) than DVM (12%). For decision-making, the **CAPM value is generally preferred** because:
- It incorporates market risk (beta)
- It reflects the systematic risk of the specific firm
- DVM relies heavily on the stable growth assumption which may not hold

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** A company issues 10,000 irredeemable preference shares of ₹100 each at par. The dividend rate is 9%. The company incurs an issue expense of ₹5 per share. Compute the cost of preference capital. If the company now decides to make these shares redeemable at ₹110 after 10 years, calculate the new cost of preference capital. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A firm has the following data for the equity share:
- Current price = ₹180
- Expected EPS next year = ₹18
- Constant growth rate = 5%
- Risk-free rate = 6%
- Market return = 12%
- Beta = 0.9

Calculate the cost of equity using (i) Earnings Yield Approach, (ii) CAPM. Which method is more reliable and why? **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Irredeemable vs Redeemable Cost (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Identify given data.
- Face value = ₹100
- Issue price = ₹100 (at par)
- Dividend rate = 9%
- Flotation cost = ₹5
- Net proceeds = ₹100 − ₹5 = **₹95**

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Cost of irredeemable preference shares.

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_p - F} = \frac{9}{95} \times 100 = 9.47\%$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Identify new redeemable parameters.
- Redemption price $R_p$ = ₹110
- $n$ = 10 years
- Issue price $P_p$ = ₹95 (after flotation)

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Apply redeemable formula.

$$K_p = \frac{D_p + \frac{(R_p - P_p)}{n}}{\frac{(R_p + P_p)}{2}} \times 100$$

$$K_p = \frac{9 + \frac{(110 - 95)}{10}}{\frac{(110 + 95)}{2}} \times 100$$

$$K_p = \frac{9 + 1.5}{102.5} \times 100$$

$$K_p = \frac{10.5}{102.5} \times 100 = 10.24\%$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Comparison.

**Answer:** Cost increased from **9.47% (irredeemable)** to **10.24% (redeemable)** because of the premium on redemption.

---

#### Part (b) — Earnings Yield and CAPM (7 Marks)

**Method (i): Earnings Yield Approach (3 Marks)**

$$K_e = \frac{E_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{18}{180} + 0.05$$

$$K_e = 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.15 = 15\%$$

**Method (ii): CAPM (3 Marks)**

$$K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f) = 6 + 0.9 \times (12 - 6)$$

$$K_e = 6 + 0.9 \times 6 = 6 + 5.4 = 11.4\%$$

**Reliability Comment (1 Mark):**

**CAPM (11.4%) is more reliable** for this firm because:
- The Earnings Yield Approach assumes constant earnings growth, which may be unrealistic
- CAPM explicitly incorporates market risk via beta (0.9 indicates below-average risk)
- CAPM is based on market equilibrium theory and is widely accepted

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / COMMON PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES — READ CAREFULLY BEFORE EXAM!**

### 🚫 Pitfall 1: Tax Treatment Confusion
**Mistake:** Applying tax adjustment to preference dividend.
**Reality:** Preference dividends are paid from **after-tax profits (PAT)**. They are **NOT tax-deductible**. The formula gives the **after-tax cost directly** — no further tax adjustment needed.
**Mark Loss:** Up to **2 marks** if applied incorrectly.

### 🚫 Pitfall 2: Using $D_0$ Instead of $D_1$ in Gordon's Model
**Mistake:** Using the dividend just paid ($D_0$) without adjusting for growth.
**Reality:** The formula uses **next year's expected dividend** $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$.
**Mark Loss:** Entire problem may be marked wrong (**7 marks**).

### 🚫 Pitfall 3: Forgetting to Subtract Flotation Cost
**Mistake:** Using issue price instead of net proceeds.
**Reality:** If issue expenses are given, use $P_p - F$ as net proceeds.
**Mark Loss:** **1-2 marks**.

### 🚫 Pitfall 4: Wrong Formula for Redeemable vs Irredeemable
**Mistake:** Using the simple formula $D_p/P_p$ for redeemable shares.
**Reality:** Redeemable shares require the full formula including redemption price and period.
**Mark Loss:** **3-4 marks**.

### 🚫 Pitfall 5: CAPM Sign Errors
**Mistake:** Writing $R_m + R_f$ instead of $R_m - R_f$.
**Reality:** It is the **market risk premium** ($R_m - R_f$), not sum.
**Mark Loss:** **2-3 marks**.

### 🚫 Pitfall 6: Not Specifying Units / Percentages
**Mistake:** Writing $K_p = 0.10$ without clarification.
**Reality:** Always write **10%** or **10 percent** to get full marks.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY QUICK REVISION — Memorize Before Exam!**

### 🔑 Key Formulas to Memorize

- ✅ Irredeemable Preference: $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_p} \times 100$
- ✅ With Flotation: $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_p - F} \times 100$
- ✅ Redeemable Preference: $K_p = \dfrac{D_p + \frac{(R_p - P_p)}{n}}{\frac{(R_p + P_p)}{2}} \times 100$
- ✅ DVM (Zero Growth): $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} \times 100$
- ✅ DVM (Constant Growth / Gordon): $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$
- ✅ CAPM: $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$
- ✅ Earnings Yield: $K_e = \dfrac{E_1}{P_0} \times 100$ (or with growth: $+ g$)

### 🔑 Critical Concepts to Remember

- ✅ Preference dividend is **NOT tax-deductible** (paid from PAT)
- ✅ Equity has **residual claim** — paid last, bears highest risk
- ✅ Gordon's Model validity: $K_e > g$ always
- ✅ Beta interpretation: $>1$ aggressive, $<1$ defensive, $=1$ neutral
- ✅ Cost order: $K_d < K_p < K_e$ (always)
- ✅ Cost of capital is used as **discount rate** in NPV, IRR calculations
- ✅ WACC = $W_d K_d + W_p K_p + W_e K_e + W_r K_r$

### 🔑 Numerical Problem-Solving Sequence

1. **Read question carefully** — identify what is given (dividend? price? growth? beta?)
2. **Select appropriate formula** — based on type (redeemable? irredeemable?) and method (DVM? CAPM?)
3. **Calculate net proceeds** — remember to subtract flotation cost
4. **Apply formula step-by-step** — show all intermediate calculations
5. **State final answer with units** — e.g., "Cost = 11.63%"
6. **Add brief interpretation** — what does this number mean for the firm?

### 🔑 Common Exam Traps

- ⚠️ Issue price ≠ Net proceeds (flotation cost must be subtracted)
- ⚠️ $D_0$ vs $D_1$ — always use the **expected future dividend**
- ⚠️ Redeemable shares need **redemption price** in calculation
- ⚠️ CAPM uses **risk premium** $(R_m - R_f)$, not the sum
- ⚠️ If growth rate $g = 0$, then Gordon's reduces to simple $D_1/P_0$

### 🔑 Real-World Anchors

- 🌍 Indian preference shares typically yield **8%–12%**
- 🌍 Indian equity cost ranges **12%–18%**
- 🌍 US Treasury 10-year yield (proxy for $R_f$): **~4%–5%** (2024)
- 🌍 Indian 10-year G-Sec yield (proxy for $R_f$): **~7%**
- 🌍 Indian market risk premium: **~5%–7%**

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always **label your formulas**, **show all substitutions**, and **box your final answer**. KTU examiners award step marks generously — even if the final answer is wrong, intermediate correct steps earn 60-70% marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Market Lines and Use of Models in Investment Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET834 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security Market Lines and Use of Models in Investment Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Security Market Line (SML)

The **Security Market Line (SML)** is the graphical representation of the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**. It plots the **expected return** of an individual security on the vertical (Y) axis against its **systematic risk, measured by Beta (β)**, on the horizontal (X) axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> "The Security Market Line is the locus of all securities and portfolios whose expected returns and systematic risks (betas) satisfy the equilibrium condition of the Capital Asset Pricing Model, expressed as: **E(Ri) = Rf + βi[E(Rm) − Rf]**."

In a fair and efficient market, **every security MUST lie ON the SML** in equilibrium. If a security plots *above* the SML, it is **underpriced** (offers more return for its risk). If it plots *below* the SML, it is **overpriced** (offers less return for its risk).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Mountain Hiking Trail" 🏔️

Imagine you are hiking up a mountain. The trail's slope represents **risk**, and the altitude represents **return**.

- The **trailhead (starting point)** is the **risk-free rate (Rf)** — what you earn by doing nothing risky (e.g., Government Treasury Bills).
- The **slope of the trail** is the **market risk premium** — the "extra reward" for taking on each additional unit of risk.
- Each hiker (investor) choosing a steeper or gentler trail chooses their own risk-return trade-off.
- The SML is the *only fair, equilibrium trail*. Any hiker (stock) found to the *right* of the trail (taking more risk than rewarded) or *above* the trail (rewarded more than their risk deserves) signals a **mispricing**.

## 1.2 The Capital Market Line (CML)

The **Capital Market Line (CML)** represents the **risk-return combinations available to investors who hold efficient portfolios** (combinations of the risk-free asset and the market portfolio). Unlike the SML, the CML uses **total risk (standard deviation, σ)** on the X-axis, not beta.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **SML** → uses **Beta (β)** → applies to *all assets* (efficient & inefficient)
> - **CML** → uses **Standard Deviation (σ)** → applies *only* to *efficient portfolios*

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Sip of Lemonade" 🥤

Think of an investor's portfolio as lemonade:
- **Pure water** = Risk-free asset (Rf) — safe but boring taste.
- **Pure lemon juice** = Market portfolio (M) — risky, tangy, full of flavour.
- **Your lemonade glass** = A mix of water + lemon juice in any proportion.
- The **CML** is the recipe line for *every possible glass of lemonade* — showing the trade-off between safety and tanginess. Any "efficient lemonade" is on this line.

## 1.3 The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)

CAPM is the foundational equilibrium model that describes the relationship between **systematic risk** and **expected return** for assets. It was developed independently by **William Sharpe (1964)**, **John Lintner (1965)**, and **Jan Mossin (1966)**, building on **Harry Markowitz's Modern Portfolio Theory (1952)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAPM Assumptions (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):**
> 1. Investors are **rational** and **risk-averse**, maximizing expected utility.
> 2. All investors have a **single-period investment horizon**.
> 3. All investors have **homogeneous expectations** about returns, variances, and covariances.
> 4. There are **no taxes, transaction costs, or restrictions** on borrowing/lending at the risk-free rate.
> 5. All assets are **infinitely divisible** and **perfectly marketable**.
> 6. Markets are **perfectly competitive** and **frictionless**.

## 1.4 Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT)

Developed by **Stephen Ross (1976)**, APT is a **multi-factor** model that relaxes several CAPM assumptions. It states that an asset's expected return is explained by its **sensitivity to multiple macroeconomic factors**, not just market risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **APT vs CAPM — Quick Insight:**
> APT does NOT require the market portfolio to exist, does NOT assume normal returns, and does NOT rely on a single risk factor. It is more flexible but harder to operationalize.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building the SML — The Logic Chain

The SML is derived from the CAPM through the following logical sequence:

1. **Step 1 — Risk Decomposition:** Total risk = Systematic Risk (β) + Unsystematic Risk (σe).
   * Systematic risk is *market-wide* (recessions, interest rates, wars) — **non-diversifiable**.
   * Unsystematic risk is *firm-specific* (management failure, product recall) — **diversifiable**.

2. **Step 2 — Diversification Argument:** As the number of stocks in a portfolio increases, unsystematic risk approaches **zero**, but systematic risk remains. Therefore, in equilibrium, investors are only rewarded for **systematic (non-diversifiable) risk**.

3. **Step 3 — Beta as the Only Pricing Factor:** Since only systematic risk matters, the expected return must be a linear function of **β**.

4. **Step 4 — Two-Point Calibration:**
   * When **β = 0** → Expected return = **Rf** (risk-free asset).
   * When **β = 1** → Expected return = **E(Rm)** (market portfolio).

5. **Step 5 — Linear Interpolation:** The straight line through these two points gives the **SML equation**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Component

| Component | Symbol | Economic Meaning |
|-----------|--------|------------------|
| Risk-Free Rate | Rf | The "time value of money" baseline; reward for postponing consumption. |
| Beta | βi | Sensitivity of stock i's returns to market returns. **β = 1** = same volatility as market. |
| Market Risk Premium | E(Rm) − Rf | The "excess reward" for bearing average market risk. |
| Risk Premium for Asset i | βi[E(Rm) − Rf] | The compensation an investor demands for holding stock i's systematic risk. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR EXAMS**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables Explained | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **CAPM / SML** | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$ | $E(R_i)$ = expected return on asset; $R_f$ = risk-free rate; $\beta_i$ = beta of asset; $E(R_m)$ = expected market return | Returns in **% per annum**; $\beta$ is dimensionless |
| 2 | **Beta Formula** | $\beta_i = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$ | Covariance of asset return with market; Variance of market return | $\beta$ is dimensionless |
| 3 | **CML Equation** | $E(R_p) = R_f + \left[\dfrac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m}\right] \sigma_p$ | $\sigma_p$ = portfolio standard deviation; $\sigma_m$ = market standard deviation | Slope = **Sharpe Ratio of Market** |
| 4 | **Sharpe Ratio** | $S = \dfrac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Reward-to-total-risk ratio of a portfolio | Higher is better |
| 5 | **Treynor Ratio** | $T = \dfrac{R_p - R_f}{\beta_p}$ | Reward-to-systematic-risk ratio of a portfolio | Used to rank well-diversified funds |
| 6 | **Jensen's Alpha** | $\alpha_p = R_p - \left[R_f + \beta_p (R_m - R_f)\right]$ | Excess return over CAPM prediction | $\alpha > 0$ = outperformance |
| 7 | **APT (Multi-Factor)** | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_{i1} RP_1 + \beta_{i2} RP_2 + \ldots + \beta_{in} RP_n$ | $RP_k$ = risk premium of factor k | n factors can be macro/micro |
| 8 | **Fama-French 3-Factor** | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_M(R_m - R_f) + \beta_{SMB} \cdot SMB + \beta_{HML} \cdot HML$ | SMB = size factor; HML = value factor | Adds Size & Value dimensions |
| 9 | **Characteristic Line** | $R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_m + \varepsilon_i$ | Regression of stock return on market return | Slope = β; Intercept = α (Jensen's alpha) |
| 10 | **Portfolio Beta** | $\beta_p = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \beta_i$ | Weighted average of individual betas | $w_i$ = weight in asset i |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance Fields

- **Portfolio Management:** Fund managers use β to construct portfolios aligned with clients' risk tolerance (e.g., pension funds → low β; hedge funds → high β).
- **Corporate Finance:** Companies use CAPM as the **discount rate (cost of equity)** in **DCF valuation models**.
- **Capital Budgeting:** Engineers and project managers use CAPM-derived hurdle rates for **NPV calculations** on large infrastructure projects.
- **Risk Management:** Banks use the SML framework to price **risk-weighted assets** under Basel norms.
- **Performance Evaluation:** Sharpe, Treynor, and Jensen's Alpha are standard KPIs in **investment performance attribution reports**.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The SML from CAPM (Two-Point Form)

**Given:**
- The risk-free asset yields $R_f$ with $\beta = 0$.
- The market portfolio has expected return $E(R_m)$ with $\beta = 1$.

**Step 1:** Set up the linear equation form:

$$E(R_i) = a + b \cdot \beta_i$$

**Step 2:** Apply the boundary condition at $\beta = 0$:

$$E(R_i) = E(R_f) = R_f \quad \text{when} \quad \beta_i = 0$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\beta_i = 0$ into the linear equation:

$$R_f = a + b(0) \implies a = R_f$$

**Step 4:** Apply the second boundary condition at $\beta = 1$:

$$E(R_m) = a + b(1)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $a = R_f$:

$$E(R_m) = R_f + b \implies b = E(R_m) - R_f$$

**Step 6:** Substitute both $a$ and $b$ back into the general linear form:

$$E(R_i) = R_f + [E(R_m) - R_f] \cdot \beta_i$$

**Step 7:** Final compact form (CAPM / SML equation):

$$\boxed{E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The intercept $a$ equals $R_f$ because the SML crosses the Y-axis at the risk-free rate (zero beta asset). The slope $b$ equals the market risk premium because increasing β by 1 (full unit of market risk) raises expected return by exactly $E(R_m) - R_f$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Jensen's Alpha from the Security Characteristic Line

**Given:** The regression of stock returns on market returns over $T$ periods:

$$R_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_{m,t} + \varepsilon_{i,t}$$

**Step 1:** The CAPM-implied return for the asset in any period $t$ is:

$$E(R_{i,t} \vert R_{m,t}) = R_f + \beta_i [R_{m,t} - R_f]$$

**Step 2:** The actual realized return is:

$$R_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_{m,t} + \varepsilon_{i,t}$$

**Step 3:** The realized *excess* return over the risk-free rate is:

$$R_{i,t} - R_f = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_{m,t} + \varepsilon_{i,t} - R_f$$

**Step 4:** The CAPM-implied *excess* return is:

$$R_f + \beta_i [R_{m,t} - R_f] - R_f = \beta_i [R_{m,t} - R_f]$$

**Step 5:** Jensen's Alpha is the average *excess* return over the CAPM prediction:

$$\alpha_i = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ (R_{i,t} - R_f) - \beta_i (R_{m,t} - R_f) \right]$$

**Step 6:** Final compact form:

$$\boxed{\alpha_i = \bar{R}_i - \left[ R_f + \beta_i (\bar{R}_m - R_f) \right]}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $\alpha_i$ measures whether the security "beat" its CAPM-implied benchmark. A positive $\alpha_i$ means the security is plotted above the SML (underpriced), while a negative $\alpha_i$ means it is plotted below the SML (overpriced).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capital Market Line (CML) from the Two-Fund Separation Theorem

**Given:** Investor can split wealth between the risk-free asset ($R_f$, $\sigma = 0$) and the market portfolio $M$ ($E(R_m)$, $\sigma_m$).

**Step 1:** The expected return of a combined portfolio $P$ with weight $w$ in the market portfolio:

$$E(R_p) = w \cdot E(R_m) + (1-w) \cdot R_f$$

**Step 2:** Standard deviation of the combined portfolio (assuming zero correlation between $R_f$ and $R_m$):

$$\sigma_p = w \cdot \sigma_m + (1-w) \cdot 0 = w \cdot \sigma_m$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $w$ from the standard deviation equation:

$$w = \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $w$ back into the expected return equation:

$$E(R_p) = \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m} \cdot E(R_m) + \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m}\right) \cdot R_f$$

**Step 5:** Expand and group the constants:

$$E(R_p) = R_f \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m}\right) + E(R_m) \cdot \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify by grouping $R_f$ and the risk-premium term:

$$E(R_p) = R_f + \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m} \cdot \left[ E(R_m) - R_f \right]$$

**Step 7:** Final compact CML form:

$$\boxed{E(R_p) = R_f + \left[ \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \right] \cdot \sigma_p}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The slope of the CML, $\frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m}$, is the **Sharpe Ratio of the market portfolio**. It represents the price of total risk (in return units) per unit of standard deviation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — SML & Alpha Calculation

**Problem Data:**
- Risk-free rate, $R_f = 6\%$
- Expected market return, $E(R_m) = 14\%$
- Stock XYZ: $\beta_{XYZ} = 1.4$, Actual return realized $R_{XYZ} = 18\%$

**Step 1:** Calculate the Market Risk Premium:

$$E(R_m) - R_f = 14\% - 6\% = 8\%$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the CAPM-implied expected return for XYZ:

$$E(R_{XYZ}) = 6\% + 1.4 \times 8\% = 6\% + 11.2\% = 17.2\%$$

**Step 3:** Calculate Jensen's Alpha for XYZ:

$$\alpha_{XYZ} = R_{XYZ} - E(R_{XYZ}) = 18\% - 17.2\% = 0.8\%$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation:

$$\alpha_{XYZ} = +0.8\% > 0 \implies \text{Stock XYZ OUTPERFORMED the SML benchmark by 80 basis points.}$$

> **Conclusion:** XYZ is **underpriced** (or "wins") relative to CAPM equilibrium. A rational investor should BUY or OVERWEIGHT XYZ.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of SML, CML, CAPM & APT

| Dimension | **SML** | **CML** | **CAPM** | **APT** |
|-----------|---------|---------|----------|---------|
| **Type** | Graph (line) | Graph (line) | Model | Model |
| **X-axis** | Beta (β) | Std. Deviation (σ) | N/A (conceptual) | N/A (conceptual) |
| **Y-axis** | Expected Return | Expected Return | Expected Return | Expected Return |
| **Risk Measured** | Systematic only | Total risk | Systematic only | Multiple factors |
| **Applies To** | All assets & portfolios | Efficient portfolios only | All assets | All assets |
| **No. of Factors** | 1 (market) | 1 (market) | 1 (market) | n (multiple) |
| **Market Portfolio Required** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Assumptions** | Many (CAPM) | Many (CAPM) | Strict | Fewer, more flexible |
| **Equilibrium Required** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (Arbitrage-based) |
| **Developer(s)** | Sharpe, Lintner, Mossin | Markowitz / Tobin | Sharpe (1964) | Ross (1976) |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SML Diagram (Block-Level Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Risk-Free Asset] -->|beta = 0| B[SML Equilibrium Line]
    C[Stock with beta = 1] -->|market portfolio| B
    D[Stock with beta = 1.5] -->|above market| B
    E[Stock with beta = 0.6] -->|below market| B
    B --> F[Intercept = Rf]
    B --> G[Slope = E Rm minus Rf]
    B --> H[Above SML = Underpriced]
    B --> I[Below SML = Overpriced]
    H --> J[BUY Signal]
    I --> K[SELL Signal]
```

## 4.2 CAPM Framework Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Total Risk of Asset] --> B[Systematic Risk Beta]
    A --> C[Unsystematic Risk]
    C --> D[Diversifiable in Portfolio]
    B --> E[Non-Diversifiable]
    E --> F[CAPM Equation]
    F --> G[Expected Return E Ri]
    D --> H[Eliminated via Portfolio]
    H --> I[No Reward in Equilibrium]
    F --> J[SML Graphical Plot]
    J --> K[Above SML: Alpha > 0 Buy]
    J --> L[Below SML: Alpha less than 0 Sell]
    J --> M[On SML: Fairly Priced Hold]
```

## 4.3 CAPM vs APT Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Investment Model Selection] --> B{Number of Risk Factors?}
    B -->|Single Market Factor| C[Use CAPM]
    B -->|Multiple Macro Factors| D[Use APT]
    B -->|Size and Value and Market| E[Use Fama French 3 Factor]
    C --> F[Requires Market Portfolio]
    D --> G[No Market Portfolio Needed]
    E --> H[Empirical Multi Factor Model]
    F --> I[SML Beta based Valuation]
    G --> J[Factor Betas and Risk Premiums]
    H --> K[Market SMB HML Betas]
```

## 4.4 Performance Evaluation Process

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Portfolio Returns Data] --> B[Calculate Beta]
    A --> C[Calculate Standard Deviation]
    A --> D[Calculate Mean Return]
    B --> E[Treynor Ratio]
    C --> F[Sharpe Ratio]
    D --> G[Jensen Alpha]
    E --> H[Rank by Systematic Risk Reward]
    F --> I[Rank by Total Risk Reward]
    G --> J[Rank by Risk Adjusted Alpha]
    H --> K[Final Portfolio Selection]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**Q: Define the Security Market Line (SML). What does it mean when a security plots above the SML?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Security Market Line (SML)** is the graphical representation of the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**. It depicts the relationship between the **expected return** of a security (Y-axis) and its **systematic risk measured by Beta (β)** (X-axis), in equilibrium.

$$\text{SML Equation: } E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$$

When a security plots **above the SML**, it implies that the security's **actual expected return is higher than the return predicted by CAPM** for its level of systematic risk. Mathematically, this is equivalent to a **positive Jensen's Alpha ($\alpha > 0$)**.

> **Interpretation:** The security is **underpriced (undervalued)** in the market. A rational investor should **BUY** or **OVERWEIGHT** this security in their portfolio, as it offers excess return per unit of systematic risk.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: Distinguish between the Capital Market Line (CML) and the Security Market Line (SML).**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **CML** | **SML** |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Underlying Model | Two-fund separation (Tobin) | CAPM |
| Risk Measure on X-axis | Total Risk ($\sigma$) | Systematic Risk ($\beta$) |
| Applicable To | **Efficient portfolios only** | **All assets & portfolios** |
| Slope Represents | Sharpe Ratio of Market | Market Risk Premium |
| Source | Markowitz / Tobin | Sharpe, Lintner, Mossin |
| Depicts | Risk-Return trade-off for efficient portfolios | Equilibrium pricing of individual assets |

> **Key Distinction:** The CML applies *only* to efficient portfolios that are perfect combinations of the risk-free asset and the market portfolio. The SML extends the relationship to *all* individual assets, regardless of diversification status, using beta as the relevant risk measure.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** in detail. State its assumptions and derive the **Security Market Line (SML) equation**.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The **CAPM**, developed by **William Sharpe (1964)**, describes the equilibrium relationship between the **expected return** and **systematic risk** of an asset. It asserts that, in a competitive market, an asset's expected return is a linear function of its **non-diversifiable (beta) risk**.

**Assumptions of CAPM:**
1. Investors are **rational** and **risk-averse** (utility maximizers).
2. A **single-period** investment horizon exists for all investors.
3. **Homogeneous expectations** about returns, variances, and covariances.
4. Existence of a **risk-free asset** with borrowing and lending at $R_f$.
5. **No taxes, transaction costs, or restrictions** on short-selling.
6. All assets are **infinitely divisible and perfectly marketable**.
7. Markets are **perfectly competitive and informationally efficient**.

**Derivation of SML Equation:**

The SML is a straight line in $(β, E(R))$ space. Two points determine it:

- **Point 1:** $(\beta = 0, E(R) = R_f)$ — the risk-free asset.
- **Point 2:** $(\beta = 1, E(R) = E(R_m))$ — the market portfolio.

Let the line be $E(R_i) = a + b\beta_i$.

**Step 1 — Apply Point 1:**

$$R_f = a + b(0) \implies a = R_f$$

**Step 2 — Apply Point 2:**

$$E(R_m) = a + b(1) \implies E(R_m) = R_f + b \implies b = E(R_m) - R_f$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$\boxed{E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]}$$

> **Interpretation:** The intercept is $R_f$ and the slope is the **Market Risk Premium** $[E(R_m) - R_f]$. Every security in equilibrium plots ON this line.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given the following data, compute the **expected return** and **Jensen's Alpha** for each stock. Comment on whether the stocks are fairly priced, underpriced, or overpriced.

| Stock | Beta ($\beta$) | Actual Return ($R_i$) |
|-------|---------------|----------------------|
| A | 0.8 | 11% |
| B | 1.2 | 15% |
| C | 1.5 | 16% |

**Additional Data:** $R_f = 6\%$, $E(R_m) = 12\%$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the **Market Risk Premium**:

$$E(R_m) - R_f = 12\% - 6\% = 6\%$$

**Step 2:** Apply CAPM for each stock:

$$E(R_i) = 6\% + \beta_i \times 6\%$$

**For Stock A:** $E(R_A) = 6\% + 0.8 \times 6\% = 6\% + 4.8\% = 10.8\%$

**For Stock B:** $E(R_B) = 6\% + 1.2 \times 6\% = 6\% + 7.2\% = 13.2\%$

**For Stock C:** $E(R_C) = 6\% + 1.5 \times 6\% = 6\% + 9.0\% = 15.0\%$

**Step 3:** Calculate **Jensen's Alpha** for each stock ($\alpha_i = R_i - E(R_i)$):

| Stock | $\beta$ | $R_i$ | $E(R_i)$ (CAPM) | $\alpha_i$ | Verdict |
|-------|---------|-------|------------------|-----------|---------|
| A | 0.8 | 11% | 10.8% | **+0.2%** | Underpriced → **BUY** |
| B | 1.2 | 15% | 13.2% | **+1.8%** | Underpriced → **BUY** |
| C | 1.5 | 16% | 15.0% | **+1.0%** | Underpriced → **BUY** |

**Step 4: Conclusion:**
All three stocks have **positive alphas**, meaning they all plot *above* the SML. They are **underpriced** relative to CAPM equilibrium. A rational investor should **overweight** all three, with **Stock B** offering the highest risk-adjusted outperformance ($\alpha_B = +1.8\%$).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT)** model. How is it different from the **CAPM**? State the multi-factor APT equation.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The **Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT)**, developed by **Stephen Ross (1976)**, is a **multi-factor asset pricing model** that relates the expected return of an asset to its sensitivity to multiple macroeconomic risk factors, without requiring the existence of a market portfolio.

**APT Equation (n-factor form):**

$$\boxed{E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_{i1} RP_1 + \beta_{i2} RP_2 + \ldots + \beta_{in} RP_n}$$

Where:
- $E(R_i)$ = expected return of asset $i$
- $R_f$ = risk-free rate
- $\beta_{ik}$ = sensitivity (factor loading) of asset $i$ to factor $k$
- $RP_k$ = risk premium associated with factor $k$ (e.g., $E(R_k) - R_f$)

**Key Differences Between APT and CAPM:**

| Feature | **CAPM** | **APT** |
|---------|----------|---------|
| Number of Factors | Single (market) | Multiple (n) |
| Market Portfolio | Required | **Not required** |
| Distribution Assumption | Returns are normal | No distribution assumption |
| Basis | Equilibrium (mean-variance) | **Arbitrage (no-arbitrage)** |
| Assumptions | Many, restrictive | Fewer, more realistic |
| Empirical Test | Easier (single regression) | Harder (must identify factors) |
| Theoretical Foundation | Utility-based | Arbitrage-based |

**Example — Fama-French Three-Factor APT Model:**

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_{M}(R_m - R_f) + \beta_{SMB} \cdot SMB + \beta_{HML} \cdot HML$$

- **SMB (Small Minus Big):** Size premium.
- **HML (High Minus Low):** Value premium (book-to-market ratio).

> **Conclusion:** APT is more general and realistic than CAPM, but its practical implementation requires identifying the correct set of macroeconomic factors, which is empirical rather than theoretical.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a **two-factor APT model** with the following data. Calculate the expected return of the portfolio.

**Data:**
- Risk-free rate, $R_f = 5\%$
- Factor 1 (Inflation): Risk premium $RP_1 = 3\%$
- Factor 2 (Industrial Production): Risk premium $RP_2 = 4\%$
- Portfolio factor loadings: $\beta_1 = 0.7$, $\beta_2 = 1.3$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Recall the two-factor APT equation:

$$E(R_p) = R_f + \beta_1 \cdot RP_1 + \beta_2 \cdot RP_2$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$E(R_p) = 5\% + (0.7 \times 3\%) + (1.3 \times 4\%)$$

**Step 3:** Compute each term:

$$E(R_p) = 5\% + 2.1\% + 5.2\%$$

**Step 4:** Final answer:

$$\boxed{E(R_p) = 12.3\%}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation:**

The portfolio's expected return of **12.3%** exceeds the risk-free rate by **7.3 percentage points** in risk premiums. This excess is the compensation demanded by investors for bearing the systematic risks associated with inflation and industrial production sensitivities. Since both $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ are positive, the portfolio is positively exposed to both macro factors.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SML and CML:** Students frequently interchange the X-axis variables. Remember — **SML uses β (Beta), CML uses σ (Standard Deviation)**. Mixing them up is an instant 2-mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Formulas:** Always specify that returns are in **% per annum** and beta is **dimensionless**. Examiners check this.
>
> 3. **Misinterpreting Alpha Sign:** A positive $\alpha$ means **underpriced → BUY**. A negative $\alpha$ means **overpriced → SELL**. Do NOT confuse with "good return = positive alpha for the market."
>
> 4. **Skipping Assumptions:** When asked to "explain CAPM," **always list at least 5 assumptions** before the equation. Marks are explicitly allocated for assumptions.
>
> 5. **CAPM vs APT Confusion:** APT does NOT use the market portfolio. If you write "CAPM is a special case of APT" — you are correct, but be ready to justify it.
>
> 6. **Calculation Errors in Beta:** Beta is a **regression slope**, not a correlation. Do not write $\beta = \rho$; use the full formula $\beta = \text{Cov}(R_i, R_m) / \text{Var}(R_m)$.
>
> 7. **Sign of Risk Premium:** Always $[E(R_m) - R_f]$, **never** $[R_f - E(R_m)]$. The market premium is positive in normal markets.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 ULTIMATE REVISION CHECKLIST — MODULE 4**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- [ ] **SML** = CAPM in graphical form, X-axis = β, Y-axis = E(R).
- [ ] **CML** = Efficient portfolios only, X-axis = σ, Y-axis = E(R).
- [ ] **CAPM** = Single-factor equilibrium model, requires market portfolio.
- [ ] **APT** = Multi-factor no-arbitrage model, no market portfolio required.
- [ ] **Fama-French** = Three-factor APT extension (Market + Size + Value).
- [ ] **SML Slope** = Market Risk Premium = E(Rm) − Rf.
- [ ] **CML Slope** = Sharpe Ratio of Market = [E(Rm) − Rf] / σm.

### 📐 Must-Memorize Formulas
- [ ] $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$ (CAPM / SML)
- [ ] $\beta_i = \text{Cov}(R_i, R_m) / \text{Var}(R_m)$
- [ ] $E(R_p) = R_f + \{[E(R_m) - R_f]/\sigma_m\} \cdot \sigma_p$ (CML)
- [ ] $\text{Sharpe} = (R_p - R_f)/\sigma_p$
- [ ] $\text{Treynor} = (R_p - R_f)/\beta_p$
- [ ] $\text{Jensen's Alpha} = R_p - [R_f + \beta_p(R_m - R_f)]$
- [ ] APT: $E(R_i) = R_f + \sum \beta_{ik} RP_k$
- [ ] Fama-French: $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_M(R_m - R_f) + \beta_{SMB} \cdot SMB + \beta_{HML} \cdot HML$

### 🚦 Decision Rules
- [ ] **Above SML** → Underpriced → **BUY** ($\alpha > 0$)
- [ ] **Below SML** → Overpriced → **SELL** ($\alpha < 0$)
- [ ] **On SML** → Fairly priced → **HOLD** ($\alpha = 0$)
- [ ] **High Sharpe** → Better total risk-adjusted return
- [ ] **High Treynor** → Better systematic risk-adjusted return
- [ ] **High Jensen's Alpha** → Manager skill / outperformance

### 🏛️ Key Contributors to Remember
- [ ] **Harry Markowitz** (1952) — Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)
- [ ] **William Sharpe** (1964) — CAPM / SML
- [ ] **John Lintner** & **Jan Mossin** (1965–66) — Independent CAPM derivations
- [ ] **Stephen Ross** (1976) — APT
- [ ] **Eugene Fama & Kenneth French** (1993) — Three-Factor Model
- [ ] **Michael Jensen** (1968) — Performance evaluation (Alpha)

### ⚡ Quick-Fire Distinctions
- [ ] **Systematic Risk** = Market-wide = Non-diversifiable = Measured by β
- [ ] **Unsystematic Risk** = Firm-specific = Diversifiable = Measured by σe
- [ ] **CML applies only to efficient portfolios; SML applies to all assets.**
- [ ] **β = 1** means same volatility as market; **β > 1** = aggressive; **β < 1** = defensive.
- [ ] **Diversification eliminates unsystematic risk but NOT systematic risk.**
